## Page 44

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
Motor sport: the DNA of the brand
In 2024, Alpine continued its ascent in the world of motor
sport with a notable presence in several high-level
competitions. The Alpine Endurance Team finished 4th in the
World Endurance Championship in the Manufacturers
category, a performance for its first season in Hypercars
with Alpine A424. The BWT Alpine F1 Team stood out with a
double podium finish at the São Paulo Grand Prix.
Alpine has also confirmed its involvement in several other
disciplines: the Alpine ELF Cup Series (with the A110 Cup), the
Alpine ELF Rally trophy (with the A110 R-GT) and the
A110 Rallye GT+. Alpine will also be present on international
and national circuits with its Alpine GT4 range, particularly in
the FFSA French Championship and in the GT4 European
Series (by SRO).
Models for 2024
A110 R Turini, the “R” for on-road driving
Alpine revamped its A110 range by adding a new version of
the A110 R to its catalogue: the A110 R Turini. It is fitted with
18-inch GT Race Matte Black aluminium wheels for added
versatility on the road. Referring to the famous pass of the
same name in the southern French Alps, this car combines
speed, agility and vitality.
A110 R Ultime, the new ultra-sporty limited edition
Presented at the Paris Motor Show, the A110 R Ultime is Alpine’s
sportiest road-going model, with a 345  hp engine, combined
with 420 NM torque, delivering acceleration from 0 to 100 km/h
in 3.8 seconds. The promise of a sporty experience, combined
with an unprecedented customisation programme with
Poltrona Frau®. This ultra-exclusive series is available in a
limited edition of 110.
Alpenglow Hy6, the hydrogen-powered prototype equipped
with an Alpine 6-cylinder engine
After unveiling the concept car in 2022, Alpine presented an
initial operational version with a 4-cylinder engine at the
24 Hours of Le Mans (Alpenglow Hy4). Unveiled at the Paris
Motor Show, this new hydrogen-powered version is fitted
with a new 6-cylinder internal combustion engine, boosted
from 340 to 740 horsepower compared with the Hy4 version.
Its design has also been conceived to highlight the engine
and optimise aerodynamic performance.
1.3.1.3 Alpine: ambitions driven by success

Highlights of 2024
A290 launches the Alpine Dream Garage
2024 marked the entry of Alpine into the electric era, a turning point
in its history. Alpine introduced A290, its first electric sports city-
fleet car at the 24 Hours of Le Mans, and the showcar of its future
A390 sports fastback. These two vehicles, together with the future
electric A110, will make up Alpine’s 100% electric Dream Garage
devised by the brand.
Sporting commitment
The iconic A110
Alpenglow Hy6
Alpine is one of the most committed car brands in motor sport,
with two major competitions, Formula 1 and the World Endurance
Championship. Alpine is also involved in other sports disciplines
such as Padel and the America’s Cup.
Alpine also launched an
exclusive series of 110 units of
the ultra-sporty A110 R, dubbed
“Ultime”.
Two years after the 2022
concept car, Alpine presented
Alpenglow Hy6, a hydrogen-
powered prototype equipped
with a 6-cylinder engine fully
developed by Alpine, at the
2024 Paris Motor Show.

General presentation of Renault Group
01
Alpine embarks on its electric journey
and continues its international expansion
Within Renault Group, Alpine stands out as the brand at the
cutting edge of innovation with a range of exclusive sports
cars. In 2024, Alpine expanded its A110 range with the
A110  R  Turini and the A110 R Ultime and launched a brand-
new model: A290. The brand’s first sporty city car, it’s also its
first electric car. By 2030, Alpine will develop a range of
seven new electric models, starting with the three from the
Dream Garage: A290, A390 and electric A110. The ambition is
to expand the range while continuing to develop lightweight,
agile sports cars. Alpine is investing in developing its own
APP (Alpine Performance Platform) for its future electric
vehicles and converting its Dieppe plant to produce the
A390 in 2025.
A290, Alpine’s first sporty city car
A290 brings together the sensations much appreciated
behind the wheel of A110 in the body of a sporty city car.
Perfectly tuned in line with Alpine’s DNA, A290 is compact,
agile, and offers high performance that is easily exploitable,
all while ensuring comfortable everyday use.
With an electric ecosystem and complete connectivity, it is a
next-generation Alpine designed to offer an optimal
experience. This model is also designed to be high-end and
immersive, featuring a Nappa leather-covered cockpit and
a Devialet high-fidelity audio system.
A390_ β, the showcar of the future Alpine fastback sport
Alpine’s Dream Garage continues to expand with the arrival
of A390_ β, the forerunner of the brand’s first electric
fastback sports car. This new product offers a glimpse of the
future production model, designed for everyday use but with
a sporty DNA. A true emblematic creation of French know-
how, the A390_ β is a blend of boldness and innovation,
drawing its inspiration from the mountain ranges of the Alps.
It boasts a dynamic silhouette, a multi-faceted blue hue and
a brand light in the tradition of Alpenglow.
Alpine is continuing its international expansion by opening
up in new countries, such as Türkiye, and is rolling out a
concept store network in major European cities. Alpine has
opened its first “Atelier Alpine“ in Barcelona in June, before
moving on Paris and London in 2025.
Inclusion and diversity
Launched in 2022, the Rac(h)er programme is actively
pursuing its activities in favour of inclusion and equal
opportunities. Alpine has also renewed its commitment to
young people, in particular with the organisation of the third
edition of the Alpine Mechanical Excellence Competition
in 2024.
And to bolster these two projects and make them more
widely known, Zinédine Zidane joined the Alpine at the
beginning of 2023 as ambassador and sponsor of its equal
opportunity and electric development programmes.
Even further in 2025
Founded in 1955 by Jean Rédélé, Alpine will celebrate its
70th anniversary in 2025. An opportunity to reminisce about
history but also to demonstrate a passion and innovations,
constantly renewed, passed on by the brand in the world of
automobiles.

## Page 46

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
1.3.1.4 Main production sites
Map of Renault Group manufacturing sites: 25 production sites
Production figures
To meet the demands of its customers, Renault Group relies
on an industrial footprint consisting of 25 industrial sites
(excluding HORSE sites) located around the world as close
as possible to the markets in which it sells its brands’
vehicles.
All these sites operate under common principles:
●making employee safety a priority;
●making customer satisfaction a priority;
●ongoing work to improve the competitiveness of our sites
through the widespread implementation of the first
industrial and quality Metaverse;
●integration of the value chain.
Renault Group’s strategic partnerships offer opportunities
for synergies based on the pooling of production resources
and enable us to increase the industrial activity of our sites.
Thus:
●the plants at Batilly, Electricity Maubeuge (Ampere),
Sandouville and Córdoba (in Argentina) produce vehicles
for Nissan;
●Electricity Maubeuge (Ampere) produces vehicles for
Mercedes-Benz Group;
●the
Sandouville
and
Batilly
plants
produce
light
commercial vehicles for Renault Trucks;
●the plants in Valladolid (Spain) and Bursa (Oyak-Renault in
Türkiye) produce for Mitsubishi;
●the Renault Korea Motors plant in Busan (South Korea) will
produce for Geely;
●the Nissan plant in Chennai (India) produces vehicles for
Renault Group;
●in Shiyan (China), eGT produces the Spring electric vehicle
for the Dacia brand.

## Page 48

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
1.3.1.5 Renault Group Sales Network
1.3.1.5.1 Organisation of the
distribution networks
Renault Group distributes the vehicles under its brands
through brand networks, comprising a primary and
secondary network.
The
primary
network
includes
companies
that
are
independent of Renault Group and establishments owned
by Renault Group through its subsidiary Renault Retail Group
(RRG).
These
companies
and
establishments
are
contractually bound to Renault Group through a distribution
contract (or importer contract depending on the country).
The secondary network includes companies independent of
Renault Group, contractually bound to the primary network,
most often through a service agent or authorised repair
centre contract. The purpose of the secondary network is to
supplement the territorial coverage of the primary network
to offer the best service quality to all Renault Group brand
customers.
Since January 2024, a new distribution contract has been in
force for the Renault and Dacia brands in most European
countries. This partnership agreement, drawn up in close
collaboration with the Renault Europe dealer group (GCRE),
reaffirms Renault Group’s strong commitment to dealer
distribution. Renault Group has thus confirmed its ambition
to ensure sustainable and profitable growth for the
Manufacturer and for its network by relying on strong
independent players who promote the image of Renault
Group’s brands.
In 2025, Renault Group will continue to update the visual
identities of the dealerships so that each showroom
perfectly reflects the universe, spirit and identity of each
brand. The presence of the renew label (used vehicles) will
also be stepped up at points of sale.
In 2025, Renault Group will also launch a programme to
support
its
distribution
network
in
improving
its
environmental and economic performance beyond the
purely regulatory framework. This programme will help
structure the environmental and ESG approach at each
point of sale, making it easier for distributors to produce
CSRD reports.
To support the acceleration of electric vehicle sales, Renault
has a complete E-Tech range for both private and business
customers. The entire Renault Group distribution network is
equipped with electric charging solutions enabling our
customers to drive with complete peace of mind and is also
committed
to
developing
modern
fast
charging
infrastructures, both in dealerships and in high-potential
areas. Renault Group also relies on its distribution network to
market new mobility and energy services through the
Mobilize brand.
To ensure the highest level of customer satisfaction, Renault
Group ensures that its methods, processes and skills are
kept up to date by training its networks in both Sales and
After-Sales, in particular through its own training centre:
Renault Academy.
Renault Pro+: the expert utility vehicle network for
Professionals
The
Renault 
Pro+
network
serves
and
supports
professionals in more than 20 countries. The Pro+ network,
entirely dedicated to professionals, has a wide range of
electric and combustion-engine vehicles, from traditional
light commercial vehicles to customised and converted
vehicles. This network relies on expert sales teams to
propose the solution best suited to customers’ needs and on
an after-sale offering focused on the needs and challenges
of professionals.
Nearly 600 Renault  Pro+ centres make up this specialised
network, which applies specific service standards that meet
the requirements of business customers.
46

1.3.1.5.2 Renault Retail Group (RRG)
RRG has a presence in five European countries through 104
points of sale and service: Germany, France, the UK,
Switzerland and Spain.
RRG, a wholly owned subsidiary of Renault, is the leading
Renault Group distributor in Europe for the sale of vehicles
and associated services, as well as after-sales.
RRG’s mission is to distribute all products and services
(Renault, Dacia, Alpine and Nissan in some countries) to
business and individual customers.
Activities cover new vehicles, used vehicles and spare parts
and also includes maintenance, mechanics, bodywork,
express repairs (Renault Minute Services), short-term rental
(Mobilize), mobility services, financing and brokerage.
In 2024, RRG acquired the two Lyon Ouest sites in France
from the Thivolle group and sold the Liverpool and Wirral
sites to the Arnold Clark group in the United Kingdom.
Profitability in line with the Group’s objectives, continuing to
be positive despite the decline in the Used Vehicle market.
This profitability exceeded €50  million by the end of
December, driven by the growth in NV and after-sales
activities.

General presentation of Renault Group
1.3.2 Finance and mobility services
Brand strategy
Attentive to the needs of all its customers, Mobilize
Financial Services1 creates innovative financing services to
contribute to sustainable mobility for all.
To support Renault Group in its ambitions to contribute to a
more sustainable mobility, Mobilize Financial Services draws
on its 100 years of expertise, its commercial and financial
performance, and a portfolio of over 4 million customers,
whose satisfaction continues to grow.
The products
1. Tailor-made offerings for each type of customer
For Retail customers, we offer financing solutions and
services tailored to their projects and usage, aiming to
facilitate, accompany, and enhance their experience
throughout their automotive mobility journey. Our solutions
and services apply to both new and used vehicles.
For Business customers, we provide a wide range of mobility
solutions to free them from the constraints associated with
managing their vehicle fleet, allowing them to focus on their
core business.
For the Renault Group Network and its partner brands
Nissan and Mitsubishi2, we provide active support by
financing stocks of new vehicles, used vehicles, and spare
parts, as well as addressing short‑term cash flow needs.
1 RCI Banque S.A. has operated under the commercial name RCI Bank and Services since February 2016 and adopted Mobilize Financial Services as its new
commercial identity in May 2022. Its legal name remains unchanged as RCI Banque S.A. This commercial name, as well as its acronym Mobilize F.S., may be used
by the group as an alias for its legal name.RCI Banque S.A. and its subsidiaries may be referred to as the “Mobilize F.S. group”.
2 Mobilize Financial Services supports Renault Group’s brands (Renault, Dacia, Alpine & Renault Korea Motors) worldwide, the Nissan Group (Nissan) mainly in
Europe, Brazil, Argentina, South Korea and in the form of joint ventures in India, and Mitsubishi Motors in France, the Netherlands and Italy.

2. The savings bank business, a pillar of corporate
refinancing
Launched in 2012, the savings business activity is present in
six markets: France, Germany, Austria, the United Kingdom,
Spain, and the Netherlands. Deposits collection serves as a
lever to diversify the refinancing sources for the group’s
operations. The amounts collected totaled €30.5 billion, i.e.
around 50% of the net assets at the end of December 2024.
3. More than 4,000 employees fully committed to creating
sustainable mobility for all
Mobilize Financial Services focuses on four key priorities:
●Offers based on usage throughout the vehicle’s life cycle:
Meet the changing mobility needs of individual and
professional
customers,
Mobilize
Financial
Services
continues to develop loyalty‑based long‑term leasing offers
with the goal of developing a pan‑European range of offers
for new and used vehicles.
●Insurance and services adapted to the new mobility
needs:
New offers will be tested and rolled out according to the
value provided to our customers and to Renault Group, to
cover new uses and actual customer needs.
●The continuous evolution of information systems:
Mobilize Financial Services continues to invest to transform
its digital tools so that it can benefit from the latest
technological standards and increased flexibility in the
management of its activities. These changes are carried out
with particular attention to the customer experience, in
compliance
with
cybersecurity
and
data
protection
requirements.
●Operational excellence:
The group will take the greatest care to improve its
efficiency, simplifying and harmonizing its processes for all
its activities. 
In pursuit of these strategic goals, Mobilize Financial Services
relies on two fundamental levers:
– Consolidating the management of the sustainable
development strategy, in line with Renault Group’s ESG
requirements
– Managing risks and ensuring compliance throughout
the Group to protect its customers and activities
Sales Activity3 2024
Mobilize Financial Services new financings increased by
2.4% compared with 2023, thanks to the growth of the
registrations of Renault Group, Nissan and Mitsubishi
brands, the average financed amounts, and MeinAuto
acquisition.
In an automotive market up by 2.3%4, the volumes of Renault
Group, Nissan and Mitsubishi brands stood at 2.25 million
vehicles in 2024, up 3.9%. The penetration rate  amounts to
42.3%, down 1.1pt compared with 2023. Electric new vehicle
financing penetration rate reached  45% in 2024, i.e. +2.9pt
compared with the penetration rate on other type of engines.
Mobilize Financial Services financed 1,282,066 contracts in
2024, a stable volume compared with 2023 (+0.6%). Used Car
Financing decreased by 5.9% compared with 2023, reaching
310,747 financed contracts. 
New financings (excluding credit cards and personal loans)
stood at €21.5 billion, up 2.4% thanks to the growth  of the
registrations, the increase of the average financed amount
and the acquisition of MeinAuto, in the beginning of 2024. 
Average performing assets (APA)5 related to the Retail and
Professional Activity totalled €41.5 billion in 2024. The
amount increased by 10.8%, thanks to the progression
observed on the new financings since the beginning of 2023
and the integration of MeinAuto’s portfolio in the beginning
of 2024. Average performing assets (APA) related to the
Wholesale
Activity
amounted
to
€10.9
billion,
up
4.2%. Overall, average performing assets totalled €56 billion,
up 9.4% compared with 2023. 
Mobilize Financial Services sold 3.7  million in service and
insurance contracts in 2024, down 4.4% compared with 2023.
Europe remains the region where the bulk of Mobilize
Financial Services business is concentrated, with new
financings (excluding credit cards and personal loans)
totalizing  €19.7  billion, up 2.2% compared with 2023, and
representing 92% of Mobilize  Financial Services new
financings.  
For Americas, the new financings are down 1.1% compared
with 2023, reaching  €1.3 billion, linked to  growth of the
registrations in Brazil.
New financings for Africa – Middle East – India and Pacific
amounted to €0.5 billion, up 17.5% compared with 2023. This
increase is mainly due to growth of Mobilize Financial
Services business in Morocco.

2024 consolidated financial information
Mobilize Financial Services posted strong financial growth
in its results, which confirms the relevance of its strategy.
Results
The Net Banking Income  stood at €2,180 million,  up
11.2%  compared with 2023. This increase results from the
growth in outstandings, the non‑reoccurrence of a negative
impact on the valuation of swaps observed in 2023 and the
acquisition of MeinAuto at the beginning of 2024.
Services activity’s contribution to the Net Banking Income is
down 2.8pt compared with 2023, representing 34%. 
The Operating Expenses  totalized €727 million, up 22 M€
compared with 2023, due to the integration of MeinAuto’s
operating expenses in 2024.  The Operating Expenses
represent 1.30% of the Average Performing Assets, meaning
an improvement of 8 base point compared with 2023.
The Cost of Risk stood 0.31% of the APA in 2024, compared
0.30% in 2023.
The Result Before Tax lands at €1,194 million compared with
€ 1,034 million in 2023, thanks to the Net Banking Income
increase. The share of result attributable to equity affiliated
companies is up €14 million.

Balance sheet
In 2024, assets grew, driven by the increase in new financing
since the beginning of 2023 and the integration of Mein
Auto’s portfolio in early 2024.
At the end of the year, net assets reached €61.0  billion
compared with €54.7 billion in 2023, an increase of 11.6%.
Profitability
ROE6 increased to 15.67% compared with 12.95% in 2023.
RoRWA7 was 2.34% for 2024, an increase of +24bps
compared with 2023 due to the increase in pre-tax profit.
Solvency
The overall solvency ratio8 was 17.69% (including CET1 ratio
of 13.96%) at the end of 2024, compared with 16.05%
(including CET1 ratio of 13.88%) at the end of December 2023.
The increase in the global ratio is explained by an increase in
CET1 equity (+€504 million), mainly related to the integration
of the result deducted from the expected dividend.
This increase in the global ratio is also explained by an
increase in Tier 2 equity (+€742  million) following the
issuance of subordinated debt.
The change in equity is partially offset by an increase in REA9
(+€3,374  million) due to the growth in activity since early
2023

01
Financial policy
The decline in inflation has paved the way for a new phase
of monetary policy, characterized by the start of key rate
cuts. The European Central Bank (ECB) was the first to act in
June 2024, implementing four consecutive 25 basis point
reductions, totaling a 100‑basis point decrease. This trend is
expected to continue in 2025 to support European economic
growth. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve (FED) implemented
three rate cuts, totaling 100 basis points, but remains more
cautious about further reductions due to the strength of the
U.S economy and potential inflationary risks.
In the United States, maintaining high key rates has helped
reduce inflation. from 3.4%. After reaching a peak of 3.5% at
the end of March 2024, inflation decreased from 3.4% at the
end of 2023 to 2.7% by the end of November 2024. Economic
growth was dynamic in 2024, averaging 2.6%, compared to
3.2% in 2023. The job market remained strong but showed
signs of slowing down. Indeed, job creation declined, and the
unemployment rate increased since March 2024, reaching
4.2% in November. As a result, the Fed began easing its
monetary policy. After keeping key rates unchanged since
July 2023, it reduced its key rates by 50 basis points in
September, followed by 25 basis points in November and
December 2024. The market expects two more rate cuts in
2025, bringing the rate to 3.9%.
In Europe, the tightening of monetary policy by the ECB since
2022 helped lower inflation from 2.9% at the end of 2023 to
2.4% at the end of December 2024. The interest rate cuts
started in June 2024 opened a new phase of monetary
normalization. The ECB has thus reduced its rates four times
(a total of 100 basis points) since 2024. The market
anticipates four more cuts in 2025 (100 basis points), mainly
in the first half, to reach a level of 2% in June. The goal is to
reach this neutral rate, or even a lower level in the event of
low growth in the major European countries (Q3 GDP: 0.9%
annual). The institution has given no indication regarding its
upcoming monetary policy decisions and reiterated the
dependence of decisions on the available economic data at
each meeting.
In the United Kingdom, inflation has significantly decreased,
falling from 4% at the end of 2023 to 1.7% at the end of
September 2024, and stabilizing at 2.6% at year end. The
economy has slightly rebounded, with a GDP growing by
0.9% at the end of September, compared to -0.3% at the end
of 2023. The labor market is normalizing, with the
unemployment rate returning to 4.3%, close to end of 2023.
Wage growth significantly accelerated in the first half of the
year, then slowed down reaching a low of 3.8% at the end of
August before rising to 4.6% at the end of October,
compared to 5.8% at the end of 2023. The general elections
in July 2024 marked the return to power of the Labor Party
after 15 years of Conservative government. The new
government’s priority is to restore growth after a prolonged
period of economic instability. The Bank of England (BOE)
cut its key interest rate twice by 25bps in July and
November 2024. The market expects two more 25bps cut in
2025, bringing the rate to 4.1% by the end of 2025.
In this environment of monetary easing both short‑term and
long‑term government bond yields increased in the first half
of the year, before following opposite trajectories in the
second half. The yields of 2‑year German bonds dropped by
30bps to 2.1% at the end of December, compared to 2.4% at
the end of 2023 (with a peak of 3.08% in mid‑June). The yields
on 10‑year German bonds rose by 34bps, reaching 2.4% at
the end of December, compared to 2.02% at the beginning
of January (reaching a high of 2.67% in mid‑June). 
Stock markets continue to show strong performance
despite some periods of brief reversals. US and European
stocks benefit from the decline in inflation and the strength
of corporate earnings in an uncertain economic and
geopolitical context. The Eurostoxx 50 and the S&P 500 have
risen by +8.3% and +23.3% respectively since end of 2023.
After having widened in early 2024, the IBOXX Corporate
Bond Euro index, which had closed the year 2023 at 91bps,
tightened until early July, reaching a low point of 82 bps.
Since early October, it has begun to widen sharply, reaching
99 basis points by the end of December. 
In this context, the group issued the equivalent of €5.1 billion
on the bond market in 2024. The group made six senior
public Euro issuances with maturities of respectively
of  2  years (€400 million), 3.5 years (€800 million), 4 years
(€600 million green bond), 5 years (€600 million increased to
€700 million in October), 6 years (€800 million) and 7 years
(€700 million), as well as a 5‑year and  a CHF issuance of
5  years (CHF 120 million). The polish subsidiary also issued
two 3‑year bonds for a  totalling of PLN 850 million.
Additionally, the group also  strengthened its capital
structure by issuing a second strain of Tier 2 10.25Y NC 5.25
subordinated debt (€750 million).
In the securitization market, the group launched two public
operations during 2024. A transaction of €822 million
backed by automobile loans granted by its German branch
was placed during the first half of 2024. The second
transaction was issued for €765 million backed by credits
granted by its French subsidiary. The Italian branch also set
up during the second half of 2024, its first private
securitization of automobile loans for a financing amount of
€600 million. The private securitizations of car loans in the
United Kingdom, leasing in Germany and the residual value
component of LOA contracts in France have seen their
renewable period extended for two additional years. The
amount of the first two operations was slightly increased to
reach £700 million in the United Kingdom and €450 million in
Germany.
The savings collection activity remained competitive in
terms of the cost of the collected resources. The
outstanding of savings increased by €2.3 billion since the
beginning of the year to reach €30.5 billion.

General presentation of Renault Group
In addition, Mobilize Financial Services has strengthened its
liquidity risk monitoring system, introducing multiple liquidity
crisis scenarios whose severity and speed of spread vary.
These scenarios include stress assumptions on market
funding and massive outflows of deposits. The bank has
defined a business continuity horizon for each scenario and
regularly performs stress tests to assess its resilience. In
December 2024, the liquidity reserve (European scope)
amounted to €14.8bn, making it possible to meet the appetite
for internal business continuity risk in each liquidity stress
scenario. This reserve breaks down into €4.4bn in undrawn
confirmed bank lines, €4.6bn in collateral eligible for central
bank monetary policy transactions, €5.6bn in high‑quality
liquid assets (HQLA) and €0.2bn in financial assets. 
RCI Banque’s overall sensitivity to interest rate risk
remained below group’s limit of €70 million.
On 31 December 2024, a parallel rate increase would have an
impact on the Group’s net interest margin (NIM) of -
€10.2 million, with the following contribution per currency

General presentation of Renault Group
01
Brand strategy
Mobilize is the Renault Group brand dedicated to new forms
of mobility. It was created in January 2021, alongside the
longstanding Renault, Dacia and Alpine brands.
By offering a complete range of services, Mobilize simplifies
access to new mobility: financing solutions (through
Mobilize Financial Services), electric vehicle charging
solutions and mobility solutions, including the Duo & Bento
quadricycles.
For individuals, companies, operators and territories.
Mobilize goes beyond the automobile:
●towards mobility that is more environmentally friendly
and accessible to all;
●towards better energy management;
●towards solutions tailored to the needs of people and
cities.
Mobility: vehicles and services
Vehicles
Duo and Bento are aimed at both individuals and
professionals. Duo has two seats and can be driven with or
without a licence, depending on the version and local
regulations. The Bento utility version, reserved for holders of
a driving licence, has a 649-litre closed boot in place of the
passenger seat for transporting small equipment or goods.
Duo and Bento are compact (2.43 and 2.54 metres long
respectively) and extremely manoeuvrable in city traffic.
Their small footprint makes parking easy.
Duo and Bento offer a unique driving experience thanks to
the driver’s central position and optimised suspension
comfort. They are equipped as standard with a driver-side
airbag, a unique feature in the quadricycle segment. Their
generous range – up to 161 km for Duo and 149 km for Bento –
allows for longer intervals between recharges.
Designed with eco-responsibility in mind, Duo and Bento use
over 40% recycled materials and are 95% recyclable.
Duo and Bento will be sold in the Renault network, while Duo
has been available to order in France, Italy and Belgium
since October 2024.

## Page 56

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
Technological solutions and services
for mobility
iCabbi
iCabbi is a cloud-based fleet management platform
designed for taxis and private companies. It provides tools
to streamline driver booking, dispatching and management,
improving operational efficiency and customer service.
iCabbi helps fleets modernise and compete in the ever-
changing transport market.
This was an important year for iCabbi with two new
acquisitions:
Moovex, which marks a significant step forward in
innovation for taxi and non-emergency medical transport
(NEMT) operators with the launch of a brand-new artificial
intelligence product Move AI. Move AI harnesses the power
of machine learning and artificial intelligence to provide
fleet managers with advanced real-time route optimisation
and re-optimisation capabilities.
Javelin has been iCabbi’s payment partner since 2020. The
integration of Javelin into iCabbi’s organisational structure
ensures a seamless relationship between the iCabbi and
Javelin platforms, from both a technical and commercial
perspective.
Power
In addition to the commercial launch of V2G and the opening
of the first Fast Charge stations in France, Mobilize is
continuing to market its services designed to make
everyday life in an electric vehicle easier and therefore
facilitate its adoption.
Charging on the road
●“Mobilize Charge Pass”: a payment card giving access to
a network of over 800,000 charging points in 25 European
countries. It includes the IONITY rapid-charge network.
The map is linked to the My Renault and My Alpine apps,
which locate charging stations and indicate the best
routes to them.
●Mobilize Fast Charge: an ultra-fast charging network for
all makes of electric vehicles, launched in partnership
with the Renault dealer network.
Charging at home or at work
●Mobilize Power Box: a smart, connected, secure, two-way
charging station for some versions. The charging station
is manufactured at the Symbiose electronics plant in
Lacroix (Maine-et-Loire, France) with an initial capacity of
65,000 units/year.
●“Mobilize Smart Charge“: an app allowing Renault EV
owners to optimise the cost of charging their car at home
while reducing their carbon footprint. The app is available
in France, the Netherlands, Belgium and the UK.
●“V2G” Vehicle To Grid: electric vehicles equipped with
V2G technology can act as energy storage units. By
harnessing the battery’s energy and returning electricity
to the grid during peak consumption, for example, V2G
technology benefits both the entire power grid and the
owners of these next-generation cars.
Facilitating the sale of second-hand electric
vehicles
●“Battery Certificate”: this service is now provided by
Ampere. With this app, owners of Renault and Dacia
electric vehicles in France can generate a certificate
showing their battery’s remaining capacity directly on
their smartphone and the internet.
Mobilize is also working downstream and is committed to
carbon neutrality, the circular economy and extending the life
cycle of vehicles. Within the Flins Refactory,  the brand is
centralising initiatives to extend the life cycle of vehicles and
batteries. It is developing solutions for the reuse of batteries,
particularly for energy storage solutions.

1.3.3 HORSE
1.3.3.1 Overview
Renault Group and Geely, following the agreements signed
on 11 July 2023, and after the approval of the competent
authorities, announced the official creation of HORSE
Powertrain Limited on 31 May 2024. Renault Group and Geely
Group each held a 50% stake in this company.
On 28 June 2024, Aramco (Saudi Aramco, an integrated
global energy and chemical company based in Saudi
Arabia, one of the world’s largest oil company in terms of
production) signed agreements to acquire a 10% stake in
HORSE Powertrain.
On 2 December 2024, HORSE Powertrain Limited finalised
the acquisition of a 10% stake by Aramco, valuing the
company at €7.4  billion. Renault Group and Geely each
retained a 45% stake.
HORSE Powertrain is the global leader in the market for
internal combustion engines, transmissions and hybrids. It
designs, develops, produces and sells all solutions and
systems for engines, transmissions, hybrid systems (xHEV),
and batteries. HORSE Powertrain aims to become a unique,
top-tier partner offering cutting-edge technologies at a
competitive cost, contributing to the reduction of global
vehicle emissions.
With this project, both Renault Group and Geely benefit from
immediate economies of scale, better market coverage and a
complementary product portfolio and regional footprint, which
allows them to offer solutions for 80% of a growing global
powertrain market. HORSE Powertrain will allow Renault Group
to achieve greater competitiveness in engine costs. This is
estimated at over €2 billion between 2024 and 2030.
Thanks to the intellectual property transfer from Renault
Group and Geely, HORSE Powertrain is autonomous in
developing future engine and transmission technologies
that the market will need, particularly in the field of
alternative fuels such as green methanol, ethanol and
hydrogen. Aramco’s investment will accelerate these
developments. Furthermore, as part of the deal, Aramco and
its subsidiary, Valvoline Global Operations, will collaborate
with HORSE Powertrain on innovation in thermal engines,
fuels and lubricants.
HORSE Powertrain will enable the Renault Group to benefit
from the best alternative fuels and thermal hydrogen
solutions, and to offer an attractive range of low-carbon
solutions based on internal combustion vehicles and light
commercial vehicles. 
HORSE Powertrain is ready to serve all customers and
partners worldwide, offering them comprehensive solutions
in powertrain technologies and welcoming partners to
strengthen the value chain coverage.
1.3.3.2 Governance
Renault Group and Geely, as the investor shareholders of the
joint venture, have approved the overall governance framework
and the strategic appointments of HORSE Powertrain.
Matias Giannini has been appointed CEO of HORSE
Powertrain Limited.
The management of the two subgroups Aurobay and HORSE,
grouping
industrial
assets
and
know-how,
remains
unchanged: Ruiping Wang, CEO of Aurobay, based in
Hangzhou (China), and Patrice Haettel, CEO of HORSE, based
in Madrid (Spain). They report directly to Matias Giannini.
Following Aramco’s entry, the board of directors of HORSE
Powertrain is now composed of seven members:
●3 directors appointed by Geely, including Daniel Donghui
Li (Vice President of Geely Auto and CEO of Geely Holding)
as chairman of the board;
●3 directors appointed by Renault Group;
●1 director appointed by Aramco.
1.3.3.3 Operations
With a strategic focus on China, Europe and Latin America,
HORSE Powertrain, headquartered in London (UK), operates
in seven countries (Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Spain, Portugal,
Romania and Türkiye) through its HORSE division based in
Madrid (Spain) and two countries (China and Sweden)
through its Aurobay division based in Hangzhou (China).
HORSE Powertrain brings together the industrial expertise
and assets of Renault Group and Geely in the development,
production and supply of next-generation low-emission
thermal, hybrid powertrains and offers a complete range of
technologies for all components: engine, gearbox, hybrid
system (xHEV), and batteries, at the highest level.
The new group has 17 manufacturing plants for mechanical
engines and transmissions and 5 research and development
centers, with 19,000 employees contributing.
This new structure, based on a solid and global industrial
base, is an autonomous entity that provides complete
powertrains
in
a
highly
integrated
model:
design,
engineering, production, sales and after-sales, finance, etc.
It serves its current industrial customers, including Renault
Group, Geely Auto, Volvo Cars, Proton, Nissan, and Mitsubishi
Motors Company, as well as other industrial customers,
across 130 countries.
HORSE Powertrain is a platform serving the Group by
becoming a global supplier for Renault Group brands and
members of the Alliance, but also aims to become a supplier to
other automakers through innovative agreements, with
forecasts of approximately 5  million powertrains produced
annually.

1.3.4 Renault-Nissan-Mitsubishi alliance
1.3.4.1 Overview
2024 was devoted to the implementation of the New
Alliance Agreement between Renault Group and Nissan,
which entered into force on 8 November 2023. This
agreement marked an important milestone for the three
partners. It laid the foundation for a new long-term,
balanced, efficient and agile collaboration that will
maximise value creation for each of its members and all our
stakeholders. This new Alliance, focused on concrete
projects with high added value, will continue its efforts to
identify key projects in all markets, creating additional
growth
opportunities,
relying
on
each
company’s
technologies and improving their operational efficiency. It
will boost their innovations and their transformation in the
rapidly changing automotive and new mobility markets.
In 2024, a year of transition, Renault Group, Nissan and
Mitsubishi Motors focused on developing joint projects in
connection with the revised processes associated with the
New
Alliance
Agreement,
as
well
as
finding
new
opportunities for synergies. These opportunities (vehicles
but also new automotive ecosystems) aim to achieve
mutually beneficial, large-scale and tangible results along
three dimensions: markets, vehicles and technologies. Each
company will benefit from these projects that create value
and generate synergies such as significant short-term cost
reductions.
1.3.4.2 Operational projects with high
value creation
In Latin America
●The successful collaboration on the existing family of 1 ton
Nissan Frontier/Renault Alaskan pick-ups will continue at
least until the end of 2025: Renault Group will continue to
produce pick-ups in Córdoba (Argentina) for Renault
Group and Nissan.
●In addition, in 2023, Nissan announced the new
generation of Kicks for Latin America, based on the
CMF-B platform.
In India
●For the domestic market and export, Renault Group and
Nissan will collaborate on several new vehicle projects,
including 4 new SUVs, and a new Nissan car derived from
Renault Triber, which will complement the existing line on
the A platform.
In Europe
●The ASX and COLT models by Mitsubishi Motors,
marketed in its network, have been developed and are
already manufactured by Renault. They are based on the
CMF-B platform (which already carries Clio & Captur); this
cooperation is likely to extend to other projects between
Renault Group and Mitsubishi Motors.
●In the field of electric vehicles, Ampere, the new Renault
Group entity dedicated to electric vehicles and software,
will develop their first-ever C-segment electric SUV for
Mitsubishi Motors, which will market it under its brand.
This vehicle will first be launched on the European market
and will be rolled out internationally.
●Building on its dual expertise in commercial vehicles, on
the one hand, and electric mobility on the other, Renault
Group will launch its all-new generation of electric vans in
Europe in 2026, the first vehicles to benefit from the
application of Software-Defined Vehicle technology
developed by Ampere and could share them with Nissan
as part of joint projects.
●These initiatives will reinforce previous commitments,
including Nissan’s future compact electric vehicle
(B-segment), based on the CMF-BEV platform, which is
under development and will be produced by Ampere at
Renault Group’s ElectriCity plant in France from 2026.
Nissan
also
plans
to
entrust
Ampere
with
the
development of its next A-segment electric vehicle.
Also in Europe, the scope of collaboration between Renault
Group, Nissan and Mitsubishi Motors already extends beyond
the vehicle itself, and this scope of collaboration will continue
to expand:
●for years, Renault Group and Nissan have been
capitalising on Mobilize’s strong presence in Europe, both
in network financing and that of customers, but also in
insurance and services;
●in the area of the circular economy, Nissan plans to
acquire a stake in The Future is NEUTRAL, the entity
created by Renault Group, which will enable it to address
key activities such as the treatment of end-of-life vehicles
and the recovery of raw materials from these centres or
production sites with the best efficiency, in a context of
stricter regulations.
All these projects are therefore in line with
the successes and initiatives already launched,
which they confirm and complement
●The common platform for the C and D segments carries
Renault Austral, Rafale and Espace, Nissan Qashqai,
Rogue and X-Trail, Mitsubishi Outlander. All these cars are
now on the market and have benefited from the “smart
differentiation” logic developed by Renault Group, Nissan
and Mitsubishi Motors. This logic guarantees, for each
vehicle, the necessary level of specificities (design,
features, brand identifiers, etc.) while defining the desired
sharing concerning platforms, plants, powertrains, to
generate economies of scale.
●The five common electrical platforms currently cover
most of the markets in the largest regions:
– CMF-AEV, the most affordable platform, currently used
for Dacia Spring;
– KEI-EV, intended for ultra-compact electric vehicles
mainly for the Japanese market;

a customer is defined around the full Software-Defined
Vehicle and will be launched in 2026.
1.3.4.3 Operation of the Alliance
Rebalanced cross-shareholdings between
Renault Group and Nissan and strengthened
Alliance governance
The New Alliance Agreement between Renault Group and
Nissan came into force on 8 November 2023. It formalises
the rebalancing of cross-shareholdings between Renault
Group and Nissan.
As a result, when the agreement came into force, Renault
Group and Nissan held a 15% cross-shareholding, with
respective obligations to retain and cap their holdings at
15%. The voting rights of Renault Group and Nissan are
capped at 15% of the exercisable voting rights, with the
possibility for everyone to freely exercise their voting rights
within this limit.
On 8 November 2023, Renault Group transferred 28.4% of
the Nissan shares (out of a total of 43.4%) to a French trust,
with full discretion to dispose of these shares, in a process
organised and coordinated with Nissan that gives Nissan or
a designated third party the right of first refusal.
Renault Group has begun monetising the 28.4% of Nissan
shares transferred to the French trust:
●on 13 December 2023, Renault Group sold 211,000,000
Nissan shares for €764 million, representing around 5% of
Nissan’s capital,
●in 2024, Renault Group carried out two other disposal
transactions:
– on 28 March 2024, by selling to Nissan 99,132,100 shares
representing approximately 2.5% of Nissan’s capital,
– on
27
September
2024,
by
selling
to
Nissan
195,473,600 shares representing approximately 5.0% of
Nissan’s capital,
– In total, these 2 sales generated positive cash flow of
€852 million in 2024.
Therefore,
up
to
31/12/2024,
Renault
Group
had
sold
505,605,700  Nissan shares to Nissan, representing 12.5% of
Nissan’s capital, for a sale value of €1,616  million. For each of
these 3  transactions, Nissan decided to cancel all the shares
bought back, resulting in an accretive effect for its shareholders.
The Alliance Operating Board
The Alliance Operating Board (AOB: Alliance Operational
Board), created on 12 March 2019, is responsible for
coordination between Renault Group, Nissan and Mitsubishi
Motors and for new initiatives to create value for their
respective shareholders and employees.
Following the rebalancing of cross-shareholdings between
Renault Group and Nissan, it is agreed that the Alliance
Operating Board is an ad hoc body which aims to promote
the search for synergies between companies and oversee
the identification, operation and governance of joint
projects. It will also lead the Alliance’s new way of working,
suggest additional work and topics to be explored, facilitate
the resolution of disagreements and organise joint events.
It will ensure that no competitively sensitive information
from the participating members will be disclosed or used in
discussions between members of the Alliance Operating
Board. All joint projects will be presented and dealt with in
strict compliance with this rule.
As of 31 December 2024, this new Alliance Operating Board was
composed of Jean-Dominique Senard, Chairman of the Board
of Directors of Renault and Chairman of the Alliance Operating
Board; Luca de  Meo, CEO of Renault; Makoto Uchida, CEO of
Nissan, and Takao Kato, CEO of Mitsubishi Motors.
The decisions made by the Alliance Operating Board are
adopted by consensus of all its members. The Alliance
Operating Board meets as often as the interests of the
3 Companies and the Alliance require and around once a month,
in France, Japan, or by videoconference when necessary.
The
Alliance
Operating
Board’s
General
Secretary,
Véronique Sarlat-Depotte, appointed in April 2021, is tasked
with coordinating and facilitating the Alliance’s major
projects launched to accelerate the operating efficiency of
the respective companies and reports to the Alliance
Operating Board.
Opportunities for future cooperation are assessed by
dedicated committees.

Over the course of 2024, Renault Group, Nissan and
Mitsubishi Motors ensured that they remain at the top of the

In 2024, the Alliance Operating Board met once a month on

1.3.4.4 Renault-Nissan B.V. (RNBV)
Since 2002, RNBV has had decision-making powers and
recommendations related to Alliance-wide strategy and
planning.
Since the entry into force on 8 November 2023 of the New
Alliance Agreement between Renault Group and Nissan, RNBV
has become a pure holding company with no functions.
1.3.4.5 Alliance Ventures
Founded in January 2018, Alliance Ventures is a strategic
venture capital fund operated by the Renault-Nissan-
Mitsubishi Alliance.
The fund, which was launched in 2018 with an initial
investment of $200 million, is located in Amsterdam, where it
targets innovative technologies and business models in the
fields of new mobility, autonomous driving, connected
services, electric vehicles and “Enterprise  2.0” throughout
the world.
Alliance Ventures aims to support innovation and targets
strategic investments, at all stages of development, in start-
ups developing breakthrough technologies or businesses.
Alliance Ventures has eleven holdings, including:
The Mobility House, which aims to create a more
environmentally friendly future for energy and mobility, is
developing a technology platform that brings together the
automotive and energy sectors, integrating vehicle batteries
into the electricity grid using smart charging and storage
solutions (V2G, Vehicle to Grid). In 2024, Renault Group via
Mobilize and its partner The Mobility House launched one of
the first V2G solutions on the French market: the Renault 5
electric is the first Renault car equipped with a bidirectional
on-board charger. Combined with the Mobilize Powerbox
bidirectional charger and the Mobilize V2G service, it allows
drivers to save on charging and reduce their overall electricity
bill by selling electricity on the energy market. The innovative
architecture integrates hardware such as natively reversible
electrotechnical components and software that controls
electrical current management. This makes it possible to
benefit from the Mobilize V2G service while preserving
battery capacity. The Mobilize electricity contract, provided
by our technology partner The Mobility House, will guarantee
carbon neutral electricity and monetise the energy returned
to the grid through automated bidirectional charging.
WeRide is the world’s first publicly traded company in
universal autonomous driving technology and Robotaxi
services. The company was listed on Nasdaq on 25 October
2024. WeRide raised $120 million in its IPO and $320.5 million
in a private placement. During the 2024 edition of Roland
Garros, Renault Group and WeRide successfully piloted a
robobus shuttle, which aroused great interest from
transport authorities. In addition, Renault Group unveiled its
autonomous driving strategy in May 2024, with WeRide as its
central
partner.
Together,
the
two
companies
are
accelerating the advancement of low-carbon public. This
partnership highlights the commitment of Renault Group
and WeRide to cooperation and innovation in shaping the
future of mobility.
Tekion has developed an internet sales platform for car
dealers. This platform connects all their activities (DMS,
CRM, Digital Retail, Analytics, etc.).
1.3.4.6 Joint ventures and contribution
of Nissan
Renault Group’s purchases and sales from Nissan in 2024, as
well as Nissan’s contribution to Renault’s results in 2024, are
detailed in Chapter 5, Note 12 of the consolidated financial
statements. This note also explains how the financial
statements of Nissan, which are prepared in accordance
with the Japanese accounting standards, are adjusted,
differing from those used by Renault. Nissan’s financial year
begins on 1 April and ends on 31 March of the following year.

1.3.5 Partnerships and cooperation
Strategic cooperation between the Renault-
Nissan-Mitsubishi Alliance and Mercedes-Benz AG 
Refer to the “Strategic cooperation” paragraph in section
1.3.4 of this document. 
A horizontal integration strategy: 
In order to accelerate its transformation and excel in each
of the new value chains (technological, industrial, circular,
services, etc.), the Group adopts a collaborative approach
whenever possible, with the best players in their field. This
active policy of partnerships makes it possible to co-invest,
co-develop, cover a wider range of innovations and share
risks. Depending on the activities, partnerships are of
different
types,
such
as
joint
ventures,
strategic
partnerships, to co-develop a product, supply contracts or
equity investments in companies.
In the field of electric vehicles
Partnerships have been signed with Whylot (a French start-
up in which Renault Group holds a 21% stake) to develop and
industrialise an innovative axial flow e-engine on a large
scale, with Airbus to develop next-generation battery
systems and with the CEA to develop a very high-efficiency
bidirectional charger.
For the production of batteries for its future electric vehicles,
Renault Group has mainly chosen two strategic partners,
whose battery plants will be located in France, close to the
ElectriCity division: Envision AESC and Verkor.
Envision AESC, a global player in battery technology and
plants. This partner will install a gigafactory in Douai with a
capacity of 9  GWh in 2024, with the objective of reaching
24 GWh by 2030. The aim is to produce state-of-the-art, low-
carbon batteries that are cost-competitive for the Group’s
electric models, including the future R5.
Verkor, a French startup specialising in the development of
battery cells, in which the Group holds a 10% stake. Its future
gigafactory located in Dunkirk will supply each year the
equivalent of 12 GWh of batteries that will equip vehicles in the
upper segments of the Renault Group brands and in
particular, from 2025, the future 100% electric C-Crossover GT
from Alpine which will be manufactured in Dieppe, France.
The Group also announced the creation of a joint venture with
Minth Group to produce battery boxes for electric vehicles. This
joint venture will install two new production lines in Ruitz in 2023,
with a capacity of 300,000 battery boxes per year by 2025.
In line with its industrial decarbonisation trajectory, in 2022
the company signed three major partnerships, including the
largest green electricity supply contract in France with
Voltalia.
To secure its supply of raw materials, the Group has signed
strategic partnerships with Vulcan Energy, Arverne Group,
Terrafame and Managem Group.
In the field of Software-Defined Vehicle
The Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) is the future of the
automotive industry by enabling the vehicle to be constantly
updated throughout its life cycle, learning from its users,
and maintaining the link with the manufacturer from design
to the end of the vehicle’s life. To launch its first open and
horizontal SDV, in 2026, Renault Group has built strong
partnerships with two major Tech players: 
●In May 2022, Renault Group and Google launched a new
phase in their partnership with the signing of new
contracts relating to the design and production of the
digital architecture of the Software Defined Vehicle and
the strengthening of the Group’s digitisation.  
●In the last two years, Renault Group and Qualcomm
Technologies also strengthened their technological
collaboration in the development of a centralised
electronic
architecture
dedicated
to
the
future
generation of Software-Defined Vehicles.
But Renault Group also benefits from strong in-house
software skills and expertise: Ampere boasts a ratio of 35%
of engineers, of which half are specialized in software.
Light commercial vehicles 
Renault manages several agreements with Nissan, Renault
Trucks and Mercedes-Benz Group.
In the small van segment, derived from Kangoo platform
and manufactured in Maubeuge plant.
●For Mercedes-Benz, the manufacturing of the vehicle
Citan started in 2012;
●For Nissan, the manufacturing the vehicle NV250 started
in late 2019, to replace the NV200;
●New agreements were signed for the manufacture of its
successor and its electric version based on the new
Kangoo. Manufacturing began in 2021 under the names
Citan (van) and Tourer (passenger) for Mercedes-Benz,
and Townstar for Nissan.
In the light van segment, derived from the Trafic platform and
manufactured at the Sandouville plant
●For Nissan, the manufacturing of the vehicle NV300/
Primastar started in 2016.
●Trafic is also distributed by Renault Trucks under a
commercial agreement signed in 2022.
●In the heavy van segment, based on Master (and New
Master) platform and manufactured in Batilly plant.
●For Nissan, the manufacturing the vehicle NV400/
Interstar started in 2011.
●Master is also distributed by Renault Trucks under a
commercial agreement signed in 2009.

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
In the pick-up segment.
An agreement was signed with Nissan in 2015 for the
development and production of a Renault pick-up, called
Alaskan and based on the Nissan Frontier. Both vehicles are
manufactured at the Santa Isabel plant (Argentina). Alaskan
has been launched in late 2020.
On top of those partnerships.
●In March 2024, Renault Group, Volvo Group and CMA CGM
Group joined forces to create a new company, named
Flexis, for the next generation of mobility and logistics
solutions. Renault Group and Volvo Group respectively
hold 45% of the capital, while CMA CGM Group acquired a
10% stake. It will develop, produce and market a new
family of medium-sized electric vans and associated
services. Based on a connected electronics platform, the
vehicles will be produced at the Sandouville plant.
In the field of new mobility, Renault Group and Plug Power
signed an agreement in 2021 to create a joint venture
dedicated to hydrogen mobility. The company HYVIA was
founded in June 2021 in France. It offers a complete
ecosystem of turnkey solutions: light commercial vehicles
with fuel cells, charging stations, supply of decarbonised
hydrogen and fleet maintenance. However, due to the too
slow evolution of hydrogen mobility ecosystems in Europe
and the very significant development costs required for H2
innovation, HYVIA was placed in compulsory liquidation by
the Versailles Commercial Court on 18th February 2025.
In terms of innovation for mobility
Renault Group, Eviden (formerly Atos, Dassault Systèmes,
Orange, STMicroelectronics and Thales) created, in 2021,
under the form of a GIE (Economic Interest Group), an open
innovation ecosystem for intelligent, secure, and sustainable
mobility. JC Decaux joined this ecosystem in 2024.
Software République has made it possible to create and
strengthen collaborations with players from the private world
(start-ups, SMEs, mid-caps, large groups), the public (local
authorities) and academia (schools, universities).
Several projects have already come to fruition:
The Mobilize Powerbox® range, launched in 2024, integrates
the expertise of several Software République partners
(Renault Group, Orange, STMicroelectronics, Thales) as well
as IoTecha Corp and Lacroix for manufacturing carried out
in its French electronics assembly plant, Symbiose. The
charging station with power ranging up to 22  kW meets
several major objectives in Europe: 
●it facilitates access to charging for all users of electric
vehicles;
●it contributes to the smart management of electrical
networks;
●it provides a solution to cybersecurity risks when integrating
them into the vehicle’s electric charging networks;
●it
strengthens
the
industrial
and
technological
sovereignty of this new market segment.  
A “detect & respond” cybersecurity solution.
Cybersecurity is one of the major challenges for mobility.
Orange, via its subsidiary Orange Cyberdefense, Renault
Group and Thales, announced the co-development of a
solution aimed at detecting cyberattack attempts and
analysing them in order to provide a rapid response, thus
making it possible to protect the vehicle. This solution based
on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and machine learning will be
embedded in Renault vehicles from 2025. The project is
supported by BPI France.
Modelling and optimisation tools for territories
The safety and fluidity of road traffic are key issues for the
regions. In this context, the Software République partners
are working on a range of tools to help improve safety,
infrastructure development and maintenance. Several
experiments have been carried out in territories such as
Saint-Quentin-en-Yvelines,
Grand-Reims
or
Bordeaux-
Métropole, for example around the modelling of flows or the
management of vulnerable users.
Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) projects.
This project aims to improve the driver and passenger
experience by developing new features using the customer’s
smartphone or tablet. Under the impetus of its first Dacia
customer, Software République is working with an ecosystem
of start-ups to develop unique functions that will enhance the
driving experience, such as drowsiness monitoring.
The
Software
Republique
Academy
to
meet
the
cybersecurity skills challenge.
To meet the growing need for cybersecurity skills and
recruitment, the seven partners have launched two training
programmes starting in September 2022. The first offers a
selection of partner cybersecurity training modules for the
Software République ecosystem. The second shared
actions with students to show them all our challenges and
increase the pool of young talent in the “tech” fields.
Software République incubator
In March 2022, the Software République incubator was
launched, with a tailor-made support programme to
accelerate joint projects and support start-ups. Seventeen
start-ups are or have been incubated.
All of these projects are part of the innovation approach of
Software République in line with its purpose “Together to
revolutionise mobility”.

The environment 
In October 2022, Renault Group announced the creation of
The Future Is NEUTRAL, the first automotive circular
economy company in Europe, with the aim of providing the
tools to enable the automotive industry to move towards
resource neutrality. The Future Is NEUTRAL operates
throughout
the
value
chain
through
its
specialised
subsidiaries and long-standing partners. The Future is
NEUTRAL is also developing new partnerships for its new
businesses, including closed-loop battery recycling.
In 2024, Suez acquired a 20% stake in The Future Is NEUTRAL,
alongside Renault Group which holds a 80% stake. In
addition, Renault Group and Suez have also signed a
cooperation agreement covering areas such as design for
recycling, reverse logistics and closed-loop recycling of
automotive materials.
For further details, please refer to section 2.2.2.1.5.2 of this
document. 
Accelerating international expansion 
International development is continuing thanks to several
partnerships. Through a horizontal approach, we are
pursuing three strategic objectives: access to technology
building blocks, achieving scale and streamlining our
time-to-market.
South Korea case
In South Korea, we launched successfully in 2024 the Grand
Koleos for the South Korean domestic market and for
export. This vehicle was the first joint cooperation between
Geely Holding Group (Geely Group), China’s largest private
carmaker and Renault Group. It is produced by Renault
Korea Motors (RKM) in its Busan plant. A 2nd vehicle, Aurora2,
will follow, and be produced in Busan from end of 2025.
Since December 2022, RKM is jointly owned by Renault
Group (which remains the majority shareholder and
continues to fully consolidate the entity), Geely Auto (who
subscribed to 34,02% of the shares through a capital
increase mechanism), and Samsung, via Samsung Cards, a
minority shareholder.
HORSE Powertrain limited
Renault Group and Geely set up a joint venture on
31 May 2024: HORSE Powertrain Limited. Renault Group and
the Geely Group each hold a 50% stake in this company,
which aims to become a world leader in the market for
internal combustion, hybrid and low-emission powertrains
and transmissions. Its role is to offer a complete portfolio of
cutting-edge technologies to customers, manufacturers
and equipment suppliers worldwide.
On June 28th, 2024, Aramco (Saudi Aramco, a global
integrated energy and chemicals company based in Saudi
Arabia) signed definitive agreements to acquire a 10% stake
in HORSE, valuing the company at €7.4 billion. Renault Group
and Geely Group each retain a 45% stake.
HORSE will enable Renault Group to benefit from the best
alternative fuels and thermal Hydrogen solutions, and to
offer an attractive range of decarbonized solutions based
on thermal vehicles and light commercial applications.
(See 1.3.3)
China case
China is the leader in electric vehicles. In 2024, nearly 68% of
battery electric vehicles (BEV) sold globally were sold in China.
China is at the forefront of technology and the electric value
chain, particularly in the battery sector. In 2024, the ACDC
entity was created in China, with the mission of identifying
trends and finding the best partners in China. One of ACDC’s
main objectives is to deepen our understanding of the
Chinese ecosystem, integrate into it, and collaborate closely
and locally with our partners.
Renault Group already has several Chinese partners
(Dongfeng, Geely, and a network of suppliers in the battery
sector in particular).
Our partnership with Dongfeng has been taking shape since
September 2017 through our eGT joint venture. This
company is owned by Renault Group (25%), Nissan (25%)
and Dongfeng (50%). It is based in the city of Shiyan (Hubei
province) and is dedicated to the development of K-ZE
(China)/Spring (Europe). By the end of 2024, eGT had
produced 190,172 vehicles, exported to Europe (Dacia Spring)
and Latin America (Renault E-Kwid), and sold 72,480 vehicles
under the Dongfeng brand.
Twingo project
With the future Twingo, Renault Group is aiming for a 2-year
time-to-market (compared with an internal benchmark of
4 years before 2021, and 3 years thanks to the Renaulution
plan). The launch of Twingo is therefore scheduled for 2026.
The EV platform for Twingo is being developed within the
Group, and the project is being led by Ampere. Design and
advanced engineering are carried out in-house in France. Its
development will be achieved through a Chinese network of
partners (engineering service providers, suppliers) to
improve lead times and costs.
The production site will be Novo Mesto in Slovenia (as
announced in July 2024).

1.4.1 Research and Advanced Engineering
Renault Group identifies the key innovations of tomorrow
very early on. This might be a very innovative product
concept, such as the minivan in 1983 or the first Renault
Twizy and Zoe electric vehicles in the early 2010s. A real
breakthrough can also be a major technological advance,
such as the hydrogen engine. New uses can also give rise to
high-value breakthroughs such as new mobility services,
data monetisation and battery second life. Regulatory
changes such as the ban on combustion-engine vehicles in
Europe by 2035 for the sale of new cars should also be
considered.
To implement the innovation plan needed to ensure the
necessary technological shift, the Research and Advanced
Engineering community represents around 1,000 employees,
plus 80 PhD students, assigned as closely as possible to the
specific fields or professions. Innovation within the Renault
Group is addressed at every level, involving every stage from
the design to the production of a vehicle. The ability to
innovate is supported by levers such as the network of
experts or the numerous partnerships. Research and
Advanced Engineering activities are managed transversally
within the various Engineering sectors via a structured plan
shared throughout the company. This global plan covers all
vehicle and mechanical, product, process, after-sales and
service applications. The innovations being developed are
designed to serve the brands, the business, and current and
future customers.
Research and Advanced Engineering activities are divided
into 5 perfectly complementary categories.
The Research Plan and doctoral theses (most of which are
under the CIFRE Convention) make it possible to enlighten
the
future
and
prepare
for
future
technological
breakthroughs. Their purpose is to identify, anticipate and
prepare Renault Group for future trends to be incorporated
into developments over the next 5 to 10 years.
The Technological Plan makes it possible to prepare
innovations for vehicle or component applications by
developing new technologies within 3 to 5 years. It shows the
innovations to be introduced in the platforms, engines,
systems and vehicle range plan.
These two plans are based on three main pillars: the planet,
regions and people.
Research and Advanced Engineering are also at the helm of
a large number of demonstrators (Demonstrator Plan), the
aim of which is to inspire the future range plan by bringing
together technological building blocks to test innovations
against realistic integration in a vehicle, and thus potentially
give rise to new vehicle concepts.
The development of simulation/validation tools and models
is operated through the Engineering Performance Plan. The
aim of this plan is to make breakthroughs in methodologies,
expertise and multiple skills (business, process, sales,
marketing, organisation and management).
Finally, the Process Plan makes it possible to work on the
innovations to be rolled out in manufacturing.
The budget for Research and Advanced Engineering
activities in 2024 is distributed across these 5 plans.
Engineering is a strong contributor to Technology and
Innovation through its skills but also through its product and
service offerings. In the midst of change, the automotive
world will have to face two major challenges: energy
transition and digital transition as well as changes in our
uses. To meet these challenges, three priority technical
areas guide the allocation of the budget:  tools to improve
the efficiency of engineering, the centralised electrical and
electronic architecture (Software-Defined Vehicle) and
electro-mobility (engines, load issues, etc.).

1.4.2 Innovation performance levers
Engineering performance is assessed by its ability to innovate and anticipate while controlling its diversity through
standardisation.
1.4.2.1 Network of experts
The changes affecting the world are of a nature and
magnitude unprecedented for the planet. These are also
true for the automotive sector. In the context of two
simultaneous revolutions (digital and energy transition), the
fundamentals of the market as well as the regulatory and
technological bases are or will all be called into question. For
the company, this means standing out by knowing and
listening
to
our
markets – translated
into
permanent
innovation – and gaining a position on new sectors of
activity, with a high degree of scientific and technical
knowledge. In this context, where the mobilisation and
transformation of knowledge are becoming strategic, the
expertise sector occupies a central position by providing the
means to identify and then capture the value created by
knowledge in order to use it to drive Renaulution and offer
the company a competitive advantage. The expertise
sector represents 43 strategic areas of expertise such as
“Artificial
Intelligence”,
“Electrical
and
Electronic
Architecture”, “Immersive simulation and virtual reality”,
“Manufacturing”, “logistics”, etc. This sector is organised
into four levels of expertise:
●the
Expert
Fellow
guarantees
the
definition
and
consistency of the strategic areas of expertise. It
coordinates the network of Lead Experts in order to
structure
their
output
at
both
prospective
and
operational
levels
concerning
technical
or
methodological innovations and support for projects
under development. The collaborative work carried out as
part of the expertise projects contributes to the shared
progress of the businesses involved in the company’s
major challenges (energy in the vehicle, coupling of
complex systems, additive manufacturing, etc.). The
network of experts can thus be described as an agile
organisation
serving
the
cross-business
and
the
construction of shared roadmaps up to CEO level for
strategic technologies;
●the 48 Expert Leaders, each reporting to a Department
Head, are responsible for their roadmap. Responsible for
one of the areas of strategy expertise, they structure and
guide their internal network of experts and use an
external
network
consisting
of
universities,
other
manufacturers, associations, incubation structures, etc.,
to enable the Company to work in an “extended” way. The
list of areas of expertise (48 to date) is not set in stone:
it evolves to meet the company’s strategic challenges.
For example, over the last 2 years, the “Diesel engine” and
“Petrol engine” domains have been merged, but a new
“Hydrogen”
domain
has
been
created,
and
the
“Embedded software” and “Electric vehicle charging
ecosystem” domains have been strengthened;
●the 300 Experts are in charge of secondary fields of
expertise,
responsible
for
benchmarks,
identifying
relevant partners and investing in the protection of know-
how through patents. They are also responsible for
defining and promoting standards and processes;
●the 600 Referents further the state of the art in their
practice, developing standards on which the teams can
then capitalise.

## Page 66

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
The organisation of the network of experts and its agile
mode of operation allows the way ahead to be mapped out
using a set of coherent roadmaps, the enhancement of
knowledge to be accelerated through innovation and the
performance of operations, thus allowing the business lines
to excel in their various areas of expertise.
In 2024, the expertise function initiated an international
rollout aligned with the company’s ambitions. As such, while
France remains the centre of expertise, 10% of the industry’s
workforce is now based in our international centres.
1.4.2.2 Renault Group Engineering
Centres (RTX – Renault
Technical Centre) worldwide
Renault Group’s engineering centres are located all over the
world in countries such as Spain, Romania, Korea, India and
Latin America. Thanks to their knowledge of local and
regional markets, they are tasked with adapting vehicles to
customer
needs
and
expectations,
local
regulatory
constraints and national economic situations. Through the
application of a skills upgrading policy that continues from
year to year, Renault Group continuously increases the
responsibility of engineering centres for cross-functional
activities and enables them to take charge of vehicle
project design at an increasingly early stage in the process.
This upskilling also enables the most mature RTX to move
from
regional
to
more
global
responsibilities.
The
engineering centres were assigned new responsibilities as
part of the new organisation implemented in 2023 through
the HORSE entity.
1.4.2.3 Open innovation labs
and link with start-ups
Another important lever for the company is the global
ecosystem of Renault Group’s open innovation laboratories,
known as “Labs”. The Labs have been in existence for a
decade and complement other Renault Group processes
and entities. They make it possible to do unscheduled, rapid
innovation in test & learning mode by “doing it yourself”.
Historically born in France in the wake of the Fab Labs and
Maker culture, the Creative Lab has introduced several
hundred employees to the use of rapid prototyping methods
(3D printing, engraving, cutting, thermoforming, etc.). The
Creative Lab with its themed Labs (Smart EV Lab, Electronic-
Shaker Lab, Car Data Lab, AfterSales Lab, etc.) brings
together the Group’s community of makers. It is able to
support project initiators from idea to completion, assist
with technology projects and launch challenges for Renault
Group brands. The Labs are used to develop in-house know-
how and skills on innovation topics.
The other Labs are located in strategic areas where the
automotive and services world are changing rapidly: Israel,
China and Brazil in order to ensure continuous technological
monitoring of emerging trends, increased knowledge of
customer needs as close as possible to the ground and also
to strengthen the link with technology partners worldwide.
The Labs can also be used to test the potential of a
partnership with a start-up. 2024 saw the inauguration of a
new Open Lab in Korea within the RTK (Engineering Centre in
South Korea) combining dedicated skills but also shared
business skills to facilitate the transfer between the POC
(Proof of Concept) and Advanced Engineering phase and
the development phase.
1.4.2.4 Innovation partnerships
The vehicle of tomorrow will be energy efficient, highly light,
connected and capable of replacing the driver for all or part of
their activities; this is a challenge that can only be met
collectively. For Renault Group, collaborative R&D contracts
not only speed up the development of the technologies needed
to meet these challenges but also enhance skills by sharing
costs: a key lever for bringing innovations to fruition more
quickly, so that they can be introduced more fully into vehicle
projects. In a world of competitive industry, performance and
frugality, whether with industrial or academic partners, this
type of collaborative innovation is encouraged by public
funding bodies at both French and European level. In the form
of public support (subsidies, repayable advances), it provides
additional leverage to accelerate innovation. In this context,
the participation of Renault Group in cooperative initiatives
has always been a priority in Research and Advanced
Engineering activities, which is an effective tool for the growth
of the “Renaulution” strategic plan.
In 2024, Renault managed a portfolio of 60 subsidised
projects on average, divided equally between French and
European projects. During the year, 23 projects were
submitted (stable compared with 2023): to date, 9 have been
accepted, 7 are still being examined, and 7 have been
rejected. In 2024, Renault finalised the evaluation and
launched 12 ambitious projects as part of the “Automotive
Investment Support” call for projects. The European
network,
with
active
participation
in
EUCAR
and
partnerships,
was
strengthened,
contributing
to
the
submission of 10 European collaborative projects covering
all the technologies of the future (battery, SDV, AI, etc.).
Some of Renault Group’s innovative projects were also
illustrated by a notable presence at the annual conference
celebrating the 30th anniversary of EUCAR in Brussels: Jean-
François Salessy, RES Director, first participated in a
roundtable discussion on the societal and economic impact
of mobility. Renault, Mobilize and the Software Republique
then jointly presented concrete examples of the Group’s
“human-oriented”
innovations
(Human
first
program,
H1st vision, Digitalization supporting the green transition) at a
stand for all the participants (representatives of OEMs, the
European Commission, etc.).
A fine example of a partnership in innovation with our
suppliers is the production of the Embleme demo car,
illustrating Renault Group’s position on decarbonisation
roadmaps and presented at the Paris Motor Show in
October 2024. The development of this vehicle was based on
partnerships and co-innovation.
For details about Embleme, see section 1.4.3, “Focus on
some concepts, prototypes and demonstrators of 2024
technologies”.

Academic partners
Renault Group has always worked with leading universities
and the best academic partners. In 2024, the annual CIFRE
campaign led to the collection and validation of 37 thesis
topics. As a result of these efforts, the average number of
PhD students in Renault Group entities will increase by
around 15% compared with 2023. As of the end of 2024,
Renault hosted more than 80 PhD students.
Below are some examples of theses initiated in 2024:
●Calendar ageing of Na-Ion battery cells;
●V2X data management for road safety;
●Generation of virtual data for ADAS validation, etc.
Renault is also involved in 6 research chairs in strategic areas,
which are also important drivers for innovation.
In 2024, three new initiatives were launched on the following
diverse themes:
●Safety in the context of the Software-Defined Vehicle
(SDV);
●The future of project management for sustainable
performance in a complex environment;
●Power electronics.
