The 6 current chairs
FOCUS on a flagship collaborative project: TRANSENSUS-EV
TRANSENSUS-EV is an important project for Renault Group:
funded by the European Commission as part of the Horizon
Europe programme and bringing together the main
European OEMs (VW, BMW, Toyota, Volvo, Ford, etc.), the
project aims to define and propose a common approach in
Europe on the calculations of Life Cycle Analyses of road
transport and, in particular, on those of electric vehicle
batteries.
Launched in 2023, the project continued in 2024 and this
year was able to communicate on the initial results obtained
(notably at the EUCAR annual conference).
FOCUS on the partnership with the CEA:
The strategic partnership between Renault and the
Commissariat à L’Énergie Atomique et aux Énergies
Alternatives (CEA) encompasses all collaborations between
Renault and various entities of the CEA (LIST, LETI, LITEN,
INES.2S). These collaborations cover many technological
fields: electrical and electronic vehicle architecture, power
electronics, cybersecurity, AI, battery ageing models for
traction batteries, automated battery disassembly, as well
as additive manufacturing (Renault is a partner in the HP-
CEA 3D Print Hub), Industry 4.0 and the decarbonisation of
our manufacturing sites. Renault and the CEA are also
partnering to apply for European project calls for research
funding (e.g. the European Battereverse project on battery
ageing for second-life use).
As an example, the innovation project on “structured mesh
architecture” was carried out over two years, resulting in the
filing of a dozen patents. This innovation makes it possible,
with a single material and in a single 3D additive
manufacturing step, to obtain components with adaptive
mechanical behaviour and improved performance. The
potential applications to the automotive industry are
promising, with comfort being a priority: more efficient,
lighter, and customisable, the components created could
replace other parts typically made from the assembly of
several materials, such as seat cushions and backs,
armrests or the central console.
1.4.2.5 Technology partners
In order to constantly explore new opportunities for
innovation, Renault Group partners with the best companies
on the market in multiple areas.
For battery cells, Renault Group has chosen to partner with
Envision AESC, which will develop a gigafactory in Douai
with a capacity of 9 GWh in 2024 aiming at reaching 24 GWh
by 2030. Working closely with Renault ElectriCity, Renault
Group’s partner will produce state-of-the-art batteries at
competitive prices, with a reduced carbon footprint for
future electric models, including the Renault 5.
Renault Group has also signed a Memorandum of
Understanding to become a shareholder of the French start-
up Verkor with a stake of 10%. The two partners intend to co-
develop a high-performance battery suitable for the C and
higher segments of the Renault range, as well as for the
Alpine
models.
The
partnership
also
includes
the
development of a pilot production line in France for battery
cells and module prototyping from 2022. In a second phase,
starting from 2026, Verkor aims to build the first gigafactory
for high-performance batteries in France, with an initial
capacity of 10  GWh for Renault Group, potentially rising to
20 GWh by 2030.
In terms of power electronics, Renault Group will extend
control of its value chain by integrating the inverter, DC-DC
converter and on-board charger (OBC) in a single box
developed in-house. With a compact design, this One Box
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
65

## Page 68

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
Project will be 800V compliant, with fewer parts to reduce
the cost, and will be used across all platforms and
powertrains (BEV, HEV, PHEV) for further scale effect. Power
modules for the inverter, DC-DC converter and OBC will rely
respectively on silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride
(GaN) thanks to our strategic partnership signed with
STMicroelectronics.
On top of these new technologies, Renault Group is also
working on a more compact e-powertrain called the all-in-
one system. This e-powertrain combines the electric motor,
reduction gearbox and power electronics in a single unit,
saving 45% in overall volume (equivalent to the volume of
the fuel tank in the current Clio), reducing the cost of the
overall powertrain by 30% (this saving in value being
equivalent to the cost of the electric motor), and reducing
the energy lost by 45% under WLTP, giving an additional
range gain of 20 km for an electric vehicle.
Other innovation technology partnerships are presented in
Chapter 1.3.5. Partnerships and Cooperations.
1.4.2.6 Patent registration
With the challenges posed by climate change and the
development of digital technology, mobility is changing
rapidly. This real change of era is opening up new fields, and
technological breakthroughs are occurring ever more
rapidly in a wide range of areas. Renault Group is adapting
its strategy to position itself as a key player in the sector.
Renault Group has always known how to innovate, and the
significant and constant acceleration seen since 2022
(+92% in 2024 vs. 2021) in the number of patents registered
under full ownership reflects the extent of the technological
shift that the company is taking, for example in software,
batteries and their integration, or services. For example, a
growing proportion of new patents filed relate to our
technological roadmaps centred on connected vehicles and
electric vehicles, reaching 60% at the end of 2024. This
figure demonstrates the vitality of innovation within Renault
Group and its desire to increase the protection of its
inventions internally or with its partners in these strategic
areas, also leaving room for the protection of creativity
outside these areas.
Renault Group also continues to be inventive in its
recognised areas of expertise, particularly in terms of
engines. A very good example is the coiled rotor technology,
derived from the development of the Zoe. The partnership
with Whylot, announced in 2022, to create a new type of
electric motor, is another. The new patents filed as part of
these partnerships cover a range of innovations applicable
to the engine, but also to the charging system, the battery,
the combination energies (electric and hydrogen), thermal
management, architecture and acoustics.
Industrialisation solutions have also been patented. The
ability to integrate new technology on an industrial scale is
one of Renault Group’s strengths and a definite contribution
to the partnerships in which Renault Group is involved.
In 2024, Renault Group had more than 15,000 patents in
its portfolio.
1.4.2.7 Digital Transformation
Due to the growing complexity of vehicles (electrification,
connected vehicles, driving aids, etc.), the explosion of on-
board software and increasing regulatory pressure, Renault
Group is strengthening the configuration management and
development processes for systems and software as well as
their validation through its digital transformation. This
transformation
will
enable
the
entire
company
to
collaborate securely, seamlessly and efficiently in a single
environment. All parties involved in the design (Process,
Purchasing, Costing, Design, Quality, etc.) and validation
processes, as well as partners and suppliers, will make the
data on the vehicle product accessible around Digital Twins
throughout its life cycle with the aim of reducing costs and
lead times.
Renault Group’s digital transformation continues with the
“Renaulution Virtual Twin” programme. The Enterprise PLM
(Product
Life
cycle
Management)
project,
renamed
“Renaulution Virtual Twin”, is a major programme that aims
to transform and bring Renault’s design into a new era. This
project will provide engineering and all of the company’s
upstream businesses with a new digital tool for managing
its product data and mobility services. Thanks to the
extension of the digitalisation of Engineering to the rest of
the company, this tool will offer a platform for collaboration
from design to after-sales around the digital twin (single
source of truth).
Following 20 years of collaboration, Renault Group and
Dassault Systèmes are strengthening their partnership to
contribute to the “Renaulution” strategic plan. In an
unprecedented deployment for an industrial company,
Renault
Group
is
adopting
Dassault
Systèmes’
3DEXPERIENCE platform in the Cloud on a global scale.
This enterprise platform will make it possible to reduce
vehicle development costs and time. It will form a new
backbone for sharing, in real time, all the data linked to the
product throughout its life cycle and managing digital twins
in all the required configurations.
More than 20,000 Renault Group employees will benefit from
digital twin experiences to improve data sharing and
collaboration across the company.
This programme enabled the first deployments, from design
to industrialisation and after-sales, to begin in 2022. It aims
to have a fully deployed solution by 2025.
In 2023, more than 4,000 users were trained. From the
concept freeze milestone, a new innovative vehicle for the
Renaulution (platform and upperbodies) was developed
with Renaulution Virtual Twin, bringing on board all the
upstream
functions
(product
/
process
/
after-sale
engineering, purchasing, costing, quality, etc.). Following this
first successful pilot programme, all new vehicles are now
designed on the 3DExperience platform. Reducing the cost
of vehicles in mass production was also equipped with
Dassault Systèmes’ data science and artificial intelligence
(AI) solutions by designating data managed by legacy
systems of the major functions (Engineering, Purchasing,
Costing, Manufacturing).

01
The progress made in digital simulation has made it possible
to improve design robustness, correct anomalies upstream
and improve the efficiency of validations. This breakthrough
has contributed to a reduction in validation costs
(prototypes and physical testing) by more than 50%
between 2019 and 2024 and contributes to the one-year
acceleration programme for new vehicle development.
Digital simulation can be used to simulate and assess
vehicle
performance
in
terms
of
crashworthiness,
aerodynamics, acoustics, endurance, thermodynamics, etc.,
without the need for expensive physical prototypes and
upstream of the industrial phase. It can also be used to
simulate on-board systems (ADAS, systems and software,
energy management, etc.) and anticipate their tune-ups.
Immersive simulation allows putting humans back into the
product design or validation loop through the use of digital
twins, virtual reality tools or driving simulators. Thus, Renault
Group inaugurated ROADS in 2023, the world’s largest
driving simulator. The simulator has since been used early in
the design phase to test hypotheses about chassis, tyres or
steering performance. These simulation activities help
anticipate certain technical choices and optimise tests,
which are generally more expensive and occur later.
The massive arrival of artificial intelligence and data
management technologies within engineering is a new lever
to accelerate the efficiency of digital technology in design
and help reduce development times for Renaulution
vehicles. Digital and energy transitions: a challenge for
innovation.
1.4.2.8 Digital transition
Software will play an increasingly important role in the vehicle
of
tomorrow,
whether
for
human-machine
interfaces,
multimedia and connected services, or driver assistance
systems. It is necessary to respond to the customer’s growing
need for content and services enriched over time and
throughout the life of the vehicle thanks to the software. We
also need to be able to accommodate the maturing of new
technological solutions for the automotive industry.
Software-Defined Vehicle
To meet this digital transition challenge, the company has
set itself the task of responding to the necessary changes in
on-board software architecture. The partnership with
Google and Qualcomm announced at the end of 2022 will
help develop the connected car of tomorrow. The Software-
Defined Vehicle will bring together the best of the
automotive and digital worlds, to offer new services on
demand and enable continuous updates on vehicles,
building on the existing collaboration around Android
Automotive which will be consolidated by Renault Group
with its partners in a vehicle operating system (CAR-OS).
The Software-Defined Vehicle will allow new functionalities
and services to be introduced more and more quickly from a
development in the Cloud and automatically ported to the
vehicle. This centralisation of the architecture has the
advantage of reducing costs thanks to a reduction in the
number of computers; improving the speed and fluidity of
data exchanges as well as enabling better data valuation to
extend the services offered to customers. The Software-
Defined Vehicle opens up a huge range of possibilities, with
new market opportunities and technological innovations.
1.4.3 Energy transition
Renault Group is committed to achieving a zero CO2 impact
by 2040 in Europe and by 2050 worldwide. To support the
achievement of its sustainable development goals, the
Group
develops
innovative
solutions
to
reduce
the
emissions of its vehicles and develop a low-carbon battery,
taking into account its entire life cycle.
Battery technology
Since 2022, all new models sold have had an electric or
electrified version, in a European market that will be 100%
electric by 2035.
Since announcing its electric vehicle offensive in early 2007,
Renault Group has drawn on its recognised in-house
expertise (electrochemistry, materials science, thermal
modelling,
control
electronics,
high-voltage
networks,
mechanical packaging, etc.) to make the wisest choices in
terms of lithium-ion (Li-ion) cells, modules and battery packs,
so as to offer its electric and hybrid vehicles solutions at the
highest level in terms of range (energy density), durability,
reliability, cost and, of course, safety, which remains the
priority objective. The fact that more than 760,000 Renault
electric vehicles have been sold in Europe since 2011 without
a single major battery-related incident (no electric shocks or
fires) is evidence of this.
Renault Group’s ambition today is to develop a “made in
France”
battery
that
is
green,
carbon-free
and
environmentally responsible, with the aim of reducing the
carbon footprint of its batteries by up to 35% by 2030
(reference: Zoe 2019).
Its expertise has enabled Renault Group to build effective
partnerships with world-class cell suppliers, with a view to
creating ecosystems, and promising collaborations on the
various stages of the life cycle or energy services
associated with vehicle charging.
Renault Group continues to push current Li-ion technology in
several directions: energy density (to further increase the
vehicle’s range or to reduce the volume of the battery pack),
but also the charging power or the cost of the batteries,
depending on the uses and performance targeted for
each vehicle.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
67

## Page 70

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
Renault Group is also exploring technological solutions
aimed at offering the best compromise between cost and
performance depending on the type of vehicle and its use.
These avenues of exploration concern both the chemistry of
the batteries and the structure of the cells and the pack.
“Post-Li-ion” alternatives are also being studied, in
particular All Solid-State Battery (ASSB) technologies, or
even mixed solid-liquid or gel-type solutions.
Repairability, reuse and recycling of batteries
This upstream expertise has extended to the entire life cycle
of Li-ion batteries, from the extraction of raw materials to
dismantling and recycling. Renault Group is a pioneer in this
global approach, contributing to the development of
greener industrial processes and the emergence of “second
life” applications for batteries. (See 2.2.2.5.3).
During the vehicle use phase, Renault Group is developing
vehicle-to-grid (V2G) solutions, enabling energy to be fed
back into the grid from an electric car’s battery. The new
Renault 5 electric is the first Renault car to use the Mobilize
V2G service to turn the electric vehicle into a source of
energy for the home and for the electricity grid, providing
additional flexibility and facilitating the integration of
renewable energies into the energy mix.*
At the end of their first life in the vehicle, batteries still offer
up to 2/3 of their initial capacity. Mobilize develops new
stationary or mobile storage applications, in which batteries
are reused for a second life.
Renault Group has solid know-how in the collection and
recycling of electric vehicle batteries. Overall, nearly 6,000
batteries have already been recycled. At its Refactory site in
Flins, dedicated to the circular economy, Renault Group has
facilities for retrofitting, reuse, dismantling and recycling
batteries. In 2023, the Flins Expert Battery Repair Centre
doubled its capacity to repair batteries and prepare them
for a second life.
To go further in recycling, on 13 October 2022, Renault Group
announced the creation of The Future Is NEUTRAL, the first
company to operate across the entire value chain of the
automotive circular economy, which aims in particular to
become a European leader in closed-loop battery recycling.
(See 2.2.2.5.4 and 2.2.2.5.5).
Electric motor without rare earths
Renault Group continues to stay one step ahead of the
competition, being the first manufacturer to develop its own
electric motor – without rare earths (i.e. no permanent
magnets)
and
based
on
EESM
(Electrically
Excited
Synchronous Motor) technology – and its own reduction
gearbox. Having already made most of the investments
needed to master these technologies, Renault Group has
been able to halve the cost of its batteries over the last ten
years and will halve it again over the next decade. Renault
Group
will
gradually
embed
new
technological
improvements from 2024 on its EESM, which will lower costs
and improve the efficiency of the motor.
Hydrogen
While electrification remains the backbone of Renault
Group’s strategy to reduce the CO2 emissions of its
passenger vehicles, other technologies are being studied for
utility vehicles and even for passenger vehicles to avoid
missing any opportunities in niche areas of use. Renault
Group began upstream studies at a very early stage to
assess
the
potential
of
hydrogen,
as
part
of
a
comprehensive “cradle-to-grave” life cycle approach.
Renault Group is also exploring other hydrogen applications,
in particular through prototypes and concept cars, as well
as active monitoring of the evolution of the hydrogen
ecosystem, in particular at the European level. Hydrogen is
also being studied as an energy source for our plants.
(See 2.2.2.1.5.2).
Low-emission combustion and hybrid engines
As part of its strategy to reduce the carbon footprint of
combustion and hybrid engines, Renault Group is relying on
E-Tech hybrid technology. Following the launch of the first
generation in 2020 for the Renault range and its subsequent
rollout to the Dacia range, a second generation was
launched in 2022 with Austral. This technology consists of
five main components:
●a multimode, clutchless smart gearbox resulting from our
Formula  1 experience has been developed to reduce
energy losses. It offers a strong acceleration capacity,
intensity
in
mid-range
acceleration
and
reduced
consumption and CO2 emissions;
●an e-engine, the main electric motor, which starts the
vehicle, ensures 100% electric driving, drives the wheels
and allows the battery to be recharged;
●a secondary electric motor that acts as a starter and
high-voltage generator for the petrol engine. It also
serves as a battery regenerator – and stabiliser in gear
changes;
●a battery that stores the energy necessary for vehicle
travel and then ensures the autonomy of the vehicle in
electric mode (2 kWh battery for the Astral);
●a combustion engine specially designed to reduce
consumption CO2 emissions. It is equipped with a
particulate filter and combines economy, efficiency and
performance.

1.4.4 Focus on some 2024 concepts, prototypes
and demonstrators
Engineering is there to serve Renault Group and its brands. The following selection is not exhaustive.
1.4.4.1 Autonomous shuttles
for Roland-Garros
and for public transport
As
a
premium
partner
of
the
Roland-Garros
2024
tournament, Renault innovated with WeRide by setting up a
trial of electric and autonomous shuttles. The aim was to
facilitate access to the stadium while demonstrating the
maturity of new technologies for automated public
transport services.
In Europe alone, over 400 major cities will gradually develop
sustainable urban mobility plans to promote zero- and low-
emission mobility while continuing to ensure mobility for
their populations.
For Renault Group, automated transport is a relevant and
necessary solution to meet these growing needs effectively.
Before the end of this decade, Renault Group will develop a
robotised electric miniBus platform capable of integrating
automation solutions from specialised partners.
More flexible, autonomous miniBuses without on-board
operators will be able to operate safely 24/7 and will serve as
a zero-emission alternative or an efficient complement in
terms of cost and CO2 per passenger-kilometre to existing
solutions (train, tramway, bus). A simple remote supervision
system will be needed to operate a fleet of vehicles.
1.4.4.2 “U1st Vision”, innovative, human-
centred mobile services
In May 2024, at VivaTech, the Software République – an
innovation ecosystem for smart, safe and sustainable
mobility – unveiled the “U1st Vision” (You First Vision)
concept. This is an innovative technological demonstrator
showcasing its vision of services “closer to the citizen”,
offering easy access to a range of varied, personalised, and
secure local services.
The first use case is the “Health Pop-Up” health services
module. It incorporates 21 medical parameters, featuring
12  innovations such as an avatar powered by Artificial
Intelligence. This module addresses the challenges faced in
“medical deserts” by providing an integrated, one-stop
mobile service for preventive care, diagnosis and disease
monitoring. It is managed by a medical assistant and
enables teleconsultations with healthcare professionals.
A total of 22 companies participated in this project:
●The 7 Software République member companies (Renault
Group, Dassault Systèmes, Eviden of the Atos Group,
JCDecaux, Orange, STMicroelectronics and Thales);
●3
partner
companies
in
the
healthcare
sector
(EssilorLuxottica, Praesens and Withings);
●12 other contributors (ANCT, Emsense, Flexis, Kanopymed,
Loxamed, Mon Espace Santé, NorbertHealth, OneVisage,
Orosound, Sonup, TIB and Usense).

## Page 72

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
1.4.4.3 Renault Estafette Concept:
the utility vehicle designed
for the city, 100% electric,
intelligent and adaptable
Estafette Concept is Renault’s vision of its future electric
vans and a precursor of what Flexis, the independent
company founded by Renault Group, Volvo Group and CMA
CGM Group, will produce to meet the challenges of the
energy transition and last-mile logistics.
Designed for the city, Estafette Concept is both compact
and spacious. It combines the footprint of the Kangoo L2
with the agility of Clio and the load capacity of Master.
Designed for drivers, the Estafette Concept optimises their
operations in terms of time and safety thanks to its
ergonomic design.
Connected and intelligent, the vehicle benefits from the
SDV (Software Defined Vehicle) electronic architecture of
the FlexEVan platform, developed by Ampere. It enables the
integration of advanced features such as real-time updates
and predictive maintenance and contributes to a reduction
in operating costs of around 20%.
1.4.4.4 A390_β: Alpine’s new fastback
sports silhouette with
revolutionary technologies
Fluidity, sportiness and spaciousness: the A390_β (beta)
show car is the forerunner of the second of the three models
of the Dream Garage designed by Alpine. Its sleek,
aerodynamic lines give a glimpse of the future 5-seater
production model, 100% electric, designed for everyday use
and capable of transforming itself into a racing machine for
a much sportier drive.
Based on a heavily revamped version of Ampere’s AmpR
Medium platform, it will be equipped with three electric
motors (one at the front and two at the rear) and will have an
innovative non-permanent four-wheel drive system. In
concrete terms, traction at the axles will be managed by an
intelligent torque management system developed and
perfected by Alpine engineers: Active Torque Vectoring.
By intelligently controlling the torque distribution between
the front and rear axles, and independently to the left and
right rear wheels, this technology optimises the vehicle’s
longitudinal performance and cornering dynamics, while
ensuring a high level of reassurance and confidence,
regardless of the level of grip under each wheel.
The combination of three electric motors monitored by
Active Torque Vectoring, previously reserved for hypercars
produced in limited series, is a world first in the segment.
1.4.4.5 Alpenglow Hy6, the prototype
equipped with Alpine’s first
hydrogen-powered 6-cylinder
internal combustion engine
First presented in 2022 as a concept car, Alpenglow returned
to the Paris Motor Show in 2024 in its most accomplished
version: Alpenglow Hy6.
A world first and the centrepiece of this evolution, its all-new
twin-turbocharged
3.5-litre
V6
engine
is
specifically
designed to run on hydrogen. It was developed in two years
by the Alpine teams in Viry-Châtillon. In addition to its
exceptional performance (740 hp at 600 rpm for a top speed
in excess of 330 kph) and musicality, the hydrogen internal
combustion engine technology makes Alpine a forerunner in
offering a promising new approach to the decarbonisation
of sports vehicles and motor sport.

1.4.4.6 Renault Embleme,
the demonstrator rising
to the challenge
of low-carbon mobility
Exhibited at the Paris Motor Show 2024 and unveiled by
Ampere, the Renault Embleme demo car is a masterpiece of
engineering and a laboratory of innovation. Its ambitious
goal is to design an operating family prototype – liveable,
comfortable
and
high-tech
–
while
achieving
an
unprecedented level of decarbonisation.
To achieve this, Renault Embleme was designed around a set
of specifications based on eco-design. Engineers and
designers worked together to find the best solutions for
aerodynamics and efficiency. The result is stunning: an
elegant 4.80-meter long shooting brake embodying the
brand’s future design language and featuring the new large
openR panorama screen inside. The rear-mounted 100%
electric “dual-energy” engine, powered by electricity and
hydrogen, offers an advantageous combination for low-
carbon journeys over both short and long distances.
A methodology based on the vehicle lifecycle analysis
Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) is a scientific method used to quantify a vehicle’s environmental impacts from cradle to grave. It factors in raw
material extraction and component production, as well as vehicle assembly, transport, use, maintenance and recycling. It is the
international, multi-criterion tool used by Renault Group. One of its main uses is to calculate the global warming potential associated with
greenhouse gas emissions measured in CO2 equivalent (CO2eq) per vehicle. Note that Renault Group calculates the actual consumption of its
vehicles over 200,000 km. Renault Group and Ampere also support the proposal made by the French automotive industry to measure the
impact of the entire vehicle life cycle. Renault Megane E-Tech electric, for example, the emits 24 tonnes of CO2eq during its life cycle. That is
practically half that of an equivalent model running on fossil fuels (50 tonnes of CO2eq. in the case of a Captur with a petrol engine), showing
that all-electric mobility is more virtuous in terms of CO2eq. emissions. The goal for the Embleme project is just 5 tonnes of CO2eq. from cradle
to grave, i.e. a reduction of almost 90%!
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
1.5 Regulatory Environment
1.5.1 Overarching sustainability
and ecological transition regulations
1.5.1.1 Main objectives
of this regulatory area
In the context of climate change and ecological transition,
the European Union and France have adopted ambitious
regulations such as the CSRD (Corporate Sustainability
Reporting Directive), the “Green Taxonomy”, the CBAM
(Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) and the AGEC
(French Anti-Waste and Circular Economy Act). These
regulatory initiatives aim to encourage sustainability,
stimulate investment in the green economy and ensure a
fair transition to a low-carbon economy. Carmakers,
including Renault Group, are actively involved in the
development of these regulations through working groups,
ensuring that the new standards take account of their
technical and industrial capabilities as well as their
implementation timescales.
1.5.1.2 Impacts of this regulatory
area on Renault Group
activities
These recent regulations require Renault Group to adapt its
processes and comply with stricter sustainability standards,
influencing its operations and finances. Operationally, they
require a review of existing processes and the adoption of
more stringent sustainability standards, while imposing
detailed reporting obligations. For example, the CSRD
requires Renault Group to disclose the impact of its
activities on the environment and society as well as the
related financial risks and opportunities, while the “Green
Taxonomy” assesses the alignment of activities with EU
environmental objectives. The CBAM already indirectly
requires increased reporting on the carbon footprints of
imported products to calculate the carbon tax applicable
from 2026.
From a financial perspective, these regulations generate
compliance costs, ranging from investment in information
systems to adapting processes to meet sustainability
criteria. They may also lead to additional costs associated
with
carbon
taxes.
However,
they
offer
financial
opportunities by improving access to capital through green
financial instruments and opening up new markets for
sustainable products and services, thereby helping to
mitigate environmental risks over the long term.
1.5.1.3 Reference texts
The basis for these regulations comes from various
authorities, including states, communities of states and the
United Nations. These include:
●Sustainability reporting regulations, which are evolving
with the implementation of the Corporate Sustainability
Reporting Directive (CSRD) (Directive 2022/2464) within the
EU. This directive, transposed into French law by Ordinance
2023-1142 of 6 December 2023, succeeds the Non-Financial
Performance Declaration (DPEF). It requires the publication
of an in-depth, standardised non-financial report based on
an analysis of the impact of economic activities on their
environment and the associated financial risks and
opportunities. It is based on 12 sustainability reporting
standards,
the
European
Sustainability
Reporting
Standards (ESRS), covering the environmental, social and
governance
issues
facing
companies
through
approximately 800 quantitative and qualitative data
points for this first year. As a respondent to the EFPD,
Renault Group is eligible from the 2024 financial year and
therefore includes the CSRD-compliant sustainability
report in chapter 2 of this Universal Registration Document.
●European regulations No. 2020/852 and No. 2023/2486 of
the “Green Taxonomy” aimed at promoting sustainable
investments within the EU, where the Circular Economy
objective is subject to the notion of alignment for the first
time this year, thus taking into account the notion of
alignment with all the objectives of the “Green
Taxonomy”.
●France’s “AGEC” law of 10 February 2020, to fight waste
and promote a circular economy aims to extend industrial
operators’ legal responsibility for management of their
waste according to the principle of Extended Producer
Responsibility (EPR). In 2024, Renault Group is affected by
the EPR for tyres, oils and lubricants and especially the EPR
for End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV). The Group has chosen to
meet this last obligation by setting up an Individual System
based on the expertise of INDRA Automobile Recycling, a
subsidiary of The Future Is NEUTRAL and France’s leading
OUV processing network. This Individual System received
ministerial approval on 20 June 2024.
●The new CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism)
Regulation (EU) 2023/956, which aims to subject a list of
products imported into the EU customs territory to a carbon
pricing equivalent to that applied to manufacturers
producing these products  on European territory and is
supplemented by Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/1773,
entered into force on 1 October 2023.

1.5.2 Vehicle manufacturing regulations 
1.5.2.1 Main objectives
of this regulatory area
Automotive manufacturing regulations meet governments’
requirements in terms of both personal safety and the
environmental impact of the vehicle fleet (reduction of
pollutant emissions, CO2, noise, pressure on resources, etc.). 
The European Union’s regulatory framework is based in
particular on Regulation (EU) No. 2018/858, amended by
Regulation (EU) No. 2019/2144, on the approval and market
surveillance of motor vehicles and their trailers, and of
systems,
components
and
separate
technical
units
intended for such vehicles, known as the General Safety
Regulation (GSR), which concerns general safety and the
protection of vehicle occupants and vulnerable road users,
reinforcing type-approval and production process controls,
as well as statistical and continuous monitoring of vehicles
placed on the market. 
Automotive manufacturers are also actively involved in
strengthening regulations at global level, through working
groups under the aegis of the European Commission, the UN,
etc., to ensure that these regulatory changes take account
of their technical and industrial capacities, and their
implementation timescales. 
1.5.2.2 Impacts of this regulatory
area on Renault Group
activities
This regulatory area has very significant impacts on Renault
Group activities because of the number, diversity and
frequency of application of all the regulations it covers.
These regulations directly affect the technical definitions of
our vehicles and the associated resources and information
systems and have a real impact on our design, production,
marketing and after-sale systems.
On the one hand, they involve challenges in terms of
innovation, investment and development costs to ensure
that our products meet all the defined regulatory
requirements. On the other hand, they require us to be able
to deliver our vehicles in compliance with the application
dates set by obtaining third-party certification enabling
them to be sold and registered in the various markets in
which they are sold.
This regulatory area also requires Renault Group to
systematically check the compliance of its products during
manufacture and throughout the life of the vehicles and to
be able to demonstrate this compliance to a supervisory
authority on an ongoing basis. Failure to ensure compliance
can result in financial penalties and represent a major
legal risk.
1.5.2.3 Reference texts
Globally, the sources for the regulations come from states,
communities of states or the United Nations (UN), as
represented on the world map below. In particular, the
United Nations creates UN Regulations (UN-R) and Global
Technical Regulations (GTR).
These regulations are grouped into several dozen regulatory
areas. Some of the most important regulatory areas include
the following:
●Regulation (EU) No. 2018/858, on which the European
Union’s regulatory framework is based, was amended by
Regulation (EU) No. 2019/2144, on the approval and market
surveillance of motor vehicles and their trailers, and of
systems, components and separate technical units
intended for such vehicles, known as the General Safety
Regulation (GSR), which concerns general safety and the
protection of vehicle occupants and vulnerable road
users, reinforcing type-approval and production process
controls, as well as statistical and continuous monitoring
of vehicles placed on the market. 
●For pollutant emissions, they are covered by Regulation
(EC) No 715/2007, amended by Regulation (EU) No 2018/858
on type approval of motor vehicles with respect to
emissions from light passenger and commercial vehicles
(Euro 5 and Euro 6) and on access to vehicle repair and
maintenance information, supplemented by Commission
Regulation (EU) No 2017/1151 of 1 June 2017, amended by
(EU) 2018/1832.  The next step, known as Euro7, is due to
come into force at the end of 2026.
●For CO2, Regulation (EU) 2019/631 lays down performance
standards for CO2 emissions applicable to new passenger
cars and light commercial vehicles. Amended by Regulation
(EU) 851/2023, it defines the objectives to be achieved up to
2035, with risks of financial penalties if they are not met.
●In terms of passive and active safety, the entry into force
of
Regulation
(EU)
2019/2144
on
type-approval
requirements for motor vehicles and their trailers, and
systems, components and separate technical units
intended for such vehicles, as regards their general
safety, requires manufacturers to incorporate a whole
range of safety equipment and to design the structure of
new vehicles in line with new requirements aimed at
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
73

## Page 76

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
minimising the severity of accidents.  Among the latest
major regulations, we can highlight those relating
advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), applicable
to all vehicles from mid-2024.
●The security component has also been extended to include
a Cybersecurity component with the entry into force of
Regulation
(EC)
No
661/2009,
which
introduces
requirements
for
putting
in
place
a
structured
cybersecurity governance system to ensure that all
decisions and approvals are traceable and transparent to
the supervisory authorities and that all state-of-the-art
technical solutions to limit cyber risk are incorporated into
vehicle design. This system must be accredited by a third
party. Alongside these requirements, manufacturers must
implement a Software Update Management System
(United Nations Regulation No. 156) to ensure traceability of
software update decisions, their impacts on the product
and its certification, as well as the compatibility and
configurations of vehicles in circulation. This system will
make it easier to demonstrate that vehicles remain
compliant, even after any software through FOTA
(Firmware Over The Air) updates.
●With
regard
to
electric
and/or
hybrid
vehicles,
new regulations are developing very quickly, particularly
around traction batteries. Developments in battery
regulations will therefore cover the entire life cycle of
batteries, from the supply of raw materials to their
treatment as waste at the end of their life, while at the
same time introducing conditions to make it easier to
extend the life of batteries, including in the context of a
2nd life of batteries and the reuse of their parts. The new
requirements include performance, sustainability, carbon
footprint, recyclability, the introduction of auditable
ethical rules throughout the supply chain (due diligence),
the obligation to communicate with customers through a
digital passport, and greater involvement in the operation
of collection and recycling channels through Extended
Producer Responsibility (EPR).
It is also worth noting a significant evolution in current
battery
safety
requirements
to
include
standards
addressing the risk of thermal propagation.
●Regarding
substances,
prohibited
materials
and
recycling, the regulation focuses on registering all
substances and materials present in vehicles, imposing
bans and restrictions on them with tracking for each
vehicle to prevent harmful or prohibited products from
entering the market and to facilitate recycling of vehicles
and their batteries at their end of life. The European Union
provides most of the global regulatory guidance on
substances and the recycling of vehicles and batteries.
Examples include European regulations such as REACH
(Registration, Evaluation, Authorization and Restriction of
Chemicals), POP (Persistent Organic Pollutants), and
biocidal products for substances.
●The regulation of “autonomous” vehicles can roughly be
divided into two parts. On the one hand, without being
compulsory equipment, the regulations on vehicles
offering driver delegation services provide a framework
for systems enabling driver delegation on motorways
(Advanced Lane Keeping Systems). On the other hand,
“driverless” vehicles are addressed through testing
conducted at the national level as well as authorisations
issued locally for public transport and other uses.
Regulation (EU) 2019/2144, supplemented by Implementing
Regulation (EU) 2022/1426 of 5 August 2022, already
allows these vehicles to be type-approved.
●The “connected vehicle” creates a major challenge
around data, in particular “data access and transfer”,
whether to or from the vehicle. The European Data Act is a
horizontal regulation (which applies to all kinds of
products and services) to regulate this data “business”, in
which it is necessary to ensure, in particular, for product
manufacturers and service providers, that the balance
between free competition and intellectual property is
maintained, under the arbitration of private data
protection laws (General Data Protection Regulation).
This European Data Act will come into force by the end
of 2026.
●The regulation also focuses on strengthening oversight.
For example, the Member States of the European Union
have put in place the market surveillance required by
Regulation (EU) 2018/858 on the type approval of vehicles
and ensure the sampling and compliance checks on
vehicles representative of their respective markets.
These tests, derived from type-approval tests, help
achieve the European Union’s goal of monitoring the
automotive industry (manufacturers and equipment
suppliers) and supervising the operation of technical
services to detect and correct malfunctions in the
European type-approval system.
74

1.5.3 Environmental regulations for operational sites
1.5.3.1 Main objectives
of this regulatory area
The aim of environmental regulations is to protect
ecosystems and reduce the environmental and health
impact of human activities.
Different regulations apply to the Group’s operational sites,
depending on the industrial activities and their scale. In
general, the greater the environmental impact, the more
stringent the requirements.
1.5.3.2 Impacts of this area
on Renault Group activities
The regulatory framework is very important because in
order to carry out our activities, production sites must obtain
environmental permits from the authorities. These permits
are a prerequisite for the operation of the sites and
therefore the production of our products.
Generally speaking, in all countries, operating permits are
granted after examination of a dossier presenting the
environmental impacts of the activities and the measures
put in place to reduce them and comply with the regulations
in force.
To comply with environmental standards, the sites are
equipped with equipment designed to prevent or treat
pollution. This equipment represents an investment of several
tens of millions of euros for each industrial site (CAPEX).
Operating costs (OPEX) estimated at €25 per vehicle are also
incurred as a result of these regulations, and an increase in
these costs is expected before the group’s decarbonisation
plan is fully implemented.
1.5.3.3 Reference texts
●Directive 2010/75/EU of the European Parliament and of the
Council of 24 November 2010 on industrial emissions
(integrated pollution prevention and control), known as the
IED (Industrial Emissions Directive), revised on 15/07/2024.
●The
reference
documents
on
the
best
available
techniques associated with the preceding directive (IED)
and applicable to our sites:
– The “Best Available Techniques Reference Document
for the Surface Treatment using organic Solvents” (STS
BREF) of 2020 for automotive painting activities;
– The “Best Available Techniques Reference Document
for the Surface Treatment of Metals and Plastics” (STM
BREF) of 2006 (revision in progress);
– The “Best Available Techniques Reference Document
for the Smitheries and Foundries Industry” (SF BREF) of
2005 (revision in progress);
– The “Best Available Techniques Reference Document
for Large Combustion Plants” (LCP BREF) of 2017.
●Directive (EU) 2015/2193 of the European Parliament and
of the Council of 25 November 2015 on the limitation of
emissions of certain pollutants into the atmosphere from
medium combustion plants;
●European framework directives:
– Directive 2008/98/EC of the European Parliament and of
the Council of 19 November 2008 on waste and
repealing certain directives, known as the “Waste
Framework Directive”;
– Directive 2000/60/EC of the European Parliament and
of the Council of 23 October 2000 establishing a
framework for Community action in the field of water
policy, known as the “Water Framework Directive”.
●The Basel Convention on the Control of Transboundary
Movements of Hazardous Wastes and their Disposal,
approved by Decision 93/98/EEC of the Council of the
European Communities on 1 February 1993 and its
associated texts;
●Regulation (EU) 2024/573 of the European Parliament
and of the Council of 7 February 2024 on fluorinated
greenhouse gases, amending Directive (EU) 2019/1937
and repealing Regulation (EU) 517/2014, known as “F-gas
Regulation III”;
●Directive 2003/87/EC of the European Parliament and of the
Council of 13 October 2003 establishing a scheme for
greenhouse gas emission allowance trading within the
Community and amending Council Directive 96/61/EC as
amended by Directive (EU) 2023/959 of 10 May 2023;
●In addition to European regulations, regulations specific
to each country and locality must be taken into account.

## Page 78

Renault Group
General presentation of Renault Group
1.5.4 Community design regulations
applied to spare parts
1.5.4.1 Main objectives
of this regulatory area
This European regulation, concerning spare parts and, more
specifically, Community designs, is currently evolving. The
objective is to harmonise the liberalisation of the spare parts
market across all EU countries by ending the protection of
these parts through designs and models. Today, it is still
possible
to
take
legal
action
against
third-party
counterfeiters selling exterior visible spare parts (lighting,
bumpers, doors, door mirrors, etc.), but this will no longer be
possible in a few years once the proposed directive is
adopted and transposed into national law.
1.5.4.2 Impacts of this area
on Renault Group activities
The impact on the spare parts sector (Community designs
and models) is financial and significant, as it will no longer
be possible to prevent third parties from “copying” our parts
for the replacement market. Today, it is still possible to act
on the basis of counterfeiting in some countries, such as
France, but this will soon no longer be possible.
1.5.4.3 Reference texts
European Regulation No. 6/2002 is under revision following
a proposed Directive dated 20 Dec. 2023, which aims to end
the protection by designs and models for spare parts in all
EU countries.
1.5.5 Banking regulations
1.5.5.1 Main objectives
of this regulatory area
●The aim of banking regulation is to put in place a system
that ensures the financial soundness of banks, their good
governance and the stability of the financial system by
defining, in particular:
– rules governing the approval conditions for credit
institutions, the governance of credit institutions,
internal control and senior executive compensation, in
order to harmonise the regulations applicable to credit
institutions in these areas at European level;
– prudential requirements for credit institutions, i.e. new
requirements in terms of capital ratio, liquidity ratio and
leverage ratio;
– rules on estimates of probability of default and
estimates of loss given default, good risk management
practices for credit institutions in relation to the
management of non-performing exposures (NPEs),
renegotiated exposures and foreclosed assets, as well
as
guidance
relating
to
institutions’
internal
governance arrangements and procedures in relation
to credit granting processes, and associated risk
management procedures, in particular the assessment
of creditworthiness;
– management of bankruptcies of European banks in a way
that preserves financial stability and minimises costs for
taxpayers. This gives the competent authorities the
means to intervene before difficulties arise and, if
necessary, at the start of the resolution process;
– The level of accessibility to registers of beneficial
owners to enhance transparency of legal entities and
complex legal structures, as well as the enhanced due
diligence measures to be implemented regarding
business relationships or operations involving high-risk
third countries;
and
uniform
supervision
by
authorities
to
protect
customers and their deposits by defining:
– better consumer protection by harmonising national
credit distribution rules;
– better consumer protection by harmonising national
insurance product distribution rules.
1.5.5.2 Impacts of this area
on Renault Group activities
●This regulatory area has a significant impact on Renault
Group, notably in financial terms. In particular, Mobilize
F.S. must comply at all times with certain capital
adequacy and liquidity ratios, which entails tying up a
significant amount of equity capital.

1.5.5.3 Reference texts
Directive 2013/36 of 26 June 2013 on access to the activity of
credit institutions and the prudential supervision of credit
institutions and investment firms, known as the CRD IV, was
transposed into French law by Order 2014/158 and the
Decree of 3 November 2014.
Directive  2019/878 amended Directive  2013/36 as regards
exempted entities, financial holding companies, mixed
financial holding companies, compensation, supervisory
measures and powers, and capital conservation measures.
This directive was transposed into French law by Order
2020–1635 of 21 December 2020 containing various
provisions for adapting legislation to European Union law in
financial matters. 
European Regulation 575/2013 of 26 June 2013
This text was amended by regulation  2019/630 as regards
the minimum coverage of losses on non-performing
exposures.
The CRR has also been amended by Regulation (EU) 2019/876
of 20 May 2019 as regards the leverage ratio, the net stable
funding ratio, requirements for own funds and eligible
liabilities, counterparty credit risk, market risk, exposures to
central counterparties, exposures to collective investment
undertakings, large exposures, reporting and disclosure
requirements, as well as Regulation (EU) 2020/873 of 24 June
2020 as regards certain adjustments to be made in
response to the Covid-19 pandemic. 
Directive 2014/59 of 15 May 2014 establishing a framework
for the recovery and resolution of credit institutions and
investment firms, known as the BRRD, set out a framework
for the recovery and resolution of credit institutions.
These measures were supplemented by Regulation 806/2014
of 15 July 2014 establishing a Single Resolution Mechanism
(SRM) and a Single Resolution Fund (SRF). Finally, this directive
was amended by Directive 2019/879 of 20 May 2019, as regards
the capacity to absorb losses and recapitalise credit
institutions. In particular, this directive clarifies the setting of
the MREL (Minimum Requirements for own funds and Eligible
Liabilities specific to each establishment) and was transposed
into French law through Order 2020-1636 of 21 December 2020
on the resolution regime in the banking sector. 
Directive 2008/48 of 23 April 2008 on consumer credit
agreements was transposed into French law by Law 2010-737
of 1 July 2010 on consumer credit reform. The purpose of these
texts is to provide better consumer protection and to
harmonise national credit distribution rules. These texts
require credit institutions to strengthen consumer information
by providing them with a standardised European pre-
contractual information sheet. This directive will be repealed
by Directive 2023-2225 with effect from 20 November 2026.
Directive 2018/843 of 30 May 2018 amended Directive 2015/849
on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the
purpose of money laundering or terrorist financing. This text
was transposed in France by Government Order 2020-115 of
12 February 2021.
Directive 2016/97 of 20 January 2016 on insurance distribution,
transposed in France by Order 2018361 of 16 May 2018.
On 18 January 2017, the European Banking Authority published
guidelines on the application of the definition of default (EBA/
GL/2016/07). This text applies from 1 January 2021. 
In addition, the European Commission adopted Delegated
Regulation 2018/171 entitled “Final report on materiality threshold
for credit obligation past due“ (rts/2016/06). This text introduces a
single methodology for days past due counting based on the
application of absolute and relative materiality thresholds. 
In its Regulation 2018/1845 of 21 November 2018, the ECB set
the absolute threshold at €100 for retail exposures and €500
for other exposures. These rules must be complied with
from 31 December 2020. 
Finally, the European Banking Authority also published
guidelines for estimates of probability of default and estimates
of loss given default (EBA-GL-2017-16). On 31 October 2018, the
European Banking Authority also published guidance setting
out good risk management practices for credit institutions in
relation to the management of non-performing exposures
(NPEs), renegotiated exposures and foreclosed assets
(EBA/GL/2018/06), as well as guidance relating to institutions’
internal governance arrangements and procedures in relation
to credit granting processes, and associated risk management
procedures, in particular the assessment of creditworthiness
(EBA/GL/2020/06). 
This guidance is part of wider work by the European Banking
Authority to reduce unwarranted variability in internal model
outputs, while preserving the risk sensitivity of capital
requirements and loss given default estimates (EBA–GL–2017–16).

1.6 Post-closing events
Renault Group and Geely Holding Group sign a framework agreement in Brazil
On 17 February 2025, Renault Group and Geely Holding Group
announced the signing of a framework agreement that
extends their strategic cooperation to the production and
marketing by Renault do Brasil of zero-emission and low-
emission vehicles, strengthening their strategic partnership.
Geely Holding would invest in Renault do Brasil to become
its minority shareholder, which would allow Geely Holding
access to local production, sales and service resources. The
project remains subject to the signing of final agreements
and prior approval by the relevant regulatory authorities. As
part of this cooperation, the two state-of-the-art production
plants of Renault Group’s Ayrton Senna complex in São José
dos Pinhais, Paraná, would be made available to produce
these all-new vehicles for Geely Holding and for Renault, in
addition to the current Renault range. On a commercial
level, Renault do Brasil would become a distributor of Geely
Holding’s zero-emission and low-emission vehicle portfolio
in Brazil.

## Page 84

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Introduction
The present chapter has been completely redesigned this year, to comply with European Union’s CSRD (Corporate
Sustainability Reporting Directive - 2022/2464/EU) and the French due diligence law (law 2017-399 related to the Devoir de
Vigilance).
Both reglementations are based on a risk-driven approach, with some methodological differences: for example, potential
negative consequences on environment or society are called “risks“ by the Devoir de Vigilence and assessed on a net basis,
whilst CSRD calls them “impacts“ and requires to assess them on a gross basis. They are nevertheless consistent with the
approach adopted by the Group’s major risk management system, detailed in chapter 4 of this document. Cross-references
have been established whenever applicable to avoid redundancy in statements.
Most of the information of this chapter cover the CSRD requirements. Being an industrial company that designs and
manufactures complex products and provides mobility and financing services for millions of customers all around the world,
Renault Group has strong interdependencies with the planet and the people that live in. This has led to retain 69 impacts, risks
and opportunities as material, according to the CSRD analysis methodology, on each of which Renault Group’s governance,
policies, targets and action plans are exposed, following the order of the European Sustainability Reporting standards (ESRS)
and indexed according to EFRAG’s data point list. As required by CSRD, European Taxonomy disclosure is embedded in this part
(See 2.2.2.6).
We also disclose, at the end of this chapter (See 2.4), information related to previously disclosed frameworks (SASB), with cross-
reference to CSRD and/or Devoir de Vigilance data points whenever applicable.
To help the reader in catching the essentials of Renault Group’s sustainable development, an executive summary of this
comprehensive chapter is provided below.
2.1 Creating economic, social
and environmental value
2.1.1 Sustainable Development strategy
The Group’s sustainable strategy is based on three pillars.
These
three
pillars
support
the
Renaulution,
a
transformation
into
a
greener,
more
tech-intensive
company driven by data, energy and services:
●the first pillar, the environment, involves fighting climate
change and the optimisation of resource use through the
development of our circular economy activities;
●the second pillar, safety, by leveraging the technology in
our vehicles to improve the safety of users of our vehicles
and services on the roads. This pillar also covers the
safety of employees in the workplace;
●the third pillar, inclusion. The shift towards the new
businesses of electrification, data and the circular economy
will be achieved by supporting the transformation of skills
and promoting diversity within the Group. “Carmaker and
careMakers”: we build cars while taking care of those who
design and manufacture them, those who use them, while
limiting our impact on the planet on which we produce them.
This strategy is the result of a major collaborative and unifying
process involving the Company’s management bodies, the
Group Works Council and all employees worldwide (through an
open global survey) as well as external stakeholder panels.
A study on the company’s culture was also carried out on this
occasion. The members of the Strategy and Sustainable
Development Committee and the Leadership Team were
regularly informed of the progress of the work.
82

Sustainable development
02
Announced at the General Meeting of 23 April 2021, the Sustainable Development strategy is structured around three pillars:
environment, security, inclusion.  These three pillars are broken down into measurable objectives with deadlines for 2025 and
2030. In total, 20 ambitions and objectives were identified.
Sustainable development strategy: 20 ambitions, objectives & KPIs
These KPIs are used to steer performance improvement projects.

## Page 88

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.1.2 Governance dedicated to sustainable development
The Sustainable Development Department reports to the Strategy Department. The 20 ambitions and objectives, divided into
the three ESG (Environment–Social and Societal–Governance) dimensions, are steered by identified cross-disciplinary working
groups, and a key performance indicator has been defined for each of them.
A dedicated governance
The sustainable development action areas are linked to an
Executive Committee member or a Group Management
Committee member and are coordinated by the Sustainable
Development Division. The main Departments involved in
the deployment of the Group’s commitments are:
●The Sustainable Development Department, responsible
for an interdisciplinary, partnership-based approach to
sustainable development throughout the value chain and
environmental and societal actions and innovations. It
aims to reduce the footprint and the adverse impacts of
activities, products and services over the life cycle and
introduce social and solidarity-based and/or circular
economy business models to boost the Company’s
medium- and long-term competitiveness. This Division
reports to the Strategy and Business Development
Division, whose director is a member of the Renault Group
Executive Committee;
●The Human Resources Department is responsible for
optimising
allocated
resources,
skills
development,
employee involvement, social dialogue, ethics, and health,
safety, ergonomics and environmental matters (HSEE). It
implements Renault Group’s Health and Safety policy. Its
aim is to achieve zero accidents and occupational
illnesses. The HSEE division also includes the Environment
division,
which
implements
the
reduction
of
environmental risks and impacts by defining rules,
monitoring their application, and leading a network of
correspondents;
●The
Procurement
Department,
in
charge
of
decarbonising purchased materials and components and
implementing the responsible purchasing policy;
●The Engineering Department, which is jointly responsible
with
the
Product
Planning
Department
for
the
deployment of on-board safety commitments in the
Group’s
vehicles
as
well
as
the
objectives
of
electrification and decarbonisation of the range;
●The Industry Department, which is responsible in
particular for the decarbonisation of production sites and
more generally for the environmental impact of activities;
●The Finance Department, in charge of connecting the
sustainability report with the financial statements and
conducting the external audit;
●Lastly, the management bodies of the brands (Renault,
Dacia, Alpine, Mobilize) are also deploying the objectives
of the new strategy within their Business Unit.
Individually or jointly, depending on the cross-functional
nature of the subjects, the Sustainable Development
Department and these Operational Departments bring issues
relating to strategic orientation before the decision-making
bodies at the Chief Executive Officer or Group Executive
Committee member level. These departments then roll them
out within the Company through the programs, functions and
business lines, using internal networks and by developing
external partnerships if necessary.

Sustainable development
02
The Sustainable Development Department contributes to
the analysis of the Group’s risks, notably those associated
with global warming, practices in the supply chain, health
and working conditions, and harm to the environment and
people in the event of malfunctions in the facilities operated
by the Group.
The Audit and Risks Committee of the Board of Directors
reviews the annual sustainability report.
The Strategy and Sustainability Committee of the Board
of Directors has the following main tasks:
●ensuring a high level of commitment in terms of non-
financial
compliance,
ethics
and
social
and
environmental responsibility;
●assessing the Group’s policies, guidelines and charters;
●reviewing and assessing the extra-financial indicator
reporting and control procedures;
●reviewing the deployment of projects and initiatives;
●provide expertise to the Audit and Risk Committee for the
report review;
In 2024, it met quarterly.
2.1.3 Reference systems and standards
Renault Group complies with international standards
designed to support or regulate businesses’ corporate
social responsibility practices. It is committed to respecting
the founding Principles and promoting the universal values
of the Global Compact.
The Group factors these Principles into its policies and
implements them in accordance with internal guidelines.
The main reference texts are:
●the Universal Declaration of Human Rights;
●the Ten principles of the Global Compact adopted at the
initiative of the United Nations and signed by Renault on
26 July 2001 (See below);
●the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of
Indigenous Peoples of 2007;
●the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises,
updated on May 25th, 2011;
●Renault Group publishes a report according to the SASB
Transportation (Sustainability Accounting Standards
Board) standard: see Section 2.4;
●the Renault Group Ethics Charter in all its forms, approved by
the Renault Group Board of Directors on October 3rd, 2012;
●the Global Framework Agreement covering social,
societal and environmental responsibility, signed on July
2nd, 2013, and based in particular on ILO standards and
ISO 26000, as well as its rollout to suppliers;
●the Paris Agreement of 30 November 2015 (COP 21):
Renault Group has aligned its carbon footprint reduction
with the objective of reducing greenhouse gas (GHG)
emissions in order to keep the increase in global
temperature “well below 2°C and even to continue the
action taken to limit the rise in temperatures to 1.5°C”;
●the global framework agreement of July 9th, 2019 on
“changing life at work”;
●ISO 9001 (all Renault Group manufacturing sites are ISO
9001-certified);
●ISO 14001 for environmental management;
●ISO 14040 and 14044 for vehicle life cycle assessment;
●ISO 14021 for the definition of recycled content;
●the GHG reporting protocol, for the reporting of
greenhouse gas emissions;
●IATF 16949, which is the automotive industry’s quality
management standard. Renault Group is one of the nine
founding vehicle manufacturers of the IATF World Wide
(owner of this standard);
●United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG);
●The Global Platform for Sustainable Natural Rubber
(GPSNR);
●the
deep
sea
mining
Moratorium
of
Deep
Sea
Conservation Coalition (DSCC).

## Page 90

Renault Group
Sustainable development
United Nations Global Compact
“For more than 20 years, Renault s.a.s. has been committed to the UN Global Compact and its 10 principles in the areas of
human rights, labour, the environment and anticorruption.
Today, we are reaffirming our commitment to respect and promote the universal values of the Global Compact and to
contribute to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals.
We are committed to leading “Renaulution“, the company’s strategic transformation plan, in line with our Purpose, “we make
the heart of innovation beat so that mobility brings us closer together“, and with our sustainable development strategy. With
the third phase of this strategic plan, Revolution, we roll out concrete ambitions for each of the Group’s entities, covering
climate, resource conservation, renewable energies and just transition, each representing a key lever for sustainable
performance.“
Luca de Meo, CEO, Renault Group
Our contribution to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG)
Through its geographical footprint, the diversity of its businesses and its commitment to ESG, Renault Group contributes – to a
greater or lesser extent – to the 17  Sustainable Development Goals identified by the United Nations. This contribution is
highlighted in the table below based on the targets the company has set and examples of actions. It should be noted that the
Group contributes directly to SDGs 3, 5, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, and 16.
SDG
Examples of initiatives implemented
ODD1
End poverty in all its forms
everywhere in the world
(targets: 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.b)
●The Renault Group Foundation supports 30 organisations dedicated to integration through employment for the most
disadvantaged.
●Actions of the Group’s foundations. For example:
–
The Renault Institute in Brazil supports the Borda Viva association which trains women in sewing using recycled
products from vehicles;
–
Vocational training programs for 80 young people from disadvantaged neighbourhoods in Morocco with the
Second Chance association;
–
Vocational training programs for more than 90 people set up by Renault Argentina in collaboration with the
government of Córdoba, private organisations (ML Comunicación, Aramark and ISCOT), NGOs (Las Omas, Pallets
Guru, Fundación Pircas and Fundación Holcim) and other partners (El Torgo, Hamilto, etc.);
–
Donation of bicycles by the Foundation in Colombia to enable the most disadvantaged women to work or train.
●Support for economically vulnerable people as part of the CareMakers program (CareMakers Invest investment
company, CareMakers Mobility the solidarity mobility program in purchase or rental with option to purchase.).
SDG2
End hunger, achieve food security
and improved nutrition and promote
sustainable agriculture (target 2.1)
●Deployment since 2022 of a Renault Group policy for biobased materials, refusing generation 1 biomass to avoid
contributing to food insecurity.
SDG3
Ensure healthy lives and promote
well-being for all at all ages
(targets: 3.5, 3.6, 3.8, 3.9)
●Fireman Access on all of the Group’s electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids.
●Systematic factory installation of QRescue for Renault and Dacia models.
●The Renault Group Foundation supports mobility for the visually impaired.
●More than 5,000 firefighters trained in 21 countries since 2014 (Human First program).
●Target of reducing VOC emissions to 28.5 g/m2 by 2025, based on the painted surface of vehicles, to preserve air
quality.
●Target of 50% reduction between 2021 and 2030 in the number of hazardous chemicals used on Group sites achieved
in 2024 (55.5% reduction at the end of 2024).
SDG4
Quality education: ensure inclusive
and equitable quality education and
promote lifelong learning
opportunities for all
(targets: 4.3, 4.4, 4.5)
●Continued support from the Renault Group Foundation to the Fondation Georges Besse and vocational schools.
●Actions by the Group’s Foundations in many countries (for example, mobile libraries and school reconstruction in
Morocco following the earthquake at the end of 2023, supply of automotive equipment for young people’s
apprenticeships, etc.).
●Internal training and e-learning. Deployment of ESG training for all employees.
●People@RenaultGroup: performance assessment policy.
●Apprenticeships, internships, training.
●4th edition of the Alpine Mechanical Excellence competition aimed at students registered in the Automotive
Maintenance and Automotive After-Sale sectors in France at the BAC and BAC+1 levels.
●ReKnow University supports the upskilling and reskilling of the Group’s employees to prepare for the mobility of the
future: more than 38,500 Group employees trained by ReKnow University since 2021 to ensure the just transition.

SDG
Examples of initiatives implemented
SDG5
Achieve gender equality and
empower all women and girls
(targets: 5.1, 5.5, 5.a, 5.c)
●Supporting women in precarious situations in northeastern Colombia to gain access to employment and training.
●Signatory to the WEP (Women Empowerment Principles) of UN Women.
●Signatory in France of the #StOpE Charter to combat ordinary sexism in the workplace.
●Renault Group Diversity & Inclusion Charter, which encourages its own employees, but also its partners, to embrace
diversity, promote fairness and equal opportunity, defend non-discrimination and guarantee an inclusive environment.
●Zero Discrimination corporate policy aimed at ensuring a respectful environment for all and eliminating all forms of
discrimination in day-to-day activities and at all stages of professional life, including recruitment, training,
remuneration and career development.
●Programs to raise awareness on sexism and gender discrimination.
●Global action plan with targets to increase the proportion of women, particularly in management positions.
●Women@RenaultGroup corporate affinity network: a dozen online events over the year featuring external guests,
personal development webinars and the discovery of portraits of female employees.
●Alpine Rac(H)er, an equal opportunities program in the industry and motor sport. 
SDG6
Ensure access to water and
sanitation for all and sustainable
management of water resources
(targets: 6.3, 6.4, 6.6)
●Committed to act4nature.
●Reduce discharges of heavy metals (nickel and zinc) in plant wastewater to 0.60 g/vehicle by 2025 to preserve the
quality of water resources.
●Reduce the Group’s external water supply per vehicle produced to 3.2 m3/vehicle by 2025.
SDG7
Ensure access to affordable, reliable,
sustainable and modern energy
(targets: 7.1, 7.2, 7.a)
●Reach by 2035 80% of renewable energy in electricity consumed et 40% in heat consumed.
SDG8
Promote inclusive and sustainable
economic growth, employment and
decent work for all (targets: 8.2, 8.3,
8.4, 8.5, 8.7, 8.8)
●305 solidarity garages in Metropolitan France, in total, nearly 3,400 people were able to benefit from a vehicle under
the solidarity mobility program in purchase or rental with option to purchase CareMakers Mobility.
●Listing of inclusive offers on “mesaidesverslemploi.fr”: the new platform of the French Ministry of Labour,
Employment and Economic Inclusion to identify mobility aids.
●Responsible purchasing policy.
●Responsible buying guide for buyers.
●“Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) for suppliers”.
●Global framework agreement on social, societal, and environmental responsibility (2013).
●Renault Group policy on the sourcing of cobalt and minerals from conflict or high-risk areas.
●Moratorium on deep-sea mining.
●Letter sent to suppliers informing them of their access to the whistleblower system.
SDG9
Build resilient infrastructure,
promote sustainable
industrialisation and foster
innovation (targets: 9.1, 9.2, 9.4)
●ISO 14001 certification of 100% of Renault Group manufacturing sites.
●The Group has embarked on a global roadmap for the ISO50001 certification of its industrial sites, starting with its
French sites. By the end of 2024, 50% of our sites have been certified: Sandouville, Le Mans, Flins, Ruitz, Douai,
Maubeuge, Cléon, Pitesti, Bursa and Revoz. 70% of sites will be certified by the end of 2025, landing at 100% by the end
of Q1 2026.
●The renew factory reconditions 100 vehicles per day and aims to do more, thanks to the implementation of a
standardized process.
●The Future Is NEUTRAL, a subsidiary created in 2022, is the first company to operate across the entire value chain of
the automotive circular economy, with the aim of committing the entire automotive industry to moving towards
resource neutrality. The Future Is NEUTRAL is adding the “Remanufacturing” activities of the Flins Refactory to its
portfolio of operating subsidiaries, by launching The Remakers subsidiary, with the ambition of positioning itself as
the market leader in parts reconditioning in Europe.
●Renault, the first carmaker to market a retrofit kit for commercial vehicles in partnership with start-up Tolv.
Assembled at the Refactory in Flins, these kits enable Renault Master III diesel vehicles to be converted to all-electric
power, with a range of around 200km.
●Launch of Re-Industry, an ambitious plan for the in-depth transformation of Renault Group’s industrial system (with a
target of reducing its industrial costs per vehicle by 2027 by 30% for combustion vehicles and by 50% for electric
vehicles) by relying especially on its Industrial Metaverse.
SDG10
Reduce inequality within
and among countries
(targets: 10.2, 10.3, 10.4)
●Renault Group Diversity & Inclusion Charter, which encourages its own employees, but also its partners, to embrace
diversity, promote fairness and equal opportunity, defend non-discrimination and guarantee an inclusive environment.
●Zero Discrimination corporate policy, which aims to ensure a respectful environment for all and eliminate all forms of
discrimination in day-to-day activities and at all stages of professional life, including recruitment, training,
remuneration and career development.
●“Inclusion Handicap“ corporate policy, which aims to develop an inclusive workplace and culture, including for
people with disabilities, to encourage more employees to disclose their disability status, and to position itself as an
employer of choice for everyone, including candidates with disabilities.
●Signing of the United Nations Free and Equal standards to combat discrimination against LGBT+ people.
●Signing of the Charter of Commitment of “L’Autre Cercle” in France.
●Signing of the ILO Disability Charter in France.
●Signing of the “Charte Manifeste Inclusion” in France.
●Signing of the “Charte des 50 ans +” in France.

Make cities and human settlements
inclusive, safe, resilient and

●Car sharing: Mobilize provides 10,000 car-sharing vehicles in Europe.
●Caremakers programme.
SDG12
Ensure sustainable consumption
and production patterns
(targets: 12.4, 12.5, 12.6))
●Over 85% recovery of industrial waste, excluding metal waste, which is recycled at nearly 100%.
●Refactory in Flins and Seville, European circular economy factories dedicated to mobility.
●The Future Is NEUTRAL, a subsidiary created in 2022, is the first company to operate across the entire value chain of
the automotive circular economy, and to commit the automotive industry to moving towards resource neutrality.
●Renault Group and The Future Is NEUTRAL launch a new range of reconditioned automotive parts dedicated to the

●Mobilize Duo and Bento, 100% electric micro-urban cars accessible with and without a license, incorporate a record
40% of materials from the circular economy.
●Renault 5, Renault Scenic E-Tech and Dacia Duster feature levels of recycled plastic unrivalled on the market (19.4%,
17% and 20% respectively).
●A founding member of the Ellen MacArthur Foundation since 2010, Renault Group also joined the Circul’R Club in 2024,
which aims to promote the circular economy and stimulate collaboration in this field in France.

Take urgent action to combat
climate change and its impacts
(targets: 13.1, 13.3)
●The Group’s objectives are to achieve Net Zero carbon emissions by 2040 in Europe and 2050 worldwide, thereby
contributing to carbon neutrality. The decarbonization strategy covers the entire product life cycle.
●Renault Group is engaged in lowering by minimum 62.5% its carbon footprint Scope 1 and 2 absolute compared
to 2019.
●Renault Group is engaged in lowering by minimum 27.5% its carbon footprint Scope 3 absolute compared to 2019.
●Reach 33% of materials from the circular economy (by mass) in new vehicles produced by the Group worldwide
in 2030.

Conserve and sustainably use the
oceans, seas and marine resources
for sustainable development

●Support the moratorium on deep-sea mining.

Protect, restore and promote

ecosystems, sustainably manage
forests, combat desertification and
halt and reverse land degradation
and halt biodiversity loss
(targets: 15.1, 15.2)
●Committed to act4nature. 
●Carrying out biodiversity diagnostic surveys at our industrial plants.
●Production of biodiversity good practice sheets for the Group’s sites (e.g. lighting and green spaces). 
●Engaged in the Global Platform for Sustainable Natural Rubber (GPSNR).
●Sustainable Natural Rubber Policy, a set of commitments aligned with the objectives of the GPSNR.
●Biobased materials policy.

Promote peaceful and inclusive
societies for sustainable
development, provide access to
