institutions at all levels
(targets: 16.2, 16.5, 16.7, 16.b)
●Implementation of the 8 pillars of the French Sapin II act.
●Anticorruption Code of Practice updated and rolled out at the end of 2021 to all Renault Group employees worldwide.

2.1.4 Environmental Policy of the Group
Governance
The environmental policy is managed through the ESG
governance body.
Renault Group has had an environmental policy since the
late 1990s.
The 2024 update of the Renault Group environmental policy
have been validated by the Group’s VP of Sustainability.

## Page 94

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.1.5 The Renault Group Foundation and the Art
and Culture Fund
In 2024, the Renault Group Foundation and the Fund for Art
and Culture became part of the activity of the new Art,
Heritage & Patronage Department. This Department, whose
mission is to define and manage all social, heritage and
artistic sponsorship actions for the Group, is aligned with the
company’s “Raison d’Être” and ESG strategy of the Group. 
The new roadmap of the Renault Group
Foundation
The Foundation has set up its Manifesto of the new roadmap
on Inclusive Mobility (2024-2028 cycle), “For mobility to bring
us closer together“, to bring people closer to what is
essential to their lives and projects. This Manifesto is
designed to share with Renault Group Foundation’s
audiences the emotional aspect of this new strategy.
The roadmap is organized around the pillars of Security,
Support and Rescue.
Safety Pillar
Security is a highlight of the new roadmap. We have four
major projects in 2024:
●the start of the project with the Antoine Alléno association,
the “ImpAct“ project, concerning the awareness and
protection of young people from road violence;
●the project relating to the safety of children in cars led by
the LAB collective (Laboratory of Accidentology), CEESAR
(European Centre for Studies on Road Accidentology), the
association Prévention Routière;
●the first field deployments of the Mobilisafe project, led by
the Prévention Routière association with measuring rods
distributed to children and their families in schools to
raise awareness of the choice of the right car seat and the
right way to buckle up in the car;
●a project directly supported by the Renault Group
Foundation, the Ecoles by Art, which, thanks to road
markings created by artists near schools, encourages
drivers to reduce their speed.
Support Pillar
This pillar remains predominant in value and number of
projects supported among Renault Group Foundation’s
activities with more than 20 projects supported.
Of note, 2 major supports in 2024:
●the launch of the Mobil’Derm program with the
Endowment
Fund
of
the
Société
française
de
Dermatologie (French Society of Dermatology), dedicated
to mobile consultations in medical deserts in France.
The Foundation helps with financing the vehicle for first-
level dermatological screenings;
●the deployment in Colombia of a program with the NGO
World Bicycle Relief to allow disadvantaged Colombian
women residing in the desert northeast of the country to
benefit from a bicycle, to help them create their business
or facilitate their access to training.
Rescue Pillar
The activity of this pillar is particularly linked to the
geo-climatic context.
In 2024, the Renault Group Foundation has supported the
French National Fire Brigade Federation (Fédération
Nationale des Sapeurs-Pompiers or FNSPF) to enable them
to set up an emergency intervention fleet to help victims of
natural disasters (floods and fires). Thanks to the support of
the Foundation, the FNSPF was able to acquire 18 Trafic cars,
distributed among the least equipped Departmental Fire
and Rescue Services (Services Départementaux d’Incendie
et de Secours or SDIS) in France. This fleet was deployed at
the time of the floods that hit France in October 2024.
The Renault Group Foundation also provided support to the
Renault Spain Foundation to help victims of severe rains in
the south-east and south of Spain at the end of
October 2024.
The Fund for Art and Culture
Created in July 2024, the Fund for Art and Culture
perpetuates, enriches and propagates the company’s art
collection.
Its ambition is to become a key player in the fields of art,
culture and heritage.
For 2025 and 2026, the objectives will be as follows:
●develop a unique ecosystem of Street Art stakeholders
playing a driving and unifying role;
●develop a collection of urban art that could be considered
as a reference in the field, and present it to the general
public;
●all with the aim of sparking emotions in contemporaries
and value for future generations.
In 2024, activities concerning artistic patronage made it
possible to acquire new works of urban art, with the entry of
artists into the art collection such as Dan Rawlings, JonOne,
Invader, Zlotykamien, Cleth Abraham, César Malfi or Jérôme
Mesnager.
92

who have recently joined the Renault collection.
At the end of 2024, the Fund for art and culture has been
endowed with all the works from the Renault collection.

## Page 96

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2 Sustainability statement
2.2.1 General information
2.2.1.1 Governance and Strategy
2.2.1.1.1 SBM-1 – Strategy, business model
and value chain
Renault Group activities encompass an extensive range of
products and services related to road transport at various
stages of the automotive value chain, including:
●The production of passenger and light commercial
vehicles, of spare parts and accessories, as well as the
distribution, reconditioning and recycling of these
vehicles, parts and accessories;
●Financing solutions for these vehicles intended for end-
customers and dealers, as well as other services such as
car insurance;
●Mobility solutions, such as car-sharing and charging
stations. [DP SBM-1_01]
Renault Group relies on a workforce of 98,636 employees, with
the geographical distribution presented in chapter 2.2.2.3.1.
[SBM-1_03l; SBM-1_04]
Throughout 2024, the Group continued to develop its vehicle
line-up with the following launches and discontinuations:
●The launch of four electric vehicles:
– For the Renault brand: Renault 5 E-Tech, Scenic E-Tech;
– For the Dacia brand: New Spring;
– For the Alpine brand: A290.
●The launch of for four hybrid vehicles:
– For the Renault brand: Rafale E-Tech, Symbioz E-Tech
and Grand Koleos;
– For the Dacia Brand: New Duster HEV.
●The launch of Master, a light commercial vehicle, both
internal combustion and electric;
●The discontinuation of the production of ZOE, Renault’s first
electric car, launched 12 years ago and now at the end of
its life cycle, and of Spring 1, replaced by New Spring.
Renault Group distributes its products in more than
110  countries in the world. In 2024, the Group generated
revenues of €56,232 million1, almost 80% of which was
generated in Europe. The Group is also present in Latin
America (Brazil, Argentina and Mexico), in Africa & Middle
East (notably in Morocco), and in Asia Pacific (mainly in India
and South Korea). This geographical coverage did not
experience any significant changes in 2024.
Renault Group commercializes its products and services to
three main customer categories:
●Individual customers;
●Distribution networks;
●Companies operating vehicle fleets: corporate fleets,
fleet management companies, rental and leasing
agencies, etc.
These customer and market typologies did not experience any
significant changes in 2024. [DP SBM-1_02]
The following information is available in other sections of
the URD:
●For Renault Group’s business model (See Introduction of
the URD);
●For products and services (See section 1.3 in URD) and for
markets (See section 1.3.1 in URD).[DP BP-2_20]
1 Renault Group does not generate any revenue whatsoever from chemical products production or controversial weapons. [DP SBM-1_16; DP SBM-1_18]

Sustainable development
02
Renault Group’s activities rely on two interconnected value
chains:
●Automotive:
– Upstream, the supply chain consists primarily of
suppliers of steel products, automotive parts and
equipment; these suppliers are in turn supplied by other
component manufacturers, and so on down to the
producers and distributors of raw or recycled materials.
In 2024, this chain evolved significantly due to the sale
of HORSE, the activity responsible for the design and
manufacture of thermal engines;
– Downstream,
there
are
various
distribution
and
maintenance networks (Renault Retail Group entities,
independent networks, importers, distribution partners,
etc.) and industrial players in recycling, reconditioning,
and end-of-life management of vehicles and batteries.
●Sales financing and mobility services:
– In addition to automotive manufacturers, with Renault
Group at the forefront, the supply chain forms a
diversified
network,
including
designers
and
distributors of IT services (including cloud), financial
and banking management software, savings account
marketplaces, marketing services, energy producers
and distributors, and charging station manufacturers;
– Downstream, financing and mobility services activities
interact directly with the downstream value chain,
which corresponds to end-customers and dealers. [DP
SBM-1_25; DP SBM-1_28]
An automobile is essentially composed of metals, polymers,
and glass, and Renault Group’s operations mainly depend
on the availability of these materials, which increasingly
come from the recycling industry for steel, polymers and
aluminum. These supplies are secured through long-term
contracts, as reflected in off-balance sheet liabilities. To
limit its dependency on raw materials, the Group has
increased its involvement in remanufacturing, retrofitting,
and recycling activities. [DP SBM-1_26]
Renault Group is a major player in the economic and social
development of the territories in which it operates and
considers that it has a responsibility to ensure its impact is
positive and that it contributes to their sustainable
development. The Group’s ambition is to create shared
value for the company and all its stakeholders and takes
care to identify their needs and expectations  (See chapter
2.2.1.1.3) and to respond to them. [DP SBM-1_27]
Group’s sustainability-related targets that are particularly
relevant to its products and/or customers are as follows:

2.2.1.1.2 Management and control bodies
2.2.1.1.2.1 GOV-1 – Role of the administrative,
management and supervisory bodies
The Board of Directors of Renault S.A. and its specialized
Committees represent the administrative, management
and supervisory bodies of the Renault Group.
Composition of the governance bodies
Detailed information on the composition of the Board of
Directors and its Committees is provided in the Universal
Registration Document (URD) section 3.1.
For each director, section 3.1 includes the following information:
●identity details;
●position in Renault S.A.’s Board of Directors and
membership in Board Committees
●current and past mandates and functions, including
outside Renault Group;
●expertise and experiences, particularly those relevant to
Renault Group’s activities and geographic locations. [DP
BP-2_20; DP GOV-1_08; DP GOV-1_04]
Diversity of the governance bodies
Pursuant to Article L. 22-10-10 of the French Commercial
Code, the Board of Directors has defined a diversity policy
based on its past practices. The composition of the Board of
Directors seeks a balance notably in terms of diversity
(representation of women and men, nationalities, age,
qualifications and professional experience). For more
details about the procedure for appointing Directors and the
diversity policy, please consult the URD section 3.1.2.

Roles and responsibilities of governance bodies
in relation to sustainability
A. General
In accordance with the Board Charter, the Board of Directors,
acting on a proposal from the Chief Executive Officer,
determines the strategic direction of the company’s business
and oversees its implementation, in accordance with its
corporate interests, considering the social and environmental
challenges of its business and the Group’s Purpose.
As part of its missions listed in the Board Charter, the Board of
Directors shall:
●Examine on a regular basis any opportunities and risks
associated with the strategy that it has defined;
●Ensure the integrity of the sustainability information
included in the Group’s management report and its
publication by the company in accordance with the
European Union’s reporting framework. [DP GOV-1_09]
In relation to sustainability matters, the Board of Directors
relies on the work of two specialized Committees according to
the following breakdown: [DP GOV-1_11; DP GOV-1_12]
●The Audit and Risks Committee which:
– Monitors the sustainability reporting process;
– Monitors the process used to determine the information
to be published in accordance with sustainability
reporting standards;
– Where appropriate, makes recommendations to ensure
the integrity of these processes; and
– Monitors the selection, independence, fee breakdown,
and performance of sustainability auditors.
●The Strategy and Sustainability Committee which:
– Reviews the strategy and actions to be implemented in
terms of environmental, social and societal responsibility
of the Company, as well as for sustainability;
– Validates sustainability reporting objectives and the
nature of the associated indicators; [DP GOV-1_14]
– Examines the extra-financial communication policy
and ensures a review of extra-financial ratings. [DP
GOV-1_13]
The Board of Directors is also responsible for championing
ethical, social, and environmental responsibility within the
company’s value creation processes. Furthermore, it oversees
the effectiveness of systems designed to prevent and detect
unethical practices such as bribery and influence peddling,
thereby safeguarding the company’s integrity and reputation.
[DP GOV-1_10]
96

B. Ethics and Compliance
The Ethics & Compliance SVP is central to the Group’s ethical
governance, advising leadership, managing whistleblowing
protocols, and chairing the Group’s Ethics & Compliance
Committee to ensure worldwide compliance. Tasked with
updating policies to meet new regulations, the Ethics &
Compliance SVP also collaborates with local Ethics &
Compliance representatives in each country to instill values,
oversee alert systems, and guide the Countries’ Ethics &
Compliance Committees. Together with the Group Ethics &
Compliance Committee, the Ethics & Compliance SVP
assesses and refines compliance procedures, while the
Countries’ Ethics & Compliance Committees ensure effective
policy execution and incident management.  [DP G1.GOV-1_01]
C. Political influence and interests representation
activities
The Public Affairs Department of Renault Group is
accountable for the implementation of the Code of Conduct
for Interests Representations Activities. The Audit and Risks
Committee of the Board of Directors monitors the
implementation of this Code as part of its overall oversight
of the ethical framework deployed within the Group by the
Ethics & Compliance Department. In addition, the Strategy
and
Sustainability
Committee
regularly
reviews
an
environmental, social and governance (ESG) dashboard
containing numerous performance indicators, including
those
regarding
transparency.
This
Committee
also
monitors certain interests’ representation activities as part
of its review of the strategy and actions to be implemented
in terms of environmental, social and societal responsibility
of the Group. [DP G1.GOV-1_01]
D. Management of relationships with suppliers
The Chief Executive Officer (CEO) establishes strategic
directions and policies for procurement and supply chain
management, ensuring ethical and sustainable practices
and maintaining collaborative relationships with suppliers.
He informs the Board of Directors. He oversees the rules
applicable
to
suppliers,
monitors
performance
and
manages supply chain risks. The new Renault Group
Responsible
Purchasing
Policy
for
Suppliers
notably
addresses the sourcing process, business ethics and
sustainability. [DP G1.GOV-1_01]
Skills and expertise of the governance bodies
The candidates for a directorship are chosen based on
specific criteria, especially their professional experience
and skills, in alignment with the Group’s strategic and
geographic scope. [DP GOV-1_15]
A skills mapping of the members of the Board of Directors is
disclosed in the URD section 3.1.2. Among the eight expertise
areas listed in this mapping, three are dedicated to
sustainability and covers Board members’ knowledge and
experiences and in managing environmental, social or
governance issues. [DP G1.GOV-1_02; DP GOV-1_16]
The skills matrix describes the criteria to be met to validate
each skill. It also identifies the skills of each Director, taking
into account their main areas of expertise and experience.
Thus, seven Directors are identified in the mapping as
having a specific skill in one of the three ESG topics.
In accordance with legal and regulatory requirements, the
mandates and positions held by the Directors, together with
their biographies, are described each year in the Corporate
Governance Report. Any positions held in the public
administration over the last two years, where applicable, are
included in the Directors’ biographies. [DP G1-5_11]
In addition to the individual expertise of the Directors, the
very functions of the Board and its Committees promote the
ongoing development of its collective expertise as a
governance body, particularly in relation to sustainable
development
issues,
which
are
constantly
evolving.
Discussions between Directors and both internal and
external experts on ESG topics enable the Board to develop
a deep understanding of these issues and their challenges
for Renault Group.
The Directors have been able to benefit from internal
expertise in the field of sustainability through the recurring
presentations from the following departments during Board
and Committee meetings: [DP GOV-1_16; DP GOV-1_17;
DP G1.GOV-1_02]

The Board also benefits from external expertise in
sustainability through ad hoc presentations or training
seminars. For example:
●In 2023, the Board of Directors organized an ESG seminar
dedicated to climate change. As part of this seminar, the
Board visited the Paris Mineralogy Museum at the “Ecole
des Mines” and attended a presentation on the various
climate
issues
by
a
member
of
the
IPCC
(Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change). The
Directors also took part in a “Climate Fresco” workshop, to
explore the impacts of climate change and the concrete
actions that can be taken to remedy the situation;
●In 2024, the Board dedicated the ESG seminar to the topic
of biodiversity. This seminar was organized jointly with
the Group’s Leadership Team. During this seminar, a
biology professor presented the key issues of biodiversity
and the main takeaways from the COP15 Biodiversity
conference. A consultant specializing in biodiversity
measurement methods detailed the biodiversity risks
assessed for Renault Group and the associated action
plan. In addition, potential solutions for producing without
destroying nature were presented by the Environment VP
of a major mining group and an entrepreneur and a
business leader committed to ecology. [DP GOV-1_16]
2.2.1.1.2.2 GOV-2 – Information provided to
and sustainability matters addressed
by Renault Group’s administrative,
management and supervisory bodies
The Board of Directors, including its relevant Committees, is
informed about the implementation of due diligence, and
the effectiveness of policies, actions, metrics, and targets
adopted to address material IROs.
In each of its meetings in 2024, the Audit and Risks
committee consistently followed progress points on the
development of the sustainability report process, focusing
particularly on the double materiality matrix and the
definition of IROs, with the Departments concerned. The
sustainability auditors attended meetings of the Audit and
Risks Committee to give their opinion on the Renault Group
sustainability report.
Moreover, the Audit and Risks Committee has also
monitored the Finance Department’s adaptation of its
organization to the requirements for handling non-financial
data that will be published in the sustainability report.
The Leadership Team consistently considers strategic and
critical IROs when overseeing the Group’s strategy, major
transactions, and risk management processes, as these
IROs are fully integrated into the Group’s objectives and
strategy. [DP GOV-2_01; DP GOV-2_02]

2.2.1.1.2.3 GOV-3 – Integration of sustainability-
related performance in incentive schemes
Renault Group recognizes the importance of aligning
sustainability goals with executive remuneration. The CEO
benefits
from
incentive
schemes
in
his
variable
remuneration that are linked to sustainability matters.
[DP GOV-3_01; DP GOV-3_03; DP GOV-3_04]
The amount of annual variable compensation may reach
225% of the fixed compensation paid if all the performance
criteria
are
fully
achieved.
This
annual
variable
remuneration of the CEO includes sustainability targets, for a
maximum of 75% of the fixed compensation if the following
targets are reached:
●Health and safety: target of 1.4 in 2024 for the frequency
rate of work-related accidents with days off work;
●Development of ReKnow University: target of over
5,000 people trained in 2024;
●Circular economy business: implementation of a strategic
partnership for The Future Is NEUTRAL;
●Number
of
incidents:
achievement
of
the
target
expressed as the number of cases per thousand (K°/°°).
This indicator, called the “GMF 3MIS WORLD”, measures
the number of incidents involving vehicles after three
months on the road;
●Customer satisfaction level, measured by the “Dealer E-
reputation” or digital reputation of the dealers.
The performance share plan remunerates the CEO up to
25% of the value of the 120,000 shares that were allocated to
him, based on the achievement of a climate target. The
target is a combination of equal parts of scopes 1 and 2
(industrial activities) on one hand and downstream scope 3
(emissions from vehicle use) on the other hand, assessed
over cumulative periods of 3 years. [DP E1.GOV-3_01;
DP E1.GOV-3_02; DP E1.GOV-3_03]
The Renaulution plan consists of a single allotment of
Renault shares in 2024, subject to a presence condition and
performance conditions assessed over a cumulative period
of four years, both climate and sustainability criteria for a
maximum of 60%:
●Transform Renault Group:
– Deliver sustainable performance while promoting the
Group’s Purpose and culture and elevating the common
vision of future mobility: low-carbon, responsible and
safe, driven by environmental and social innovation;
– Ensure the successful execution of this strategic
transformation and secure its future through top
management succession plans that embed diversity.
●Pioneer circular economy and energy transition for
sustainable growth:
– The Future is NEUTRAL: develop a unique platform to
provide circular economy solutions throughout the
vehicle life cycle for the entire automotive industry,
from individuals to Original Equipment Manufacturers
(OEMs), suppliers, dismantlers, and insurers;
– Mobilize: develop, fund and operate energy-transition
assets, technologies and solutions for the benefit of
Renault Group and its entities.
●Master technology development and lead by innovation:
– Ampere: develop the next EV line-up to guarantee
technical and innovation excellence while mastering a
profitable path;
– Flexis: leverage Renault Group’s historical leadership
position in EVs and LCVs to address the growing last-
mile delivery market.
More details about the CEO’s remuneration are available in
section 3.2.2.2 of the URD. [DP GOV-3_02]
The Governance and Compensation Committee and the
Board of Directors are responsible for approving and
updating the terms of these incentive schemes, ensuring
that they reflect the Group’s commitment to sustainability.
[DP GOV-3_06]

2.2.1.1.2.4 GOV-4 – Statement on due diligence
Due diligence is defined as the process by which
undertakings identify, prevent, mitigate and account for
how they address the actual and potential negative impacts
on the environment and people connected with their
business. The following table lists the main steps of due
diligence and explains how these steps are reflected in the
sustainability statement of Renault Group:
Core elements of due diligence
Paragraphs in the sustainability statement of the 2024 URD
Embedding due diligence in governance,
strategy and business model(1)
●See ESRS 2 – General information, GOV-2 – Information provided to and sustainability
matters addressed by Renault Group’s administrative, management and supervisory
bodies;
●See ESRS 2 – General information, GOV-3 – Integration of sustainability-related
performance in incentive schemes;
●See ESRS 2 – General information, SBM-3 – Material impacts, risks and opportunities and
their interaction with strategy and business model.
Engaging with affected stakeholders
in all key steps of the due diligence

2.2.1.1.2.5 GOV-5 – Risk management and internal
controls over sustainability reporting
As part of the CSRD, Renault Group has introduced a new
risk
management
and
internal
control
system
for
sustainability information.
The analyses of the CSRD project team and exchanges with
third parties (companies of comparable size or business model,
consultants, auditors) led to the identification of the following
main risks:
●Missing mandatory data points;
●Errors in the measurement scope or in calculation of
quantitative data;
●Difficulty in obtaining some data, particularly quantitative
data, within the report construction schedule;
●Non-compliance of qualitative data with ESRS requirements;
●Unverifiable nature of the information provided.
To prevent these risks, the following methods have been
adopted:
●Identifying mandatory data points by the project team,
assigning each to one or more key contributors on the
topic for the Group, and monitoring the collection of
information, point by point;
●Definition of “protocol sheets“ describing the scope and
process of collection as well as the methods for
calculating or estimating quantitative information;
●Mid-year collection of all available data (“dry-run report“)
to identify and address the collection difficulties at the
end of the year;
●Assisting
contributors
in
the
interpretation
of
requirements
and
point-by-point
monitoring
of
compliance of qualitative information;
●Point-by-point verification of the existence of audit proofs.
The project team monitored the remediation of risk points on
a weekly basis. [DP GOV-5_01; DP GOV-5_02; DP GOV-5_03]
For qualitative information, the risk assessment is similarly
rigorous. Owners are responsible for the quality of narratives
and the traceability of published information. [DP GOV-
5_02l; DP GOV-5_03]
The integration of risk assessment findings and internal
controls into the Group’s sustainability reporting process is
a dynamic and responsive mechanism. Each owner is
required to continuously review and, if necessary, enhance
the controls previously determined based on the findings.
This iterative process ensures that the internal control
framework remains effective and responsive to the evolving
nature of sustainability risks and reporting requirements.
[DP GOV-5_04]
The Board of Directors is informed of findings through the
review performed by its Audit and Risks Committee at least
once a year. [DP GOV-5_05]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
101

Renault Group

2.2.1.1.3 SBM-2 – Interests and views
of stakeholders
Stakeholder engagement is a cornerstone of Renault
Group’s
sustainability
strategy,
enabling
a
better
understanding of environmental, social, and economic
challenges and risks. Renault Group has established
channels of exchange with stakeholders at the global,
regional, and local levels, including clients and end-users,
own workforce, shareholders, suppliers and business
partners and their workers, investors, affected communities,
associations, and students. Over the past three years, the
Chairman of the Board of Directors has chaired the Purpose
Committee, made of the Leadership Team representatives
and contributors of business, social and environmental
topics representing most of Renault Group’s stakeholders.
[DP SBM-2_01; DP SBM-2_12]
The purpose of Renault Group stakeholder engagement is to
inform its strategy with the result of these consultations, such as
the needs to transition towards electric vehicle models, to
improve customer and stakeholder satisfaction, to promote
transparency, and to contribute to sustainable development in
the regions where the Group operates. [DP SBM-2_06]

2.2.1.1.4 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its metrics based on robust methods. They are subject to internal controls and verifications, without being
systematically validated by an external body.
GOV-1 – Role of the administrative, management and supervisory bodies
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Board members by gender
Percentage of Board members by gender.
Board’s gender diversity ratio
Gender diversity on the Board of Directors is calculated as the number of female
members of the Board of Directors in relation to the total number of members.
Number of executive members
A member of the Board of Directors who is a member of the Renault Group
Leadership Team.
Number of non-executive members
A member of the Board of Directors who is not a member of the Renault Group
Leadership Team.
Percentage of independent Board members
An annual review of the independence of directors is carried out by the Board of
Directors on the basis of a self-declaration questionnaire.
GOV-3 – Integration of sustainability-related performance in incentive schemes
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Percentage of variable remuneration of the Board
of Directors’ members dependent on
sustainability-related targets and (or) impacts
The variable remuneration policy for the members of the Board of Directors differs from
that of the CEO. The CEO’s variable compensation is subject to sustainability objectives
that can be linked to sustainability and climate considerations. For the members of the
Board of Directors, their variable remuneration is linked to their attendance rate at the
meetings of the Board of Directors and its Committees. For further information regarding
the Board of Directors’ compensation, please refer to URD Section 3.2.
Percentage of variable remuneration of the Board
of Directors’ members that is linked to climate
related considerations
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.1.2 Management of Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities
2.2.1.2.1 IRO-1 – Description of the process
to identify and assess material
Impacts, Risks and Opportunities
2.2.1.2.1.1 General process
To identify, assess, prioritize, and monitor material impacts,
risks, and opportunities (IROs) as part of the double-
materiality analysis, Renault Group has developed the
following methodology. [DP IRO-1_01]
The first step consisted in a comprehensive screening of the
37 sub-topics defined in the CSRD ESRS 1 General
Requirements. The identification of IROs for each of the 37
sub-topics
has
been
informed
by
existing
impact
assessments and other sources such as corporate social
responsibility frameworks, scientific studies, databases, and
stakeholder expectations, including based on media
exposure and questionnaires. For additional details about
sources used to identify IROs related to each topical ESRS,
please refer to each ESRS chapter. This review has
facilitated the identification of topics that are not relevant
to Renault Group, leading to their exclusion from further
analysis. Additional topics, specific to the automotive
sector, have been also identified as material and developed
in accordance with ESRS 2 requirements. [DP IRO-1_01; DP
IRO-1_02; DP IRO-1_14]
To evaluate the impact and financial materiality of IROs,
Renault Group CSRD project team used rating scales ranging
from 1 to 4. These scales were used to assess the severity of
impacts, risks, and opportunities as well as the likelihood of
occurrence, if applicable, based on ESRS 1 mandatory
requirements:
●The scale used for severity of impacts includes criteria on
scale, scope, and irremediability (except for positive
impacts). Each level of severity was defined with specific
descriptions for both environmental and human impacts;
●The scale used for severity of risks and opportunities was
categorized into four types: financial (impact on financial
statements), legal (severity of potential convictions),
reputational (stakeholders’ perception), and operational
(length of disturbances), incorporating both qualitative
and quantitative factors;
●The likelihood scale created for the materiality analysis
was consistent for both impact and financial materiality
ranging from 1 to 4. This scale was further detailed by the
quantitative measures and the timed frequency of IROs
that were subject to assessment. [DP IRO-1_01; DP IRO-
1_06; DP IRO-1_07; DP IRO-1_09l]
The materiality score was computed by taking the square root
of the product of severity and likelihood (if applicable), with a
materiality threshold set at a score of 2 out of 4.
For IROs related to human rights, the materiality score is the
square root of the product of severity with the maximum
value between severity and likelihood scores, to make
severity prevail over likelihood. [DP IRO-1_01; DP IRO-1_09l]
106
Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
www.renaultgroup.com

## Page 109

Information about
the company,
the capital and the
 share ownership
General
presentation of
Renault Group
Sustainable
development
Corporate
governance
Risk and
control
Financial
statements
Annual general
meeting
Additional
information
Sustainable development
02
The assessment was performed following a two-step approach:
●If existing risks and impact analyses, as well as due
diligence processes (for instance, from the French “Duty
of Vigilance” law, the corporate risk analysis) have
already identified a subject as material, the maximum
score has been assigned to the corresponding IROs for
that subject; [DP IRO-1_01]
●The IROs corresponding to each sub-topic were then
identified and their materiality assessed whenever
necessary by the experts, according to the above scoring
scales. [DP IRO-1_01; DP IRO-1_02]
The results of these assessments have been challenged and
validated by key stakeholders, leading to adjustments in
materiality conclusions for some IROs. [DP IRO-1_05]
During the double-materiality analysis process, particular
attention was given to specific activities (including the
financial activities in the scope of the subsidiary Mobilize
Financial Services), business relationships, geographic
locations, and other factors that may lead to an increased
risk of negative impacts. For instance, social impacts were
assessed in relation to the locations of Renault Group and its
supply chain operations, as well as the specific types of
actors involved in the supply chain. Additionally, Renault
Group has placed a strong emphasis on its core activities of
manufacturing and selling vehicles, as these are the primary
sources of environmental impacts. For more information on
activities, business relationships, geographies and/or other
factors which have led to the identification of material IROs,
see chapter 2.2.1.2.2. [DP IRO-1_03; DP IRO-1_04
Throughout
the
double-materiality
process,
identified
dependencies have been connected to risks, through
operational risks (disruption or interruption of operation) and
financial risks (increasing costs or loss of revenue due to
dependencies and availability). On the other hand, identified
negative impacts have been connected to reputational and
legal risks, as stronger impacts could affect the Group’s
reputation, or result in legal sanctions if these impacts were to
be regulated. [DP IRO-1_08; DP IRO-1_09]
The
identification
of
these
negative
impacts
and
dependencies has allowed the Group to determine
opportunities where relevant, to improve its reputation or
performance, and to achieve resilient financial results and
operations by breaking dependencies. [DP IRO-1_08l]
The identification and assessment of IROs was managed by
the CSRD project team, which included members from the
Group Sustainability Team and the Finance Department. It
was responsible for making decisions during the process,
reviewing
conclusions
and
presenting
them
to
the
Leadership Team and the Audit and Risks Committee.
Controls on the double-materiality analysis were performed
by the CSRD project team based on their review and
feedback from topical experts. [DP IRO-1_11]
The double-materiality process was integrated in the overall
management process as it relied on existing risks, impacts
and
opportunities
assessments,
as
well
as
on
the
identification of new risks and impacts specific to the double
materiality analysis. Additionally, the scale used for financial
materiality scoring was in line with the Risk Management
Department’s risks rating scale. However, some scale criteria,
especially the ones designed for impact materiality
assessment, did not previously exist. [DP IRO-1_12]
Material IROs are monitored by relevant Departments within
the Group, such as the Risk Management Department, the
Group Sustainability Department, the Human Resources
Department, the Health, Safety, Environment & Ergonomics
(HSEE) Department, and the Supply Chain Department,
through the collection of qualitative and quantitative
information when relevant. Most important IROs have their
related targets integrated into the Group’s sustainability goals
(See chapter 2.2.1.1.1). [DP IRO-1_13]
This comprehensive process reflects the proactive and
strategic approach of Renault Group to understanding and
managing the IROs that are most significant to its business
and stakeholders. It ensures that the sustainability reporting
is accurate, relevant, and aligned with the expectations of
Renault Group’s stakeholders and regulatory requirements.
[DP IRO-1_10]
The methodology described above has been used for the
first time this year to produce the present report. In the event
of new information either from internal experts or
stakeholders, it will be considered in the analysis through
materiality changes of relevant IROs. In any case, a review of
the double-materiality analysis will be performed each year
as part of internal controls by the CSRD project team to
ensure its conclusions are still relevant. [DP IRO-1_5]
2.2.1.2.1.2 Business conduct-related Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities
All business conduct-related material impacts, risks and
opportunities descriptions are available in the chapter 2.2.1.2.2
below.
IROs related to business conduct have been identified
following the general process outlined previously. This process
considered the Group’s extensive scale and diverse locations,
operating in over 110 countries worldwide, necessitating a
monitoring of local laws and regulations. It also recognized the
heightened expectations of stakeholders concerning business
conduct, while simultaneously providing the Group with
opportunities to disseminate its values. Furthermore, the
Group’s role as a global car manufacturer, involving a vast
volume of transactions with a wide array of suppliers was also
factored into the assessment.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
107

2.2.1.2.2 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
As required by ESRS 1, the materiality of the impacts and risks below is based on an assessment of their potential severity and
likelihood, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).
Topic
Impact, risk
or opportunity 
Description 
Time

113

## Page 116

Renault Group
Sustainable development
The design and update of the Group strategy (Renaulution),
which is aimed at transforming its business model and its
value chain, consider the above-mentioned IROs. This
strategic transition is in progress and is supported by the
entire value chain, from suppliers to consumers and
recyclers. For a holistic view of implemented and ongoing
policies and actions, please refer to the related topical
ESRS chapters hereinafter. [DP SBM-3_03]
Each impact listed above is intertwined with the strategy or
business model. It stems either directly from the nature of
the products and services marketed as well as the activities
involved in their manufacture, or from relationships (direct
or indirect) with value chain participants and local
communities.  [DP SBM-3_05]
Climate-change material IROs identified through the
double-materiality analysis had already been identified as
critical by the Risk Management Department (See section
4.2 of the URD). Since 2020, Renault Group has been
assessing the resilience of its strategy and business model
to these impacts and risks, following the recommendations
of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosure
(TCFD)
framework.
Nature
and
significance
of
the
consequences of each risk on the Group’s short-, medium-
and long-term performance have been assessed, in order to
prioritize the issues. The consequences of each risk on
performance were assessed according to several criteria,
each of which was broken down into sub-criteria for a more
granular analysis. [DP SBM-3_10]
2.2.1.3 Basis for preparation
Renault Group’s sustainability statement has been prepared
on a consolidated basis, in line with the scope of consolidated
financial statements (See section 1.1.2.2 in URD for details on
Renault Group’s entities). The consolidation scope includes
entities controlled in accordance with IFRS standards, as
detailed in section 5 URD, note 31. When certain information is
provided for a different perimeter, this perimeter is explained.
Activity data for the HORSE entity from January 1st to the date of
deconsolidation has been included in the key performance
indicators, when relevant. [DP BP-1_01; DP BP-1_02]
Under the transposition of the CSRD into the French law, all
of the Group’s entities are exempted from reporting, except
for Mobilize F.S. and DIAC. [BP-1_03]
In ESRS E1, E2 and E4, where the Group had to report not only
on its financial consolidation perimeter but also on entities
under operational control according to the CSRD, the
following entities have been added: ORFIN, Select Vehicle
Holdings and Flexis.
As of May 31, 2024, the holding company HORSE Powertrain
Solutions S.L.U. and its subsidiaries are no longer under the
control of Renault Group and are not considered to be under
its operational control.
The targets described in this report have been defined
based on the scope as of December 31, 2024.
Renault Group’s sustainability statement covers both the
upstream and downstream value chain (See description in
chapter 2.2.1.1.1). As much of the required information
regarding the value chain was not available, estimates were
made wherever possible. It should be noted that the Group
does not have the legal ability to request such information,
which remains declaratory and the provision of which may
sometimes be refused by its holder on the grounds of
business confidentiality.  [DP BP-1_04]
In the interests of clarity and readability, Renault Group has
chosen to use the ‘-’ convention in the tables presented in
the report, to indicate a zero or infinitesimal value.
Sustainability information has been drawn up in a context of
initial application of legal and regulatory requirements,
characterized by uncertainties over the interpretation of texts,
the absence of established practices or comparative data, and
difficulties in collecting data. The Group has endeavored to
apply the normative requirements set by the ESRS, as
applicable on the date the sustainability report was drawn up,
on the basis of the information available, within the timeframe
for drawing up the sustainability report. The Group draws the
attention to the following facts, limitations and potential future
developments:
●The
sustainability
statement
was
prepared
and
presented in accordance with the requirements of the
ESRS and the applicable legislation, including the French
transposition of the CSRD; [DP BP-1_06]
●A better understanding of the requirements may be
available when additional implementation guidance or
Q&A will be available;
●Estimates may be refined in future reporting periods
when more relevant information becomes available;
●There is limited information available to assess some
automotive industry benchmarks and these may only
emerge as the number of reporters increases and
reporting practices become more established;
●Internal control practices related to sustainability
reporting will be strengthened further over the future
reporting periods;
●Comparatives progressively available after the first year
of reporting would make the information more useful; and
●The sustainability statement should disclose any key
judgements made and thresholds used in applying the
materiality assessment process. It is expected that the
double materiality assessment will be refined over time.
Renault Group complies with the time horizons defined by the
CSRD, with the exception of ESRS E1, which provides specific
horizons for GHG emission reduction targets, namely a short-
term horizon by 2030, a medium-term horizon between 2030
and 2040 and a long-term horizon from 2040 to 2050.
[BP-2_01; BP-2_02]
Indicators based on data from the value chain were
determined mainly using direct data sources. Only a few
specific cases required the use of indirect information:
●Scope 3, categories 1 and 12: emissions linked to the
production and end-of-life processing of the materials
and parts making up the vehicles, in cases where the
results of life-cycle analyses were not available (i.e. 22%
of vehicles sold);

●Scope 3, category 1: emissions linked to the purchase of

●Scope 3, category 2: emissions linked to the purchase of

Robust and recognised data sources are used, such as LCA for
Experts (GaBi), CITEPA, IEA, etc. For detailed information on the
data sources used, see the methodological notes linked to
each indicator. [BP-2_04; BP-2_05]
The only indicators identified as having a high level of
uncertainty are the quantity indicators for substances of
concern and very high concern (E2-5), mainly due to
methodological difficulties in extrapolation and estimation.
To better reflect the variability of these data, they are
provided in the form of calculation ranges in square
brackets ([xx - zz]). The Group is constantly striving to
improve the accuracy and reliability of its data.  [BP-2_03;
BP-2_04; BP-2_05; BP-2-06; BP-2_07; BP-2_08; BP-2_09]
Despite the efforts made, some data points could not be
documented for the 2024 financial year
●Product repairability and durability indicators (See details
in 2.2.2.5.3 Resource use and circular economy - Resource
outflows)
●Quantity and conditions of use of certain substances of
concern (See details in 2.2.2.6.1 Taxonomy - Methodology)
●The inclusion of fixed-term contracts in the calculation of
the turnover (See details in 2.2.3.1.7.1 Own workforce -
Methodological notes)
●Payment terms calculation (See details in 2.2.4.1.6
Business conduct - Payment practices).
The effectiveness of policies is not always measured by the
Group today in the way required by the generic MDR-T
requirement. As a result, some objectives could not be
described.
Renault Group has not incorporated any information from
other legislation or frameworks into its sustainability
statement. [BP-2_16]

## Page 118

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.2 Environmental information
2.2.2.1 Climate change (ESRS E1)
2.2.2.1.1 E1-1 - Transition plan for climate
change mitigation
Following the Paris Climate Agreement (COP 21) in 2015,
Renault Group has adapted its strategy to contribute to
limiting global warming to well below 2°C, and even aims to
continue efforts to limit the temperature rise to 1.5°C.
Previously, the Group reduced its carbon footprint intensity
in the automotive sector by 28% from 2010 to 20232.
These efforts will continue until 2050, when the Group has
set a net zero target to contribute to the carbon neutrality
objective, with an interim target to achieve this ambition for
activities in Europe by 2040. This will be accomplished
through a combination of 90% emission reductions and
offsetting actions for the remaining 10% of emissions based
on the 2019 base year. [DP E1-1_01; DP E1-1_02]
Since 2010, Renault Group has been carrying out greenhouse
gas estimation and life cycle assessments to evaluate the
global warming potential of the products it sells (See
chapter 2.2.1.2.1). The global warming potential of an internal
combustion engine (ICE) vehicle shows that more than 80%
of the greenhouse gases are emitted during the use phase
(Scope 3 Cat. 11), approximately 17% during the production of
the vehicle components (Scope 3 Cat. 1) including end-of-life,
and 1% during the Group’s operations (Scope 1&2) (data from
average 2024 results of the Renault Group’s greenhouse
gas emissions in intensity per vehicle).
Global warming potential (t CO2e/vehicle)
It forms the baseline for the transition plan: Renault Group
has implemented 3 main decarbonization axes across its
operations to achieve its 2030 targets: 
●Vehicle usage: prioritizing the development of low-
carbon technologies, focusing on electric vehicles while
also developing hybrids and hydrogen particularly in light
commercial vehicles. Adapting technologies to meet
diverse market demands and regulations is also a key
long-term objective;
●Raw material procurement: implementing proactive
measures at the start of the value chain, collaborating
across the entire supply chain to adopt more responsible
and low-carbon sourcing practices; including end of life
and recycling management;
●Production processes: enhancing sites energy efficiency,
expanding the use of renewable energy in its own
operations. [DP E1-1_03]
The life cycle analysis of an electric vehicle compared to an
equivalent ICE shows that it has a reduced global warming
potential of 1/3 to 2/3 (depending on the country’s electricity
mix) (data from average 2024 results of the Renault Group’s
greenhouse gas emissions per vehicle).
Global warming potential (t CO2e/vehicle)
The transition plan aims at reducing greenhouse gas
emissions. Renault Group measures and monitors the GHG
emissions intensity per vehicle sold (including “Use of Sold
Products“ Scope 3 category 11 of the GHG protocol as locked
in emissions). GHG emissions are anticipated to decrease
due to the declining sales of internal combustion engine
vehicles and their fossil fuel consumption and the increasing
sales of electric vehicles. [DP E1-1_07]
2 Scope limited to Renault, Dacia, Alpine and Mobilize brands worldwide sales (scope 1,2 and 3).

Renault Group is not excluded from the EU Paris-aligned

The transition plan and its commitments form an integral
part of the “Renaulution” strategy. Endorsed by the Board
and CEO Luca de Meo in April 2021, it encompasses the entire
value chain, targeting GHG emissions reduction and energy
efficiency. The Strategy and Sustainability Committee, with
a strong representation of independent Board members,
regularly
reviews
the
Group
Sustainability
Strategy
ensuring alignment with its ambitious goals. [DP E1-1_13;
DP E1-1_14]
In 2024, Renault Group has already made significant
progress in implementing its transition plan through various
efforts and initiatives, achieving a 12% reduction in
emissions per vehicle sold compared to 2019 data. Renault
Group achieved its CAFÉ 2024 targets for passenger cars
and light commercial vehicles, despite a particularly
unfavorable electric vehicle market context. [DP E1-1_15]
As climate change is one of the five factors identified by the
Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity
and Ecosystem Services (IPBES), limiting global warming will
help preserve natural ecosystems and their resilience. For
more precision about biodiversity and ecosystems, see
chapter 2.2.2.4.
2.2.2.1.2 SBM-3 – Material IROs in terms
of climate change and interactions
with strategy and business model
The Group conducted a resilience analysis using climate
scenarios (See chapter 2.2.2.1.3) to evaluate the impact of
identified climate risks and opportunities on its short-,
medium-, and long-term performance, which inform its
strategic priorities. The analysis considers the entire scope
of the Group’s activities with the same time horizon as
described in chapter 2.2.2.1.3. This analysis, aligned with
TCFD recommendations, was conducted in 2020 and
finalized in 2021 with the Renaulution plan and the Business
Strategy. It also makes it possible to give guidance on the
value chain (for example on electric vehicle batteries).
[DP E1.SBM-3_02; DP E1-SBM-03_03; DP E1.SBM-3_04;
DP E1.SBM-3_05]
Renault Group Leadership Team reviews this Business
Strategy and its impact on global warming during the
Strategy Days on a biannual basis.
The input data used in the resilience analysis includes sales
volumes, line-up assumptions and powertrain mix forecasts,
brands
strategy,
and
industrial
strategy.
Regional
regulations, such as CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy)
or emissions trading schemes, provide a framework for
managing emission reduction targets. [DP E1-SBM-03_03;
DP E1-SBM-03_06]
This data is further analysed in terms of market size and
structure, revenues, cost of sales, and other costs:
●Market structure factors such as TIV impact (sales
quantity), the evolution of the vehicle fleet (average
vehicle age, renewal rates), electric vehicles mix
percentage, Renault’s market share, and new business
development (percentage of turnover, increase or
decrease compared to reference);
●Pricing impacts including electrical vehicles (EV) price,
residual values, and variable marketing expenses (VME, FME);
●Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) impacts such as battery price,
battery size, and COGS increase due to mechanisms like
the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM);
●Labor cost;
●Fixed cost impacts including Research and Development
(R&D), Capital Expenditure (CapEx) and Operational
Expenditures (OpEx) impacts. [DP E1-SBM-03_03; DP E1-
SBM-03_06]
In the context of resilience analysis, two main uncertainties
have been identified. First, the use of scenarios introduces
inherent uncertainty due to the reliance on assumptions and
projections that may not materialize as expected (for
instance, electric vehicle market trend). Second, the analysis
is initially conducted at a macro level and then further
detailed for specific market segments, which can reveal
different vulnerabilities. These uncertainties are considered
in Renault Group’s strategy, investment decisions, and
mitigation actions. Analysis results are disclosed in the table
presented at the end of chapter 2.2.2.1.3. [DP E1.SBM-3_06]
The analysis is quantitative. It yields various results,
including
trajectories
for
reducing
greenhouse
gases.
It enables sensitivity analysis to prioritize the action plans. For
instance:
●For Scopes 1 and 2: assess the effects of introducing
renewable energies;
●For upstream Scope 3: measure the impacts of increased
production of electric vehicle batteries and efforts to
decarbonize the value chain;

The climate scenario analysis has been an integral part of
the Group’s strategic thinking. The Group’s ability to adjust
or adapt its strategy and business model to climate
change over the short-, medium-, and long-term is at the
core of its Renaulution strategy. This includes the
development of advanced technologies, such as new
electric vehicles, hybrid systems, and complementary
solutions like hydrogen and other emerging technologies,
while also developing new mobility, energy and data
services
and
circular
economy
businesses
(See chapter 2.2.1.1). [DP E1.SBM-3_07]

[DP E1. IRO-1_03; DP E1. IRO-1_05, BP-2_01; DP E1. IRO-1_02]
As required by ESRS 1, the materiality of the impacts and
risks below is based on an assessment of their potential
severity and likelihood, made without taking existing
prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account
(gross assessment).

To quantify actual and potential future climate-related
impacts risks, and opportunities, Renault Group periodically
performs an evaluation of its operations and throughout its
entire value chain, particularly through the Life Cycle
Assessment (LCA) approach. Renault Group conducts Life
Cycle Assessments for three key types of activities:
●First, when a new vehicle is introduced, the Renault Group
measures
the
greenhouse
gas
(GHG)
emissions
compared to the vehicle it replaces;
●Second, during the development phases of new vehicles,
Renault Group assesses the environmental impacts of
forthcoming products within its mid-term plan (MTP);
●Finally, in the research phase, the Group evaluates future
technological components in its long-term plan (LTP) to
understand the potential environmental impacts.
These assessments are crucial for driving continuous
improvement in reducing Renault Group’s environmental
footprint. This approach permits to identify actual and
potential climate related risks. [DP E1.IRO-1_ 01]
Greenhouse gas (GHG) discharges include the direct and
indirect GHG emissions and are expressed in metric tons of
CO2 equivalent (tCO2eq). 
Climate-related risks were analysed and placed in two
categories (See chapter 2.2.1.2.2):
●Transition risks, arising from the shift to a low-carbon
economy and all the changes that it implies;
●Physical risks, along with their potential repercussions on
business activity and on supply chains.
For each risk, time horizons on which the risk might materialize
(short-term - before 2030, medium-term - 2030-2040, and long-
term - 2040-2050) have been determined. [DP E1.IRO-1_03;
DP E1.IRO-1_05; BP-2_01; DP E1.IRO-1_02]
Since 2020, in alignment with the TCFD’s recommendations,
Renault Group has expanded and deepened its analysis of
climate scenarios and the implications of climate risks on its
short-, medium- and long-term performance, which help
define the sensitivities from which the Renaulution plan is
based on, using the following scenarios: 
●New Green Deal +1.5°C Scenario: there is a global
awareness of climate risks, leading to a shift towards
sustainable regulations and lifestyles. This scenario
envisions a world where public and private sectors
collaborate for a low-carbon transition, climate-adapted
technologies are developed, and a systemic approach to
mobility ensures the growth of efficient multimodal
services (scenario used for transition risks);
●Eco-Techno Driven +3°C Scenario: economic growth is
propelled by developed regions, locally balancing low
emissions level and climate-oriented economic activities.
However, this scenario is marked by a lack of global
commitment and coordination, leading to a 3°C increase
in global temperatures with significant physical impacts,
and
a
regional
variation
in
mobility
uses
and
demands (scenario used for transition & physical risks);
●Contraction and Fragmentation +4°C Scenario: global
governance and technology are lacking, resulting in
general economic decline and a retreat from globalization
due to climate, economic and political crises. This scenario
is characterized by recurrent physical risks such as floods,
fires, and droughts, leading to population migrations and
growing inequalities, with a predominance of low-tech/
low-cost solutions and a discouragement of long-distance
travel (scenario used for physical risks). [DP E1.IRO-1_07;
DP E1.IRO-1_08; DP E1.IRO-1_09]
These scenarios incorporate also external benchmark data,
in particular:
●World Automotive Powertrain Outlook, used by the
automotive industry platform (PFA);
●1.5TECH and 1.5LIFE scenarios presented by the European
Commission;
●Energy Technology Perspectives, Global EV Outlook, Net Zero
Roadmap published by the International Energy Agency;
●IPCC Climate Scenarios, which include a range of possible
futures based on different levels of greenhouse gas
emissions and their impacts on global temperatures.
(SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5). 
A. Physical risk analysis
A physical risk analysis for the years 2030 and 2050 has
been conducted in 2022 across Renault Group sites,
identifying extreme heat, floods, and water stress as key
risks for Renault Group. With an expected increase in
frequency and intensity of these events, significant impacts
on employees, industrial and logistic facilities, and overall
risk management are anticipated. The analysis of physical
climate-related risks is based on geospatial coordinates
specific to the Group locations, broad national or regional
data, and the two scenarios: “Eco-Techno driven” and
“Contraction and Fragmentation”. [DP E1.IRO-1-04]
This analysis has highlighted the potential impact of
physical risks on the Group’s assets, with the goal of
preparing appropriate responses to enhance resilience. The
scope includes all Renault Group industrial sites, key
logistics locations, and a selection of critical supplier sites
based on their geographic location and importance to the
Group’s activities. The study’s output provides for each site
climate indicators and quantified physical risks (probability,
severity), including seasonality. Potential impacts on
operations are assessed in terms of business continuity and
financial repercussions. [E1.IRO-1-01]
The analysis employs a quantitative approach, with key
parameters including:
●Acute physical risk drivers, such as the frequency and
intensity of extreme weather events like typhoons, floods,
landslides, and wildfires;
●Chronic
risk
drivers,
including
gradual
shifts
in
temperature and precipitation patterns leading to water
stress, sea-level rise, and other long-term changes.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
119

## Page 122

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Physical risk analysis: Renault Group sites
According to this analysis, 20 sites of Renault Group are
exposed to a high risk of riverine and surface flooding,
4 sites are located in regions with extreme water stress
where water consumption exceeds availability, and 6 sites
are exposed to extreme heat conditions that could
potentially endanger employees’ health and wellbeing.
Major impacts include business interruption due to extreme
climate events and increase of operational expenditures
(OpEx) to control temperature in workshops and assembly
lines, storage warehouses. To address these challenges,
Renault Group plans to build an adaptation and resilience
action plan, based on workshops, climate adaptation field
visits, sustainable lean best practices, and adaptation to
climate change ISO 14090 standard compliance. Renault
Group will also assess the costs and benefits of each action
with local teams at priority sites, evaluate the greenhouse
gas (GHG) emissions associated with each action and
prioritize their implementation. [E1.IRO-1-04]
Physical risk analysis: suppliers’ sites
For the suppliers’ sites, a multi-criteria supplier risk management
system including short-term physical climate related risks has
been implemented. Since 2024, suppliers presenting a potential
risk to impact Renault Group business continuity are subject to
an enhanced analysis of their Business Continuity Plan with
follow-up action plans. [E1.IRO-1-06]
B. Transition risk analysis
The completed analysis of transition risks within the TCFD
framework covers the manufacturing, sales, and financing
of vehicles, as well as all the assets that the Group owns to
operate. The results of this analysis were incorporated in the
double-materiality analysis, and the risks identified were
considered material risks, rated at the maximum for
likelihood and severity. The likelihood was rated at the
maximum because certain transition events are already
certain (e.g., the end of new thermal vehicles sales in Europe
by 2035). Regarding the magnitude and duration, two
scenarios (+1.5°C and +3°C) were considered, described
above, spanning from the short term to the long term.
[DP E1.IRO-1-12; DP E1.IRO-1-10; DP IRO-1-11; DP IRO-1-13]
Four risks were identified, relating to compliance with
regulations,
technological
developments,
market
expectations and energy prices. The transformation of the
automotive industry is a long-term process. Developing a
product from its conception to commercialization can take
up to five years with the goal of reducing it to two years.,
Technological shifts, such as electric vehicles (EVs), require
that infrastructure evolves accordingly. This timeline can be
segmented as follows: in the short term (less than 10 years),
the focus is on products currently under development; in the
mid-term (10 to 20 years), scenarios play a crucial role in
understanding major trends; and in the long term (beyond
20 years), disruptive technologies and innovation are
anticipated to reshape the industry. Furthermore, consumer
acceptance and market adaptation are critical; without
market support, these transitions take significantly longer.
Ultimately, the market’s readiness and pace are pivotal in
driving the industry’s evolution. The +1.5°C (high share of
electricity sales) and +3°C (slower development of electric
markets) scenarios lead to assess the risks and impacts
according to the electrification mix of each region and to
assess the trends in locked emissions (for more information,
see chapter 2.2.2.1.1) and the impact on the Group in terms of
business and the adaptation of industrial assets. The
transition consists of gradually evolving the range for each
region and logistics and industrial sites. It is the main driver
of the fight against climate change. The Group can thus
drive the necessary transformation of the value chain
accordingly. These integrated measures ensure Renault
Group’s resilience and adaptability in the face of climate
change. [E1.IRO-1-14; E1.IRO-1-15]
Renault Group has implemented measures to address the
4 transition risks related to climate change:
●To mitigate the risks associated with non-compliance
with
climate-related
regulations
or
new
reporting
requirements, such as remediation cost, loss of revenue,
penalties and reputational damage, Renault Group has
implemented an action plan, established a thorough
compliance verification process and set up provisions for
potential penalties, of which +1,5 et +3°C scenarios make
it possible to assess the orders of magnitude;
●Coordination with Mobilize Financial Services (M.F.S.)
helps mitigate the loss of residual vehicle values due to
evolving regulations and technologies;
●To mitigate revenue loss from climate-impacted customers,
the Group identifies new technologies and conducts
sensitivity testing by vehicle type (electric, hybrid, etc.) using
temperature scenarios and life cycle assessment. It also
tests new business models, which renew the product range
and their associated business models, including mobility
services, ensuring product alignment with the necessary
transformation of the industry;
●Additionally,
an
industrial
decarbonization
plan
transitions from market energy to long-term contracts
including Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) and Heat
Purchase Agreement (HPA) to protect against energy
price inflation and volatility.

## Page 124

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.2.1.4 E1-2 – Policies related to climate
change mitigation and adaptation 
The sustainable development strategy at the heart of
Renault Group’s “Renaulution” is structured around three
key pillars that enhance its responsibility and positive
impact, with environmental policy being one of them: 
●The first pillar, the environment, involves fighting climate
change and the optimisation of resource use through the
development
of
Renault
Group
circular
economy
activities;
●The second pillar, safety, by leveraging the technology in
our vehicles to improve the safety of users of vehicles and
services on the roads. This pillar also covers the safety of
employees in the workplace;
●The third pillar, inclusion. The shift towards the new
businesses of electrification, data and the circular economy
will be achieved by supporting the transformation of skills
and promoting diversity within the Group. 
Renault Group environmental policy
Renault Group commits to:
●Creating value: by contributing to value creation,
competitiveness,
and
protection
of
tangible
and
intangible assets;
●Reducing the environmental footprint by putting in place
mitigation plans;
●Steering
the
implementation
of
environmental
management
through
mitigation
plans
to
ensure
compliance with regulations and guarantee constant
improvement of compliance;
●Communicating
and
ensuring
transparent
and
responsible stakeholder dialogue and communication.
The Renault Group environmental policy covers 5 action’s
fields representative of key material impact:
●Climate change and energy efficiency;
●Health
&
substance
(pollution,
microplastics),
see
chapter 2.2.2.2;
●Water (Renault Group water policy), see chapter 2.2.2.3;
●Biodiversity & ecosystems services (Renault Group
biodiversity policy), see chapter 2.2.2.4;
●Resource use and circular economy (Renault Group
Circular economy policy), see chapter 2.2.2.5.
Renault Group environmental policy applies throughout the
vehicle lifecycle, from design to end of life, for own activities
(Renault Group HSEE Policy), the upstream value chain
(Green Procurement Guidelines) and the downstream value
chain, worldwide. [DP E1-2_01; DP MDR-P_02]
Renault Group climate policy is applicable throughout the
entire Group and is overseen by the Board of Directors. The
Board annually reviews climate change issues (adaptation
and mitigation), the Group’s GHG emissions strategy, the
electrification of its product lineup, and the impact of new
emissions regulations. [DP MDR-P_03]
Twice a year, the Director of Strategy and Business
Development, with the approval of the CEO, sets out the
proposed strategic priorities of the Group’s environmental
policy for discussion and deliberation by the members of the
Leadership Team, with the support of the Group Sustainability
Committee.
The
Strategy
and
Business
Development
Department is responsible for the strategic plan’s operational
implementation and ensures that its timelines and milestones
are being met by all Renault Group teams. [DP – MDR-P_03]
In March 2019, Renault Group became the first carmaker to
have its emissions reduction targets validated by the
Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi). Two years later, the
Group furthered its commitment by joining the “Business
Ambition for 1.5°C” campaign led by the SBTi, pledging to
adopt robust science-based targets aligned with Paris
Agreement
expectations.
Renault
Group
extends
its
contribution
by
responding
to
questionnaires
from
organizations such as CDP and EcoVadis. [DP MDR-P_03]
In 2020, prior to the announcement of Renaulution plan in the
annual shareholder meeting, Renault Group coordinated a
multidisciplinary working group involving various business
units. This group collaborated to design and adjust the
company’s policy, strategy, and targets to ensure alignment
with budgetary goals. After board approval, these proposals
were presented in 2021 to the employees and subsequently
rolled out across the organization. [DP MDR-P_05]
To strengthen its environmental policy, Renault Group adheres
to international norms and standards that support or regulate
corporate environmental, social, and societal practices,
committing to respect the foundational principles and
promote the universal values of the Global Compact. Key
reference texts include the Paris Agreement (COP 21), ISO 9001
and 14001 for environmental management, ISO  14040, 14044
and 14021 for vehicle lifecycle analysis, and the GHG Protocol
for GHG emissions reporting. [DP MDR-P_04]
The Group considers the interests of key stakeholders
(See ESRS 2) when setting its policies. Consultations are
realized through various channels, such as direct services,
studies, trainings, product ratings, media interactions,
dedicated events and annual review of the Social and
Economic Committee on climate-related topics. [E1 MDR-1,
DP E1-2_01]
The environmental policy is made available to stakeholders
through Green Procurement Guidelines, CSR guidelines, the
climate report online and Renault Group website. [DP MDR-
P_06]
