2.2.2.1.5 E1-3 – Actions and resources in
relation to climate change policies 
2.2.2.1.5.1 Action plan on Renault Group sites climate
change adaptation
To control the physical risks associated with extreme heat,
existing prevention and protection measures allow short-term
control. These include technical measures (ventilation, space
cooling, storage conditions for batteries of electric vehicles
(EV) or rechargeable hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV), chemical
products, etc.) and organizational measures (implementation
of the heat wave plan, work schedules, breaks, etc.).
The HSEE Department in coordination with other expert
functions leads all the sites concerned on these issues.
A steering committee is organized 4 times a year to monitor
progress and decide on complementary actions. This risk is
the subject of cross-functional governance in the company,
in a multi-business team (engineering, purchasing and
supply chain functions) to have a holistic view and build
shared resilience industrial strategies.
The next step is to identify and calibrate threshold values for all
our sites in 2025 by integrating the main physical factors
ambient temperature, humidity, activity process characteristics
and then develop an adaptation plan for the medium term.
To control the physical risks related to water stress, Renault
Group has implemented since 2023 a policy dedicated to
water management known as «Full Power Water» on all
industrial sites, detailed in chapter 2.2.2.3.2.
To control the physical risks associated with flooding,
Renault Group has previously carried out actions on
industrial sites. It undertakes the necessary maintenance
actions in order to maintain the efficiency of these facilities.
The approach has been extended to rainwater drainage
systems since late 2024.
2.2.2.1.5.2 Action plan on climate change mitigation
Renault Group’s strategic actions, within its decarbonization
framework, embrace all three stages of its value chain. An
internal carbon target is set for each new vehicle project
in  the upstream design phase. Each area of expertise
involved in design – vehicle aerodynamics, weight, choice of
materials, battery size, etc. –  identifies  and  leverages the
solutions available for reducing its carbon impact.
A. Raw material procurement
Over the entire life cycle of an internal combustion engine
vehicle, 17% of the carbon footprint (in intensity) comes from
the materials it is made of. This percentage can be more than
50% for electric vehicles (average Europe), as the battery
doubles the production’s emissions when usage emissions
drop. (See graphs in chapter 2.2.2.1.1.) Renault Group has laid
out internal commitments to curb the carbon impact  of
materials and develop circularity solutions:
●Eco-design of materials: the Group is committed to an eco-
design
approach
by
opting
for
more
sustainable,
innovative and low carbon solutions, including the
incorporation
of
recycled
materials.
For
example,
aluminum is used for the covers and doors of the Megane
and Scenic models as a lighter substitute for other
materials, and the cut-outs and stamping are returned to
aluminum suppliers for re-integration into the production
of new parts. Part of ferrous waste from French and
Spanish plants is also recycled in a closed loop in the
Group’s foundries or its suppliers. In addition, copper is
recovered on end-of-life vehicles to be reused in the
production of new parts, textiles are created from recycled
plastic bottles and scraps of seat belt fabrics, and plastics
incorporate an increasing share of recycling, like the new
Renault 5 E-Tech and the new Dacia Duster which contain
between 19% and 20% recycled plastics, rates that are
unseen today in the automotive market;
●More efficient, more sustainable and low-carbon, reusable
batteries: in partnership with suppliers, Renault Group aims
to reduce the carbon footprint of battery production by
utilizing decarbonized energy and materials. The Group has
secured specific agreements with material suppliers:
Terrafame for a sustainable nickel supply (ensuring low
carbon emissions and full supply chain traceability), Vulcan
and Arverne for less carbon-intensive lithium, and Managem
for low-carbon cobalt sulfate produced in Morocco. These
agreements complement existing partnerships with Verkor
and Envision-AESC to manufacture batteries in France
starting in 2025 and LG Energy Solution in Europe. The Group
is also implementing closed-loop recycling of strategic
materials (cobalt, nickel, lithium) to produce new batteries
(See chapter 2.2.2.5). In addition, the Group is advancing the
lifetime (through repair) and the reassignment (through
second-life) of lithium-ion batteries through the deployment
of electric vehicle battery repair centers and second-life
applications for our batteries with mobile solutions and
stationary renewable energy storage (Mobilize Advanced
Battery Storage). This will help to avoid greenhouse gas
emissions associated with the production of new batteries
for these purposes;
●Engaging with all the supply chain: Renault Group has
established a common framework for suppliers, through
its Green Procurement Guidelines and the CSR guidelines
for suppliers. It also implements an independent
verification of the supplier commitments through the top
500 of Renault Group’s suppliers subject to the CDP,
elaborating a supplier mapping showing their climate
trajectory and carbon footprint; 
●Decarbonizing logistics transport: the Group joined the
Coalition New Energies which brings together the main
international players in logistics to develop innovative
solutions for freight and the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in
the supply chain. Also, the use of rail is preferred for upstream
logistics and vehicle distribution, and an optimization system
is implemented for the truck loading and delivery routes.
Finally, the Group deployed a fleet of biogas and biofuel
trucks and used 100% electric trucks around the ElectriCity
industrial cluster (See chapter 2.2.1.2.2 Material IROs in terms of
climate change and see the URD section 1 strategy and
business model for more information about ElectriCity).
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
123

## Page 126

Renault Group
Sustainable development
B. Production processes
The Group transforms its plants, which emissions account
for 2% of the carbon footprint of its vehicles:
●Renault Group has invested in ElectriCity, its industrial hub
dedicated to electric vehicles, with an installed (and
already invested) capacity of 400,000 units (to be
increased to 620,000 in 2028).
●Improving sites energy efficiency: Renault Group has
implemented
less
consumptive
manufacturing
processes, energy recovery systems, and improved site
compactness to decrease the surfaces that need to be
illuminated and heated. Also, the Group implemented a
new data management and optimization tool to monitor
electricity, gas and compressed air consumption in real
time, through Ecogy portal which is connected to a
network of 10,000 of sensors and meters;
●Increasing renewable energy use: the Group aims to
increase the use of renewable energy in its own operations,
similar to what the Tanger site is currently doing, where
90% of its energy needs are met through renewable
energy. This includes a biomass boiler room, along with a
power purchase agreement (PPA) with Green of Africa that
secures long-term renewable electricity for Tangier and
Somaca (100% wind for Tangier and Somaca in 2026).
Renault Group also engages in strategic partnerships to
fasten its use of renewable energies as with Cormec
Ernegia in Brazil which allows Renault Group to become the
self-producer of one of Castilho’s five photovoltaic panel
farms. In France, the installation of photovoltaic panels
with Voltalia aims to cover at least half of the electricity
needs for the Group activities by 2027.
C. Vehicle usage
Lineup
electrification
is
an
essential
lever
for
decarbonization as the use phase of vehicles generates
more than 80% of Renault Group’s emissions.
●Electrifying products: the Group has been investing in the
development of electric vehicles at first with ZOE and
Twingo, then Spring, Megane E-Tech, Kangoo ZE. To
accelerate the adoption of electric vehicles, the Group has
launched various initiatives. The creation of Ampere, its
entity dedicated to electric vehicles and software,
develops, manufactures and markets electric passenger
vehicles under the Renault brand for the European market
(Renault 5 E-Tech, Scenic E-Tech in 2024 and Renault 4 in
2025). The development of Alpine aims to propose an 100%
electric vehicle range (launch of the A290 and Alpine A390
in 2025). Renault Group continues to develop its electric
range with the New Dacia Spring; a Renault brand electric
vehicle for international markets and a Master electric light
commercial
vehicle.
Additionally,
Renault
Group
is
investing solutions to electrify the last delivery kilometers
and decarbonize urban mobility, especially by creating
Flexis s.a.s with Volvo Group and CMA CGM, which aims to
create 100% electric vans (See ESRS 2);
●Developing hybrid offer: to support the transition towards
more sustainable vehicles in the short-term, the Group
continues to develop its hybrid offer both for Renault
brand (Rafale E-Tech, Symbioz E-Tech et Grand Koleos )
and Dacia brand (nouveau Duster hybrid);
●Developing hydrogen use: Renault Group is developing
hydrogen-powered light commercial vehicles project with
hydrogen charging and supply solutions, and a partnership
with HYPE (hydrogen taxi pioneer) to accelerate the urban
mobility through the installation of hydrogen stations. In
addition, the Renault Group continues through its Alpine
subsidiary engaged in competition the day before and the
development of advanced internal combustion engine
powered by gaseous hydrogen;
●The eco-driving assistance functions are a set of
complementary bricks (scoring, coaching and eco mode),
they help drivers to consume less energy (fuel or electricity)
encouraging more careful and energy-conscious driving;
●Mobilize, the brand dedicated to new mobility and
services around vehicles facilitates the adoption of
electric vehicles by providing solutions such as Charge
Pass (card access to public terminals in Europe) and
Smart Charge to optimize costs and reduce carbon
footprint. Mobilize goes even further with the “vehicle-to-
grid” technology (that manages home charging and the
energy return to the grid during peak hours); charging
stations via the Mobilize Power Solutions subsidiary
(to support professionals and individuals), and with the
creation of a network of ultra-fast stations;
●Monitoring usage emissions: Renault Group created the
CAFE Control Tower to monitor the reduction of GHG
emissions from tailpipes and to oversee the roadmap each
year (locked emissions tracking, for more information, see
chapter 2.2.2.1.1). The Group also developed a tool to
forecast CO2 levels for its registered vehicles in Europe. The
CAFE Control Tower reports its results to the Operations
Committee of Renault Group Leadership Team every
month. Outside Europe, the Group is subject to regulatory
standards similar to those in Europe. In total, around 70% of
the Group’s sales worldwide are thus subject to CAFE-type
regulations. The Renault Group achieved its CAFE 2024
targets in Europe for passengers’ cars and light
commercial vehicles, despite a particularly unfavorable
electric vehicle market context.
D. End-of-life management
Integrating circular economy principles into the value chain:
Renault Group created the entity “The Future is NEUTRAL” in
2022 to manage the supply of recycled raw materials in closed
loops, ensure the distribution of used and remanufactured
parts, and oversee the management of the end of life of
products (See chapter 2.2.2.5). [DP E1-MDR-7; DP E1-3_01]
E. Resources mobilized to implement the actions
The Group’s actions are guided by its strategic review and
cover multiple aspects of the value chain, such as material
selection, supplier partnerships, and production processes.
For instance, the choice of materials is a strategic decision
that is influenced by meeting both sustainable development
and technological innovation targets. Similarly, work is
being done with suppliers to align production capacities and
quality standards with new strategic directions. To ensure
the effective implementation of its climate actions, Renault

Group allocates an annual budget organized by category

vehicle GHG emissions are closely monitored in addition of
Renault Group carbon footprint, in the Leadership Team
monthly dashboard, and specific product competitiveness
targets are set in terms of fuel consumption and GHG
emissions. Renault Group Leadership Team reviews these
indicators and the execution of the action plan twice a year
to ensure their alignment with short-, medium- and long-
term strategy. [DP E1-3_0]
OpEx and CapEx resources allocated by Renault Group to its
climate-related actions are focused on three main areas:
●Electric vehicles: the Group has been investing in the
development of electric vehicles for almost 15 years, with
close to 1 million vehicles sold. Over the 2025-2027 period,
Renault Group plans to invest a minimum of € 1.2 Bn every
year on electric vehicles;
●Raw materials’ carbon footprint optimization, whether
through their substitution with lower CO2-emission
materials or the improvement of their production’s
energetical efficiency: concerned raw materials have
been identified, as well as the vehicle projects they apply
to, and according to the Group’s current volume
estimates, the first additional costs for these initiatives
will be incurred in 2028. Associated OpEx are expected to
grow over the years to reach an annual several tens of
million euros at steady speed over the known horizon;
●Group’s
industrial
sites’
energy
consumption
optimization: renewable energy supply for these sites is
notably illustrated by the implementation of long-term
purchasing agreements in France, Spain and Brazil, as per
URD
section
5,
note
28
on
off-balance
sheet
commitments, representing € 476 M, an amount that will
be accounted for in cost of goods sold over the duration
of these contracts. An additional € 22 M of financial debt
are booked for a solar energy project that came into
operation in Brazil in 2023.
The implementation of above actions depends on the
resources availability and allocation. [DP E1 MDR-12-13-14-
15-16; E1-3_05]
Investment and R&D expenses presented herein are an
integral part of those disclosed in the Green Taxonomy.
[E1-3_06; E1-3_07; E1-3_08; E1-1_08]
2.2.2.1.6 E1-4 – Targets related to climate
change mitigation and adaptation 
Targets were established by Renault Group to effectively
address the material climate-related impacts, risks, and
opportunities identified for the Group (See chapter 2.2.2.1.3).
They aim to manage both the negative impacts on GHG
emissions and the associated transition risks. These targets
also make it possible to monitor the implementation of
mitigation plans, particularly about Scope 3 emissions.
They are regularly reviewed in Strategy Day to plan the next
steps, for example setting the objectives and reference
point beyond 2030. [DP E1-4_01]

Renault Group’s performance aligns with its targets set for
2025, 2030, 2035, 2040 and 2050. [E1 MDR-32]
The Group complies with various international norms and
standards related to climate change mitigation and adaptation
(See chapter 2.2.2.1.4). As all targets flow from the transition plan,
see chapter 2.2.2.1.1 for more details. The targets were defined by
the same multidisciplinary group that developed the policy. [DP
E1 MDR-29; DP E1 MDR-28; DP E1 MDR-30]
2019, the baseline year for calculating the Group’s GHG
emissions targets is representative of an activity not
disrupted by major events (such as COVID or supply
shortages). It was fully recalculated in the new methodology
in 2024 (See chapter 2.2.2.1.8). The energy intensity reduction
objective was added in 2021 (with this reference year) due to
increased energy costs during that period. [DP E1-4-21]
Targets to reduce energy intensity (MWh per vehicle
produced), increase the proportion of renewable energy in
electricity consumption, and boost the share of renewables
in heating supply are designed to monitor and mitigate the
negative impacts from GHG emissions and energy usage, as
well as to manage risks related to energy cost inflation and
market changes. Additionally, contributing to the objective
of carbon neutrality by reducing emissions is also crucial for
managing the adverse effects of GHG emissions and energy
consumption. [DP E1-MDR-20; DP E1-MDR-18]

In compliance with DR E1-5, the energy intensity ratio is
calculated as the ratio of the energy consumption of
climate-high activities to the turnover of these activities.
Renault Group points out the difficulty of interpreting this
ratio, equal to 68 MWh/MEUR in 2024, due to its sensitivity to
many factors such as the level of industrial integration or
the product range. [E1-5_18]
2.2.2.1.8 E1-6 – Gross Scopes 1, 2, 3
and Total GHG emissions
As 2024 marks the first year of compliance with the CSRD,
Renault Group has reviewed its scope, methodology, and
other aspects to ensure alignment with the requirements.
No subsequent event post end of reporting year has been
identified. [E1-6_16] 
Renault
Group
calculates
its
carbon
footprint,
with
200,000 kilometers mileage in WLTP standard (Worldwide
Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure) for the usage phase.
The mileage assumption of 200,000 km for all vehicles is based
on external sources such as the study carried out by Ricardo for
the European Commission (Determining the environmental
impacts of conventional and alternatively fueled vehicles
through LCA - European Commission - 2020), supplemented by
internal data on the distribution of Renault Group sales across
the various vehicle segments. To provide a more realistic
assessment Renault Group increases the values for CO2
emissions and electricity consumption of vehicles during the
use-phase by 20%, aiming to better represent actual driving
conditions. This estimation factor is based on an analysis of
OBFCM (On-board fuel consumption monitoring) data collected
by the European Commission. See in particular “Report on real-
world CO2 emissions of cars and vans using data from
on-board fuel consumption monitoring devices”. In 2024, Renault
Group updated the calculation methodology for the materials
production phase and completed the inventory of scope 3 GHG
protocol category with categories 2, 3 and 15. Emissions from the
baseline year were recalculated to align with this new
methodology. [DP E1-6_15]
The Group applies both location-based and market-based
methods to calculate Scope 2 GHG emissions and uses
Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) and Heat Purchase
Agreements (HPAs). [DP E1-6_19]
The following data sources are used for the GHG emissions calculation:
Aspect of GHG Emissions Calculation 
Methodology and Data Source/Standard Reference 
Purchased Electricity 
National energy mix emission factors reported by the International Energy Agency (IEA) 
Steam Emissions 
Local calculations or biomass recovery assessment 
Refrigerant Leak Emissions 
Supplier or local data estimates 
Vehicle Testing Emissions 
International standards compliance 
Foundry CO2 Emissions 
Internal material balance method 
Incinerated Volatile Organic Compounds 
EU-ETS emission factor 
[DP E1-6_23; DP E1-6_15]
In the reporting of Scope 3 greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions,
categories 8 (Upstream leased assets), 10 (Processing of
sold products) and 13 (Downstream leased assets) are
deemed non-applicable for the following reasons:
●Category 8 is non-applicable because Renault Group
does not have any leased assets in the upstream portion
of its value chain;
●Category 10 is non-applicable as there is no downstream
processing of Renault’s sold products;
●Category 13 is non-applicable because Renault Group
does not have any leased assets in the downstream
portion of its value chain. [DP E1-6_26; DP E1-6_27]
The scope of non-financial reporting aligns with that of
financial reporting as outlined in the URD section 5.1. For all
the information on the operational control and the scope of
the calculation, see chapter 2.2.1.3. [E1-6_32]
Renault Group has made significant progress in achieving
an environmental reduction compared to the base year,
with a 38% reduction in carbon gross (GHG in absolute).
[DP E1-4_19; DP E1 MDR-27; DP E1 MDR-25; DP E1 MDR-23;
DP E1 MDR-21; DP E1 MDR-26; DP E1 MDR-22]
Targets were established to manage the negative impacts of
GHG emissions, and the risks associated with regulatory
transitions, such as reducing carbon intensity (GHG per
vehicle produced) and achieving scope 3 related targets.
Renault Group ensures the validity of its carbon calculation
methodology through regular critical reviews by third parties,
review conducted by a specialized external consultant. The
Group complies with the ISO 14064 standard for its GHG
inventory. [DP E1-MDR-20; DP E1-MDR-28; DP E1-4_18]
The reference year 2019 was chosen because it represents
the last year before the COVID-19 pandemic, and the data is
most representative of a standard level of business activity
in terms of sales and production. [DP E1-MDR-20]

Until 2024, Renault Group’s targets were set in intensity, more
precisely in tons of CO2 equivalent per Renault Group brands’
vehicle sold. These targets will be converted into absolute
values once the sector-specific approach to decarbonization
in the automotive industry is established. During the transition
period, Renault Group has set up minimum absolute
objectives on combined scope 1 and 2 (market-based only)
and on the overall scope 3. These intermediate objectives
allow the Group to minimum ensure a well-below-2°C
trajectory. The Group continues to develop new vehicle
projects with objectives in intensity. [DP E1 MDR-28]
Renault Group is diligently pursuing its commitment for
2030, aiming to reduce emission to meet a well-below 2°C
target (See chapter 2.2.2.1.1). [DP E1-4-22; DP E1-4-24]

02
2.2.2.1.9 E1-7 – GHG removals
and GHG mitigation projects
financed through carbon credits 
In its ambition to contribute to carbon neutrality (See
chapter 2.2.2.1.6), Renault Group is focusing its efforts on
reducing emissions by 90% and addressing the remaining
10% through targeted carbon offset investments. Those 10%
remaining emissions correspond to what Renault Group
calls residual emissions.
In 2024, Renault Group did not purchase any carbon credits.
In the future, these offsets will be purchased on the
voluntary carbon market, certified according to national or
international standards such as the Gold Standard, Verra
(VCS), or the French low-carbon label. The projects will vary
in nature, including renewable energy production, forest
maintenance, creation of forests or mangroves, and agro-
ecological projects, all aiming to have positive impacts on
multiple Sustainable Development Goals (SDG). Renault
Group does not plan to invest in carbon offset on short term.
[DP E1-7-20; DP E1-7_21]
2.2.2.1.10 E1-8 – Internal carbon pricing
Renault Group applies internal carbon pricing schemes,
through three different internal prices, to inform decision-
making
across
all
the
Group
activities:
industrial
installations,
vehicle
projects,
and
materials
and
components of vehicles throughout the value chain.
●Emissions from industrial activities (Scope 1 and 2
emissions) are covered by an internal price applied for
CapEx and OpEx. The internal price, which is reviewed
annually and is based on a projection influenced by the
cost of emissions under Emission Trading Schemes (ETS),
such as the European Union ETS. Current scenarios vary
from between €75 per ton of CO2eq in 2025 to €120 per ton
of CO2eq in 2030;
●For the usage phase emissions of vehicles, including
electric vehicles (Scope 3 downstream), an internal price
of between €15 and €95 per gram of CO2eq is applied to all
decisions on the technological bricks that improve the
efficiency of vehicles. This price is indexed to the regional
CAFE penalties applied to the intensity of kilograms of
CO2 per kilometer;
●The
emissions
from
materials
and
components
production (Scope 3 upstream) are subject to an internal
price per ton of CO2eq, driven by the CBAM (Carbon
Border Adjustment Mechanism) and ETS (same amount
as ETS scenario).
Internal carbon prices are consistent with provisions for the
risk
of
CAFE
penalties
in
several countries and potential pooling cost in the EU.
Renault Group ensures the consistency of the internal
carbon prices used in financial statements for the
assessment of the useful life and residual value of assets,
impairment of the net recoverable value of its assets (See
the URD section 5.1). [DP E1-8_02; DP E1-8_03; DP E1-8_04;
DP E1-8_05; DP E1-8_09]

2.2.2.1.11 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its environmental metrics through physical
measures based on robust methods. The carbon footprint
calculation is reviewed by the consulting firm EcoAct. The other
indicators are subject to internal controls and verifications,
without being systematically validated by an external body.
If a metric is reported and not listed below, it indicates that it
has been calculated based on several metrics listed below
and that the calculation formula is indicated either in the
metric label or in the description of the associated
disclosure requirement.
E1-5 – Energy consumption and mix
All metrics listed below are expressed in MWh LHV (Lower
Heating Value), if not specified. All indicators with a value of
zero have not been listed below, as this indicates that no
Renault Group site
has reported any associated consumption. Sites with a very
small headcount, the impact of which is therefore negligible
(mainly tertiary sites), are excluded from the application
scope.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
131

## Page 134

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02; DP E3-4_06]
Fuel consumption from crude oil and petroleum
products
These indicators represent the heavy fuel oil, the home heating oil, gasoline, diesel, and LPG
(for crude oil and petroleum indicators) or gas (for natural gas indicator) consumed at Renault
Group sites. These indicators are consolidated based on the reporting of direct measures of
quantities consumed by each Renault Group sites, in their delivery unit. If the site is not able to
report consumed quantities, it has the possibility to estimate them through the reporting of
supplied quantities. The reported quantities are finally converted from their delivery unit to
MWh LHV in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2018/2066, allowing exceptions.
Fuel consumption from natural gas
Consumption of purchased or acquired electricity,
heat, steam or cooling from fossil sources
For electricity, these indicators are determined by using the reported electricity consumption
of Renault Group sites and applying the energy mix of purchased electricity communicated
directly by suppliers or, if this information is not available, those published by the IEA
(International Energy Agency). Only the hot water purchased by the Tanger site is of
renewable origin. All steam and cooling purchased by Renault Group sites are of fossil origin.
The reported consumptions of Renault Group sites are obtained from energy suppliers.
In case not all electricity, heating, and cooling consumptions are available, back-up
methodologies such as the ratio of consumption in kWh per square meter (kWh/m²) are used.
Consumption of purchased or acquired electricity,
heat, steam, and cooling from renewable sources
Total energy consumption from nuclear sources
Consumption of self- generated non-fuel
renewable energy
These indicators correspond to the quantity of electricity consumed that comes from
primary energies of renewable origin, which is produced and self-consumed on Renault
Group sites. This indicator is consolidated based on the reporting of direct measures of
self-generated renewable electricity each Renault Group sites. Renault Group consumes
all self-produced renewable energy.
Renewable energy production
Fuel consumption from renewable sources
including biomass (also comprising industrial and
municipal waste of biologic origin, biogas,
renewable hydrogen, etc…)
This indicator measures biomass consumed at Renault Group sites. The value is obtained
by directly measuring the energy produced from wood-fired boilers. To obtain the
quantity of burned biomass, a yield of 80% and a LHV have been considered, based on
the study “Measures of characteristics of wood fuels for softwood” by the French
Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME).
Total energy consumption from activities in high
climate impact sectors
Renault Group specifies that all its activities are considered high climate impact sector
activities, including the manufacturing of motor vehicles, trailers, semi-trailers, parts, and
accessories for motor vehicles, as well as the repair and maintenance of other transport
equipment. This also extends to the wholesale and retail trade and repair of motor
vehicles and motorcycles. The reporting scope of non-financial data is aligned with the
financial one, as detailed in the URD section 5.1. Therefore, this indicator is the same as
the calculated total energy consumption. [DP E1-5_20]
E1-6 – Gross scopes 1, 2, 3 and total GHG emissions
Scope 1, 2, and 3 calculations include entities financially
consolidated and under operational control. Entities under
operational control have been determined according to the
following criteria:
●Renault Group fully controls the entity under the meaning
of IFRS 10;
●The entity operates in Renault Group facilities (solely);
●The entity operates as a joint operation, meaning it has
rights to assets and obligations for liabilities;
●Renault Group has a license or an authorization to
operate the industrial assets;
●The entity is managed only by dispatched Renault Group
employees who apply Renault Group policies;
●The entity is contractually tied to Renault Group’s
expectations related to environmental policies beyond
normal terms and conditions of sale. [DP E1-6_29]

Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02; DP E1-6_29]
Gross Scope 1 GHG emissions
Direct emissions come from burning fuels, incinerating volatile organic compounds, foundry
processes, leaks during filling air-conditioning systems of premises and buildings, leaks during
filling air-conditioning systems in manufactured and repaired vehicles, engine, gearbox and
vehicle testing, and company vehicles. These direct emissions are calculated by applying
emission factors to the reported fuel consumption. Emission factors used are obtained from
Regulation (EU) 2018/2066 for CO2, from the CITEPA report for CH4 and N2O and converted to
CO2eq with GIEC Global Warming Potential factors.
Percentage of Scope 1 emissions regulated
from emission trading schemes
The percentage has been determined by extracting the GHG emissions from installations
regulated by an emission trading scheme. The Group possesses installations regulated by the EU
ETS and the Korea Emissions Trading Scheme.
Gross location-based Scope 2 GHG emissions
Indirect emissions from purchased electrical and thermal energy are calculated by applying
emission factors to the reported electricity and thermal energy consumption of Renault Group
sites. Emission factors for location-based scope 2 calculation is obtained from the IEA. Emission
factors for market-based calculation is obtained from energy supplier, especially when the
Group uses Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or Heat Purchase Agreements (HPAs).
Otherwise, IEA’s emission factors are used for the calculation.
Gross market-based Scope 2 GHG emissions
Gross Scope 3 GHG
emissions
Purchased goods –
materials and parts
Cradle-to-gate emissions related to the extraction of materials and fuels, the transformation of
materials into parts, and the logistics between the extraction and the tier 1 supplier site relative to
the number of vehicles sold. Emissions are obtained from the LCA for Expert database (emissions
from the production of materials, spare parts, and required processing for one type of vehicle).
These LCAs estimate emissions from The Renault Group design database (vehicle composition,
recycled materials). Emissions for each vehicle are multiplied by the number of vehicles sold by
vehicle type, which is obtained from the sales overview by country.
Purchased goods –
vehicles
Emissions linked to manufacturing of Renault Group vehicles in plants where Renault Group does
not hold a majority stake. These emissions are directly calculated from sales volume and annual
production of Renault Group’s vehicles.
Waste generated in
operations
Emissions related to the treatment of waste generated on the Group sites. Quantities of waste
generated are obtained from the reporting of each Renault Group sites. Emission factors applied
are those used in vehicles LCAs, from the LCA for Expert database.
Capital goods
Emissions related to the purchase of long-term assets used in the production of goods, such as
machinery, equipment, and buildings. To determine the emissions generated, Renault Group uses
emission factors based on monetary ratios per category of purchased goods from the French
Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME).
Logistics (upstream)
Emissions from road, sea, and rail transport for parts and materials are calculated based on
logistics activities reporting on the type of transport used, kilometers traveled, volumes of vehicles
transported and the filling rate of containers. Emission factors used are obtained from the Clean
Cargo Working Group for sea transport and from ADEME  for other types of transport.
Logistics (downstream)
Business travel
Emissions from employee business travel (train, plane) are calculated based on the reporting from
the travel agency, which integrates the number of kilometers traveled, the type of transport, type of
service (economy, business, etc.) and uses emission factors published by the Department for
Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) of the United Kingdom, which are updated annually.
Daily commuting
Emissions from vehicles and public transport for employee commuting are calculated from
information provided by employees in the Renault Group personnel database. Employees provide
information on the type of transport used (private vehicle or public transport) and the home-work
distance. To calculate emissions generated, the Group uses average emission factors from ADEME.
Emissions prevented through homeworking are deducted. This indicator is calculated for the daily
commuting of Renault Group France employees and extrapolated worldwide.
Franchises
Direct and indirect emissions from sales network for vehicles not sold by the Renault Retail Group
(RRG) network are estimated by extrapolating the value obtained through RRG. Emission factors
used are the same as those used for scope 1 and scope 2 GHG emissions.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
133

## Page 136

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02; DP E1-6_29]
Use of sold products
All vehicles sold (passenger and light commercial) worldwide. “Well-to-wheel” emissions are
calculated for a life cycle of 15 years/200,000 km. Emissions of vehicles are obtained from
homologation data or technical definitions (for countries in which there is no CO2 certification).
“Tank-to-wheel” emissions are based on the Worldwide Harmonized Light-duty Vehicle Protocol
(WLTP). Values obtained from the WLTP procedure are increased by 20% to obtain values closer
to actual use by consumers. The calculation integrates gains from eco-innovations and eco-
driving features, which are obtained from internal studies and are applied according to the fitted
vehicle rates using engineering data.
“Well-to-tank” emissions are linked to the production of energy consumed by vehicles sold
(extraction of oil, transport, refining; production of electricity consumed by electric vehicles) and
are calculated for a 15-year/200,000 km lifespan. For fuels, emission factors are obtained from
the JRC-Eucar-Concawe (JEC) “well-to-tank” report on CO2 emissions based on the “tank-to-
wheel” CO2. These factors are applied to the “tank-to-wheel’ emissions calculation (See above),
taking into account the type of fuel and the geographic or country plate. Electricity emission
factors are obtained from the Internation Energy Agency (IEA), updated annually.
End-of-life treatment of
sold products
Emissions from the end-of-life vehicles are calculated using data from the LCA for Expert
database, which includes the carbon intensity of manufacturing materials, parts, and treatment.
These estimates are based on the Renault Group’s design database, considering vehicle
composition and recycled materials. Emissions for each vehicle type are multiplied by the number
of vehicles sold, as recorded in the sales overview by country. The end-of-life treatment follows ISO
22628 standards for recyclability and recovery rates for each vehicle type.
Other emissions
related to fuel and
energy-related
activities (not included
in Scope 1 or Scope 2)
Other emissions from the use of fuels at Group’s sites (such as natural gas, fuels, etc.) are
calculated using data from the LCA for Expert database. This database includes the carbon
intensity of the extraction and transformation of raw materials necessary for production, as
well as the transportation of these materials to the sites
Investments
Vehicles financed by Mobilize Financial Services (Mobilize F.S.) that are not from Renault, Dacia,
Alpine, or Mobilize brands are included in this category. The calculation considers vehicles in the
current Mobilize F.S. portfolio. Emissions are calculated from well-to-wheel, using the same
emission factors and methodology as in the “Use of sold products“ and “Fuel“ categories. This is
based on vehicle specifications (approval data, technical definitions) and sales volumes by
country.
Biogenic emissions of CO2 from the
combustion or bio-degradation of biomass not
included in Scope 1 GHG emissions
CO2 emissions from biomass combustion at Renault Group sites are calculated using emission
factors from CITEPA, applied to the energy consumption reported by each site.
Biogenic emissions of CO2 from the
combustion or bio-degradation of biomass not
included in Scope 2 GHG emissions
Biogenic CO2 emissions from purchased electricity are calculated using CITEPA emission factors
and the percentage of biogenic electricity, based on IEA data, with a biomass boiler efficiency of
70%. Purchased hot water comes entirely from biomass sources, using the CITEPA emission
factors. Conversely, purchased steam comes entirely from fossil sources, no biogenic CO2
emissions.
Biogenic emissions of CO2 from combustion or
bio-degradation of biomass that occur in value
chain not included in Scope 3 GHG emissions
Biogenic emissions from biofuel combustion, included in the average fuel mix, are a relatively
recent topic. Therefore, to date, there is no widely recognized emission factor for biogenic
emissions from biofuel combustion. Given that the biofuel mix is about 7%, the biogenic
emissions are considered insignificant in relation to the total Scope 3 emissions of Renault Group,
and as such, are not reported.
Percentage of contractual instruments, Scope
2 GHG emissions
This indicator corresponds to the share of contractual instruments, in MWh LHV, that are bundled
or unbundled with attributes about energy generation. The share is calculated based on the total
energy purchased, in MWh LHV, used to calculate Scope 2 GHG emissions.
Percentage of contractual instruments used
for sale and purchase of energy bundled with
attributes about energy generation in relation
to Scope 2 GHG emissions
This indicator corresponds to the share of contractual instruments, in MWh LHV, that are bundled
with attributes about energy generation. Renault Group contracts, as for example, PPA and HPA
with its energy suppliers. The share is calculated by dividing the energy purchased through these
contracts by the total energy purchased in MWh LHV used to calculate Scope 2 GHG emissions.
Percentage of contractual instruments used
for sale and purchase of unbundled energy
attribute claims in relation to Scope 2 GHG
emissions
This indicator corresponds to the share of contractual instruments, in MWh LHV, that are
unbundled with attributes about energy generation. The share is calculated by dividing the
energy purchased through these contracts by the total energy purchased, in MWh LHV used to
calculate Scope 2 GHG emissions.
Percentage of GHG Scope 3 calculated using
primary data
Data is considered primary when the emission factor or the activity data are directly collected
from specific activities, processes, or sources within the value chain. Depending on the subject
matter, the data can originate from external sources (such as the IEA, CITEPA, GaBi, etc.) or from
data provided by suppliers (quantity, specific emission factor).

2.2.2.2.1 IRO-1 – Description of the processes
for identifying and scoring Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities
Within Renault Group’s own operations, activities with the
most significant impacts are subject to impact studies
overseen by local authorities. These studies are submitted
for public consultation in most countries where Renault
Group operates. [DP E2.IRO-1_02
Consultations, in particular with affected communities, have
been conducted for all major impacting projects or
modifications whenever required by law. [DP E2.IRO-1_02]
In addition, regarding air, water and soil pollution, the Group
follows the positions and guidelines from the local
administrations and agencies, which develop frameworks
based on field consultations with relevant stakeholders.
Such consultations are carried out as part of process
modifications when preparing operating permit application
files for sites. [DP E2.IRO-1_02]
Stakeholders’ interests and complaints are managed
through an Environmental Management System, which is
implemented across all sites. [DP E2.IRO-1_02]
In the upstream part of its value chain, Renault Group
engages in consultations with NGOs to understand pollution
impacts in the indirect supply chain, such as those from
mining. During these discussions, NGOs share their analytical
findings with Renault Group, while the Group makes specific
inquiries for clarification. Additionally, NGOs request official
responses from Renault Group to ensure transparency in
addressing pollution concerns. [DP E2.IRO-1_02l]
To complete its materiality assessment, Renault Group
ranked its sites and business activities involving material
pollution impacts, along with the corresponding risks and
opportunities
identified.
Material
sites
and
business
activities are listed in chapter 2.2.2.4.1. [DP E2.IRO-1_03]
Simultaneously, Renault Group evaluates pollution impacts
of its products and services through life cycle assessments
(LCAs) (See chapter 2.2.2.1.3 for more details on LCAs). [DP
E2.IRO-1_03]
Regarding microplastics pollution, Renault Group has
conducted an internal analysis of its own activities to
identify actual and potential pollution-related impacts, risks,
and opportunities. The assessment identified vehicle
testing and R&D as the main sources of microplastics
pollution
within
Renault
Group’s
own
operations.
Additionally, studies of the automotive value chain conclude
that microplastics pollution is significant in the downstream
value chain, particularly due to tire abrasion in the use-
phase of vehicles. As tire abrasion is not significant during
vehicle testing and R&D compared to the volume of vehicles
sold by Renault Group, the Group considers microplastics
pollution material only in the downstream part of its value
chain. [DP E2.IRO-1_01]
Impacts,
risks
and
opportunities
related
to
water
consumption are presented in water and marine resources-
related impacts, risks and opportunities (See chapter 2.2.2.3).
2.2.2.2.2 E2-1 – Policies related to pollution
Overall pollution 
Health and substances (pollution, microplastics) are one of the
five fields of action of Renault Group’s environmental policy.
For more information about this policy, see chapter 2.2.1.1.1.
This policy has been translated into a Health, Safety,
Environment and Ergonomics (HSEE) policy applicable to all
Group industrial sites and signed by the HSEE Director, the
Group Environment Director, the VP Human Resources, Work
Environment and Organization Director and the Group CEO.
It describes the Group’s commitment to providing a safe
and healthy workplace and environment for all those
impacted by its activities and specifies the roles and
responsibilities of managers and employees.
The HSEE policy, which is validated by the Leadership Team,
complies with local regulations and takes into account the
interests of stakeholders in the environmental, economic,
health and safety fields. Interests are determined through
public consultations and exchanges with public authorities.
Internally, the policy is available on the Group’s intranet and
in environmental reports. [DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_04;
DP MDR-P_05; DP MDR-P_06]
Renault Group applies the ISO 14001 standard, which aims to
reduce an organization’s environmental impact by taking
into account the expectations of its various stakeholders. In
order to monitor the application of this ISO 14001 standard
and internal environmental protection standards, the Group
carries out internal audits of all its industrial sites and its
main logistics and engineering sites. These internal audits
complement the external audits carried out annually by an
accredited third-party organization. [DP MDR-P_01; DP
MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_04; DP MDR-P_05]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
137

## Page 140

Renault Group
Sustainable development
On the other hand, risk management is monitored under the
Mandatory Rules Environment (MR#E), launched in 2021. This
framework requires the implementation of adapted actions
plans at each site, according to the level of risk identified. By
laying down precise requirements to be met in all areas, the
Mandatory Rules Environment (MR#E) help to prevent
incidents and emergency situations. In the event of an
emergency, procedures established at site and company
level are applied to prevent possible impacts and restore
normal operating conditions. [DP E2-1_03]
Renault Group has developed a methodological note to help
avoid and prevent incidents and emergency situations.
These documents are available to all employees. 
Every year, Renault Group carries out a risk mapping to identify,
analyse and prioritise risks in terms of environment, human
rights, health and safety and governance in its value chain.
Renault Group’s approach to identifying material risks and
opportunities applies to its entire value chain rather than to
specific sectors. However, two topics stand out that concern
the subject of soil pollution: the implementation of the
European Union Deforestation Regulation, which extends the
responsibility of due diligence of companies to production
plots, and the European Battery Regulation, which imposes due
diligence on four materials (cobalt, lithium, nickel and natural
graphite) throughout the supply chain, up to the mines.
All Renault Group suppliers, whether direct or indirect, are
covered by the responsible procurement strategy.
Renault Group has implemented the following policies to
prevent negative impacts and risks related to ESG, including
the environment, of which soil pollution:
●The Vigilance Plan;
●The 2013 Global Framework Agreement (GFA) on social
and environmental responsibility;
●Renault Group’s procurement policy for Suppliers;
●The Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) Guidelines for
Suppliers;
●The Green Procurement Guidelines (GPG);
●Renault Group’s policy on the supply of cobalt and
minerals from conflict-affected and high-risk areas;
●Renault Group’s sustainable natural rubber policy.
These policies are available on Renault Group’s website and
are accessible to all stakeholders. [DP MDR-P_06]
In particular, the Green Procurement Guidelines describe
Renault Group’s expectations of all its suppliers on
environmental matters, including soil pollution. [DP E2-1_01;
DP E2-1_03; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03;
DP MDR-P_04]
Regarding the downstream value chain, the contracts
between Renault Group and its distribution networks imply
that
the
latter
comply
with
the
Group’s
network
environmental policy, including the disposal of end-of-life
vehicles with the required regulatory approvals and
authorisations. [DP MDR-P_06]
Air pollution 
Renault Group’s air pollution policy, as part of the Group’s
environmental policy (See chapter 2.2.1.1.4), focuses on
reducing emissions across its operations and downstream
value chain and is based on three pillars:
●Reduction and treatment of Volatile Organic Compounds
(VOCs) emissions from vehicle paint;
●Implementation of substitution and improvement plans in
thermal power plants to reduce sulphur dioxide (SO2) and
nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions;
●Reduction
of
air
pollution
from
Renault
Group
products.  [DP E2-1_01; DP E2-1_03; DP MDR-P_01; DP
MDR-P_02] 
The environmental policy is notably supported by the ISO
14001 standard, with all manufacturing sites certified. GHG
reduction is based on the GHG Protocol. [DP MDR-P_04]
Soil pollution 
Renault Group has implemented a soil pollution prevention
policy to avoid any impact on soil due to pollution incidents.
Its implementation is supervised by the Leadership Team. It
covers all industrial sites and is implemented in compliance
with the ISO 14001 standard.
Assessment guidelines for current and new installations
have been set. Site installations are assessed every three
years, with action plans and allocated resources depending
on the assessment results (See chapter 2.2.2.2.3).
Water pollution 
Renault Group intends to minimize the impact of its activities
on water quality through the application of the HSEE policy:
●Minimizing the environmental footprint of treated
wastewater discharges by:
– Optimizing the efficiency of water treatment facilities;
– Promoting less polluting industrial processes (such as
phosphating);
– Using wastewater as a resource as much as possible to
minimize the supply of external water (implementing
best practices for reuse and recycling);
– Aiming for excellence in water management at each site.
●Managing operational risks by:
– Reducing risks related to the capacity, safety, and
obsolescence of effluent treatment facilities;
– Maintaining
effluent
treatment
facilities
at
the
appropriate level;
– Monitoring discharges with appropriate monitoring
systems;
– Controlling and preventing accidental pollution risks.

The policy extends to all Renault Group industrial, logistic,
engineering and commercial sites, with the Leadership Team
bearing
ultimate
responsibility
for
its
execution,
in
compliance with the ISO 14001 standard. Alongside its water
policy, Renault Group has developed a methodological note
to help avoid and prevent incidents and emergencies. The
details of these documents are shared internally with
employees. [DP E2-1_01; DP E2-1_03; DP MDR-P_01; DP
MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_04]
Microplastics
The Group is preparing to comply with incoming regulation
addressing tire abrasion rate, by setting specifications to
reduce tire abrasion and limit microplastics from tire impact
on
the
environment.
Upcoming
regulations
will
be
applicable in Europe for the use-phase of vehicles. It will
apply to car manufacturers and tire manufacturers. The
Group is awaiting the application of the “tire abrasion rate“
standard to formalize policies with a focus on the upstream
value chain. [DP E2.MDR-P_07-08]
Substances of concern and substances
of very high concern
Renault Group’s substances policy aims to manage the use of
chemical products worldwide across its industrial, logistics
and tertiary sites, as well as the presence of regulated
hazardous substances in manufactured products. The policy
ensures
regulatory
compliance
concerning
regulated
substances and  anticipates requirements for not yet
regulated Substances of Very High Concern (SVHC) and
CMR1  (carcinogenic, mutagenic, reprotoxic substances) to
avoid any disruption of the supply chain related to restrictions
or prohibitions of these substances and to ensure the safe
use of products through appropriate management.
Renault Group has an organization dedicated to the
management of hazardous substances, which takes into
account regulations related to substances and its proactive
policies. The three teams dedicated to this activity support the
deployment of actions in their daily processes:
●Materials Engineering Division controls the use of
substances in vehicle design and anticipates the banning
of the most problematic substances several years before
they are banned by regulation, via Renault Group’s
standard GDN-1903-2024-0002. This division monitors the
substances contained in parts and vehicles based on
information declared by suppliers via IMDS (International
Material Data System), a system shared with 35
international vehicle manufacturers and equipment
manufacturers. It enables Renault Group to meet the
information and transparency requirements from REACH,
the European directive on waste, and the CSRD reporting
requirements. It guides the search for alternative
technical solutions for priority risk substances, in
collaboration with each engineering branch, and after-
sales. It defines and manages the compliance actions of
the entities concerned and anticipates the risks of
upstream supply chain disruptions. In addition to the
usual substances and proven risks, Renault Group pays
attention to the potential impacts of innovative materials;
●the
HSEE
Division,
together
with
the
Engineering
Department and sites, ensures that employees and the
environment are properly protected, in compliance with the
Health and Safety policy signed in 2021 by the CEO and the
Human Resources Director. In anticipation of regulation, this
policy guarantees that chemical products used in the Group
comply not only with European regulations (REACH, CLP,
etc.) but also with its own 00-10-050 standard, which
anticipates and bans substances that are highly hazardous
to health or the environment, such as SVHC, not yet
regulated SVHC, CMR1s or endocrine disruptors and those
specified in the GDN-1903-2024-0002 Renault Group internal
norms. Together with the Engineering Department and sites,
the HSEE team works to find suitable technical solutions to
eliminate or substitute the substance. If it is impossible, due
to a technical issue or a lack of alternative solutions on the
market, a semi-annual committee reviews requests for
exemptions, which are rigorously controlled to ensure the
protection of employees and the environment. Finally,
guidelines on Mandatory Rules Health (MR#Health) and
Mandatory Rules Environment (MR#E) have been defined
and implemented since 2022 to manage chemical products
and prevent any damage to the environment due to
accidental spillage; [DP MDR-A_02; DP E2.MDR-A_13-14]
●The After-sales Regulatory Compliance Division ensures that
after-sales suppliers comply with applicable regulations and
00-10-050 Renault Group standard, and, if necessary, replace
substances in parts, accessories and other chemicals sold. In
addition, Renault Group decided not to sell any CMR1
chemicals once an alternative solution is available. [DP E2-
1_02; DP MDR-1_01; DP MDR-1_02; DP MDR-1_04]
The implementation of substance-related policies is the
responsibility of the HSEE Department and the Materials
Engineering Department. [MDR-1_03]
Renault Group’s current policies are in line with European
regulations on substances, including SVHC and CMR1
substances. As soon as Substances of Concern (SOC) or part
of SOC are covered by any restriction of use, Renault Group
will expand the scope of its policy to include those
substances. [DP E2.MDR-P_07-08]

## Page 142

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.2.2.3 E2-2 – Actions and resources
related to pollution
Overall pollution 
Renault Group has undertaken a series of actions to reduce
pollution across its operations and value chain, covering
different pollution aspects, with a long-term commitment to
maintain and further develop these actions. [DP MDR-A_03]
Numerous action plans are being implemented in the Group to
ensure regulatory compliance with new regulations or
regulations that will be applied in the near future, in particular
to prevent any risk of penalties, reputational damage or
decontamination. These action plans are explained in the
Vade-Mecum, in particular:
●The VOC Roadmap (€18 million in investments between
2018 and 2024) aimed at bringing all the Group’s plants
into compliance with the new VOC regulation applicable
from 1/1/2025;
●the “Full Power Water“ action plan also aimed at
complying with the new regulations on water stress (and
going far beyond);
●the Group decarbonization plan;
●the Biodiversity Roadmap aimed at being compliant and
anticipating biodiversity regulations (and beyond);
●the
management
of
environmental
regulatory
compliance and the monitoring of draft texts.
Pollution-related initiatives are frequently integrated into
broader action plans, which often lack the necessary data
granularity to monitor the budget with the required details
for each specific pollution-related action.
Actions regarding the upstream value chain are described in
chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.F [E4.MDR-A_06]
Air pollution 
The Group focuses its actions on its own operations and
downstream value chain. Renault Group developed its air
pollution action plan around two pillars:
●Reduction of emissions from Volatile Organic Compounds
(VOC) on all Renault Group sites: practices implemented by
Renault Group to reduce the consumption of solvent paint
products, and the associated emissions involve: 
– Installing or replacing VOC-processing incineration
facilities where necessary; 
– Installing robotics to reduce quantities of products
used;
– Optimizing mastic and paint consumption to reduce
emissions at source (continued deployment initiated in
2021, for example by optimizing the quantity of solvent
used for dilution at the Douai site);
– Improving the recovery of used solvents (purging and
cleaning solvents) at all sites (e.g., installation of
automatic purge boxes at the Busan site in 2024);
– NOx reduction: on all its sites, NOx emissions are monitored,
mainly from combustion plants. Decarbonisation actions
(power reduction and removal of equipment) contribute to
reducing these NOx emissions, without a reduction target
being defined. Within this scope, efforts are focused on the
main pollutants that are VOCs.
●Reduction of air pollution for its vehicles: Renault Group
vehicles comply with local regulations on pollutant
emissions. Technologies that aim to significantly reduce
particulate matter and NOx emissions are implemented to
achieve these reductions. For example: NOx traps,
devices that capture nitrogen oxides from the gases of
lean
combustion
engines
and
selective
catalytic
reduction (SCR) systems that reduce NOx emissions by
injecting urea into the exhaust gases which reacts with
NOx on a catalyst to form nitrogen and water. Renault
Group, when designing its vehicles, takes into account the
problems of pollutants in the outdoor air used for heating
and air conditioning as well as pollutant emissions from
the interior materials to maintain a good air quality in the
cabin. [DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_04]
The main air pollution impact in the downstream value chain
is due to the vehicles use-phase. Renault Group has many
actions already launched or planned to significantly reduce
the pollution of its vehicles during the use-phase, especially
by developing hybrid and electric vehicles (See chapter
2.2.2.1.5). [DP E2-2_02]
Soil pollution 
The Group focuses it actions on its own current operations.
The Group’s voluntary approach related to soil pollution
prevention is applied across all sites with potential pollution
risks, and is constructed around two pillars:
●For new and existing facilities, the risk of soil pollution is
evaluated. An appropriate action plan is established for
facilities identified as being at risk, and Environment
Mandatory Rules (MR#E) are implemented;
●For new sites, a systemic environmental Due Diligence process
is implemented. [DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP E2-2_02]
Considering the preventive measures in place, Renault
Group’s current activities have not generated any soil
pollution in 2024. In the event of an accident generating
pollution,
local
authorities
are
informed,
and
their
requirements are implemented. [DP MDR-A_04]
Regarding mining and forestry:
●Since 2021, Renault Group has been developing strategic
partnerships with suppliers to secure the supply of certain
raw materials in the electric battery supply chain and build
a more sustainable and transparent battery value chain;

Sustainable development
02
●In 2024, Renault Group commissioned TRANSITIONS to
carry out an in-depth study entitled “Material/Country Risk
Mapping“. This study aims to assess and prioritize
environmental, social, human rights, health & safety and
governance risks for the extraction processes of 18
minerals and for 81 countries including the main mineral
extraction countries, as well as the countries of Renault
Group’s supplier base. The methodology of this risk
mapping is based on 14 main categories of risks, one of
which is soil pollution. This study guides the group’s actions
for risk management (See section of the URD 2.3.3.1);
●The Group is a member of the Responsible Minerals Initiative
(RMI), the Global Battery Alliance (GBA), Recharge (European
industrial association for advanced rechargeable and lithium
batteries), the Plateforme Automobile Française (PFA, which
brings together the automotive industry in France) and the
Global Platform for Sustainable Natural Rubber (GPSNR).
Renault Group also joins the multi-stakeholder Initiative for
Responsible Mining Assurance (IRMA), a multi-stakeholder
organization dedicated to promoting responsible mining
practices. This initiative establishes a responsible mine
standard and conducts mine assessments to assess their
compliance with this standard. This standard includes the
risks regarding soil pollution.
Water pollution 
Renault Group is committed to reducing the discharge in
industrial effluents across its sites. Renault Group’s
wastewater roadmap has set targets for 2030. This
comprehensive plan is built on two main pillars and applies
to Renault Group’s own operations: 
●Optimisation of wastewater treatment processes: the
enhanced deployment of good practices helps to reduce
residual pollution and adapt the efficiency of treatment,
combined with improved monitoring of consumption and
discharges. In the long term, these actions also promote
the recycling of effluents and the reduction of pollution.
Furthermore, oil pollutants are either collected for
external disposal or subjected to treatment through
filtration and evaporation to reuse more than 90% of the
water and reduce waste;
●Progressive implementation of technological changes:
the transition from conventional technology to nickel-free
technology is gradually being deployed in the painting
process by the teams at the Renault Group paint shops.
These actions are being implemented; their results are
expected to be available on a long-term basis. [DP MDR-A_01;
DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_04; DP E2-2_02]
Microplastics 
Currently, there are no existing thresholds for emissions of
microplastic particles from tires in Renault Group’s own
operations, such as microplastics from vehicles testing.
European regulations on tire abrasion rates are expected to
be clarified soon and will define tire degradation thresholds
(wear rates) and associated reduction roadmaps. Once the
regulation is released, Renault Group plans to integrate
these thresholds for abrasion rates and particle emissions
into its supplier selection criteria. This measure will promote
the integration of technologies designed to emit fewer
particles when using Renault Group vehicles.
Within its own operations, Renault Group actively quantifies
vehicle testing microplastics emissions by monitoring tires
abrasion rates in order to better understand the impact on
its downstream value chain prior to any action plan
implementation. [DP E2.MDR-A_13-14]
Substances of concern and substances of very high
concern 
Renault Group has implemented an action plan covering its
upstream value chain and own operations, as well as
downstream activities:
●Registration and documentation, in its data management
systems, of all substances which are either restricted by
regulation (REACH Annexes XIV and XVII) or by its own
decision (CMR1), or identified as SVHC, or subject to
reporting such as SOC;
●Ban of the use of SVHC and CMR1 in new projects within its
own operations to safeguard its workforce and substitute
them when alternative solutions are available;
●Minimization of the use of SVHC and CMR1 within its
upstream and downstream value chain by requiring its
suppliers to comply with both 00-10-050 and GDN-1903-2024-
0002 Renault Group standards (respectively for chemical
products and vehicles); and to declare the substances;
●In accordance with Decree no. 2022-748 of the French
Council of State, which pertains to consumer information
on the qualities and environmental characteristics of
waste-generating products, Renault Group publishes
information
on
the
regulated
substances
and
environmental characteristics of its vehicles on its
website (https://www.renaultgroup.com/reglementation-
reach/). Similar information is declared in the SCIP
database (“Substances of Concern In articles as such or
in complex objects (Products)” database, established by
the European Chemicals Agency) for information on
substances of concern in articles established under the
Waste Framework Directive (WFD);
●Regular audits on Renault Group’s industrial, logistics and
after-sales sites on Health and Environment Mandatory Rules.
[DP E2-2_02; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_04]
Renault Group actions constantly evolve in accordance with
the pace of regulation changes (up to two yearly updates for
REACH and at least once a year for CLP). [DP MDR-A_05]
Renault Group’s investment is a continuous process, in
compliance with the evolution of legislation, whether
existing or to come. [DP MDR-A_06]

2.2.2.2.4 E2-3 – Targets related to pollution
Overall pollution 
To define air, water, and substances targets, the Group
adopted a mixed approach, by setting a high level of
ambition at Group level and analysing information and
possibilities from sites to refine and adjust these targets.
This approach considers continuous site improvement, local
and global environmental constraints and challenges,
regulatory developments, planned production and projects,
technical progress, and allocated resources for each site.
When setting targets, Renault Group considers legal
authorities’ position. Following a scientific approach,
Renault Group sets its environmental targets through
physical measures based on recognized standards and
follows a continuous improvement process as requested by
ISO 14001. [DP MDR-T_09; DP MDR-T_10; DP MDR-T_11]
The targets are adapted based on the pollutants and their
potential environmental and health impacts, within a risk-
based approach, in order to achieve the objectives of the
defined policies. [DP MDR-T_01]
Performance against air and water pollution targets is
monitored monthly throughout the ESG dashboard and
reviewed quarterly by the Leadership Team and the
Strategy and Sustainability Committee. Any deviations or
trends are analysed, and action plans are followed up.
Controls are conducted by the site’s environmental
manager with the support of the HSEE department
specialists, with reinforced control at the end of the year
during an audit conducted by a third party. The targets
regarding the upstream value chain are described in
chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.F. [DP MDR-T_13]
As 2024 marks the first year of compliance with the CSRD,
Renault Group has reviewed its scope, methodology, and
other
aspects
to
ensure
alignment
with
the
new
requirements introduced by the directive. [DP MDR-T_12]

Air pollution 
Renault Group sets its targets considering the specific load
and impact of the emitted pollutants, to ensure efficient
prevention and control. As such, a target has been set for
VOCs, which are the main pollutants linked to the car
manufacturing process. The target related to air pollution is
voluntary and adapted to sites. [DP E2-3_09]

2.2.2.2.5 E2-4 – Pollution of air, water, and soil
Renault Group has implemented a comprehensive protocol
for environmental reporting within its Environmental and
Energy Reporting (REE) tools to monitor the environmental
footprint of its sites and of the Group as a whole. The Group
reporting protocols are applied to all own operations and
entities under the operational control of Renault Group and
detail the measurement methodologies and processes for
collecting data related to pollution. These methods account
for changes over time, primarily due to the geographical
evolution of the scope (addition or removal of sites) and the
inclusion of emissions previously unaccounted, such as CO2
from VOC incineration. Methodological changes are
infrequent, except for this first year of CSRD (for instance.
addition of new definition for water consumption), barring
updates emission factors. However, the methodologies
themselves remain consistent. [DP E2-4_08; DP E2-4_09; DP
E2-4_10]

Renault Group discloses emissions by pollutant to air from
its own operations, in accordance with the pollutants listed
in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 166/2006 of the European
Parliament,

disclosed

chapter 2.2.2.1.

2.2.2.2.6 E2-5 – Substances of concern and
substances of very high concern
Substances of concern
The definition of substances of concern, given in Annex II of
the CSRD, covers several thousand molecules. Neither
Renault Group’s vehicle bill of materials nor the automotive
Industry’s substance databases (IMDS) allow for easy
extraction of the data required by the E2-5 requirement.
Renault Group’s teams have first identified these substances
on several sub-scopes deemed representative - the Renault
Megane, Renault Scenic and Dacia Duster models for the
substances present in the products, and the Douai site for the
substances used in the manufacturing processes - then the
quantities used of each of these substances were evaluated
according to different methods; finally, an extrapolation was
carried out to estimate the total quantities used and put into
circulation over the year 2024.
These analyses, carried out over several months, have
identified that 90% of the total annual mass of these
substances is made up of:
●copper, present in electrical cables and brass parts,
●zinc, which is also present in brass parts and on the
surface of galvanised sheets, and
●petroleum distillates, used for the lubrication of moving
parts.
However, the results were inconclusive for the other
substances of concern, due to too wide a dispersion of the
values obtained.
A working group will be set up in 2025 to establish a more
precise method, based on the lessons learned from this
first year.
144

2.2.2.2.8 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its environmental metrics based on
robust methods. They are subject to internal controls and
verifications, without being systematically validated by an
external body.
E2-4 - Pollution of air, water and soil
All metrics listed below are expressed in tons, if not
otherwise specified. No null indicator has been listed below,
as this means no Renault Group site has reported any
associated quantity.
The volume of pollutants emitted is documented by each
site based its regulatory measurements and consolidated at
Group level.
Sites with a very small headcount, the impact of which is
therefore negligible  (mainly tertiary sites), are excluded
from the application scope. [DP E2-4_09]
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02; DP E2-4_10; DP E2-4_15]
Air pollution
The atmospheric emissions of SO2 and NOx counted represent the emissions from the
combustion of fossil energies in fixed combustion installations at all sites, excluding
on-site transport.
SO2 and NOx emissions generated by engine tests are excluded from the reporting scope.
This exclusion is due to the insignificance of such pollutant emissions.
Emission factors used in the calculations of SO2 and NOx are taken from the CITEPA’s
OMINEA National Inventory Report (updated in July 2024)
Emission factors from fixed combustion installations of low- NOx natural gas burners
were calculated in 2011, based on an internal study of 88% of sites within the scope having
low-NOx burners. The factor thus obtained (0.0266kg/MWh LHV) is the average of the
factors obtained at each site, weighted by the power of the installations.
The atmospheric discharges of Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) included in the data
represent the emissions produced when bodywork and bumpers are painted (bodywork-
assembly plants and Renault Retail Group (RRG) sales network). The application of paint
on parts and accessories is not taken into account.
The VOC emissions are calculated by mass balance between the solvents that enter into the
painting process and those solvents that are reused or destroyed during the painting process.
For RRG sales network sites where calculation is not possible, estimates are made on the
basis of emissions from other network sites (ratio per hours billed for bodywork).
Water pollution
Data on pollutant flows are based on measurements of effluents after they have been
treated in the Group’s plants and/ or before they are discharged to the outside. Discharges
from some plants may subsequently be treated in municipal treatment plants.
Discharge analysis frequency is at minimum compliant with local regulations.
The data presented only take into account the discharges for which concentration and
flow must be measured by law, and the discharges from the plants in Bursa (Türkiye),
Curitiba (Brazil), Santa Isabel de Córdoba (Argentina) and Casablanca (Morocco), for
which voluntary controls are taken into account in light of the significant contribution of
these discharges to the Group’s impacts
The Santa Isabel de Córdoba, Curitiba, Casablanca (Somaca), and Batilly (Sovab) sites
are subject to specific regulatory requirements and receive an exemption on the
calculation methodology of specific flows.
Domestic water discharges, for which there is no regulatory measurement and/or
reporting obligation, are not included in the water discharges.
Soil pollution
Renault Group’s current operations do not introduce any substances into the soil.
To prevent soil pollution, all new and existing facilities are assessed every 3 years,
and an appropriate action plan is established for facilities identified as being at risk.
146
