02
E2-5 – Substances of very high concern
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Total amount of substances of very high concern
(SVHC) in vehicles and aftermarket products
released in 2024
Renault Group reports specifically the following information relevant to its operations:
●“Substances of very high concern procured / used” corresponding to the chemical
products used in the Group’s industrial facilities to manufacture vehicles;
●“Substances of very high concern that leave facilities as product” corresponding to the
substances present in the vehicles manufactured as well as the parts sold in after-sales;
●“Substances of very high concern that leave facilities as service” corresponding to the
chemical products sold by the after-sales.
As part of its activities, Renault Group does not generate substances. Indeed, the Group
uses chemicals in its industrial activities but does not manufacture any. In addition,
the emissions of substances of very high concern that may be released by
Renault Group sites are captured through the impacts, risks, and opportunities
outlined in chapter 2.2.2.2. Refer to the metrics in chapter 2.2.2.2.5. to obtain the values.
Substances of very high concern procured / used:
To determine the volume of substances procured and used by Renault Group, an analysis
of all products for which substances were declared under Article 57 of the REACH
regulation, present and used in two sites, is conducted. The first site selected is Douai, a
bodywork assembly plant manufacturing electric vehicle. The second site is Cléon, a
Castings & Mechanical plant manufacturing engines and gearboxes mostly for electric
vehicles. Since the substances are predominantly present in the batteries of the vehicles,
the analysis is deliberately conducted on two sites that contribute to the manufacturing
of electric vehicles, which are considered more impactful.
To estimate the substances procured across Renault Group, the following extrapolation
rules are established:
●For industrial activities (bodywork and assembly plants and Castings & Mechanical plants),
the ratio of the quantity of substances purchased by Douai and Cléon to the quantity of cars
manufactured by Douai is applied to the total number of vehicles produced by the group.
The number of manufactured cars is consistent with the figures used in chapter 2.2.2.2.5;
●For Research & Development activities, the volume of substances is not significant with
regard to manufacturing and is excluded from the perimeter.
To improve the reliability and accuracy of the communicated data, Renault Group has
initiated an overhaul of its chemical management system, which will be implemented by
the end of 2025 and will enable the enlargement of the perimeter of the sites covered.
The volumes of chemical products purchased are based on actual data as of the end of
November, and the annual quantities of substances are extrapolated on this basis.
Substances of very high concern that leave facilities as product
To determine the quantities of substances that leave Renault Group’s sites through the
produced vehicles, a detailed analysis of the composition of an electric vehicle and a
thermal vehicle is carried out. For each vehicle, the quantity of substances is determined
using its technical definition, which provides a detailed breakdown of the parts that
compose it. The quantities of substances contained in each part are then identified
based on the “Material Data Sheets“ (MdS). This analysis allows for determining a
minimum and maximum quantity of substance contained in these vehicles. To
extrapolate the results to the entire scope, the interval values are multiplied by the
number of vehicles manufactured during the reporting period.
The same analysis is carried out on 80% of after-sales volumes, corresponding to the
minimum values of the intervals. Maximum values are extrapolated to the entire
perimeter by proportionality.
To improve the reliability and accuracy of the communicated data, Renault Group is
working on software developments to automate data collection. This will allow Renault
Group to consider more vehicles to adjust the accuracy and reduce the range of the values.
Substances of very high concern that leave facilities as service
To determine the volume of substances that leave the facilities as services, an analysis of
chemical products sold by the after-sales division has been conducted (e.g., windshield washer
fluid, lubricant, polish, etc.). The list of products for which at least one substance of very high
concern is declared under Article 57 of the REACH regulation is established. Based on this
information and the quantities sold, the amounts of substances have been determined.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

To estimate the substances that leave the facilities as service, an extrapolation rule have
been established: application of a rule of three based on the percentage of Corporate
business to total Aftersales business.
The volumes of chemical products purchased are based on actual data as of the end of
November, and the annual quantities of substances are extrapolated on this basis.
E2-6 - Anticipated financial effects from pollution-related impacts, risks and opportunities
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Operating expenditures (OpEx) in conjunction with
major incidents and deposits (pollution)
A major environmental incident is defined as an event resulting from uncontrolled
developments of major importance, such as the release of hazardous substances or a fire,
occurring during the operation of a Renault Group site and causing serious immediate or
delayed danger to human health or the environment either inside or outside the site.
In the case of a pollution incident, a list of events and indicative thresholds to qualify
them as major environmental incidents has been established. However, this list is
indicative and not exhaustive. The final decision to qualify an event as “major“ is made on
a case-by-case basis by the site’s Environmental Manager and the relevant corporate
experts, depending on the specific conditions of the event and the site in question.
Capital expenditures (CapEx) in conjunction with
major incidents and deposits (pollution)

2.2.2.3.1 IRO-1 – Description of the processes
for identifying and scoring impacts,
risks and opportunities
Renault Group has analysed the sites’ annual water
consumption data, adhering to methodologies consistent with
published water indicators, to identify impacts, risks, and
opportunities concerning water and marine resources. Water-
intensive manufacturing activities represent the largest share
of Renault Group’s water consumption (See chapter 2.2.2.3.4).
The Aqueduct tool is used to identify areas at water risk and
high-water
stress
where
Renault
Group
operates.
Additionally,
the
Group
considered
its
value
chain,
particularly through its Code of Conduct, updated in 2024,
whereby suppliers shall establish and operate a company-
wide environmental management scheme to continuously
evaluate, prevent and mitigate environmental impacts.
Acknowledging the water-related risks previously identified
in risk analyses, Renault Group has incorporated these
considerations
into
its
operational
and
cost
risk
management. [DP- E3.IRO-1_01]
In parallel, Renault Group evaluates impacts associated with
water and marine resources through life cycle assessments
(LCAs) conducted on its products and services (See chapter
2.2.2.1.3 for more details on LCAs). [DP- E3.IRO-1_01]
In most countries where Renault Group operates, impact
studies are subject to public consultation and opinion
gathering.
Consultations,
particularly
with
affected
communities, have been conducted for all major impacting
projects or modifications whenever requested by law. For
example, all the impacting projects in France have to go
through a public inquiry process where affected and local
communities can express their opinions. Impacts and risks
on stakeholders’ interests are taken into account through
an Environmental Management System that is implemented
across all sites. Prevention and reduction of water pollution-
related impacts, risks and opportunities are disclosed in
chapter 2.2.2. [DP- E3.IRO-1_02]

02
2.2.2.3.2 E3-1 – Policies related to water
and marine resources 
Water resources are one of the five fields of action of
Renault Group’s environmental policy. For more information
on this policy, see the chapter 2.2.2.1 on climate change
mitigation and adaptation policies.
This policy has been translated into a Health, Safety,
Environment and Ergonomics (HSEE) policy applicable to all
the Group’s industrial sites (See chapter 2.2.2.2.2).
From the HSEE commitment, Renault Group developed and
implemented, at the end of 2023, a dedicated water
management policy called “Full Power Water”, which is applied
across all Renault Group’s industrial sites (car assembly plants,
mechanical plants and foundries). For all other Renault Group’s
material sites, the Group’s global policy is applied. Renault Group
implemented the “Full Power Water” policy to better manage
water resources and risks, its sourcing and treatment, anticipate
water scarcity as a consequence of climate change and future
water restrictions, and reduce business risk and environmental
footprint of Renault Group’s operations. The overarching goal is
to eliminate the withdrawal of water from natural resources for
industrial use, excluding rainwater. Priority is given to sites
situated in regions facing water risks, such as areas
experiencing water scarcity or subject to existing or emerging
regulations on water-related resources and the implementation
of water policy measures. [DP E3-1_01; DP E3-1_02; DP E3-1_03;
DP E3-1_04; DP- E3-1_06; DP MDR-P_01]
Main axes of this policy are:
●Designing
new
facilities
following
best
water
management practices;
●Monitoring water consumption: real-time accounting of all
water quantities entering sites using connected meters and
equipping the most water-consuming internal processes
with connected water meters;
●Improving water consumption: minimizing the need for
water and reaching the level of excellence in water
management;
●Recycling water: implementing water recycling loops for
the remaining water consumption;
●Mitigating water stress risk: assessing water restriction risks,
implementing emergency countermeasures and monitoring
the water resource status in real time for each site at risk.
[DP E3-1_01; DP E3-1_05; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_02]
2.2.2.3.3 E3-2 – Actions and resources
related to water
and marine resources
Renault Group has taken various actions to manage the
impact on water and marine resources with a long-term
commitment to maintain and further develop these
initiatives. [DP MDR-A_03]
Renault Group’s “Full Power Water“ policy is designed to
address risks associated with water scarcity, account for
climate change, and aims to reduce the environmental
footprint of the company. The main pillars of the roadmap at
2027 horizon are:
●Designing
new
facilities
following
best
water
management practices. The key action in 2024-2025 is to
work with the Industrial Strategy and Corporate
Operations Division to harmonize the list of best practices
and to refer to them in specifications for new facilities.
Follow-up meetings to assess the effectiveness and
progress, along with a quarterly committee, are regularly
organized with the Industrial Strategy and Corporate
Operations Division to harmonize best practices and
share common topics;
●Monitoring water consumption during the industrial
process: achieving 100% connection and monitoring of
external water supply in real time by the end of 2025,
improving the internal water consumption monitoring
process by deploying water-meters, detecting leaks, and
repairing them. The key actions for this pillar include
identifying each site’s needs in 2024 (through a
questionnaire and direct exchanges) about water-meters
to monitor accurately consumption and discharges at
the site level (step 1), then at the workshop level (step 2),
and finally the connection of existing and new meters to
the “Looker“ monitoring platform to obtain real time data
(to date 78% of industrial sites have water meters
connected to “Looker” and 88% of the water supply is
monitored in real time);
●Improving water consumption efficiency: optimization of
water consumption by minimizing the need for water and
reaching a level of excellence in water management.
A water pilot was appointed on each industrial site in 2024.
Monthly committees have been held on reducing water
consumption of the industrial sites. Each site shares its
results from the past month, along with its action plan and
progress. The best practices were identified and listed at
each stage of the process in 2024 and made available in the
“PEMLA WATER” tool which is currently under development.
Campaigns have been launched to search for water leaks in
the networks. In 2024, major leaks were found and repaired
on Douai and Flins sites, for example. An inventory of the
obsolescence
of
water
treatment
and
production
equipment at the sites was established in 2024, prioritizing
the
risks
to
production,
environment
and
safety.
A replacement plan will be defined from 2025, taking this
prioritization into account. Finally, water consumption
targets are set and monitored taking in account on-site
improvement opportunities and actions described above
(See chapter 2.2.1.1.4);
●Recycling water: implementation of water recycling loops
for the remaining water consumption. The key actions for
this pillar includes the launch of technical and economic
studies to assess the possibilities of recycling wastewater
on sites in 2024 (launched for Sandouville, Casablanca and
Tanger plants this year) and the establishment of a
roadmap by 2030 to recycle water on sites that
demonstrate good water management according to
“PEMLA WATER“ quotations. The budgets for this roadmap
will be decided as soon as the studies are completed;
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
149

## Page 152

Renault Group
Sustainable development
●Mitigating water stress risks: the assessment of water
restriction risks at each plant and the real-time monitoring
of water resource status for each at-risk site are
conducted annually. At the same time, site consumption is
monitored in real time. For this pillar, four actions have
been identified. Firstly, the risk of water restriction at each
plant of industrial sites is being assessed in 2024, based on
geographic data on water stress and current or emerging
regulations
on
this
topic.
Secondly,
emergency
countermeasures plans are defined, and for the sites most
at risk, depending on the situation, an emergency plan is
established to reduce water consumption to varying
degrees depending on the level of urgency. Thirdly, the
state of external water resources is regularly monitored to
anticipate drought episodes. The frequency of this
monitoring depending on water stress situation put in
place this year. Finally, alerts are sent to the site directors
according to the situations, with three alerts sent in 2024.
[DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_06; DP MDR-
A_07; DP MDR-A_08; DP MDR-A_09; DP MDR-A_10;
DP MDR-A_11; DP MDR-A_12; DP E3-2_03]
This action plan encompasses all industrial sites and
prioritizes those located in regions at risk of water
shortages, including areas with high levels of water stress. It
takes into account current or upcoming regulations for
implementation and resource allocation. [DP MDR-A_02]
As the studies are ongoing, their conclusions are not
available for all Group sites yet, but water treatment and
production facilities renewal plans, and wastewater-
recycling and water stress reduction actions will require
significant resources from the Group over the coming years.
Product life cycle assessments have shown that the
upstream value chain may have a material impact on water
stress.
Value
chain
mapping
and
analysis
of
the
sustainability reports of key suppliers are planned to identify
priority areas for action in the upstream value chain and
define policies and action plans in relation to them.
[DP MDR-A_06]

[DP MDR-T_02; DP MDR-T_03; DP MDR-T_04; DP MDR-T_05; DP MDR-T_06; DP MDR-T_07; DP MDR-T_08]
With this target of reducing the water externally supplied
per vehicle, the Group aims to reduce its total water
consumption. The external water supply per vehicle
produced target is linked to the water policy set up by
Renault Group, as performance of the target being defined
by the “Full Power Water“ policy (See chapter 2.2.2.3.2).
[DP MDR-T_01; DP- E3-3_03]
This target on water consumption is voluntary, based on the
Group’s water policy objectives, and aims at minimizing
Renault Group’s water footprint, particularly in areas at
water risk. The target on water discharges in water bodies
and their link to the Group’s policy is addressed within
chapter 2.2.2.2.4. [DP E3-3_0; DP E3-3_08]
To determine its water consumption target, the Group
adopted a mixed approach by setting a high level of
ambition at Group level and analyzing possibilities from
sites to breakdown and adjust this target. This approach
considers continuous site improvement, local and global
environmental constraints and challenges, regulatory
developments, planned production and projects, technical
advancements and allocated resources for each site. The
definition of targets is based on recommendations from
external
stakeholders,
such
as
water
agencies.
Prescriptions from prefectural decrees are considered
during the implementation of the target and their
adaptation
to
sites
by
going
beyond
regulatory
specifications. Following a scientific approach, Renault
Group set its water consumption targets through physical
measures based on recognized standards. [DP MDR-T_09;
DP MDR-T_10; DP MDR-T_11]
The monitoring of performance is reviewed monthly by the
sites
of
the
Group.
Since
its
implementation,
the
methodologies related to the water consumption reduction
target have not fundamentally changed. The water
consumption reduction target is recalculated each year to
consider the changes in the Group’s scope of consolidation. In
May 2024, the loss of control of the so-called HORSE entities,
sold to HORSE Powertrain Limited, resulted in an estimated
reduction in consumption of 0.25 m3/vehicle, and the
exclusion of four additional sites, which have been sold or
merged with other sites (Fonderie de Bretagne, Choisy-le-Roi,
ACI Villeurbanne, Moscow), representing 0.18 m³/vehicle. Any
deviations or trends are analysed, and action plans are
followed up on. Controls are conducted by the environmental
manager of the site and at the corporate level, with
reinforced control at the end of the year during the audit
conducted by a third party. [DP MDR-T_12; DP MDR-T_13]

In accordance with DR E3-4, the water intensity ratio is

2.2.2.3.6 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its environmental metrics through
physical measures based on recognized standards. They
are subject to internal controls and verifications, without
being systematically validated by an external body.
E3-4 – Water consumption
All metrics listed below are expressed in cubic meters, if not
specified. All indicators with a value of zero have not been
listed below, as this indicates that no Renault Group site has
reported any associated quantity.
Sites with a very small headcount, the impact of which is
therefore negligible  (mainly tertiary sites), are excluded
from the application scope.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Total water consumption
To determine the quantity of water consumed, two distinct approaches were adopted
depending on technological facilities available on the sites:
●For sites with rejection measures (representing 39% of the sites in the perimeter),
the actual quantities of external water supplies, recycled rainwater, and discharges
are used1);
●For sites without rejection measures (representing 61% of the sites in the perimeter),
water consumption is estimated as a percentage of the quantities of external water
supply based on the type of site’s activity (tertiary/after-sales/industrial)2).
External water supply includes water obtained by pumping (underground and surface
water) and/or external networks (drinking water, industrial water, etc.).
Total water consumption in areas at water risk,
including areas of high-water stress
The aqueduct water risk atlas tool of the World Resources Institute (WRI) has been
chosen to identify sites that are part of areas exposed to water risks and/or areas subject
to high water stress.
Sites in areas at water risk are those with an “overall water risk” score between
moderately high and extremely high (with the default weighting profile).
Sites in areas of high-water stress are those with a “water depletion” score between High
and Extremely high.
Total water recycled and reused
The reported quantities correspond to the on-site measurements of industrial and
domestic wastewater recycled on sites. Recycled waters may or may not have
undergone treatment.
(1) The estimates of the purges from the Cooling Towers (CT) and the effluents/concentrates from the production of demineralized water are deducted in the calculation of
water consumption if they are not sent to the discharges considered in the calculation.
(2) The estimates of evaporation from the CT and the watering of green spaces are added where applicable.

2.2.2.4.1 IRO 1 – Description of processes to
identify and assess material
biodiversity and ecosystem-related
Impacts, Risks, dependencies and
Opportunities
2.2.2.4.1.1 Dependencies identification
Renault Group’s automotive activities rely 100% directly and
indirectly on biodiversity and ecosystem services provided
by nature, especially on water provisioning and climate
regulation.
The dependencies of Renault Group to biodiversity and
ecosystems have been assessed at Group level and covers
both Renault Group’s own operations and upstream value
chain. The analyses used the “ENCORE” tool, which
highlights how companies depend on nature according to
their economic sector3.
The assessment of Renault Group’s own operations is based
on the automotive sector general assessment. For the
upstream value chain, the analysis focuses on the mining
industry sector, which represents the upstream link in the
supply chain for automobile manufacturers. The “ENCORE”
tool
does
not
include
downstream
dependencies.
Dependency levels have been rated on a scale from very low
to very high. Among the eleven dependencies assessed, the
most significant dependencies for the Renault Group’s
business model are in the upstream value chain, specifically
on
ground
and
surface
water
supply,
water
flow
maintenance, and climate regulation. No dependency was
rated as very high. [DP E4.IRO-1_02]
2.2.2.4.1.2 Impacts identification
A. Impacts at Group level
Renault Group has identified and assessed impacts related
to biodiversity and ecosystems. The analyses of Renault
Group’s biodiversity impacts were based on the Corporate
Biodiversity Footprint methodology (CBF), which uses the
metric of the Mean Species Abundance (MSA) to evaluate
ecosystems’ ecological integrity in relation to its pristine
state. By combining MSA metric with surface and time units
(km2.MSA.yr), it is possible to quantify the impact on
biodiversity in terms of the loss of mean species abundance
over specific areas and periods. The results enable
comparisons of Renault Group’s biodiversity impacts from
various loss drivers4 and across different ecosystems5,
helping to prioritize the issues.
According to the Biodiversity Footprint 2023 results, the
most material impacts for Renault Group’s business model
(current and potential) are as follows:
●Distribution by pressure: the major pressures are climate
change (>50% of total impacts), followed by pollution and
land-use change (>20% each);
●Distribution by phases: the major phases are the use
phase of products (>50% of total impacts, mainly through
GHG emissions), followed by upstream value chain (>20%,
mainly through pollution). Renault Group’s direct own
operations impacts are insignificant (< 1%);
Renault
Group
recognizes
its
contribution
to
the
Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity
and Ecosystem Services (IPBES)2 biodiversity pressure factors
at varying levels depending on the pressure factors and
phases. [DP E4.IRO-1_01]
3 ENCORE platform: https://www.encorenature.org/en
4 Biodiversity loss driver according to IPBES are land and sea use changes, natural resource use and exploitation, climate change, pollution, invasive alien species.
The impact of mineral exploitation is taken into account in land use change and pollution.
5 Aquatic and terrestrial, excluding the marine environment.

B. Impacts at operational sites level
Renault Group sites have an environmental management
system in place to limit pressures on biodiversity and
ecosystems. For sites with potential major environmental
impacts, the environmental management system complies
with the requirements of the ISO 14001 standard, with annual
audits leading to the certification of these sites.
Each site, when obtaining its administrative authorization,
provides an impact study to the authorities, leading to the
drafting of prescriptions consistent with the identified
impacts
and
the
sensitivity
of
the
surrounding
environments. Today, the Group’s sites comply with the
defined discharge thresholds, and the Group ensures the
monitoring
of
pressure
factors
on
biodiversity.
Consequently, it is considered that the impacts on
biodiversity are limited. [DP E4.IRO-1_01]
Renault Group decided to deepen its knowledge of
biodiversity
issues
thanks
to
on-site
biodiversity
assessments. 37 on-site biodiversity assessments have
already been completed since 2021. The assessments have
led to the identification of the main ecological sensitivities
of each site and relevant actions to encourage biodiversity.
The biodiversity stakes are categorized into three levels
(high, moderate, and low) established by external experts
using scientific references and factors such as the presence
of protected species, habitats, or wetlands identified during
site visits. [DP E4.IRO-1_01]
The table below is listing material sites and its level of
ecological sensitivity. The material sites are the production,
logistics, engineering, and tertiary sites of Renault Group.
They are considered as ’material’ due to their potential
impacts and the green areas available on these sites.

The most impactful sites on biodiversity are those with
industrial activities (vehicle assembly, foundries and
powertrain) and the main engineering and logistics sites,
considering their size and activities. For other sites, the
impact on biodiversity is more limited. [DP E4.SBM-3_03]
All sites rely on ecosystem services. Manufacturing sites are
the most dependent on these services, primarily for their
water supply needs. [DP E4.SBM-3_03]
In addition to identifying the biodiversity stakes of the sites,
a survey of sensitive areas located within 2.5 km of the sites
was conducted6.
The survey of the sites located near biodiversity-sensitive
areas focused on the material sites listed above. All other
sites (garages, dealerships and tertiary sites) are considered
to have a low impact on biodiversity and were therefore
excluded from this analysis. The impacts of the new sites
considered as material within the scope of Renault Group
(for example, the dismantling sites in 2024), will be analysed
in the coming years. Sensitive areas summary table below:

Due to their proximity, Renault Group has a potential impact
on these areas. Nevertheless, Renault Group considers that
this impact does not constitute a contribution to the
destruction of natural habitats or the exploitation of
species, whether protected or not, except potential light
disturbances that may occasionally disrupt species near
Renault Group sites. [E4.IRO-1_15; DP E4.SBM-3_02]
For every new industrial project, Renault Group conducts an
impact analysis to determine whether the project affects
negatively
biodiversity,
including
areas
sensitive
biodiversity. These studies have not concluded that there is
a need to implement mitigation measures. [DP E4.IRO-1_16]
Although the construction of Renault Group sites has
inevitably led to soil sealing and, in most cases, a change in
land use, they have been carried out in compliance with
local urbanization rules and applicable regulations. Since
2023, in the absence of local regulation, biodiversity has
been voluntarily considered in project development, and the
“avoid, reduce, offset” approach has been implemented.
[DP E4.SBM-3_05]
No negative impact on any threatened species has been
identified as a result of Renault Group’s operations on its
sites. [DP E4.SBM-3_06]
2.2.2.4.1.3 Risks identification
Gross risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems have
been identified and assessed based on Renault Group’s net
risk assessment method, which has been adapted to align
with the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures’
(TNFD) LEAP approach7. Each risk is quantified based on two
criteria: magnitude (ranging from minor to major) and
likelihood (ranging from very unlikely to very likely). A total of
21 risks have been assessed, covering the entire Renault
Group’s value chain. Qualitative risks are assessed for the
years 2023 to 2027, quantitative risks are assessed for 2024
and 2030.
6 Renault Group does not have sites located within sensitive areas (Natura 2000 or Key Biodiversity Areas).
7 LEAP approach https://tnfd.global/publication/additional-guidance-on-assessment-of-nature-related-issues-the-leap-approach/

According to the TNFD biodiversity criticality matrix, the
major biodiversity-related gross risks for the Renault
Group’s business model are as follows:
●Significant
biodiversity-related
risks
consist
of
remediation costs, penalties and reputation damage due
to non-compliance of the Groupe with new regulations on
air pollution, tighter GHG emissions standards for cars,
and new end-of-life regulations, especially battery
recycling requirements;
●Major biodiversity-related risks are mainly reputational
risks linked to mineral extraction impacts on biodiversity,
ecosystem and affecting local population and indigenous
communities (LPIC), and residual deforestation risks;
●Water-related risks, such as usage restrictions or
shortages, are significant regulatory requirements or
threats that are likely to arise. [DP E4.IRO-1_03]
The risk assessment extends up to the 2030-time horizon,
and
at
this
horizon,
no
material
biodiversity-
and
ecosystems-related systemic risks for Renault Group have
been identified. [DP E4.IRO-1_04]
2.2.2.4.1.4 Affected communities’ identification
and consultations
A. At the Group level
A Group-level analysis has identified affected communities
related to biodiversity and ecosystems topics. The analysis
on potential negative impacts on these communities was
based on a combination of internal and external sources of
knowledge and analysis, including:
●Collective
initiatives
measuring
CSR
risks
(ERMA
referential, RECORD researches);
●Available reports and studies (NGO, collective initiatives);
●The purchase of generic risk mapping and monitoring;
●The development of a risk assessment map for each
country and material, which evaluates and categorizes
environment, health & safety, human rights and governance
risks for 18 critical materials, based on the country;
●Vehicles life cycle assessment to prioritize key materials
(See chapter 2.2.2.1.3). [DP E4.IRO-1_05]
Renault Group maintains an ongoing dialogue with NGOs
addressing
CSR
and
resources
issues,
particularly
concerning
the
sourcing
of
minerals
and
metals.
Consultations of potentially affected communities are
conducted by NGOs, which present local community impact
reports. Those are considered by Renault Group and by the
actors of the value chain. [DP E4.IRO-1_05; DP E4.IRO-1_07]
Based on a combination of internal and external analysis,
the potential negative impacts on affected communities for
the Renault Group’s business model are in mining extraction
areas. [DP E4.IRO-1_06]
B. At the operational sites level
At operational sites level, consultations, particularly with
affected communities, have been conducted for all major
impacting projects or modifications whenever required by
law. For example, in the European Union, all impactful
projects must undergo a public inquiry process, allowing
affected and local communities to express their opinion.
[DP E4.IRO-1_08; DP E4.IRO-1_05; DP E4.IRO-1_07]
2.2.2.4.2 E4-1 – Transition plan and
consideration of biodiversity
and ecosystems in strategy
and business model
The resilience of the current business model and strategy to
biodiversity-related systemic risks and opportunities has
not been assessed yet. [DP E4.1_01, E4.1_02; E4.1_03;
E4.1_04; E4.1_05, E4.1_06]
2.2.2.4.3 E4-2 – Policies related to
biodiversity and ecosystems
2.2.2.4.3.1 Renault Group environmental policy
The preservation of biodiversity and ecosystems is one of the
five fields of action of Renault Group’s environmental policy.
For more information on this policy, see the chapter 2.2.2.1 on
climate change mitigation and adaptation policies. This
environmental policy covers all the impacts, dependencies,
risks and opportunities related to Renault Group’s biodiversity
and ecosystems. [DP E4-2_02; DP E4-2_03]
This policy does not address social consequences of
biodiversity and ecosystems-related impacts. [DP E4-2_06]
Renault Group Environmental policy has been defined in line
with the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) of COP15, the
European Biodiversity Strategy and the French National
Biodiversity Strategy for French sites, as the policy involves
monitoring, assessing, and transparently disclosing their risks,
dependencies and impacts on biodiversity through their
operations, value chains and portfolios, as well as implementing
actions to reduce their negative impacts on biodiversity and
ecosystems. In 2018, Renault Group has joined the Act4nature
initiative to contribute to the protection, enhancement, and
restoration of biodiversity8. [DP E4.MDR-P_04]
Especially, the biodiversity-related topics in the value chain
are covered by the implementation by suppliers of Renault
Group Green Procurement and CSR Guidelines for Suppliers
(See chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1). These guidelines require suppliers to
evaluate dependencies on services provided by nature and
impacts on ecosystems to set priorities, to adopt a “measure,
avoid, reduce, restore“ approach, to evaluate the quality of
ecosystems in biodiversity-sensitive areas on or near sites
and the risk of ecological quality modification, to avoid
changes in land use (artificialization) and overexploitation of
land (deforestation), to plan a trajectory in line with the
Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework Goals and
to set targets for 2030 and 2050. [DP E4.MDR-P_01-06]
8 Renault Group’s individual commitments to act4nature are available here: https://www.act4nature.com/bilans-engagements-entreprises/

of
products,

[DP E4-2_04]
Renault Group’s environmental policy is implemented on its
sites through its HSEE policy to monitor and minimize the
impact of the sites activities on biodiversity and promote
restoration
of

(See
chapter
2.2.2.2.2).
[DP E4.MDR-P_01-06]
This policy applies to all operational sites, including those
located near sensitive areas. [DP E4-2_17]
2.2.2.4.3.2 Policy addressing deforestation
The sustainable natural rubber policy implemented since 2022,
comprises a set of commitments aligned with the objectives of
the GPSNR (Global Platform for Sustainable Natural Rubber),
an organization aiming to improve the environmental and
socioeconomic performance of the natural rubber sector,
including by supporting sustainable agriculture practices, of
which Renault Group has been a member since 2019.
The sustainable natural rubber policy named “Renault Group’s
Hight level commitment for sustainable natural rubber”:
●Outlines Renault Group’s action plan to sourcing
sustainable natural rubber;
●Is consistent with the GPSNR Policy Framework and with
the principles and guidelines laid out in the International
Labour Organization (ILO) fundamental conventions
quoted in Renault Group 2013 and 2019 Global Framework
Agreement (GFA);
●Addresses production, sourcing or consumption from
ecosystems that are managed to maintain or enhance
conditions for biodiversity. [DP E4-2_20; DP E4-2_05DP
E4-2_18]
The above policy addresses the social consequences of
biodiversity and ecosystem-related impacts. [RFP E4-2_06]
2.2.2.4.3.3 Sustainable ocean
and sea practices adopted
In 2022, Renault Group joined the “No deep seabed Mining”
coalition of NGOs, scientists, and companies calling for a
global moratorium on seabed mining until it can be
scientifically proven that such extraction can be done
sustainably9. [DP E4-2_19]
2.2.2.4.3.4 Biobased materials policy
In 2022, Renault Group adopted a Bio-based Materials Policy
developed with internal experts and NGOs, to ensure that the
expected gains from carbon emission reductions do not lead
to adverse effects on people and ecosystems, such as
competition with food crops, deforestation, loss of marine
and terrestrial biodiversity, and pollution. Renault Group
requires potential suppliers to demonstrate that risks have
been identified, assessed, and controlled. Each bio-based
material proposal must be approved by an internal dedicated
committee before being used in vehicles. In order not to
contribute to conflicts over the use of land intended for food
production, Renault Group’s policy is to avoid the use of bio-
based materials containing first-generation biomass10. [DP
E4.MDR-P_01-06]
2.2.2.4.4 E4-3 – Actions and resources related
to biodiversity and ecosystems
Renault Group constructed its sites action plan for
biodiversity and ecosystems around three pillars:
●Measure impacts and identify sensitive sites by:
– Carrying out biodiversity diagnoses for each site by an
external company specialized in biodiversity. By 2025,
all the industrial sites will be covered by a biodiversity
diagnoses;
– Assessing the impacts of all projects leading to the
impermeabilization of an area greater than 1,000 m2.
This action is and will continue to be conducted for
every new project.
●Avoid and minimize impacts by:
– Mitigating indirect environmental impacts, including
COVs emissions, GHG emissions, water consumption and
discharge (See chapters 2.2.2.1, 2.2.2.2, 2.2.2.3 and 2.2.2.5).
Renault Group reduce its inbound carbon footprint
through the purchase of parts and materials less GHG-
emitting. Additionally, the Group avoid pollutants in its
sourcing in order to limit the potential contamination;
– Limiting the impact of new site installation or project on
existing site: all deployed projects on operational sites
are conceived considering environmental matters,
ensuring that the preservation of the environment is at
the core of the project from the outset. This approach,
implemented on the long-term, encompasses a range
of considerations, such as the proactive elimination of
soil contamination risks, the reduction of waste
generation,
and
the
enhancement
of
recovery
opportunities to mitigate potential biodiversity and
ecosystem losses during construction. Environmental
considerations are integral to the project’s lifecycle,
with environmental compliance and sustainability
objectives being reviewed and reinforced at each
milestone to ensure adherence to Renault Group’s
commitment to environmental responsibility;
– Implementing ecological management best practices by
2030 in the European material sites: on-site ecological
best practices are being standardized, for instance the
sustainable management of green spaces (differentiated
grass cutting for instance), pesticide-free maintenance,
and the strategic placement of nesting boxes according
to habitat analysis. The application of these practices is
based on the biodiversity diagnoses done by an external
specialized   company;
9 Details: https://www.noseabedmining.org/
10 First-generation biomass refers to organic materials used for energy or biofuels that come directly from food crops like corn, wheat, sugarcane, and vegetable
oils. Their production poses a risk of competition with food production and can have negative environmental impacts.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
157

## Page 160

Renault Group
Sustainable development
– Managing light pollutions on all the sites of the Group by
optimizing lighting period and adapting lighting
orientation;
– Reducing dependencies on raw materials: to curtail
direct consumption of natural resources, the Group
integrates circular economy practices in its own
operations, but also by decreasing the dependency on
raw materials throughout the supply chain (See chapter
2.2.2.5.3.1).
●Compensate and restore by:
– Conducting a study about environmental incidence for
all the projects involving the sealing of more than
1000  m2, and studying compensation actions for any
residual impact. In 2024, no project resulted in
compensation;
– Undertaking in 2024 a voluntary project to rewild one
parcel of a Renault Group site at Flins.
From 2023 Renault Group co-finances a biodiversity-related
project with Michelin called “Agroforestry Capacity Building
Thailand,” which is part of the Global Platform for
Sustainable Natural Rubber. The project’s objectives are to:
●Support smallholder farmers through training;
●Combat deforestation;
●Develop agroforestry;
●Improve
livelihoods,
food
security,
and
revenue
diversification for smallholders.
This project is available on the “Sustainable Natural Rubber
website”. [DP E4.MDR-A_01-12; DP E4.MDR-A_01; DP E4-3_02;
DP E4.MDR-A_02; DP E4.MDR-A_03]
Renault Group takes into account local and indigenous
knowledge through an ongoing mapping of countries and
mineral risks. [DP E4-3_09]
Biodiversity- and ecosystem-related actions are often part
of broader action plans, which does not allow for sufficient
data granularity needed to track the budget on the detail
required for each biodiversity- and ecosystem-related
action. [E4.MDR-A_06]
Product life cycle assessments have shown that the value
chain can have a material impact on biodiversity and
ecosystem services. Value chain mapping and analysis of
the sustainability reports of key suppliers are planned to
identify priority areas for action in the upstream value chain.
Since this is the first year of CSRD reporting for Renault
Group, the progress of the action plans will be disclosed in
the next reporting cycle. [E4.MDR-A_05]
Numerous
action
plans
that
help
ensure
regulatory
compliance with new regulations or regulations to be applied
soon are implemented in the Group, in particular in order to
prevent any risk of penalties, reputational damage or
remediation. These action plans are explained in the Vade-
Mecum, in particular:
●The VOC Roadmap;
●The “Full Power Water“ action plan;
●The Group decarbonization plan;
●The Biodiversity Roadmap;
●The
management
of
environmental
regulatory
compliance and the monitoring of draft texts.

T_07; DP E4.MDR-T_08; DP E4.MDR-T_13; DP E4-4_07; DP E4-4_09]
Renault Group is aiming for zero deforestation by applying
the EU Deforestation-free Regulation (EUDR) regulation.
The following targets, detailed in other ESRS, are also
important to manage impacts and dependencies on
biodiversity and ecosystems issues:
●Reduce the Group external water supply per vehicle
produced (See chapter 2.2.2.3.4);
●Reduce nickel and zinc discharges in the wastewater of
the Group’s manufactories per vehicle produced (See
chapter 2.2.2.2.4);
●Reduce VOC emissions form the assembly plants (See
chapter 2.2.2.2.4);
●Reduce GHG emissions (See chapter 2.2.2.1.6).
All of these objectives aim to reduce the pressures exerted by
Renault Group on biodiversity (See chapter 2.2.2.4.1.2) and are
part of Renault Group’s biodiversity approach to avoid, reduce,
and compensate. They also address the most material
impacts, risks and dependencies of Renault Group on
biodiversity, especially through mitigation of climate change
(See chapter 2.2.2.1), pollution reduction (See chapter 2.2.2.2)
and water management (See chapter 2.2.2.3). [DP E4.MDR-
T_01; DP E4-4_06]
The biodiversity- and ecosystem-related targets are aligned
with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework
(GBF), the EU Biodiversity Strategy and the French National
Biodiversity Strategy. The alignment is achieved through the
reduction of pesticide use, the assessment of biodiversity
stakes, and contribution to climate change mitigation (See
chapter 2.2.2.1), pollution reduction (See chapter 2.2.2.2), and
the circular economy (See chapter 2.2.2.5) [DP E4-4_05]
Own sites operate in compliance with regulatory thresholds.
Targets are set beyond compliance thresholds while awaiting
the ongoing definition of ecological thresholds by Science
Based Initiatives, which will then be adapted for all Renault
Group activities. [DP E4-4_01; DP E4-4_03; DP E4-4_04]
The objectives have been defined after having assessed
and prioritized the Group’s impacts on biodiversity and
ecosystems. They are based on the avoid, reduce and
restore approach, which is recommended by the SBTn
(Science Based Targets Network) and the TNFD (Taskforce
on Nature-related Financial Disclosures). The stakeholders
were involved in the development of the Act4Nature
commitments, which were used to define Renault Group’s
objectives for reducing its impacts on biodiversity and
ecosystems. The methodologies of the targets detailed in
the other ESRS are developed in the corresponding
chapters. Their settings do not imply biodiversity offsets.
[DP E4.MDR-T_09; DP E4.MDR-T_10; DP E4 MDR-T_11;
DP E4-4_08]
2.2.2.4.6 E4-5 – Impact metrics related to
biodiversity and ecosystems
change 
According to the Biodiversity Footprint 2023 results, the
most material impacts, per pressures, for the Renault
Group’s business model (current and potential) are climate
change (>50% of total impacts), pollution (>20%) and land-
use (>20%) (See metrics in chapters 2.2.2.1, 2.2.2.1.7, 2.2.2.1.8,
2.2.2.2.5). Additionally, the most significant dependency is
linked to water consumption (See metrics in chapter
2.2.2.3.5). Renault Group has not yet identified relevant
metrics for land-use impact at Group level. [DP E4-5_04]

2.2.2.4.7 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its environmental metrics through physical measures based on robust methods. They are subject to
internal controls and verifications, without being systematically validated by an external body.
E4-5 – Impact metrics related to biodiversity and ecosystems change
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Number and areas of sites owned, leased or
managed in or near protected areas or key
biodiversity areas that undertaking is negatively
affecting
The analysis of the sites located near biodiversity-sensitive areas focused on the
material sites listed in chapter 2.2.2.4.1.
All other sites (garages, dealerships, and tertiary sites) are considered to have a low
impact on biodiversity and were therefore excluded from this analysis. The impact of new
sites will be analysed in the coming years (for example in 2024, the dismantling sites).
To determine whether a site is located near biodiversity-sensitive areas, an external body
has conducted a diagnostic evaluation of Renault Group’s sites near biodiversity-
sensitive areas. In cases where a diagnostic has not yet been performed, the analysis
was carried out based on a literature review and the WWF “Biodiversity Risk Filter” tool.

2.2.2.5.1 IRO-1 – Description of the processes
for identifying and scoring Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities
All resources use and circular economy-related material
impacts, risks and opportunities description are available in
chapter 2.2.1.2.2 [DP E5.IRO-1_01]
Renault Group evaluates environmental impacts associated
with resource use and circular economy through life cycle
assessments (LCAs) conducted on its products and services
(See chapter 2.2.1.2.1 for more details on LCAs). A qualitative
grid
for
evaluating
the
environmental
impacts
of
innovations has been established to guide decision-making
at
key
innovation
milestones,
considering
climate,
biodiversity, and resource consumption. Supply risks are
identified as part of the Vigilance Plan (See URD section 2.3
for more details on the Vigilance Plan). [DP E5.IRO-1_01]
Renault Group maintains an ongoing dialogue with NGOs
addressing
CSR
and
resources
issues,
particularly
concerning the sourcing of minerals and metals. NGOs
consults affected communities, and present local community
impact reports. Those are considered by Renault Group and
by the actors of the value chain. The Green Procurement
Guidelines and the Vigilance Plan have been updated to
consider affected communities (See chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1 for
more details on the Green Procurement Guidelines and the
Vigilance Plan). [DP E5.IRO-1_02]
Renault Group has a longstanding commitment to circular
economy and is widely recognized as a pioneer within the
automotive industry, through its engagement in end-of-life
vehicle dismantling and closed loop recycling since 2008,
the successful launching of the iconic Refactory project in
2020 or the creation of The Future Is NEUTRAL in 2022 (See
2.2.2.1). The Group’s transition to a circular economy
contributes to mitigating risks such as loss of revenue due to
raw material shortage leading to operational stoppages,
price inflation, and price volatility, and potential remediation
costs, penalties or reputational damage in case of non-
compliance
with
end-of
life
products
management,
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) regulations or waste
management and treatment regulations. [DP E5.IRO-1_01] 
New business opportunities from recycling and reuse arise
from the Group’s transition to a circular economy, such as
the development of The Future Is NEUTRAL, new industrial
activities at Flins’ Refactory and offers of circular economy
parts and services in after-sales. [DP E5.IRO-1_01] 
Combustion vehicles have bigger environmental impacts
during the use-phase (related to climate change, land use
and water eutrophication due to CO2 emissions from
burning fuels), while electric vehicles have higher impacts
during production, especially related to climate change and
water use, due to the extraction and processing of raw
materials for batteries (for more information, see chapter
2.2.2.1). The negative impacts identified by the Group are the
contribution to resource depletion due to important use of
primary raw materials, the environmental impacts of raw
material extraction and primary processing, and the
environmental impact due to waste. [DP E5.IRO-1_01]
The main raw materials used in vehicle manufacturing are
steel, cast iron, plastics, and aluminum. For logistics and
after-sales packaging, the materials include steel, wood,
plastic, and cardboard. Cobalt, Nickel and Lithium are used
for batteries (See chapter 2.2.2.5.5.2). [DP E5.IRO-1_01]

## Page 164

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.2.5.2 E5-1 – Policies related to resource
use and circular economy
2.2.2.5.2.1 Overall resource use and circular
economy strategy and management
of associated IROs
The circular economy and the use of resources constitute
one of the five fields of action of Renault Group’s
environmental policy. For more information on this policy,
see the chapter on climate change mitigation and
adaptation policies in chapter 2.2.2.1.
This policy has been translated into a Health, Safety,
Environment and Ergonomics (HSEE) policy applicable to all
Renault Group industrial sites (See chapter 2.2.2.2).
It has also been translated into a Circular Economy Policy,
aimed at reducing the Group’s reliance on virgin raw
materials for economic and sustainable development
reasons, and preserving the availability of these limited
natural resources for future generations. The Group
implements circular economy principles at all stages of its
products’ life cycles to minimize its contribution to resource
depletion and the environmental impacts of raw material
extraction and processing. This strategy has accelerated
since the launch of the Renaulution plan in 2021 and its
“Green as a Business” environmental component. [DP E5-
1_01; DP MDR-P_01]
The Circular Economy Policy of Renault Group encompasses
all stages of the value chain and product life cycle, without
geographical limits. All suppliers are involved in Renault
Group’s circular economy approach through the Green
Procurement Guidelines and strategies deployed by the
Group’s Engineering Department and the Supply Chain
Department (See chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1). [DP MDR-P_02]
Renault Group’s policies are aligned with ISO 14001 for
environmental management systems and continuous
improvement, and ISO 14021 for the accounting and
communication
on
recycled
materials.
NGOs,
social
partners
and
other
concerned
stakeholders
were
extensively consulted for the most significant projects or
policy choices. For instance, social partners were consulted
for the Refactory project and the transfer of the historic
remanufacturing activity to The Future Is NEUTRAL. The
Group is also engaged in an ongoing dialogue with the
French end-of-life vehicle recycling industry regarding the
implementation
of
its
Individual
End-of-Life
Vehicle
Management System, which was approved by the French
authorities in June 2024. Renault Group also collaborates
with material suppliers and recyclers, directly and through
its subsidiary The Future Is NEUTRAL, on the incorporation of
recycled materials in its vehicles and the closed loop
recycling of materials from end-of-life vehicles. [DP MDR-
P_04; DP MDR-P_05]
2.2.2.5.2.2 Resource inflows strategy and
management of associated IROs
In 2022, Renault Group adopted a Bio-based materials
Policy (See chapter 2.2.2.4.3) developed with internal experts
and NGOs, to ensure that the use of biomass-derived
materials does not have negative impact on humans or
ecosystems and to promote the use of responsibly sourced
renewable resources. Renault Group requires potential
suppliers to demonstrate that risks have been identified,
assessed, and controlled. Each bio-based material proposal
must be approved by an internal dedicated committee
before being used in vehicles. In order not to contribute to
conflicts over the use of land intended for food production,
Renault Group’s policy is to avoid the use of bio-based
materials containing first-generation biomass.
The resource inflows strategy has also been formalized in
the sites HSEE policy, with the commitment to minimize the
Group’s resource inflows. For more information on this
policy, see chapter 2.2.2.5.2. [DP MDR-P_01; DP E5-1_02]
2.2.2.5.2.3 Resource outflows strategy and
management of associated IROs
Renault Group’s Bio-based Materials Policy (See chapter
2.2.2.4.3) and the management of the resource outflows
impacts, risks and opportunities are covered by the Group’s
Circular Economy Policy.
Renault Group is committed to designing and producing
vehicles in accordance with circular economy principles,
ensuring that its products and materials are durable,
reusable, repairable, and recyclable. Its approach to
resource outflows is multifaceted, involving for instance the
closed loop recycling of plastics and the reuse of metal
scraps through short loops within its foundries and selected
suppliers. Renault Group applies eco-design standards to its
vehicles
and
batteries,
which
are
also
developed
considering repair, disassembly and recycling criteria,
favoring recyclable materials. Each vehicle project is
overseen
by
a
recycling
specialist,
known
as
the
Environmental Customer Performance Leader, who ensures
specific objectives are met in terms of recycled materials
content and recyclability. [DP MDR-P_01; DP E5-1_02]

2.2.2.5.2.4 Waste strategy and management
of associated IROs
The management of the waste impacts, risks and
opportunities is also part of the Group’s overall Circular
Economy Policy.  The waste strategy has also been
formalized in the HSEE policy, applicable to all sites of the
Group, with the mention of the Group’s target to reduce
waste at source and continuously decrease the quantity of
non-recycled waste. For more information on this policy, see
chapter 2.2.2.5.2.
2.2.2.5.3 Resource use and circular economy
actions
2.2.2.5.3.1 E5-2 – Actions and resources related
to resource use and circular economy
Renault Group built its action plan for circular economy
around three pillars:
●Eco design, tackling mostly raw materials use;
●Extension of the lifespan of vehicles and parts, mainly
through reconditioning, repair and remanufacturing;
●End of life management, through the reuse and recycling
of materials and part  and the management of waste.
To support this action plan, Renault Group created The
Future Is NEUTRAL and the Refactory, in Flins, described
below. [DP MDR-A_01]
Created in 2022, The Future Is NEUTRAL is a subsidiary of
Renault Group (82%) and Environment (18%) since October 2024.
The Future Is NEUTRAL and its subsidiaries are the first circular
economy enterprise designed to serve all the automotive
players in Europe (carmakers, suppliers, after-sales networks,
individuals) and support the industry in its transition towards a
more resilient and less impactful model. The company provides
a comprehensive offer of circular products and services,
from car to car, at every stage of a vehicle’s life cycle: recycled
materials for new car production; reused, refurbished and
remanufactured
parts;
end-of-life
vehicle
collection,
management and dismantling services.  [DP MDR-A_01]
The Future Is NEUTRAL benefits from a unique combination
of complementary skills and capabilities brought by its two
shareholders and covers the entire value chain thanks to its
specialized subsidiaries GAIA, INDRA, THE REMAKERS and
the associated firm Boone Comenor Metalimpex. It
contributes to structuring the automotive circular economy
through innovation, thanks to its Innovation Hub, through
consulting activities and training courses, for students and
professionals, dedicated to the circular economy. This offer
is being developed with the support of the campus Industrie
Circulaire de la Mobilité (ICM) based in Flins, as part of the
Group’s corporate ReKnow University. [DP MDR-A_01]
Operational since 2021, the Refactory is Europe’s first factory
dedicated to the circular economy of mobility and is built
around four activity clusters: Re-trofit, Re-energy, Re-cycle
and Re-start. [DP MDR-A_01]
Circular economy actions are often part of broader projects,
which does not allow for the sufficient data granularity
needed to track the budget at the level of detail required for
each circular economy action. [DP MDR-A_06]
2.2.2.5.3.2 Resource inflows actions
Through the first pilar of its circular economy action plan,
Renault Group applies eco design principles to its vehicles and
batteries to limit its impact on raw materials. This includes:
●to increase the use of recycled materials: this enabled the
launch of the new vehicles Scenic E-Tech, Renault 5 E-Tech,
and Dacia Duster in 2024 on the global market with high
rates of recycled materials, especially on the plastics
category. These vehicles are produced in France and
Romania. The Group is also preparing in 2025 the launch of
urban electric quadricycles (Mobilize Duo and Bento) on the
European Union market that will also incorporate a 40%
share of materials from the circular economy. These
vehicles are manufactured in the low-carbon plant of
Tangier, Morocco. The dedicated budget is confidential.
This strategy on recycled materials has resulted in the
creation of a detailed technical roadmap, with a special
emphasis on post-consumer recycled plastics and
materials sourced from vehicles that have reached the end
of their useful life. This roadmap, which applies in priority to
new vehicles manufactured in Europe, Türkiye and
Morocco, includes the anticipation of the future EU vehicle
circularity regulation. Monitoring is carried out for other
production countries (India, Brazil, China, Korea), but the
application of the roadmap depends on the establishment
of recycling sectors and the availability of recycled
materials in these countries. Renault Group, through its
entity The Future Is NEUTRAL, is committed to the closed-
loop recycling of strategic materials such as cobalt, nickel,
and lithium in Europe, by the end of the decade; [DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_06]
●to minimize its dependency on rare earths; that is a long-
term commitment of the Group. Since 2011, the company
has chosen to use Externally Excited Synchronous Motors
(EESM), an innovative rare earth-free technology, for 100%
electric Renault vehicles. In addition, when feasible, the
Group chooses materials other than rare earths in its
magnets, for example ferrite for applications such as
speakers. Renault Group’s consumption of dysprosium
(a heavy rare earth) in magnets has thus significantly
decreased since 2011, thanks to substitution with light rare
earths, which are less critical. Lastly, Renault Group is
working on rare earth recycling projects, which would
make it possible to meet part of its needs in the mid-term
and thus reduce its dependency on critical raw materials
and the associated impacts and supply risks; [DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_06 ]
●to innovate and develop the industrial know-how: the Re-
start division of the Refactory includes an advanced
manufacturing activity with renovation services for
industrial robots and production of parts from 3D printers.
This division also houses a start-up incubator as well as a
training center dedicated to circular economy, the
Refactory Campus. [DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP
MDR-A_03 ]

2.2.2.5.3.3 Resource outflows actions
To expand the lifespan of its vehicles, Renault Group works on:
●Reconditioning, remanufacturing, and repair. In the
Refactory, Renault Group hosts the following activities for
the purpose of expanding the lifespan of its vehicles:
reconditioning of second-hand vehicles (the Renew
Factory), repair of severely damaged vehicles (the
Bodywork
Factory),
retrofitting
of
thermic
light
commercial vehicles. Since July 2024, Renault Group
produced a retrofit offer homologated for the French
market within its partnership with the French start up
TOLV
by
transforming
combustion
engine
light
commercial vehicles (model Master X62) into electric
versions. The Re-energy division of the Refactory, Flins
Battery Repair Expertise Center (CERBF) assesses and
repair electric vehicle batteries. Similar but smaller
battery repair workshops exist for all the markets where
the Group sells electric vehicles. In addition to electric
batteries, Renault Group also offers to the workshops a
repair service for electronic parts of the vehicles (for
infotainment systems, engine computers, dashboards,
screens, electronic units, etc.) through a selected partner.
THE
REMAKERS
(Renault
Group’s
remanufacturing
activity within The Future Is NEUTRAL) produced
remanufactured spare parts, at a lower price and with a
lower environmental footprint than the new parts while
maintaining
the
same
quality
and
performance
requirements. While the main part of its activities remains
dedicated
to
the
production
and
supply
of
remanufactured parts for the conventional car fleet,
since 2024, the offer has been extended to meet the
needs of electric vehicles. Medium to long term plans of
THE REMAKERS are to significantly expand its activities
and its collaboration with other automakers; [DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_02 ]
●Reuse
and
recycling
of
automotive
components
(including batteries): the Refactory plans to house a line
for dismantling end-of-life vehicles. Since 2003, Renault
Group’s vehicles are engineered to facilitate disassembly
and recycling, using materials that can be recycled
together and avoiding incompatible combinations, with a
focus on plastics. A dedicated Environmental Customer
Performance Leader oversees each vehicle project,
ensuring that recyclability and recycled content targets
are taken into account in the vehicle’s design. Renault
Group has opted for an Individual end-of-life Vehicle
Management System to meet its obligations under the
Extended Producer Responsibility as part of the AGEC law.
Its Individual System was approved by the French
authorities in June 2024. To collect, process and recycle
end-of-life vehicles from all its brands, Renault draws
upon the expertise of its long-standing partner INDRA, a
100% subsidiary of The Future Is NEUTRAL since October
2024
and
France’s
leading
end-of-life
vehicles
dismantling network. The main objective (besides
compliance) is to reduce Renault Group’s impacts on
resource depletion through the recycling and reuse of
end-of-life vehicle materials and parts. Renault Group and
INDRA, are continuously expanding their collaboration to
enhance the use of reused parts by repair workshops.
Renault Group recycles also the metallic scraps of its
production plants through this partnership, and with the
short loop recycling of these scraps. This process is
already in place in France and Spain for a significant
share of aluminum, steel or cast iron scraps which are
recycled into the own foundries of Renault Group, HORSE
or their suppliers. Additionally, flat aluminum scraps are
returned
to
aluminum
suppliers
to
be
directly
reincorporated into their production (planned for 2025 in
Spain). In the mid to long term Renault Group’s ambition is
to expand such short loops to flat steel in partnership with
Boone Comenor Metalimpex (a joint venture between
Suez and The Future Is NEUTRAL) and steelmakers.
[DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03]
If The Future Is NEUTRAL invests in the extension and
compliance of its vehicle-dismantling facilities, the resources it
dedicates to recycling and the circular economy are mostly
reflected in its ambitious investment plan in companies active
in this business, and particularly in treatment centers for end-
of-life vehicles. [DP MDR-A_06]

2.2.2.5.3.4 Waste actions
For the management of waste, Renault Group implements
the following actions on all its sites, respecting the ’waste
hierarchy’ (re-use, recycling and energy recovery):
●Prevention: reduce waste generation at its source at the
Group level, for example by prioritizing the use of durable
over single-use. When the generation of waste cannot be
avoided, the Group seeks to reduce the quantity by
separating the fraction that can be reused. Several
solutions exist, such as the dehydration of sludge from the
painting process and water treatment plants, or the
separation of reusable oils contained in sludge and
machining shavings. This action is ongoing, with a focus
on optimizing production processes; [DP MDR-A_01; DP
MDR-A_02]
●Re-use: at the Group level, reuse products, waste and
production waste as much as possible. For example, the
Group reuses excess anticorrosion waxes in its assembly
plants. When undamaged some packaging (e.g. wooden
pallets or thermoformed plastic trays) is reused within the
company or resold for the same purpose. Re-use
solutions are constantly investigated, with significant
efforts on packaging reuse solutions; [DP MDR-A_01; DP
MDR-A_02]
●Recycling: if the material can’t be reused, Renault Group
recycles most non-hazardous waste, of which metals,
account for over 70% and are 100% recyclable.
Continuous efforts are made at the Group level, in
collaboration with waste management and treatment
providers, to enhance recycling potential and implement
sorting solutions specific to each site. The Group has
implemented, in collaboration with external partners, the
regeneration of rinsing solvents for painting robots at
most sites. These regenerated solvents are then reused at
several group sites. On its side, GAIA develops “short
loops“ to ensure the collection and recycling of
automotive industry waste within the same sector,
preserving the technical quality and economic value of
materials for reuse in new vehicles; [DP MDR-A_01; DP
MDR-A_02]
●Recovery: when recycling is not feasible, waste is used as
an alternative fuel or for energy recovery to produce
electricity or steam. This practice replaces natural gas for
heating paint ovens, as seen in the Sandouville, France,
and Busan, South Korea plants; [DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-
A_02]
●Disposal: at the Groupe level, minimize waste incineration
without energy recovery and landfilling. Since 2013, there
has been a 69% reduction in landfill waste (in kg/vehicle,
excluding construction and foundry waste).
All these actions are already in place and the Groupe wants to
maintain them in the long term. Waste-related actions are
integrated into broader action plans, which do not have the
level of data granularity required to extract the budgets
corresponding
specifically
to
waste
prevention
and
management. [DP MDR-A_03; DP MDR-A_06]

[DP MDR-T_02; DP MDR-T_03; DP MDR-T_04; DP MDR-T_05; DP MDR-T_06; DP MDR-T_07; DP MDR-T_08]
This voluntary target covers materials that are either
recycled or sourced from circular economy practices such
as on-site recycling of production waste. It contributes to
the reduction of primary raw material consumption and a
better management of the outflows, aligning with the
Group’s Circular Economy Policy. [DP E5-3_01; DP E5-3_02;
DP E5-3_03; DP E5-3_04; DP E5-3_13; DP MDR-T_01].
To define this target, and in the absence of a pre-existing
relevant scientific framework for setting circular economy
and
recycled
content
targets,
Renault
Group
has
established a solid and transparent approach based on the
definition of recycled materials according to the ISO 14021
standards and the consideration of metallic production
scraps reused in-house (excluded from the ISO 14021
definition of recycled materials) included in a broader
indicator called “materials from the circular economy”.
The Group has considered three criteria for setting the 33%
target in 2030. First, it has considered the rates already
achieved for both thermal and electric vehicles, by type of
material and production process. Additionally, it has studied
the trend of major material suppliers, including their planned
recycled materials integration rate and roadmaps, as
Renault Group’s progress is also dependent on its suppliers’
actions. Third, the Group has also conducted a technical
evaluation of materials sourced from the circular economy
by application type, particularly focusing on plastics, flat
steels, and aluminum alloys for wheels. Renault Group
communicates this rate of materials from the circular
economy for all new vehicles, accompanied by the rate of
recycled materials calculated according to the ISO 14021
standard. The decision to set the target on materials from
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
165

## Page 168

Renault Group
Sustainable development
the circular economy rather than solely on recycled
materials (according to the ISO 14021 standards) was made
to account for the approach of reducing production waste
at source by directly reincorporating them into production
processes within the same site, although such internal
recycling is excluded from accounting of recycled materials
(according to this ISO standard). [DP MDR-T_11; DP-MDR-
T_09; DP MDR-T_10]
Renault Group methodology used to achieve the objective
has not changed for this exercise. [MDR-T_11]
Renault Group monitors progress towards its target for its
new vehicle projects. The measurement is based on the
recycled materials or metallic scraps content declared by
suppliers in the parts and materials provided to Renault
Group, particularly through Material Data Sheets (MDS)
submitted by suppliers. When absent, average rates are
used based on feedback from suppliers and experts of the
Group. These rates are updated once a year in a dedicated
table. The Group also computes the average rate of
materials from circular economy for its vehicles sold and
updates this figure annually. This consolidated average rate
remains relatively stable from year to year, because on one
hand, there is a gradual increase in this rate from one
vehicle generation to the next, but on the other hand, the
changing mix of vehicles sold (with a rapid and continuous
increase in the share of electric vehicles) structurally
contains a lower share of materials from the circular
economy. This is due to the significant amount of new
materials, such as those necessary for the production of
batteries that are still scarcely available in the recycling
market. [DP MDR-T_13]
2.2.2.5.4.2 E5-3 – Resource inflows targets
The main resource inflows target is the target about the
share of recycled materials or from circular economy
material presented in the chapter above. Renault Group has
implemented a Bio-based Materials Policy to promote the
responsible use of these resources (for more details, see
chapter 2.2.2.4.3.4). [DP E5-3_05]
2.2.2.5.4.3 E5-3 – Resource outflows targets
The resource outflows target is the one presented in the section below.

For 2024, Renault Group has chosen to define a voluntary
target of waste per vehicle, aligning the targets of improving
recycling rates and reducing waste. Non-recycled waste
includes
waste
eliminated
through
energy
recovery,
incineration, and other disposal treatments. The sub-
targets, that were published last year, are now only tracked
internally. The value reference is the performance of the
year 2023 excluding HORSE sites, which is 17.6 kg/vehicle of
non-recycled waste. This target is reviewed every three
years and broken down into targets for each Renault Group
site. The waste target, like other environmental target, is
monitored
monthly
and
reported
quarterly
to
the
Leadership Team. [DP E5-3_06; DP E5-3_07; DP E5-3_09; DP
E5-3_13; DP MDR-T_01].
The Group has established its target using a bottom-up
approach that considers continuous site improvement, local
and global environmental constraints and challenges,
regulatory developments, planned production and projects,
technical advancements and allocated resources for each
site. This target is deliberated upon and confirmed in
consultation with various sites to maintain the consistency
of the Group’s objectives. However, since Renault Group
follows a bottom-up approach, the target does not follow a
scientific evidence-based framework. Monitoring of the
target occurs monthly at both the site and Group levels, to
examine any variances and to manage the corresponding
action plans. These action plans are aimed at optimizing
treatment
processes,
as
well
as
optimizing
waste
preparation to reduce quantities and promote the most
environmentally
friendly
processes,
through
sorting,
compaction or physico-chemical separation, such as
dewatering or evapo-concentration. In 2024, Renault Group
achieved notable progress over the previous year by
decreasing the weight of non-recycled waste by several
kilograms per vehicle. [DP MDR-T_09; DP MDR-T_10; DP
MDR-T_11; DP MDR-T_13]
Renault Group methodology used to achieve the target has
not changed for this exercise. [MDR-T_11]
2.2.2.5.5 Resource use and circular
economy metrics
2.2.2.5.5.1 Overall
Renault Group monitors different metrics to better analyze
and track the impacts, risks and opportunities linked to the
use of resources and the circular economy.
The key performance indicators for monitoring the Group’s
progress in circular economy strategy include the rates of
recycled materials and plastics in new vehicles, the revenue
(non-published) from the Group’s main circular economy
activities, and the number of reconditioned used vehicles in
the Group’s various reconditioning workshops (Renew
Factories)
and
the
Flins’heavy
bodywork
(Bodywork
Factory).
2.2.2.5.5.2 E5-4 – Resource inflows
The facilities of Renault Group and its entities are present on
four continents, including labs, design and engineering
centers, factories and dealerships. For the vehicles and
spare parts, the main materials used are steel, cast iron,
aluminum and plastics. For packaging, the materials are
steel, wood, plastic, and cardboard. Cobalt, Nickel and
Lithium are also used for batteries. Renault Group tries also
to reduce its use of rare earth in its production (See chapter
2.2.2.5.3.1). [DP E5-4_01]
Bio-based materials represent less than 1% of the materials
used by Renault Group in the production of vehicles. Natural
rubber, primarily found in tires, is by far the main bio-based
material used in vehicles.
Most automotive suppliers incorporating biomaterials into
their products are not yet able to provide independent
certificates certifying their sustainable origin. However, the
deployment of this type of certification is underway,
especially for tires in response to the new European
Regulation against deforestation and forest degradation
(EUDR).

2.2.2.5.5.3 E5-5 – Resource outflows
Renault Group is committed to designing and producing
vehicles that adhere to circular economy principles,
ensuring that its products and materials are durable,
repairable, reusable, and recyclable. (See chapter 2.2.2.5.1).
[DP E5-5_01]
There is currently no standard methodology for computing
the durability or reparability of vehicles, so no average
automotive industry value is available. [DP E5-5_03]
All Renault Group vehicles sold in the EU comply with the
regulation (EC) 715/2007 (“Euro6”), which requires a durability
of pollution control devices of 160,000 km. Controls are
achieved in assembly plants (conformity of production),
then during the use phase (in-service conformity) on
sampled vehicles for a period of up to five years or 100,000
km, whichever comes first. In-service controls are made by
laboratories, on request of national authorities. Market
surveillance can be performed by civil society stakeholders.
These durability requirements will increase and encompass
battery lifetime from 2026, when regulation EU 2024/1257
(Euro7) comes into force. [DP E5-5_02]

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Renault Group’s dedication to customer satisfaction is
reflected
in
its
after-sales
service
strategy
which
emphasizes the repairability of its vehicles. This approach to
reparability is not rated using an established system but is
integral to its commitment to customer satisfaction and
sustainability. The Group provides spare parts to ensure that
repairs can be conducted using established Renault Group
engineering methods. Its network technicians are equipped
to diagnose and repair vehicles safely and efficiently, using
approved spare parts (whether new, used, refurbished or
remanufactured) and adhering to detailed specifications
for each vehicle type and repair. Renault Group ensures the
availability of spare parts for passenger vehicles at least
10 years and commercial vehicles for 15 years after the end
of production, as integrated into its general sales conditions.
Before the launch of new vehicles, the Group ensures that
spare parts are catalogued and stocked, to guarantee a
repair solution for all customers. The Group is equipped with
specialized
tools
and
training
to
perform
repairs.
Additionally, the repair techniques have been approved and
are accessible across all the Group’s documentary
information systems. [DP E5-5_03]
Recyclability of vehicles and after-sales packaging
(In %)
2024
