85%
Recyclable content rate in after-sales packaging
85%
All vehicles sold by the Group in the European market are at
least 85% recyclable and 95% recoverable, in accordance
with the Directive 2005/64/EC. This European regulatory
requirement is applied to all vehicles sold by the Group
worldwide.
The packaging of spare parts sold in after-sales is 85%
recyclable.
Furthermore, to minimize waste generation during vehicle
production, more than 95%11 of the parts delivered to the
Group’s factories are transported in reusable packaging.
The remaining single-use packaging (transport packaging
or cushioning elements) is recycled over 89%.
2.2.2.5.5.4 E5-5 – Waste
Renault Group monitors waste composition as part of its
environmental reporting. The main categories of waste
generated by the automotive sector include metallic waste,
hazardous process waste (paint sludge and solvents,
machining sludge, lubricating oil, concentrates, sewage
sludge, etc.), packaging waste and traction batteries.

2.2.2.5.6 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its environmental metrics through physical measures based on robust methods. They are subject to
internal controls and verifications, without being systematically validated by an external body.
E5-4 – Resource Inflows
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Overall weight of products
and technical and biological
materials used during the
reporting period
To address these indicators, the following scopes have been identified:
●
Materials used to manufacture new vehicles: these materials can either be assembled into the vehicles or
end up as waste;
●
After-sales parts, whether new or remanufactured at Renault Group sites or by an external provider;
●
Packaging that enters Renault Group sites, for the purpose of marketing packaged products. Vehicles
manufactured by Renault Group are considered free of packaging.
The methodology adopted to calculate these indicators is based on the decomposition of flows and the
accounting of materials in their final states. As a result, no double counting has been identified.
The determination of the values associated with these indicators is based on assumptions and a high level of
uncertainty. Some data are reported on a partial perimeter. Efforts are underway to improve their reliability.
[DP E5-4_08; DP MDR-M_02]
Weight of secondary reused or
recycled components,
secondary intermediary
products and secondary
materials (including
packaging
Percentage of secondary
reused or recycled
components, secondary
intermediary products and
secondary materials
(including packaging)
New vehicles:
For new vehicles, the weight of recycled or reused materials is determined using “pivot“ vehicles, which
represent groups of vehicles with common characteristics. Each vehicle is associated to a pivot, and material
content is derived from the Bill of Materials (BOM). The proportion of recycled materials is based on circular
economy data. If no pivot exists, a minimum recycled material rate is applied based on the engine type.
For waste, the scope is limited waste from the manufacture of new vehicles, as other types of waste are
considered within their respective scopes. To determine the weight of recycled or reused materials, data on
waste generated is collected by material type, and the proportion of recycled or reused materials is
determined based on circular economy data.
After-sale:
For after-sales, the following methodologies have been used:
●Parts remanufactured by Renault Group: to determine the weight of reused material during the
remanufacturing process of vehicle components (mainly gearboxes, engines, and turbos), the measure is
carried out either through the technical data sheets of the components/parts or by weighing them;
●Parts remanufactured by an external provider: the data is based on declarative elements received from
Renault Group’s main supplier and relates to the following five categories of parts: starter, alternator,
compressor, dual mass flywheel (DMF), and dual wet clutch (DWC);
●New spare parts: the recycled content is not available. For the next year, the After-Sales Division intends to
collaborate with its main suppliers to document the information needed to disclose the related figures. 
Packaging:
Packaging considered in the calculation of this indicator corresponds to the packaging that enters Renault
Group’s sites for the purpose of marketing packaged products. As such, only the packaging of parts sold in the
after-sales are concerned. To determine the total weight of these packaging, an average packaging weight
has been determined and multiplied by the quantity of procured parts. To determine the proportion of material
that is reused or recycled, an average recycled rate has been calculated based on the composition of the
packaging (mainly cardboard). 
The data are reported on a limited perimeter, focusing on France, which represents around 40% of the after-
sales activity. The figures are based on the first nine months of this activity and rely on significant assumptions,
resulting in a high level of uncertainty. 
To improve the reliability and accuracy of the reported data, Renault Group needs to enhance its reporting
tools and processes in the coming years. This involves updating the Group’s logistics information systems, for
which a framing was launched in Q4 2024. First IT developments will be completed from 2026, due to budget
prioritization.

## Page 172

Renault Group
Sustainable development
E5-5 – Resource Outflows
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Recyclable content rate in
vehicles
Since 2007, the European Directive 2005/64/EC, known as the 3R Directive, mandates that all new vehicles
introduced to the market in Europe must be at least 85% recyclable. Renault Group ensures compliance with
this requirement by certifying the recyclability percentage of new vehicles during the homologation process.
Recyclable content rate in
after-sales packaging
The packaging considered in the calculation of this indicator corresponds to the packaging containing spare
parts sold by the After-Sales. To determine the total weight of packaging put on the market, an average
packaging weight has been determined and multiplied by the quantity of shipped parts. For the proportion of
materials that are recyclable, an average recyclable rate has been applied based on the composition of the
packaging (mainly cardboard, wood, and plastic).
The data are reported on a limited perimeter, focusing on France, which represents around 40% of the After-
Sales activity. The figures are based on the first nine months of this activity, then extrapolated to twelve
months and rely on significant assumptions, resulting in a high level of uncertainty.
To improve the reliability and accuracy of the reported data, Renault Group needs to enhance its reporting
tools and processes in the coming years. This involves updating the Group’s logistics information systems, for
which a framing was launched in Q4 2024. First IT developments will be completed from 2026, due to budget
prioritization.
E5-5 – Waste
All metrics listed below are expressed in tons, if not specified. All indicators with a value of zero have not been listed below, as
this indicates that no Renault Group site has reported any associated quantity.
Sites with a very small headcount, the impact of which is therefore negligible (mainly tertiary sites), are excluded from the
application scope.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Total Waste generated
The waste reported is the waste that leaves the site’s geographical scope. The quantities are expressed in
metric tons. It includes hazardous waste (HW) and non-hazardous waste (NHW).
Total amount of hazardous
waste
Renault Group is not concerned by preparation for reuse because reused materials are not considered as
waste.
Waste diverted from disposal,
breakdown by hazardous and
non-hazardous waste and
treatment type
To determine the treatment type of each considered waste, the following classification has been used:
Waste directed to disposal,
breakdown by hazardous and
non-hazardous waste and
treatment type
Non-recycled waste
Percentage of non-recycled
waste
Non-recycled waste is defined as:
●
Waste disposed of (European Code starting with D);
●
Waste incinerated with energy recovery (European Code R1);
●
R11, R12, or R13 waste, for which it is not possible to know whether this waste has been recycled or
incinerated with energy recovery (categorized as non-recycled waste as a conservative position).
Total amount of radioactive
waste
In case not all waste quantities are available, back-up methodologies such as the ratio of waste in kg per
person (kg/pers) are used.
In case not all waste treatments are available, back-up methodologies based on the knowledge of waste
specialists and bibliographical data are used (treatment stream per country). In the least-favorable scenario,
if no treatment data are available, waste must be considered as buried on or in the ground (D1 as a
conservative position).

Information about

Renault Group

Additional
information
Sustainable development
02
Turnover increases in 2024 compared to 2023, due to the
following:
●The activities related to the Circular Economy objective
(activities 2.6 and 5.4), and notably used car sales, are
taken into consideration in the alignment calculation;
●Electrification-project R&D sales to partners (activity 3.18);
●The consolidation of MeinAuto, a long-term leasing entity
(activity 6.5).
CapEx show a rise too, thanks to:
●The consolidation of MeinAuto and the inclusion of its
fleet in the portfolio (activity 6.5);
●The deconsolidation of HORSE Powertrain Ltd, an entity
dedicated to internal-combustion and hybrid engines
development;
●The increase in capitalized expenditure linked to
commercialized electric vehicle projects (activity 3.3).
OpEx are also on the rise compared to 2023, due to expenses
related to SDV developments.
2.2.2.6.1 Methodological comments
The scope of eligibility of activities is as follows:
●Turnover: sales of Automotive goods, sales of goods to
partners as well as rental income from assets and interest
income on Sales Financing transactions as described in Note
4 to the Consolidated Financial Statements are eligible under
sections 2.6, 3.3, 3.4, 3.18, 5.4, 6.5 and 7.4 of the Taxonomy; 
●Investments (CapEx): all acquisitions of intangible and
tangible fixed assets, as detailed respectively in Note 10-A1
and Note 10-B to the Consolidated Financial Statements,
excluding goodwill, are eligible because they contribute to
the design, manufacture, distribution of automotive goods
as well as their financing and rental of vehicles;
●Operational
expenditure
(OpEx):
non-capitalised
Research and Development costs, as disclosed in Note 10-
A2 to the Consolidated Financial Statements, represent
the bulk of eligible activities in addition to, for much
smaller amounts, variable, short-term or low-value rental
expenses not restated under IFRS 16 Leases, as disclosed
in Note 6-C to the Consolidated Financial Statements.
2.2.2.6.1.1 Method used to determine the scope of
activities with a substantial contribution
Turnover
To determine the substantial contribution of eligible vehicle-
related activities, Renault Group analysed turnover figures
based on vehicle model and powertrain technology for the
activities linked to the Climate objective, and based on
European emission standards (Euro6) for the activities linked
to the Circular Economy objective; where depollution
information is not available, the Group used the first
registration date for all new vehicles registered after
1 January 2014. For spare parts, as the Group does not have
an information system that would allow direct identification
of parts fitted to low-emission vehicles, it has estimated this
turnover in proportion to the “workshop visits” of low-
emission vehicles.
As for activity 2.6, GAIA’s turnover related to the recovery of
materials (copper, precious metals, plastic) and auto parts
pursuant to dismantling was taken into consideration.
Investments (CapEx)
To determine the substantial contribution of eligible vehicle-
related activities, the Group analysed the CapEx and
capitalised R&D expenditure based on vehicle model and
powertrain technology in the same way as for turnover.
These investments have been reserved for new low-
emission vehicle projects. Capital expenditure common to
both electric and internal-combustion vehicles (such as for
equipment fitted on multiple models) has been prorated for
sales of low-emission vehicles in the medium-term sales
plan. All industrial investments relating to the Rafale PHEV
have been included, as this model meets the applicable
substantial contribution criteria. 
Approximately 50% of the total capitalised R&D expenditure
corresponds to salaries and social security contributions. 
As for activity 5.4, investments made to support THE REMAKERS’
development (process and production line improvements and
new products tests) were taken into consideration.
Operational expenditure (OpEx)
The requirements of the Taxonomy limit the Opex scope to
non-capitalized R&D. Allocation to economic activities
followed
the
same
logic
as
described
for
capital
expenditure. Wherever possible, non-capitalised R&D costs
have been directly allocated to the vehicles. They have been
included if the vehicles in question make a substantial
contribution to the Climate change mitigation objective. The
Group has not included non-capitalised research and
development costs directly attributable to vehicles that do
not meet the selection criteria. Non-capitalised research
and development costs that were not clearly attributable to
a particular vehicle project – notably upstream research
projects – and other operating costs have been included on
a proportional basis using allocation formulas similar to
those used for capital expenditure.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
173

## Page 176

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.2.6.1.2 Verification of compliance to DNSH criteria
Climate change adaptation – all activities
Following the TCFD approach, an assessment of climate risks
and vulnerability, based on three scenarios:
●New Green Deal +1.5°C scenario;
●Eco-Techno Driven + 3°C scenario;
●Retreat and fragmentation +4°C scenario,
was carried out to identify sites likely to be affected (See
chapter 2.2.2.1.2)
The identified physical climate risks have been assessed on
the basis of the lifetime of the asset concerned and are
essentially of three types (extreme heat, water stress and
flooding) covered by appropriate action plans. 
Climate change mitigation – activity 5.4
Direct
emissions
related
to
heat
production
on
remanufacturing sites are under 270 g CO2eq per kWh.
Transportation-related emissions are covered by Renault
Group’s decarbonization initiative. 
Sustainable use and protection of water and marine
resources – all activities
In addition to complying with applicable regulations and the
ban on using any non-renewable water source, the factories
are committed to reducing the quantities of water needed
to produce a vehicle (See chapter 2.2.2.2.4) and to reducing
toxic metal emissions that can impact water resources (See
chapter 2.2.2.3.4).
Transition to a circular economy –
all activities except 2.6 and 5.4
Renault Group’s circular economy policy (See chapter
2.2.2.5) covers DNSH criteria related to (a) reuse and use of
secondary raw materials and re-used components in
products manufactured, (b) design for high durability,
recyclability, easy disassembly and adaptability of products
manufactured, (c) waste management that prioritizes
recycling over disposal in the manufacturing process, and
(d) substances of concern which are identified and
monitored throughout the life cycle (se chapter 2.2.2.2.2) 
Low-carbon vehicles involved in activity 6.5 are reusable or
recyclable to a minimum of 85% by weight, reusable or
recoverable to a minimum of 95% by weight, the Group
complying with the European Directive 2000/53/EC on end-
of-life vehicles.
Pollution prevention and control – activities 3.x 
As in 2023, the Group’s approach aligns with the
interpretation
memorandum
favored
by
the
French
authorities for certain criteria of Annex C of the European
Taxonomy Climate Delegated Act, published by the French
Treasury in January 2024.
Renault Group contributes to and uses the International
Material Data System (IMDS), through which the Group
manages hazardous substances throughout the value
chain, from suppliers to customers. Additionally, other
functions within the Group use IMDS to comply with all
applicable regulations (REACH, CLP, POPs, etc.).
Renault Group’s substance policy meets criteria (a) to (e) of
Annex C of Delegated Regulation 2021/2139 (See Chapter
2.2.2.2.2), including the regulations governing the use of
persistent organic pollutants (POPs), mercury and mercury
components, ozone-depleting gases, whose use is already
prohibited via Renault Group standard GDN-1903-2024-0002
applicable to the Group and its suppliers, and substances
covered by Regulation 1907/2006 (REACH). 
For any substance of very high concern (SVHC) representing
more than 0.1% of the mass of an article (See the
corresponding quantities in section 2.2.2.2.2), it has been
verified that no suitable alternative exists and that these
substances are used under controlled conditions, based on
the guidance provided by the “Draft Commission Notice on
the interpretation and implementation of certain legal
provisions of the EU Taxonomy Environmental Delegated
Act, the EU Taxonomy Climate Delegated Act and the EU
Taxonomy
Disclosures
Delegated
Act“
and
dated
November 29, 2024.  
Regarding the last paragraph of criterion (f), i.e. SOC
(substances of concern) substances, there is no list defined at
European level referencing all substances, including self-
classified ones, and there are no applicable regulation
requiring them to be traced and declared. Given the
uncertainty about the scope of the substances to be
analysed and in order to fulfil this new requirement, Renault
Group has implemented its best efforts by conducting an
analysis on the basis of harmonised substances using the
same methodology as that described in Chapter 2.2.2.2.2 of
this document and meeting the criteria described in Annex VI
to Regulation (EC) 1272/2008 for the hazard categories listed
in Article 57 of Regulation (EC) 1907/2006 (REACH Regulation). 
It should be noted that the requirements of Annex C of
Delegated Regulation 2021/2139 could be modified by the
“Omnibus“
initiative
announced
by
the
European
Commission in December 2024, which aims at simplifying
several directives and regulations including the Taxonomy. 
Due to this uncertainty, the Group cannot rule out the possibility
that the evaluation of DNSH criteria may evolve and that
certain economic activities currently classified as aligned with
the Taxonomy may no longer be qualified as such.
By definition, low-emission vehicles meet the emission
thresholds for clean light commercial vehicles set out in
Table 2 of the Annex to Directive 2009/33/EC.

Sustainable

Financial

Annual general

Additional
information
Sustainable development
02
Pollution prevention and control – other activities 
The low-emission vehicles that Renault Group sells,
finances, leases or operates are all equipped with tyres in
external road noise and resistance classes that comply with
the European requirements set out in EC Regulation 661/
2009. As the requirements of the Taxonomy go beyond
regulatory compliance on this criterion, an additional
analysis was conducted and demonstrated that most tyre
references originally fitted to low-emission vehicles meet
this criterion. However, despite the Group’s best efforts, it
was not possible to verify this for all the financed vehicles, as
information on their actual tyre fitment is not available. To
date, this criterion is considered inoperable. This position will
be reassessed in the future depending on the availability of
the necessary data. 
With a certified noise level well below 68 dBA, Renault
electric vehicles have met, since 2021, the external noise
limits applicable starting in 2024, thus contributing to the
reduction of ambient noise and the quality of life in urban
areas. All low-emission vehicles placed on the market by
Renault Group in Europe in 2024 therefore comply with
European regulation 540/2014/EC applicable to vehicles
certified since July 2016, which require a maximum of 72 dBA. 
Renault Group’s dismantling facilities are equipped to
manage and store safely and in an environmentally sound
manner hazardous substances, mixtures and components
removed during the depollution operations, and comply with
European regulation.
Protection and restoration of biodiversity and
ecosystems – all activities
In addition to the environmental impact studies conducted
in accordance with national and European regulations,
Renault Group is proactive in preserving biodiversity and
ecosystems (See chapter 2.2.2.4).
2.2.2.6.1.3 Verification of compliance with the
minimum safeguards 
These minimum guarantees cover four main areas:
●Corruption and bribery: policies, actions and results are
outlined in chapter 2.2.4.1.2;
●Competition: policies, actions and results are set out in
chapter 2.2.4.1.7;
●Human rights: policies, actions and results are set out in
chapters 2.2.3.1.6 and 2.2.3.2;
●Tax:
Renault
Group’s
Tax
Department
ensures
compliance with the tax rules applicable to its activity in
all countries where the Group operates, in accordance
with international conventions and local laws, through an
appropriate management system.
To the best of its knowledge, Renault Group was not convicted
in 2024 for corruption, tax evasion, or human rights violations.

## Page 184

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.1.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities and
their interaction with strategy
and business model – transversal
Renault Group recognizes the diverse nature of its
workforce which includes employees and non-employees,
described below as workers.
The Group defines employees as all workers having an
employment contract (permanent and fixed-term contracts)
at the end of the reporting period within a consolidated entity
of the Group. Non-employees are temporary workers and
workers with no employment contract such as interns in
France (internship contract) or VIE (volunteer for international
experience). [DP S1.SBM-3_02]
2.2.3.1.2 S1-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts
and channels for own workforce
to raise concerns – transversal
Renault Group has deployed a whistleblowing system,
WhistlB, which is a confidential and secure platform for
collecting reports, open to all employees (including
individuals seeking employment and former employees),
who can report facts contrary to the law, the Code of Ethics,
or the Anti-corruption Code of Conduct.
The WhistleB system is complemented by the regular
internal channels for reporting whistleblowing, which
include
management,
Human
Resources,
employee
representatives, the Ethics & Compliance Department and
the network of Ethics & Compliance officers.
For more information on WhistleB and the management of
whistleblowers, see chapter 2.2.4.1.2.2. [DP S1-3_01; DP S1-
3_02; DP S1-3_05 to 09]
2.2.3.1.3 Working conditions
and environment
2.2.3.1.3.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
A. Working conditions and human rights 
Ethics and sustainable development are central to Renault
Group’s strategic vision. The organization, both as an employer
and as an automotive manufacturer, is committed to
achieving excellence in the respect and promotion of human
rights. Renault Group ensures that its Purpose and values,
which are deeply ingrained among its workforce, are reflected
in its business practices. This alignment fosters a constructive
environment in the regions where it operates and strengthens
relationships with its partners. Furthermore, Renault Group
places a high priority on the capacity of its operational entities
to swiftly address and rectify any potential infringements on
human rights or fundamental freedoms that may arise from its
commercial activities. 
Renault Group acknowledges the material risks on its activity
arising from the potential impacts of human and labor rights
infringements on its own workforce. One key risk involves
potential remediation costs, penalties, and reputational
damage from non-compliance with working conditions
regulations and human rights, which can negatively affect
workers’ psychological and physical well-being. Additionally,
poor working conditions can lead to a loss of workforce
productivity, as they can have adverse psychological and
physical impacts on employees. [DP S1.SBM-3_05]
Renault Group is committed to enhancing the working
conditions that contribute to the psychological and physical
well-being of all its workers. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-3_12]
Renault Group has developed an understanding of the
widespread impacts that may be faced by its workforce
through a comprehensive approach to human rights and
working conditions. However, Renault Group considers that,
except for specificities related to the type of task performed,
all individuals within its workforce are considered at equal risk
of harm. [DP S1.SBM-3_03; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group has not identified any activity or geography
that presents a particularly high risk in terms of forced labor
or child labor. [DP S1.SBM-3_07 to 10]
B. Health and safety
Renault Group has a responsibility to protect its employees
by maintaining a secure workplace that prevents accidents
and work-related illnesses. Given the nature of its
operations, the types of tasks performed, and the materials
handled by its workforce, Renault Group faces numerous
health and safety hazards that could be harmful to its
employees and all individuals working on Renault Group
sites. Due to the nature of its activities, material negative
impacts are mostly related to individual incidents.  Renault
Group is committed to offering a work environment that not
only fulfills legal requirements but also establishes
exemplary benchmarks in HSEE (Health, Safety, Ergonomics
& Environment), guided by dedicated Mandatory Rules (MR)
related to risk prevention, which includes, for example, the
principles of the 5S methodology. The 5S methodology is a
workplace organization system focused on improving
efficiency, safety, and sustainability. It involves sorting,
organizing,
cleaning,
standardizing,
and
maintaining
discipline to enhance both productivity and workers’ well-
being. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-3_03]
Renault Group considers all individuals affected by its
activities equally, regardless of their gender, age, country,
etc., ensuring the same level of prevention and protection.
The Group acknowledges that all its employees, all
individuals working on its sites, as well as temporary
workers, could be materially impacted by the company’s
operations and value chain. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-
3_02; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group is aware of the risk of increased operational
expenditure due to absenteeism in the workplace. The Group
acknowledges that implementing an ambitious Health and
Safety policy does not only reduce costs (due to absenteeism,
turnover, legal actions, health monitoring, etc.) but also
increases
productivity,
employee
motivation,
and
182
Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
www.renaultgroup.com

02
attractiveness by preventing deaths, injuries, or illnesses due to
occupational accidents or diseases. [DP S1.SBM-3_05]
Renault Group has proactively taken into consideration
electrical risks in all its activities related to engineering,
manufacturing, sale, and after-sale through its HSEE
Department. This includes a safe inherent design policy as
well as training and the granting of proper authorizations.
[DP S1.SBM-3_06]
In compliance with legislation, Renault Group’s Health and
Safety policy identifies potential hazards to employees and
determines how they might arise. It also considers the
potential
presence
of
vulnerable
employees
and
implements
appropriate
measures
to
protect
them.
[DP S1.SBM-3_11; DP S1.SBM-3_12]
2.2.3.1.3.2 S1-1 – Policies related to own workforce
A. Working conditions and human rights 
Renault Group’s human rights policy is aligned with key
documents such as the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human
Rights, the UN Global Compact principles, and the OECD
Guidelines
for
Multinational
Enterprises.
The
Group’s
commitment to ethical labor practices is demonstrated by its
adherence to the 2013 “Committing together for sustainable
growth and development” Global Framework Agreement
(GFA), the 2019 GFA on “Working together to build a Renault
Group working environment”, and to the International Labour
Organization (ILO) Conventions, ensuring the elimination of
forced labor and setting the minimum working age at 15, in
compliance with local laws.
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with
its workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP
S1-1_04; DP S1-1_07; DP S1-1_08; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-
P_02; DP MDR-P_04]
The Group’s human rights commitments include: 
●Prohibition of child labor in accordance with ILO’s Minimum
Age Convention, 1973 (no. 138), ILO’s Worst Forms of Child
Labour Convention, 1999 (no. 182) and the 2013 and 2019 GFAs;
●Prohibition of forced labor in accordance with ILO’s
Forced Labour Convention, 1930 (no. 29), ILO’s Abolition of
Forced Labour Convention, 1957 (no. 105), and the 2013 and
2019 GFAs;
●A no harassment, violence, or retaliation policy in
accordance with Article 5 of the Universal Declaration of
Human Rights, the 2019 GFA and the Renault Group
Diversity and Inclusion Charter;
●Freedom of association, dialogue and consultation with
employees, effective recognition of the right to collective
bargaining in accordance with ILO’s Freedom of
Association and Protection of the Right to Organize
Convention, 1948 (No. 87), ILO’s Right to Organize and
Collective Bargaining Convention, 1949 (no. 98), ILO’s
Workers’ Representatives Convention, 1971 (no. 135) aiming
to prevent any form of discrimination on the grounds of
trade union involvement, and with the 2013 and 2019 GFAs
(including the 2019 GFA’s 2021 addendum). [DP S1-1_04;
DP S1-1_07; DP MDR-P_04]
Moreover, by endorsing the United Nations Women’s
Empowerment Principles (WEP), Renault Group reaffirms
that the Universal Declaration of Human Rights must apply
equally to women and men.  [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-
1_07; DP MDR-P_02]
Renault Group adheres to ILO’s Convention no. 100,
advocating for equal pay for work of equal value. The Group
ensures that all its employees are paid an adequate wage.
[DP S1-10_01; DP MDR-P_04]
Renault Group fosters a culture of open dialogue between
management and workforce, facilitated by its Group Works
Council at a global level and local employee representative
bodies. The Group commits to international norms and
standards aimed at supporting or regulating corporate
environmental, social, and societal practices. This adherence
to international principles and standards for the protection of
human rights is also enshrined in the 2013 and 2019 GFAs.
Renault
Group
has
signed
various
agreements
(See
chapter 2.2.3.1.5) that emphasize the importance of balancing
professional
and
personal
life,
offering
flexible
work
arrangements such as remote working, fostering inclusion, and
implementing progressive management styles. 
The
Group
diligently
ensures
effective
employee
representation across its entities in accordance with local
legislation
through
elected
or
union-affiliated
staff
members, reflecting the organisation’s dedication to its
workforce’s well-being and rights.  [DP S1-1_05; DP MDR-
P_01; DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_04; DP MDR-P_05]
The Group Works Council plays a pivotal role in representing
employees
globally.
The
Chief
People,
Workplace,
Organisation Officer represents the most senior level within
Renault Group accountable for the implementation of the
working conditions and human rights policies. [DP MDR-P_03] 
Renault Group addresses psychological and physical
impacts on workers through its human rights governance
framework. Risk mapping identifies significant human rights
risks, and ensures that, as the case may be, corrective
actions are implemented to mitigate negative impacts and
enhance workers’ well-being. [DP S1-1_06]
The Group’s policies are publicly available on its website and
communicated internally via its intranet, regular meetings,
and training sessions. [DP MDR-P_06]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
183

## Page 186

Renault Group
Sustainable development
B. Health and safety
Renault Group’s Health and Safety policy is dedicated to
creating a safe and healthy work environment for all
individuals working on a Renault Group site through
continuous improvement and preventive measures. The
policy implements a robust risk assessment framework and
incorporates legal requirements such as the ILO’s Health
and
Safety
Convention
into
reporting
and
ensures
corporate and local rules, procedures and standards are
applied correctly. Additionally, Renault Group is in the
planning stages of implementing ISO 45001 for all its sites.
The HSEE policy, published in 2024, is another critical tool to
foster health, safety, ergonomics and environment culture
and promote sustainable performance within the Group to
reduce accidents, occupational illnesses as well as the
environmental footprint of its sites (See chapter 2.2.2.2.2).
Renault Group has adopted a specific program, “OneHealth”,
to apply the human and social values of the Group to the health
and well-being of all its  employees. It consists of four
commitments to enable each employee to take care of their
health and well-being wherever their working location:
●Act is about empowering employees with respect to their
physical and mental health in an enjoyable manner;
●Prevent, to help employees identify any risk factors they
may have in order to anticipate potential health problems
through regular confidential health check-ups, online
and/or in medical centers;
●Protect means giving the same attention to every Renault
Group employee, wherever they are in the world. In
particular, this means ensuring that health coverage is at
least equal to or better than the standards in force in the
applicable
country,
especially
for
cardiovascular
diseases and cancer;
●Support means being there for everyone. This includes
accident and occupational illness prevention and 24/7
psychological assistance.
“OneHealth” contributes to one of the Renault Group’s priority
objectives, which is to combat all forms of discrimination,
promote diversity and facilitate inclusion, particularly for
people with disabilities. On a day-to-day basis, every effort is
made to ensure that everyone can express their talent in the
best possible conditions. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-1_09; DP
MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_02]
Additionally,
the
Group’s
Health
&
Safety
policy
acknowledges the responsibilities of managers at all levels
to enforce HSEE standards, ensuring that every individual in
the organization contributes to a safe and healthy working
environment. [DP MDR-P_05]
The HSEE policy and the “One Health“ Program, respectively
endorsed by the Chief Executive Officer and the Group Human
Resources VP in October 2021 and April 2024, are accessible on
the Renault Group website. Progress and results are monitored
by the Sustainable Development Department, which reports to
the Leadership Team by the Strategy and Sustainable
Development Committee. The Health & Safety policy complies
with the Group’s human rights commitments, which are
aligned with international standards and the principles of the
Global Compact. [DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_04; DP MDR-
P_05; DP MDR-P_06]
2.2.3.1.3.3 S1-2 – Processes for engaging
with own workforce and workers’
representatives about impacts  
A. Working conditions and human rights 
Renault Group engages with its workforce and their
representatives
through
regular
meetings,
including
information sessions, follow-ups on the Global Framework
Agreements (GFAs) of 2013 and 2019, and negotiations on
significant changes within the Group. In 2024, the Group
Works Council gathered 19 times, highlighting the Group’s
proactive approach to social dialogue. [DP S1-2_01 to 06] 
The 2013 and 2019 GFAs are subject to follow-up meetings
during which the views of employee representatives are
shared, on the one hand with the members of the Restricted
Group Works Council (in 2024, six follow-up meetings for the
GFA 2013 took place), and on the other hand with the
members of the Worldwide Group Works Council during the
annual plenary session (which dedicated an entire day to
this in 2024). [S1-2_05]
B. Health and safety
Dialogue on occupational health and safety occurs through
established social dialogue frameworks at local, national,
and international levels. For example, in France, this includes
the sites’ Social and Economic Committee, which has a
commission on health, safety, and working conditions
(CSSCT), and at the international level, the Group Works
Council. These committees meet, if necessary, on a monthly
to quarterly basis at a local level, and bi-annually to annually
at a national and Group level, under the supervision of
Human Resources. [DP S1-2_01 to 06] 
To gain insight into various perspectives, risks are identified,
analysed, and prioritized by specialists predominantly from
the HSEE, Occupational Health, Sustainable Development
and Risk Management Departments. This risk assessment is
updated regularly, at least once a year. [DP S1-2_07]
A risk mapping is performed based on their assessment, the
statistical analysis of accidents and occupational illnesses
that occurred over the past fifteen years at Group sites, and
the knowledge of activities at industrial, engineering, sales,
after-sales and tertiary sites. [DP S1-2_07]
The risk mapping specific to health and safety highlights
twelve main risks: 
●Diseases of physical origin (vibrations, noise, etc.);
●Diseases of chemical origin;
●Diseases of psychosocial origin;
●Accidents with machinery;
●Slips, trips and falls;
●Traffic;
●Fall from height;
●Burns and asphyxiations;

02
●Lifting;
●Electrical;
●Musculo-Skeletal Disorders (MSDs) and ergonomics-
related accidents;
●Manipulation (MSDs excluded). [DP S1-2_07]
To address risks, the Group has established qualitative
references, namely the Mandatory Rules (MR) and key
requirements, which cover health, safety, ergonomics, as well
as burns, and asphyxiation. [DP S1-2_07]
2.2.3.1.3.4 S1-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate and
remediate negative impacts and channels
for own workforce to raise concerns 
With respect to safety, the Group underscores that workers’
ability to detect and report anomalies is a critical
requirement, firmly established in the Mandatory Rules (MR)
and rigorously audited. [DP S1-3_02]
For more information on WhistleB and the management of
whistleblowers, see chapter 2.2.4.1.2.2. [DP S1-3_01; DP S1-
3_02; DP S1-3_05 to 09]
2.2.3.1.3.5 S1-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on own workforce, and approaches
to managing material risks and pursuing
material opportunities related
to own workforce, and effectiveness
of those actions 
A. Working conditions and human rights 
Renault Group actively monitors its impact on human rights
through a risk mapping process that aligns with its broader risk
management strategy. Updated in 2023, this process evaluates
the Group and its subsidiaries, focusing on two main areas:
●First, the enforcement of human rights policies as outlined
in the Global Framework Agreements (GFAs) of 2013 and
2019 and internal documentation, with more than 88% of
employees covered by collective agreements;
●And second, a structured response mechanism for any
human rights alerts, which are reviewed during the Group
Works Council’s plenary sessions. [DP S1-4_05]
Renault Group conducts a risk mapping in order to give an
appropriate response to potential negative human rights
impacts that may affect its workforce. Through this mapping,
the Group has identified ten significant risks, including: 
●Risks to personal integrity;
●Discrimination in recruitment; 
●Discrimination in employment; 
●Forced labor;
●Child labor;
●Indecent working conditions;
●Renumeration (living wage);
●Infringement of trade union freedom; 
●Freedom of association and the right to collective
bargaining;
●Negative impacts on local communities. [DP S1-4_05]
In 2024, Renault Group was not aware of any negative impact
that it should have remedied. Therefore, the Group has
concentrated on preventive policies and actions rather than
remediation. This includes the follow-up of the 2013 GFA
“Committing
together
for
sustainable
growth
and
development”, and of the 2019 GFA “Working together to build
a Renault Group working environment”, adherence to various
ILO conventions, and the development of action plans detailed
in chapter 2.2.3.1.3.5.B. [DP S1.MDR-A_01-12; DP S1-4_01; DP
S1-4_02; DP S1-4_06; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-
A_04] 
Additionally, for remuneration matters, Renault Group
applies salary scales that have been shared with staff
representative bodies. [DP S1-10_01]
Renault Group also conducted a risk assessment on social
protection and work-life balance, identifying two primary
risks for its workforce: the potential for personal harm,
including privacy breaches and harassment, and the
possibility of suboptimal working conditions. [DP S1-4_05]
The Group has implemented specific actions to improve work-
life balance related to remote working: 
●Employees working remotely may, as already provided
for in the 2019 GFA on “Working together to build a Renault
Group working environment”, benefit from flexible
working hours in specific circumstances;
●Remote working arrangements offered to employees
must continue to comply with legal working hours and
ensure a reasonable workload for each employee;
●Managers, team members and, more generally, all
employees, have a joint responsibility to respect and
ensure respect for working hours and rest periods;
●Managers and employees can follow additional training
courses to detect the possible warning signs of hyper-
connectivity. [DP S1.MDR-A_01-12; DP S1-4_01 to 03; DP
S1-4_06; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_04]
Renault Group tracks and assesses the effectiveness of its
workforce initiatives through a risk mapping process and
regular reviews of human rights alerts. The Group conducts
regular meetings and follow-ups on the GFAs of 2013 and 2019.
Specific actions, such as flexible working arrangements and
hyper-connectivity training, are implemented to address
identified risks and improve workers’ well-being. [DP S1-4_04] 
Since the risks come from the impacts generated by the
company, managing these impacts allows for simultaneous
risk management. This is demonstrated by Renault Group’s
commitment to ensuring its practices do not cause or
contribute to potential negative impacts on worker well-
being through its risk mapping process, adherence to
human rights policies, and implementation of the Global
Framework
Agreements
(GFAs)
of
2013
and
2019.
[DP S1-4_08] 
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
185

## Page 188

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Renault Group has outlined several key actions to enhance
working conditions and uphold human rights, with specific
time horizons for their completion:
●An annual risk assessment is conducted yearly to identify
and address potential human rights and safety risks;
●The 2013 GFA is applied, with regular updates and
adherence to ILO conventions;
●The Health and Safety policy is continuously improved,
with progress monitored and reported quarterly and
annually;
●Work-life balance initiatives were implemented as part of
the 2019 GFA and are subject to regular adjustments to
ensure compliance with legal working hours and
workload management;
●Training programs are regularly updated and mandatory
for all employees, with specific courses on hyper-
connectivity and remote working;
●The risk mapping was updated in 2023, with continuous
evaluation of human rights policies and structured
response mechanisms for alerts. [DP MDR-A_03]
Renault
Group’s
qualitative
information
regarding
the
progress of its actions includes the enhancement of work-life
balance through structured policies, the application of fair
remuneration practices, the positive changes in workplace
culture and employee satisfaction. [DP MDR-A_05]
These actions are integrated into day-to-day operations
and
deployed
by
the
whole
management
line.
[DP-MDR-A_06] 
B. Health and safety
Renault Group prioritizes the health and safety of its
workforce, understanding that a robust and safe working
environment is essential for both individual well-being and
the overall success of the organization.
Moreover,
through
the
mapping
mentioned
in
chapter  2.2.3.1.3.5.A, these actions are categorized under
three pillars: health, safety, and ergonomics. They are
addressed through targeted strategies aimed at mitigating
risks, improving the work experience, and promoting
physical and mental health of all employees.  [DP S1.MDR-
A_01-12; DP S1-4_01 to 03; DP S1-4_05]
Thus, main health actions consist of: 
●Deploying the Health Mandatory Rules (MR-Health); 
●Substituting harmful substances (replacing products
containing carcinogenic, mutagenic, and reprotoxic
substances and endocrine disruptors, such as many
paints containing cumene);
●Assessing risks for chemical-related diseases and accidents;
●Introducing mental health measures for deseases of
psychosocial origin;
●Implementing the actions of the “OneHealth” program’s
four commitments:
– Act: via a well-being smartphone application, employees
have access to simple, actionable advice and engage in
motivating daily challenges. They also have access to a
personalised space that they can configure according to
their areas of interest and availability. This allows to define
a set of customised objectives and challenges that are
within the reach of each employee;
– Prevent: tools are available to offer personalised and
confidential
monitoring
of
both
physical
(cardiovascular risks, respiratory diseases, cancers,
etc.) and psychological factors (stress, anxiety, sleep,
etc.). These solutions will be applicable in all countries
where Renault Group operates, and medical centers are
accessible near every Renault Group site;
– Protect: Renault Group commits to providing each
employee with high-quality health coverage and easily
accessible tools throughout each geographic location
where the Group operates;
– Support: each employee has access to competent
professionals and relevant information on physical and
mental health. Renault Group commits to offering
appropriate monitoring to prevent accidents and
occupational illnesses, as well as an assistance
program when needed. Dedicated occupational health
services advise employees with respect to health and
professional
risks
prevention.
Additionally,
the
“OneHealth” program’s “Support” commitment gives
access to an online psychologist, available 24/7. This
professional listens to, accompanies or helps the calling
employee in the various situations they encounter such
as emotional support for them or their loved ones,
seeking advice on how to respond to a situation in their
team, etc. [DP S1.MDR-A_01-12; DP S1-4_01 to 03]
Primary safety actions consist of: 
●Deploying the Safety Mandatory Rules (MR-S);
●Focusing on key risks, such as: 
– Slips, trips and falls prevention guidance;
– Machinery safety through the machinery inspections
and Mandatory Rules (MR) No. 2 and No. 3, respectively
covering the eleven key requirements regarding risk
prevention related to machinery intervention and the
twelve key requirements regarding the safety of
machinery installations;
– Traffic risk, notably with the traffic memorandum;
– Burns and asphyxiation, with dedicated Mandatory
Rules (MR);
– Road behaviours and road safety training. [DP S1.MDR-
A_01-12; DP S1-4_01 to 03]

Primary ergonomics actions consist of: 
●Deploying ergonomics memorandums to industrial sites
and Engineering Divisions;
●Assessing risks for MSDs and ergonomics-related accidents;
●Deploying and auditing the six Ergonomics Mandatory
Rules (MR-Erg) and 36 key requirements.  [DP S1.MDR-
A_01-12; DP S1-4_01 to 03; DP MDR-A_01] 
These actions apply to all Renault Group sites and all
individuals working on those sites, as well as future projects.
The Group assesses the effectiveness of its actions based
on self-evaluation, audit, coaching based on the previously
mentioned Mandatory Rules and quarterly and annual
reviews by the Group Works Council.  [DP S1-4_04;
DP MDR-A_02; DP S1-4_06] 
The right to health, safety and decent working conditions is
also required in the Renault Group Procurement Code of
conduct. In this regard, Renault Group ensures its practices do
not contribute to potential negative impacts to non-
employees, such as death, injuries or illnesses of non-
employees resulting from occupational accidents or diseases.
[DP S1-4_08] 
The actions mentioned above underline Renault Group’s
commitment to ensure that its practices do not contribute to
potential negative impacts, such as death, injuries or
illnesses
of
employees
resulting
from
occupational
accidents or diseases. Renault Group’ strategic approach to
Health & Safety also highlights how the Group provides
remedies for actual significant impacts and has led to a
further reduction in the number and frequency of accidents
since 2016. [DP S1-4_02; MDR-A_04] 
Renault Group has defined a roadmap to follow progress in
each HSEE field since 2021 and is tracking progress annually
against it. Additionally, Renault Group ensures transparency on
the progress of its action plans through regular internal
communication. [DP MDR-A_04; DP S1-4_05; DP MDR-A_03] 
Renault Group has enhanced workplace safety by enforcing
the Mandatory Rules (MR) methodology, conducting annual
risk assessments, and introducing targeted safety and
health policies, resulting in a notable reduction in accidents
and days lost since 2016. Continuous improvement is driven
by management commitment, comprehensive training, and
diligent monitoring. [DP MDR-A_05] 
Renault Group commits resources to its Health & Safety
action
plan,
investing
in
the
5S
methodology,
risk
assessments, and safety measures, with future funding
allocated for advanced technologies, continuous training,
and policy enhancements to achieve “zero work-related
accidents and illnesses“ by 2030. [DP-MDR-A_06] 
The actions taken within the framework of the Group’s
Health & Safety policies are handled by the HSEE
Department, of which the corporate teams’ annual
operating budget amounts to an approximate €2 M (OpEx).
Additionally, the “OneHealth“ program, launched in 2024
and the scope of which keeping on expanding in 2025, is
granted a budget of €12 M for the latter year (OpEx). [DP-
MDR-A_06]

## Page 190

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.1.3.6 S1-5 – Targets related to managing material
negative impacts, advancing positive
impacts, and managing material risks
and opportunities 
A. Working conditions and human rights 
The Group ensures proper working conditions and respect
of fundamental labor rights. Additionally, it monitors the
effectiveness of its policies and actions concerning the
related material impacts, risks, and opportunities. This
monitoring is conducted through processes outlined in the
Vigilance Plan. [DP MDR-T_14-19] 
B. Health and safety
In 2021, Renault Group set the target of “zero work-related
accidents and illnesses“ by 2030. The accident coverage
includes Renault Group employees, temporary workers, and
extends to contractors when it comes to serious accidents,
while professional illness reporting is limited to conditions
affecting the Group’s employees. [DP MDR-T_01 to 04; DP
MDR-T_07; DP MDR-T_12] 
The target is broken down into yearly targets in the HSEE
roadmap and is monitored on a quarterly basis by the Strategy
and Sustainability Committee, then on an annual basis by the
Group Works Council. The target is defined based on the
experience of HSEE experts, and the target setting involves
Country HR Heads. It is published on the Renault Group
corporate website, and results are presented to employee
representatives on a regular basis. [DP MDR-T_08; DP MDR-
T_09; DP MDR-T_11; DP S1-5_01; DP S1-5_02]
The reference value for monitoring accidents is 4,691, based
on data from the 2017 HSEE dashboard, the first full year
since the establishment of the HSEE Department. Progress
measurement against targets is based on actual country
headcounts. [DP MDR-T_05; DP MDR-T_06; DP MDR-T_12]
A preventive approach, called days lost mapping, is based on
analyzing and mapping the severe accidents with days lost in
the Departments. The objective is to better understand these
accidents and, more importantly, to systematically deploy
Renault Group’s preventive actions to avoid them. This
approach, implemented by the HSEE Department, relies on
the managers of the concerned sites to reinforce the
implementation of preventive measures. Such an approach
contributes to reducing the number of days lost. [DP S1-5_03] 
Targets for “OneHealth” are to cover 80% of Group
employees by 2025 and 100% by 2030. These targets have
been defined based on the analysis of country practices and
their possible integration in the program, as well as the
possibility to make more languages available in the
application. Country HR Heads have been involved during
target
setting.
Since
their
implementation
in
2024,
methodologies related to the “OneHealth” targets have not
changed. Progress measurement is based on actual country
headcounts. At the end of 2024, “OneHealth” covers 63% of
employees. [DP MDR-T_01 to 04; DP MDR-T_07; MDR-T_09;
MDR-T_11 to 13] 
2.2.3.1.4 Equal opportunities for all
2.2.3.1.4.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
A. Diversity and inclusion
Renault Group is determined to set the standard as an
employer that champions diversity, especially when it
comes to the inclusion of women in the automotive sector –
a field where technical roles often see fewer female
applicants, as is the case for science, technology,
engineering,
and
mathematics
education
programs.
[DP S1.SBM-3_12]
In addressing material negative impacts, the Group has
identified few individual incidents of discrimination (defined as
the unfavorable treatment of a person or a group of persons
based on criteria unrelated to professional skills) or
harassment, which are handled on a case-by-case basis. The
double-materiality assessment has identified one major risk
associated with discrimination. There are possible remediation
costs, penalties, and reputational damage resulting from non-
compliance with equal treatment, non-discrimination or non-
harassment regulations. The most at-risk populations for
discrimination are those underrepresented in Renault Group’s
workforce. [DP S1.SBM-3_03; DP S1.SBM-3_05; DP S1.SBM-
3_11; DP S1.SBM-3_12]
All companies under the Group’s control, in France and abroad,
as well as their employees, must apply the Diversity & Inclusion
policy within it and with their partners (service providers,
suppliers, stakeholders). [DP S1.SBM-3_01; S1.SBM-3_02]
B. Training, skills development, attractivity and talent
retention 
Renault Group is committed to enhancing the employability
of
its
employees
in
all
entities
under
its
control.
[DP S1.SBM-3_01; S1.SBM-3_02]
The Group acknowledges that providing adequate training
will
impact
competencies
and
overall
company
performance,
potentially
improving
employees’
employability over time. [DP S1.SBM-3_05]
Renault Group relies on human capital to ensure the
continuity of its business development and to remain
competitive and innovative in an environment impacted by
changes in regulations and strong competition on vehicles
and associated services. Renault Group acknowledges the
risk of workforce productivity loss due to employee turnover
or insufficiently qualified employees. Additionally, the Group
has capitalized on new business opportunities arising from
the workers’ upskilling. [DP S1.SBM-3_04; S1.SBM-3_05]
The loss of workforce productivity per employee may vary
depending on the country. Renault Group addresses this
issue through tailored training programs that are adapted
to the needs and contexts of each country. [DP S1.SBM-
3_03; S1.SBM-3_11; S1.SBM-3_12]
188

2.2.3.1.4.2 S1-1 – Policies related to own workforce 
A. Diversity and inclusion 
The Group has adopted various policies aimed at improving
Diversity and Inclusion (D&I) within the Group. Firstly, it has
adopted a D&I strategy with clear objectives aimed at
boosting the number of women across the entire
organization, and at guaranteeing equal pay. This strategy
also encompasses initiatives to nurture an inclusive
environment for all forms of diversity, including people with
disabilities. The Group’s disability policy is dedicated to
improving the recruitment and retention of employees with
disabilities by proposing an adapted work environment to
allow them to express their potential to the fullest.
Additionally, the zero-discrimination policy, along with
corresponding resources, is designed to combat all types of
discrimination within the Group and among those who
collaborate with it. [DP S1-1_01]
The Group’s D&I Charter serves as the reference for its D&I
policies and its implementation of a diverse and inclusive
workplace. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-1_10; DP S1-1_11]
Two major principles are driving the Group’s D&I strategy: 
●Ensuring a respectful environment for everyone and
eliminating all types of discrimination in daily operations
and throughout all stages of professional life — this
includes recruitment, training, remuneration, and career
development — in line with the zero-discrimination policy.
Discrimination is sanctioned by Renault Group for any
grounds including gender, age, racial, ethnic, national,
social or cultural origin, sexual orientation or gender
identity, disability or health problem, political or religious
opinion, trade union activities, etc;
●Prioritizing competencies and performance at the heart
of Human Resources policies, with the aim of integrating,
developing, and supporting employees in an equitable
manner. [DP S1-1_10; DP S1-1_11; DP MDR-P_01]
The D&I Charter advocates for all Renault Group’s workers to
champion diversity, fairness, and inclusion within the
workplace. Regardless of location, legal structure, or level of
authority, each individual is expected to actively participate
and contribute to the effective execution of the D&I policy,
committing to uphold the principles of D&I as set forth by
Renault Group. 
The policy is verified annually through internal control
campaigns across all legal entities of the Group. Each year,
the Group’s D&I Department, established in 2021, presents its
strategy and action plan to the Leadership Team, the
Strategy and Sustainable Development Committee of the
Board of Directors, and social partners within the Group
Works Council. In addition, progress reviews, with quarterly
updates, are carried out by a dedicated committee made up
of HR Heads from different brands, countries and
functions. [DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03]
Renault Group actively upholds recognized standards and
initiatives to ensure its policies are fair and inclusive: 
●The Group supports gender equality by following the ILO’s
Convention No. 111 since 1958 and by signing the United
Nations’ Women’s Empowerment Principles in 2019;
●For disability inclusion, Renault Group endorsed the ILO’s
Global Business and Disability Network Charter in 2022
and, in France, the “Manifeste Inclusion“ in 2021;
●In France, the “Charte des 50+” was introduced by the
Group in 2022 to support workers over 50 years of age;
●To protect LGBTQI+ rights, the Group signed the United
Nations Human Rights Office’s Free and Equal Charter in
2020 and the charter of the French organization
“l’Autre Cercle”;
●Renault Group also commits to international cooperation
with the Group Works Council through the 2013 and 2019
GFAs (including the 2019 GFA’s 2021 addendum).
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with its
workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5. [DP S1-1_12; DP MDR-P_04]
The Group formulates its policies by engaging with internal
stakeholders, gathering their needs and impressions
regarding the current status, notably through focus groups,
one-on-one
interviews
and
surveys.
[DP
MDR-P_05;
DP S1-1_02]
Resources such as the D&I Policy, foundational texts, guides
and tools, along with both external and internal pledges, can
be found on the Group’s internal D&I website. These
documents are communicated to all staff members via
internal communication campaigns at both the corporate
and country levels. Moreover, Renault Group’s D&I Charter is
public and available on the Group’s website, inviting all
stakeholders to adopt it. Annually, the Group’s D&I
Department outlines its strategy and action plan to the
Leadership
Team,
the
Strategy
and
Sustainable
Development Committee of the Board of Directors and to
social partners within the Group Works Council. Additionally,
progress reviews, with quarterly updates, are conducted by a
dedicated committee comprised of HR Heads from various
brands, countries and functions. [DP S1-1_13; DP MDR-P_06]
The Vice President of Diversity & Inclusion is in charge of
ensuring the implementation of the policy for Renault Group.
[DP MDR-P_03]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
189

## Page 192

Renault Group
Sustainable development
B. Training, skills development, attractivity and talent
retention 
The Group has an internal mobility policy and a policy
ensuring skills development, with priorities defined by the
Group and relying on the 70/20/10 approach, 70% of learning
being acquired through experience, via on-the-job tasks and
problem-solving, 20% through social interactions like
mentoring and collaboration, and 10% through formal
education, including courses and training. This emphasizes
practical,
hands-on
learning
supported
by
social
connections and structured education. The Group also has a
talent acquisition and attraction policy managed at country
level. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_02;
DP S1-1_10; DP S1-1_11]
The Group ensures that its policies are accessible to internal
stakeholders
through
online
tools
(such
as
People@RenaultGroup,
Learning@RenaultGroup
and
Grow@RenaultGroup), the intranet, and via management or
direct communication with HR teams. [DP MDR-P_06]
Skills and talent development activities are at the heart of the
Human Resources function at Renault Group and directly
report to the Chief People, Workplace & Organization Officer,
who is in charge of ensuring the implementation of HR
policies. [DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_05]
In France, Renault s.a.s. and ReKnow University are Qualiopi-
certified
by
independent
third-party
certification
bodies. [DP MDR-P_04]
2.2.3.1.4.3 S1-2 - Processes for engaging
with own workforce and workers’
representatives about impacts  
A. Diversity and inclusion
Every two years, Renault Group conducts a D&I Survey
addressed to all employees within the Group to understand
their perceptions and to identify their expectations and areas
of improvement. The Group Works Council is also informed on
the results and main key learnings of the D&I Survey. [DP S1-
2_02; DP S1-2_03; DP S1-2_07] 
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with
its workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5. [DP S1-2_01 to 07] 
B. Training, skills development, attractivity and talent
retention
Renault Group’s engagement with its workforce includes an
annual
career
and
development
interview
between
employees and their managers during which training needs,
feedback on the yearly achievements and actions foreseen
for the year to come are collected. Line managers and HR
managers are responsible for ensuring engagement occurs.
The training effort is illustrated by the access rate to training
and the average number of training hours, which is
monitored monthly by the training teams. [DP S1-2_01 to 04
and DP S1-2_06] 
Renault Group has taken steps to gain insight into
perspectives of people in workforce who may be particularly
vulnerable to impacts through diversity metrics, monitoring
access rates by gender, and the HR tools shared at Group
level. [DP S1-2_07] 
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with
its workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5. [DP S1-2_01 to 07] 
2.2.3.1.4.4 S1-3 - Processes to prevent, mitigate and
remediate negative impacts and channels
for own workforce to raise concerns 
A. Diversity and inclusion
Employees at the Group have access to six distinct channels
for
voicing
their
concerns:
Human
Resources,
line
management, Ethics & Compliance referents, the WhistleB
system, zero-discrimination referrals and the D&I Survey.
[DP-S1-3_02; DP S1-3_05]
Part of the D&I Survey is dedicated to assessing employees’
awareness of and confidence in the reporting channels and
their management. [DP S1-3_08]
The guide for handling discrimination and harassment
alerts was designed to support Human Resources in
investigating instances of discrimination. It ensures the
effectiveness of the alert mechanism by providing a
structured and compliant approach in line with the General
Data
Protection
Regulation
(GDPR),
which
prohibits
retention of confidential personal information. [DP S1-3_07]
Renault Group has taken a series of measures to address and
remedy any negative impact occurrence:
●The
Group’s
zero-discrimination
policy
is
widely
disseminated throughout the organization via targeted
communication campaigns aimed at raising awareness
about the policy, as well as the associated support
processes and tools;
●Compulsory
educational
programs
are
routinely
implemented to enhance awareness among staff and
management;
●The Group has developed and extensively implemented a
Group investigation protocol;
●The zero-discrimination pack has been introduced to
assist Human Resources and managers in preventing and
addressing discriminatory incidents. [DP S1-3_01]

2.2.3.1.4.5 S1-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on own workforce, and approaches
to managing material risks and pursuing
material opportunities related
to own workforce, and effectiveness
of those actions 
A. Diversity and inclusion
The D&I policy manifests in various initiatives, such as:
●The zero-discrimination pack, which is designed to prevent,
detect, and address discrimination. It includes reference
materials, guides, communication kits, and tools to support
Human Resources and managers in responding to and
resolving specific instances of discrimination at different
stages of the employees’ professional life;
●Initiatives such as appointing zero-discrimination contacts at
each site and organizing days dedicated to raising
awareness about sexism. The awareness days against
sexism are intensive yet focused events that involve team
members in focus groups at a given site and a maximum of
managers at all the levels of the organization. Activities
include pinpointing gender discrimination issues unique to
that site, educating all levels of site management,
brainstorming potential remedies, committing the site’s
senior leadership to a detailed plan of action to tackle sexism
and enhance the workplace for women, and routinely
checking on the progress of this plan. This approach is
instrumental in turning the abstract concept of workplace
discrimination into tangible, actionable steps.  [DP S1.MDR-
A_01-12; DP S1-4_01; DP S1-4_05; DP-MDR-A_01]
These initiatives are progressively rolled out across the Group,
with an emphasis on locations where there is a significant
imbalance in gender representation. At the end of 2024, 29
sites were covered by these actions across the Group. [DP S1-
4_06; DP-MDR-A_05]
Renault Group implements several actions to remedy to
material negative impacts regarding D&I, including: 
●Training on D&I for all employees and managers:
– Renault Group has implemented an e-learning program
titled “Together in Diversity“ for all white-collar workers
since 2019. The objective is to raise awareness about
unconscious
biases
and
combat
all
forms
of
discrimination. Expected outcomes include an increased
awareness of these biases and understanding of how to
overcome them in everyday situations. Since 2022, all
white-collar workers have been encouraged to take the
training, which has also become compulsory for all new
white-collar workers; [DP S1-4_02; DP MDR-A_01 to 04]
– A new “Inclusive Management” e-learning, launched in
2024, is mandatory for all the Group’s managers, with
a deadline at the beginning of 2025.  Afterwards, all
new managers, whether they are recruited or
promoted internally, will have to complete the e-
learning when taking up their new role. [DP S1-4_02;
DP MDR-A_01 to 04]
●“W-Journey” and “Be-Your-Own-Leader” for female staff
members:
– The Group supports career advancement for women with
initiatives like “W-Journey” and “Be-Your-Own-Leader”;
– Renault
Group
has
co-constructed
with
SKEMA
Business School an executive education program called
“W-Journey”, targeted for women in management
positions. With “W-Journey”, Renault Group is seeking to
promote the female leadership of managers from
middle management, to enable them to progress within
the Group to positions of greater responsibility. The
initiative has been recognized with the Cegos Digital
Learning Excellence Award; 
– “Be-Your-Own-Leader” has been co-constructed with
the London Business School and is dedicated to female
senior leaders;
– Moreover, the Group tracks skills and career progression.
[DP S1-4_02; DP S1-4_03; DP MDR-A_01 to 04]
●Trainings dedicated to the inclusion of people with
disabilities: the launch of a new policy dedicated to the
inclusion of people with disabilities in 2024 introduces
initiatives to adapt the workstations of people with
disabilities (disability catalogue, digital inclusion training)
and to foster their inclusion (8 e-learning modules available
in 12  languages). The expected result is to develop an
inclusive culture within the organization. This, in turn, will
enable the Group to provide more targeted support to
employees with disabilities in their work environment.
[DP S1-4_02; DP S1-4_03; DP MDR-A_01 to 12; DP S1-4_01]
External commitments were reinforced through various
charters and principles related to women’s and LGBTQI+ rights,
disability and age, with representatives from the D&I
Department participating in external working groups to
develop tools and share best practices. [DP S1-4_01]
The effectiveness of these actions is tracked through the
D&I Survey, the internal control framework and through the
monitoring of women’s representation in management to
achieve gender equality. Progress regarding these actions
is communicated across the Group’s sites. [DP S1-4_04]
Current resources for the D&I actions include funding for
mandatory training programs such as “Together in
Diversity“ and “Inclusive Management“, as well as initiatives
like “W-Journey“ and “Be-Your-Own-Leader“ for female
career advancement. The Group also invests in adapting
workstations for employees with disabilities and developing
e-learning modules available in multiple languages. Future
resources will continue to support these initiatives, with
additional funding allocated to new programs.
Finally, Renault Group ensures its practices do not cause
material negative impacts on its workforce through its D&I
strategy. Specific initiatives are implemented to prevent,
detect, and address discrimination. The Group also provides
multiple channels for employees to raise concerns.
The Group’s D&I Department monitors and reports on
progress quarterly and annually. [DP S1-4_08; DP MDR-
A_06]

## Page 194

Renault Group
Sustainable development
B. Training, skills development, attractivity and talent
retention 
Renault Group implements several actions to remedy
against the lack of engagement with its workforce. To
mitigate the risks of losing workforce productivity due to
employee turnover and insufficient qualification, Renault
Group has developed comprehensive strategies aimed at
enhancing employee engagement and retention. These
actions focus on creating a supportive work environment,
offering
continuous
professional
development
opportunities, and ensuring that employees feel valued and
motivated. The ongoing initiatives supporting these efforts
are detailed below:
●Supporting employees in their career and development:
– Employees are encouraged to consider their career
progression and development plan during an annual
career and development interview with their manager.
These discussions are also held during HR Committees
and “People and Talent“ follow-up meetings, with the
aim of bolstering support to employee development;
[DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
– To develop the competencies needed to address the
challenges
of
future
mobility,
Renault
Group
established ReKnow University in 2021. The company’s
university supports the development of training
programs in areas such as electrification, circular
economy,
data
and
AI,
software
development,
cybersecurity and operational excellence, while also
aiming to seize new opportunities; [DP S1-4_01; DP S1-
4_07; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
– All employees can explore internal job postings on the
Group’s “Job Opportunities” website. In France, the
“OPTIMUM” scheme offers short-term assignments in
different company sectors, allowing employees to
explore new career directions.  [DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
●Grow@RenaultGroup: the Grow@RenaultGroup digital
platform, launched in 2022, provides employees with
resources to help them in self-discovery, future planning,
and development plan creation. It includes activities and
materials
for
development
through
experience,
interaction, and formal training. In 2023, resources
dedicated to managers were added to support their role
in employee development;  [DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-A_01;
DP MDR-A_02]
●People@RenaultGroup
and
Learning@RenaultGroup:
since 2019, the Group has deployed a digital solution
called People@RenaultGroup, which provides employees
with tools for feedback and features that enable them to
showcase their career paths and skills, as well as to
formalize their career aspirations. This allows the Group
to adapt its actions accordingly. Learning@RenaultGroup
is
the
Group’s
digital
training
platform,
offering
employees access to training sessions and e-learning
resources to support their skills development. Training is
essential for meeting the Group’s sustainable growth and
social responsibility targets, with progress monitored
monthly. [DP S1-4_01; DP S1-4_02; DP MDR-A_01 to 05]
The Group actively promotes training participation through
communication campaigns. The development of the Learning
& Development community and ReKnow University are
integral to Renault Group’s actions for nurturing talent and
achieving operational excellence, highlighting the Group’s
efforts to mitigate material risks. [DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-A_01;
DP MDR-A_02; DP-MDR-A_05]
The Group also provides support for educational projects and
promotes the integration of young people into the automotive
industry. [DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
Renault
Group’s
training
efforts
concentrate
on
management, digitalization, new work methods, and
cutting-edge technologies such as cybersecurity and the
industrial metaverse. Leveraging internal trainers, the Group
has delivered over one million hours of internal training,
accounting for more than 50% of the in-person training
followed in Learning@RenaultGroup in 2024.  [DP S1-4_07;
DP MDR-A_05]
The effectiveness of training is tracked through the internal
platform, Learning@RenaultGroup, assessed for impact,
and upheld by quality standards such as France’s Qualiopi.
Feedback surveys gauge the training’s influence on skills
and career progression, and the regular monitoring of these
mandatory training actions ensures they are effectively
deployed across the Group. [DP-S1-4_04; DP S1-4_06; DPS1-
4_08]
Renault Group dedicates monetary and human resources to
enhance employee engagement, focusing on development
interviews, training plans and the internal mobility policy.
[DP-MDR-A_06] 
Renault Group participates in numerous benchmarking
initiatives and professional exchanges with other international
groups to assess the relevance of its training initiatives. In
France, Renault Group is active in the development of training
actions sponsored by the Plateforme automobile (PFA) and is
integrated in the Automotive Skills Alliance (ASA) initiatives in
Europe. In 2024, Renault Group won a Digital Learning Trophy
for the quality of its training marketing and deployment
initiatives. [DP-S1-4_04]

2.2.3.1.4.6 S1-5 – Targets related to managing material
negative impacts, advancing positive
