A. Diversity and inclusion
Renault Group is committed to creating an inclusive
workplace by:
●Promoting gender equality through the setting of targets
to achieve higher percentages of female representation;
●Strengthening awareness to the D&I policy and inclusion
within
the
teams,
notably
through
equal
career
opportunities for all employees and training.
The targets cover the entire Group in all countries.
The Group achieved gender pay equality in 2023 based on
Renault Group’s methodology, two years ahead of its 2025
target. Renault Group’s methodology concerns white collar
workers and comparable job positions. Renault Group aims
for balanced gender representation in management
positions, with specific targets such as: [DP MDR-T_01 to 08,
DP MDR-T_12; DP MDR-T_13]
Indicator 
Target 
Baseline 
Deadline 
Gender pay equality based on Renault Group’s
methodology 
Gender pay equality
2020 (-3.9%) 
Achieved in 2023, two years ahead
of the 2025 target 
Balanced gender representation in management 
30% of women by 2030, 
40% by 2040,
50% by 2050 
2021 (24.7%) 
2030,
2040,
2050 
Female apprentices/interns recruited
50% 
2024 (47.5%) 
2025 
[DP MDR-T_02] 
With the awareness that increasing gender diversity in
teams is particularly challenging when women are under-
represented in science, technology, engineering, and
mathematics schools and, consequently, in the automotive
industry, the Group monitors indicators such as recruitment
and promotions to proactively ensure equal gender
representation and achieve diversity objectives. As an
illustration, the Group aims for 50% women in recruitment
processes with the ambition of having women in every
shortlist. The recruitment targets are established for each
business line.  [DP S1.MDR-T_01; DP MDR-T_01 to 07, DP
MDR-T_12; DP MDR-T_13]
Employees were involved in setting the target through social
dialogue. These efforts ensure that women are fairly
considered for all positions. [DP-S1-5_01; DP S1-5_02]
The targets are monitored by the Group’s executive panels,
such as the Leadership Team and the management teams
of the Renault, Dacia, Alpine and Mobilize brands, and of
Ampere, as well as by the country-level management teams.
They are also relayed to the D&I Strategy Committee via the
HR Heads, as well as to the Board of Directors. [DP S1_06; DP
MDR-T_11] 
Renault Group’s pursuit of gender parity relies on local
legislation and voluntary commitments. [DP MDR-T_09] 
Targets related to diversity objectives are reviewed on a
quarterly basis at Group level, while it is more frequent at
country, function, and brand level. Corrective actions are
implemented in order to ensure continuous progress
towards
achieving
these
targets
by
country-level
operations and global functions, which may be subject to
controls and audits. [DP-S1-5_02]
Finally, the Group’s D&I roadmap, reviewed every year, is
deployed and followed-up with the support of the D&I
Leaders appointed by countries and global functions to
ensure the achievement of the targets to increase
representation
of
diversity
within
the
teams,
the
deployment of the development, inclusion and diversity
programs, and the initiatives and tools to fight against
discrimination. [DP-S1-5_03]

## Page 196

Renault Group
Sustainable development
B. Training, skills development, attractivity and talent
retention 
Renault Group’s evolving targets for training and development
are directly influenced by its comprehensive policies that aim
to provide employees with diverse career and skills
development opportunities. The focus on the training access
rate, average number of training hours per employee and the
number of people trained by ReKnow University reflects the
Group’s commitment to the 70/20/10 learning model (for more
information, see chapter 2.2.3.1.4.2.B), which values experiential
learning, exposure, and formal education.
The 2025 targets are:
●90% training access rate;
●35,000 employees trained through ReKnow University.
[DP S1.MDR-T_01; DP MDR-T_02; DP MDR-T_03] 
The internal mobility and talent development policies are
designed to align with the Group’s strategic priorities, and the
targets related to recruitment and internal mobility are set to
ensure these policies are effectively contributing to the
permanent adaptation of the workforce. [DP S1.MDR-T_01; DP
S1.MDR-T_04 to 08]
The Group utilizes a combination of historical data, employee
feedback, and market research to inform the setting of
specific goals for training access rates, average training hours,
and the reach of its educational initiatives like ReKnow
University. Renault Group involves internal stakeholders, such
as employees and management, in the target-setting process
for training and development. Feedback from these internal
stakeholders is instrumental in shaping a talent development
strategy. [DP S1.MDR-T_09; DP S1.MDR-T_11; DP S1-5_01]
The Group uses Learning@RenaultGroup to monitor the training
activities and hours. The Group also relies on HR data to monitor
the impact of training on skills development and job
advancement, particularly for specific actions targeted at
women, such as the “W-Journey” program. Additionally, a quality
indicator for training is integrated into the monitoring of training
indicators. Every year, the indicators are reviewed and targets
are redefined and shared with the countries. [DP S1.MDR-T_01;
DP MDR-T_04 to 09; DP MDR-T_12; DP MDR-T_13]
The continuous progress of the training access rate within
Renault Group is a key indicator that is monitored monthly
by the training teams. Over the past few years, Renault
Group has undertaken numerous initiatives across the
countries
where
it
operates
to
enhance
training
accessibility for its employees. [DP MDR-T_01] 
Renault
Group
has
further
advanced
its
training
management system by streamlining access to targeted
training on critical themes such as managing a team
remotely, improving language skills, and understanding
anti-corruption and ethics. This enhancement allows for the
individualization of training paths, giving employees the
autonomy to select their preferred subjects. On a yearly
basis, a specific communication campaign is addressed to
managers and employees specifying stakes and priorities
with respect to the skills development plan. This action helps
managers and their teams to select the trainings most
adapted to their expectations. [DP S1-5_01; DP S1-5_03] 
Recruitment objectives are set for each function as part of a
proactive approach to equity of representation across the
organization. Renault Group is committed to ensuring 100%
transparency on internal job vacancies thereby providing all
employees with equal access to career advancement
opportunities. [DP MDR-T_02]
2.2.3.1.5 Social dialogue and collective
bargaining
2.2.3.1.5.1 SBM-3-Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model 
Renault Group enhances its workplace by actively engaging in
social dialogue and collective bargaining, which provides
valuable insights into the key issues faced by employees and
informs the adaptation of its policies and communication
strategies. The Group invests in collective bargaining and
social dialogue to improve the psychological and physical well-
being of workers. The Group is extremely conscious of
guaranteeing collective bargaining regulations and ensures
that they are strictly applied.  [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-
3_03; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group maintains a risk mapping process that
highlights specific risks, including that of infringement on the
freedom of association and collective bargaining rights. For
more information, see chapter 2.2.3.1.3.5. [DP S1.SBM-3_05]
The Group’s risk mapping process identifies risks, that are
not linked to specific worker groups, of remediation costs,
penalties or reputational damages, arising from non-
compliance with collective bargaining regulations, or
potential activity discontinuity caused by workforce or
unions protests or strikes. [DP S1.SBM-3_12]
2.2.3.1.5.2 S1-1 – Policies related to own workforce
Year by year, Renault Group reaffirms its commitment to
fostering a dynamic work environment through social
dialogue, both globally and locally. This commitment is
exemplified by the signing of the Global Framework
Agreements (GFAs) of 2013 and 2019 that balance the interests
of the Group and its workforce. The inaugural agreement, titled
“Committing
together
for
sustainable
growth
and
development”, was signed on July 2, 2013. It outlines the Group’s
social, societal, and environmental responsibilities and was
signed by Renault Group’s Top Management, the Group Works
Council, and IndustriALL Global Union. The agreement focuses
on five key areas of action:
●Respecting fundamental social rights;
●Societal responsibility towards employees;
●Corporate social responsibility in communities where the
Group is active;
●Ethical practices in purchasing and dealings with
suppliers and subcontractors;

Additional
information
Sustainable development
02
●Environmental preservation through minimizing the
ecological footprint. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_01]
Renault Group’s second Global Framework Agreement (GFA),
titled “Working together to build a Renault Group working
environment” was signed on July 9, 2019. This agreement
involved Renault Group’s Top Management, IndustriALL Global
Union, French trade union federations, and other unions
represented on the Group Works Council. It aims to proactively
address and manage the ongoing changes in the automotive
industry that significantly affect the labor market. It focuses on
five key mechanisms: 
●Engaging in dialogue about the evolving work environment;
●Implementing a collaborative management system;
●Maintaining a strong commitment to inclusion;
●Supporting work-life balance;
●Adapting the working environment to new needs.
[DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_01]
An addendum to this 2019 agreement was signed on April 26,
2021, outlining remote working conditions for Renault Group
employees. This addendum, signed by the CEO of Renault
Group, the Group Works Council, and IndustriALL Global Union,
was formulated to guide the transformation of work practices
globally. It addresses employees’ wish to combine in-person
and remote work, a need that increased during the Covid-19
crisis, in order to strengthen their work-life balance. This new
hybrid work model seeks to optimize both in-person and
remote activities and is based on voluntary participation. Its
principles are as follows: 
●Greater flexibility in personal work organization, with a
suggested minimum of two days of telework per week
and at least 20% of monthly work time in person;
●Empowerment and autonomy, allowing employees to
freely organize their work around their individual goals,
collaboration, and team meetings;
●Respect for work-life balance, adhering to company
working hours, break times (including the right to
disconnect), and vacation periods. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06;
DP MDR-P_01]
Renault Group’s GFAs 2013 and 2019 cover all employees. These
two GFAs and the 2021 addendum constitute structuring social
dialogue and collective bargaining policies.  [DP S1-1_01;
DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_02]
Renault Group’s commitment to respecting freedom of
association and collective bargaining rights is in line with
the principles set by the ILO’s Convention No. 87 of 1948 on
Freedom of Association and Protection of the Right to
Organize. Recognition of freedom of association includes
the right of each employee to join or not. Renault Group also
undertakes to respect the terms of ILO’s Convention No. 98
on the right to organize and collective bargaining. In this
respect, the Group respects the right of its employees to
organize collectively and maintains strict neutrality. The
Group ensures that employee representation is present in
entities, when applicable through elected or union-affiliated
employees. [DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_04; DP MDR-P_05;
DP S1-1_07]
The Chief People, Workplace, Organization Officer represents
the most senior level within Renault Group’s organizational
structure that is accountable for the implementation of the
GFAs of 2013 and 2019. [DP MDR-P_03] 
Renault Group ensures the availability of its GFAs of 2013
and 2019 to stakeholders and employees, including non-
permanent staff, through its website and structured social
dialogue,
ensuring
widespread
accessibility
to
and
awareness of this information. [DP MDR-P_06] 
2.2.3.1.5.3 S1-2 – Processes for engaging
with own workforce and workers’
representatives about impacts 
The Group Works Council is a forum for responsible social
dialogue at Renault Group, capped at 40 members, including
up to 31 members from the European Economic Area. It
serves as a key platform for international social dialogue,
where members engage with top management and
operations
about
significant
changes
and
strategic
orientations of the Group. When an exceptional draft
decision
has
transnational
consequences
likely
to
significantly affect the interests of employees, the Group
Works Council is convened in a European formation. [DP S1-
2_01; DP S1-2_02] 
Moreover, engagement with the workforce and their
representatives is conducted by the Worldwide Group
Works
Council
through
regular
meetings,
including
information sessions, follow-ups on the GFAs of 2013 and
2019, and discussions on significant changes within the
Group. In 2024, the World Group Committee gathered
19  times, highlighting the Group’s proactive approach to
social dialogue. [DP S1-2_01 to 03] 
Gathering employee insights is crucial for pinpointing areas
to enhance workplace quality of life. In 2022, Renault Group
implemented
a
modern
digital
solution,
People@RenaultGroup,
designed
to
gather
employee
feedback
more
swiftly
and
thoroughly
(for
more
information, see chapter 2.2.3.1.4.5.B). The platform’s user-
friendly interfaces encourage employee engagement, while
ensuring that individual responses remain confidential. The
system’s features for analyzing both quantitative and
qualitative data enable managers and Human Resources
teams to promptly identify and address pressing concerns,
monitor the Group’s performance, and correlate information
across different surveys within a unified system. 
Renault Group also launched a psychological health survey in
France in December 2022, followed by the global employee
survey in January 2023, and maintains a global communication
framework to provide regular updates to the employee
community. [DP S1-2_01; DP S1-2_02; DP S1-2_07] 
The effectiveness of this dialogue is assessed through
annual plenary sessions of the Group Works Council and a
comprehensive review of the application of the agreements.
The social dialogue architecture is built around global
communications, with country-specific forums determined
by local legislation. [DP S1-2_06]

Renault Group

The Chief People, Workplace, Organization Officer is the
most senior role within Renault Group that has operational
responsibility for ensuring that engagement with the
workforce occurs and that the results of this engagement
inform the Group’s approach. [DP S1-2_04] 
2.2.3.1.5.4 S1-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate and
remediate negative impacts and channels
for own workforce to raise concerns 
Renault Group has deployed a whistleblowing system using
in particular a confidential, secure platform for collecting
reports open to all: WhistleB. [DP S1-3_02]
2.2.3.1.5.5 S1-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on own workforce, and approaches
to managing material risks and pursuing
material opportunities related
to own workforce, and effectiveness
of those actions 
The insights from the 2022 psychological health survey in
France and the 2023 global survey informed the creation of
action plans aimed at enhancing employees’ well-being.
These plans were developed in conjunction with the well-
being and mental health promotion committees established
at various Group locations. [DP S1-4_05] 
As social dialogue within Renault Group relies on the results
of surveys on psychosocial risks, which help to better focus
discussions
and
actions
to
be
taken,
preventing
psychosocial risks and fostering psychological health
continues to be a top priority for Renault Group. Thus,
Renault Group has implemented action plans based on
these surveys to enhance employees’ well-being, involving
occupational
health
services,
coordinators,
and
psychologists in France. The Group also fosters local and
international dialogue with social partners, leading to
measures like flexible working hours in Spain, Slovenia, and
Morocco. [DP S1-4_01; DP MDR-A_04]
Renault Group tracks and assesses the effectiveness of its
actions and initiatives while ensuring its practices do not
cause material negative impacts on its workforce through
the answers to the biannual surveys, the implementation of
support
frameworks
and
its
whistleblowing
system.
[DP S1-4_04; DP S1-4_08 
To mitigate the risk of violation to freedom of unions,
association and collective bargaining, Renault Group
fosters local dialogue through significant actions such as
the implementation of the new Collective Bargaining
Agreement for Metallurgy in France in 2024. These efforts
are complemented by transparent communication with
employees and productive exchanges with local social
partners internationally, ensuring an open and responsible
dialogue on key workforce matters. 
Internationally, there were several substantial exchanges
with local social partners, particularly in connection with the
HORSE and Ampere projects and measures to make
working hours more flexible. Several countries (Spain,
Slovenia, Morocco) have taken specific measures in this
area.  [DP S1-4_01; DP S1-4_02; DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-
A_02] 
The Group’s key actions regarding social dialogue are ongoing
initiatives, with continuous efforts to adapt and improve based
on feedback and evolving needs. [DP MDR-A_03]
Progress is assessed on the basis of the quality of
exchanges and communication established with the
stakeholders concerned. Renault Group monitors the
effectiveness
of
these
actions
through
established
processes, including regular consultations with employee
representatives and feedback mechanisms. [DP MDR-A_05;
DP-MDR-A_06] 
2.2.3.1.5.6 S1-5 – Targets related to managing material
negative impacts, advancing positive
impacts, and managing material risks
and opportunities 
The Group maintains a commitment to assessing the impact of
these initiatives using qualitative assessments to gauge
progress and inform continuous improvement in its approach
to employee engagement and dialogue. [DP S1.MDR-T_14-19]

2.2.3.1.6 Other work-related rights
2.2.3.1.6.1  Data privacy of employees
A. SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities and
their interaction with strategy and business model
To prevent potential material impacts related to the Group’s
operations and its value chain, such as excessively
prolonged data retention periods, the misuse, collection of
excessive data beyond the intended purpose, and security
vulnerabilities in the use of personal data, Renault Group is
committed to ensuring the protection of personal data for
all its workforce (employees and non-employees). In
addition, Renault Group has identified risks related to the
preservation of confidentiality of data privacy for its
workforce, and in particular the risk of remediation costs
and penalties due to non-compliance with data regulations
and that of a deteriorated reputation due to data breaches.
The Group thus acknowledges the need for an adequate
organization
and
information
systems
security.
[DP S1.SBM-3_01 to 05; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group’s data privacy policies address material risks
and opportunities related to the personal data, specifically for
employees and candidates. [DP S1.SBM-3_12]
B. S1-1 – Policies related to own workforce 
Renault Group has implemented a personal data protection
compliance system for its employees. Renault Group aligns its
policies with international regulations such as the General
Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and applies local personal
data regulations in the countries where it operates.  [DP S1.
MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-1_07; DP MDR-P_01] 
The Group Data Protection Officer, the Legal Department, and
local
Data
Protection
Officers
are
responsible
for
implementing personal data protection compliance system in
different countries. Renault Group’s data protection policy is
based on compliance with the requirements of the RGPD
regulation (or other equivalent regulations) and applies in all
countries where Renault Group is present, including outside the
European Union. These policies are accessible on the Group’s
website. Renault Group ensures that when new corporate-level
data privacy policies are launched, they are communicated to
local Data Protection Officers for adaptation to local law. [DP
S1-1_07; DP MDR-P_02 to 06] 
Renault Group’s personal data protection compliance
system encompasses a comprehensive approach to
managing material sustainability matters, with a focus on
responsible collection, secure management, appropriate
retention, controlled sharing, and respect for user rights
regarding personal data. The policy’s overarching objective
is to safeguard personal data in accordance with legal
standards
and
ethical
considerations
by
informing
employees and candidates about the use of their personal
data (which purpose, what kind of data, retention,
international transfers, IT security, and guarantees taken to
preserve confidentiality and privacy), thereby addressing
material impacts and risks related to data privacy.
[DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-1_05; DP MDR-P_01] 
The Group ensures the effectiveness of this policy through
its Data Protection Officers. [DP MDR-P_03] 
C. S1-2 – Processes for engaging with own workforce
and workers’ representatives about impacts 
Employees are empowered to exercise their privacy rights
to their personal data (access, modification, deletion,
portability) at any time and can report any malfunctions in
the management of their personal data through the Group’s
internal website on privacy. [DP S1-2_02] 
The Group Data Protection Officer and the local Data
Protection Officers ensure engagement with the workforce
occurs and facilitate the exercise of privacy rights by
employees. The effectiveness of this engagement is
assessed through internal control campaigns, audits, and
monitoring committees that ultimately enable a continuous
reduction of impacts on employees’ privacy. In terms of data
privacy, Renault Group ensures all employees are equally
protected. [DP S1-2_02; S1-2_03; DP S1-2_06; DP S1-2_07]
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with
its workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5.[DP S1-2_01 to 07] 
D. S1-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate and remediate
negative impacts and channels for own workforce to
raise concerns 
In the event of a negative impact on its workforce, Renault Group
determines the appropriate action with the Legal, IT Security,
Risk Management and Communication Departments, in line
with local data protection regulations, such as the GDPR and
other international regulations. [DP S1-3_01]
Employees can raise concerns directly with the Group through
forms, postal or email addresses accessible via the personal
data policies of their country. If employees are dissatisfied with
the responses provided by Renault Group, they have the option
to contact their national data protection authority such as the
Commission Nationale de l’Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
in France. The same process exists in the rest of the European
Economic Community and outside Europe. [DP S1-3_02; DP
S1-3_05; DP S1-3_06]
Renault Group has deployed a whistleblowing system using
in particular a confidential, secure platform for collecting
reports open to all: WhistleB. [DP S1-3_02]
The effectiveness of these channels is ensured through
regular monitoring and prompt reporting upon receipt of
requests. Renault Group is committed to ensuring that its
workforce is aware of and trusts these structures for raising
concerns. The Group provides privacy training to all
employees, explaining how to exercise their rights, and has
established indicators to track the number of employees
trained. Moreover, Renault Group has policies in place to
protect individuals, including workers’ representatives, from
retaliation for using these channels, thereby fostering an
environment where employees can assert their rights
without fear of negative consequences. [DP S1-3_07 to DP
S1-3_09]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
197

## Page 200

Renault Group
Sustainable development
E. S1-4 – Taking action on material impacts on own
workforce, and approaches to managing material
risks and pursuing material opportunities related to
own workforce, and effectiveness of those actions 
To prevent, mitigate, or remedy potential negative impacts,
Renault Group has strengthened GDPR compliance by
implementing a comprehensive data protection structure,
with a Data Protection Officer at Group level, Data
Protection
Officers
for
each
subsidiary,
privacy
ambassadors for each corporate department, and business
relays in each function, all supported by legal advisors.
Renault Group ensures that personal data processing is
compliant with GDPR and does not contribute to negative
impacts through internal processes that adhere to Privacy
by Design principles. All employees and project managers
are informed of these processes, projects involving personal
data collection being subject to validation by IT security,
following the Security by Design principle.  [DP S1.MDR-
A_01-12; DP S1-4_01; DP S1-4_08] 
Renault Group identifies necessary actions in response to
actual or potential negative impacts on its workforce
through a structured process, which begins with regular risk
mapping and monitoring by the Group Data Protection
Officer, the Legal Department, and local Data Protection
Officers. When a negative impact is identified, the Legal,
Risks, IT Security and Communications Departments will
drive the appropriate remediation plan. This collaboration
ensures that the Group not only complies with GDPR and
other relevant regulations but also prioritizes the protection
of its workforce. The Group’s commitment is to safeguard
the interests of those affected by the breach, ensuring that
they receive the necessary information and support to
protect their personal data. [DP S1-4_05]
The scope of the Group’s key actions covers all geographical
areas where Renault Group operates, with responsibilities
assigned to the Group Data Protection Officer, the Legal
Department, and local Data Protection Officers. Key actions
include regular monitoring and evaluation, privacy training for
employees, and mechanisms for exercising privacy rights and
whistleblowing. [DP MDR-A_02] 
Renault Group recognizes the expectation to outline its
strategy and resources for addressing significant risks and
opportunities related to its workforce. The Group’s action
plans are widely shared and accessible internally. [DP S1-
4_02 to 04; DP S1-4_06; DP MDR-A_01] 
F. S1-5 – Targets related to managing material negative
impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing
material risks and opportunities 
Renault Group has confidential internal data privacy
commitments for its workforce. These commitments are
defined according to regulatory obligations. [MDR-T]
The Group Data Protection Officer, the Legal Department,
and local Data Protection Officers are responsible for
regular monitoring and evaluation of the effectiveness of
the policies regarding data protection. The Group monitors
progress based on the GDPR. [DP S1-5_01 to 03]

2.2.3.1.8 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its social metrics through robust
methods. They are subject to internal controls and
verifications, without being systematically validated by an
external body.
The scope covers:
●The Group’s fully consolidated subsidiaries;
●Joint operations proportionally consolidated (Renault
Nissan Technology & Business Centre India (RNTBCI)
for 51 %).
“Employee” refers to all individuals having an employment
contract
(permanent
or
fixed-term
contract)
with
a
consolidated company of the Group.
Temporary workers, interns not having an employment
contract, and VIE (volunteer for international experience)
are considered as non-employees and are excluded from
the reporting scope.
Two methods are used to collect employee data: 
●The HR management system, People@RenaultGroup,
covering approximately 93% of the reporting perimeter;
●A questionnaire sent to the countries to collect the data
for
the
entities
which
are
not
yet
included
in
People@RenaultGroup.
Every employee is counted as “one” regardless of their
contractual working time (or activity rate). The headcounts
are based on data reported at the end of the reporting
period. Employees of Mobility Services subsidiaries are
distributed based on the rest of the Group [DP MDR-M_02;
DP S1-6_13 to 15].

In compliance with the applicable legislations, Renault Group resorts to fixed-term contracts for the
execution of a project, a temporary or a seasonal event. [DP S1-6_16] 
Headcounts by contract
and by gender 
Number of employees
who have left the Group during
the reporting period 
Calculation is performed on permanent contracts. All departures resulting in a termination of these
contracts are considered, including voluntary leave, dismissal, retirement, death and mutual decision.
The average number of employees having a permanent contract during the reporting period is used as
the denominator of the turnover rate to capture fluctuations in headcount over the period.

Metric label 
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02] 
Gender distribution in number
of employees (headcount)
at top management level
According to Renault Group, top management is defined as the top governing bodies,
which correspond to:
●The Renault Group Leadership Team;
●The Management Committees of Renault, Dacia, Alpine and Mobilize brands, and of Ampere.
Gender distribution in percentage
of employees at top

S1-10 - Adequate wages
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Percentage of employees paid
below the applicable adequate
wage benchmark
For this first year, Renault Group has used an international database to establish adequate wage
reference levels. In regions where the Group has a significant presence, Renault Group has opted for
national minimum wages as the reference. Exchanges with peers are planned for 2025 to improve the
method and/or find a more reliable database. Central Human Resources teams conduct an annual
review to ensure that no employee, excluding interns and apprentices with a contract, is paid below the
set threshold.
S1-14 - Health and safety metrics
To monitor health and safety data, Renault Group has
developed a reporting tool that covers approximately 99%
of the perimeter. For the few tertiary entities not directly
reporting their data in the tool, estimates are made based
on the ratio of tertiary sites from the rest of the Group.
For data related to the joint operation RNTBCI, a ratio of 51%
is applied to the total site hours to estimate the hours
worked by Renault Group’s employees. If any accidents or
fatalities are identified, they are recorded as “one”.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Percentage of employees
covered by health and safety
management system
The health and safety management system of Renault Group is implemented and validated:
●Either by an internal audit conducted based on the 10 Mandatory Rules-Safety (MR-S) by the HSEE
Department or its representatives. This indicator accounts for the sites that have been audited at least
once since the establishment of the 10 Mandatory Rules-Safety;
●Or by an external audit conducted by an accredited organization to obtain ISO 45001 certification.
Each employee counts as “one,“ regardless of their contractual working hours (or activity rate).

## Page 204

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Number of fatalities
in the Group’s workforce
resulting of work-related injuries
Number of fatalities of other
workers working on Group’s sites
resulting of work-related injuries
Number of work-related
accidents among Group
employees
All cases of accidents with bodily injury, whether or not medical care was provided, are recorded in the
Group reporting tool.
Rate of work-related
accidents among Group
employees
To calculate the rate of recordable work-related accidents, the number of accidents is related to the
exposure hours of employees. These exposure hours correspond to the actual working hours increased
by a coefficient of 10%, which accounts for the non-working presence time of employees
at the Group’s sites.
The number of hours worked is determined based on actual working hours. When this information is not
available, the following assumptions are made:
●The number of hours worked is based on data from the previous month;
●The number of hours worked is based on standard working hours in accordance with local regulations.
S1-16 – Compensation metrics (pay gap and total
annual remuneration ratio)
Due to the complexity of data collection, it was not possible
to compute the metrics for the entire Renault Group
perimeter. Therefore, the scope of application for these
metrics is as follows: 
●Data is derived from the five most significant countries
(France, Romania, Spain, Morocco, and Brazil), which
collectively
represent
approximately
65%
of
the
consolidation scope in terms of headcount;
●All employees holding an employment contract with the
company on the last day of the reporting period
(permanent or fixed-term employment contract) are
included, except interns and expatriates due to their
insignificant number (less than 5%); 
●Employees with zero hours of presence throughout the
year are excluded. 
The remuneration elements include annual base salary,
variable compensation, overtime, profit sharing, other types
of cash benefits, benefits in kind, and other direct
remuneration, with paid absences excluded. 
Working hours include presence hours and overtime,
excluding paid absences. 
The data is based on actual remuneration elements from
January to December.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Gender pay gap
The calculation method used to determine the gender pay gap is the one imposed by the ESRS S1
standard: the difference between the average gross hourly pay level of men and women, reported to the
average gross hourly pay level of men, with the application of COLA (cost of living adjustment).
Since the calculation is not technically feasible at the Group level this year, it has been carried out in the
five most significant countries in terms of the number of employees, covering 65% of the workforce.
A working group will be established within Renault Group to obtain the necessary information for this
calculation in other countries, thus covering the remaining 35% of the workforce in the coming years. 
Annual total remuneration ratio
To ensure cost of living comparability between France and the other countries where Renault Group
operates, salaries have been reassessed using a Cost of Living Allowance (COLA) index based on data
published by Mercer, a consulting firm.
The remaining scope has not been estimated.
S1-17 - Incidents, complaints and severe
human rights impacts
A. Number of incidents
Information on incidents pertaining to (severe) human rights
matters is collected through WhistleB, the system available
for use by all employees and suppliers. The data collected
through this platform complements incidents reported
through traditional reporting channels such as line
management,
the
HR
Department,
employee
representatives, the Ethics & Compliance Department, etc.
Considering the confidential nature of these elements and
the abovementioned process, the number of cases reported
might include double counting as incidents might be
reported on both the whistleblowing system and traditional
reporting channels.

2.2.3.2 Workers in the value chain (ESRS S2)
As required by ESRS 1, the materiality of the impacts and risks below is based on an assessment of their potential severity and
likelihood, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).

2.2.3.2.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and
Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
Workers within Renault Group’s value chain correspond to
those working on Renault Group’s sites who are not part its
own workforce, those in the upstream and downstream
value chain, and those involved in the operations of joint
ventures. [DP S2.SBM-3_02; DP S2.SBM-3_03]
The Group recognizes the potential negative impact on the
psychological and physical well-being of workers in the
value
chain
due
to
poor
working
conditions
and
environment or violations of work-related rights. The risk
identified for Renault Group includes remediation costs or
reputational damage due to breaches in work-related and/
or human rights or non-compliance with the duty of
vigilance (or CS3D). Finally, Renault Group has identified the
positive impact related to reskilling of workers whose jobs
are threatened by the transition  from internal combustion
engines to electric vehicles. [DP S2.SBM-3_05 to 07]
Renault Group conducts an annual risk mapping to identify,
analyze, and prioritize human rights, labor rights and
fundamental freedoms risks faced by workers in its value
chain.   Renault Group’s approach to identifying material
risks and opportunities focuses on its entire value chain
rather than specific groups of employees. However, two
topics
stand
out:
the
EU
Deforestation
Regulation
implementation, which extends accountability to the
plantation level, and the EU Battery Regulation, mandating
due diligence for 4 materials (cobalt, lithium, nickel, and
natural graphite), impacting supply chains down to the
mines. [DP S2.SBM-3_01; DP S2.SBM-3_04; DP S2.SBM-
3_08; DP S2.SBM-3_09]
2.2.3.2.1.1 Workers of the upstream value chain
of Renault Group 
A. SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities
and their interaction with strategy and business model
In its responsible procurement strategy, Renault Group
considers
all
its
suppliers,
for
direct
and
indirect
procurement. The top 500 suppliers of the Group,
representing 87% of the purchased amount in 2023, are
closely monitored. In 2024, Renault Group identified five
countries – Brazil, China, India, Morocco and Türkiye – from a
third-party database, as posing the highest risks in terms of
overall
Corporate
Sustainability
Responsibility
(CSR)
assessment, especially regarding human rights issues such
as child labor and forced labor. [DP S2.SBM-3_01; DP
S2.SBM-3_04]
B. S2-1 – Policies related to value chain workers
Renault Group has implemented the following policies to
prevent negative impacts and risks related to value chain
workers:
●The Vigilance Plan;
●Renault Group Purchasing Policy for Suppliers;
●Renault Group Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)
Guidelines for Suppliers;
●Renault Group Green Procurement Guidelines (GGP);
●Renault Group 2013 Global Framework Agreement (GFA)
on social, societal and environmental responsibility;
●Renault Group Policy on the Supply of Cobalt and Minerals
from Conflict or High-Risk Areas;
●Renault Group Sustainable Natural Rubber Policy.
[DP S2.MDR-P_01-06]

02
These policies are available on Renault Group’s website and
are accessible to all stakeholders. [DP MDR-P_06]
The policies showcase the Group’s commitment to social
responsibility and improvement of working conditions.
Renault Group is dedicated to upholding human rights
across its value chain. The Group actively contributes to the
United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs),
emphasizing health, gender equality, and decent work
conditions. Renault Group has validated a specific human
rights policy. The Group’s policies are inspired by multiple
international third-party standards such as ISO standards,
the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the ten
principles of the Global Compact, the United Nations
Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, the OECD
Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible
Business
Conduct,
and
the
International
Labour
Organisation (ILO) standards. For over two decades, Renault
Group has been a proponent of the UN Global Compact
principles, reinforcing its dedication to ethical practices
regarding human rights, labor, environment, and anti-
corruption. To address potential non-compliance with
regard to human rights, Renault Group annually updates its
Vigilance Plan, which includes a qualitative mapping of past
audit non-conformities and plans for a new audit campaign
to report on major non-conformities in key areas. More
specifically, in its CSR Guidelines for Suppliers (2023), the
Group commits to respect the fundamental rights of its
employees and workers in the value chain, to ban child labor
under 15 years old, to prevent discrimination and to ensure
diversity and inclusion as well as freedom of association, the
right to health and safety and decent working conditions.
[DP S2-1_01; DP S2-1_02; DP S2-1_08; DP S2-1_09; DP
MDR-P_04]
Renault Group’s Vigilance Plan 
In accordance with Renault Group’s Vigilance Plan, suppliers
must commit to the Group’s standards in terms of ethics,
human rights, health, safety, security, and respect for the
environment and extend these to their own suppliers.
The Group actively manages its global social, societal and
environmental impact by recognizing and reducing the
effects on ecosystems and stakeholders. The Vigilance Plan
is signed by Renault Group’s Chief Strategy Officer, Chief
People, Workplace, Organization Officer, and Chief of
Procurement, Partnerships and Public Affairs Officer. The
policy applies in all 36 countries where the Group is present
and is available to all on the Group’s website. The results of
stakeholder dialogue, as described in chapter 2.2.1.1.3, were
considered when setting the policy. [DP MDR-P_01; MDR-
P_02; MDR-P_03]
Renault Group’s Purchasing Policy for Suppliers
The Purchasing Policy for Suppliers presents and reminds
the main principles of Renault Group’s purchasing approach
including its values, its supplier selection principles, its
ethical principles shared with suppliers, its environmental,
social, and governance criteria. Renault Group asks its
suppliers to adhere to these principles, to apply them in their
field of activity, and to ensure their dissemination
throughout the value chain.
Renault Group Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)
Guidelines for Suppliers
Renault Group’s CSR Guidelines for Suppliers underline the
role of suppliers as key partners to improve the positive
impact that the Group and its suppliers have on society, as
well as to reduce or limit their negative impacts. Thus,
Renault Group ensures that all suppliers are committing to
the key CSR principles and to strengthen their CSR
management systems. [DP MDR-P_01; MDR-P_02]
Renault Group requires its suppliers to sign the CSR
Guidelines for Suppliers. Internal stakeholders are involved
in establishing and monitoring this policy, particularly in
relation to human rights. The Chief of Procurement,
Partnerships and Public Affairs Officer is responsible for the
proper application of this policy, ensuring it is signed by all
concerned suppliers. [MDR-P_05; MDR-P_03]
Green Procurement Guidelines (GPG)
The GPG outline Renault Group’s expectations for all its
suppliers to adopt strategies including environmentally
responsible practices aligned with the Group’s sustainable
development and conformity to regulation targets. The GPG
are provided to the suppliers and list expectations and
recommendations for suppliers on the environmental pillar.
These guidelines are applicable to all Renault Group
suppliers. [DP MDR-P_01, MDR-P_02]
Renault Group 2013 Global Framework Agreement
(GFA), “Committing together for sustainable growth
and development”
In Renault Group’s 2013 GFA, the Group Works Council (which
represents workers worldwide), and IndustriALL Global
Union (a global union federation) commit to five major areas
of action: respect for fundamental social rights, social
responsibility towards workers, social responsibility in the
territories where Renault Group operates, relations with
suppliers, and preservation of the planet by reducing the
environmental footprint. Through this GFA, Renault Group
commits to informing its suppliers about the agreement and
requires them to adhere and apply the fundamental social
rights outlined in chapter 1 of this agreement within their
own company. This chapter addresses the risk of breaches
to international standard on human rights, labor rights and
fundamental freedoms.
The Chief of Procurement, Partnerships and Public Affairs
Officer is responsible for the application of this policy,
ensuring it is signed by its suppliers. Renault Group requires
its suppliers to sign this agreement, along with Renault
Group CSR Guidelines for Suppliers. Internal stakeholders
were involved in the setting and the follow-up of this policy,
notably through an annual report to Renault Group Strategy
and Sustainability Committee. [DP MDR-P_01 to 03; DP
MDR-P_05; DP MDR-P_06; DP S2-1_05; DP S2-2_05]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
205

## Page 208

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Policies on critical raw materials 
Policies on critical materials include:
●Renault Group’s Policy on the Supply of Cobalt and Minerals
from Conflict or High-Risk Areas focuses on human rights
and child labor in the upstream supply chain and applies in
geographical areas where the Group has cobalt and
minerals suppliers. The Chief of Procurement, Partnerships
and Public Affairs Officer is responsible for the proper
application of this policy, ensuring it is respected by the
concerned suppliers. [DP MDR-P_02; MDR-P_03]
●The Sustainable Natural Rubber Policy in which the Group
committed, in 2022, to working with its suppliers to make
the natural rubber sector more sustainable.
Through these policies focused on key raw materials, Renault
Group aims to prevent the risk of reputational damage due to
labour and/or human rights violations and negative impacts on
the psychological and physical well-being of workers in the
value chain. [DP MDR-P_01]
Policy adopted to manage material sustainability
matters related to fair transition
In 2021, Renault Group launched ReKnow University, a new
corporate university, to support workers whose jobs are
threatened by the transition from internal combustion
engines to electric vehicles. It aims to develop the skills
needed for the future jobs in the automotive industry in the
following areas:
●Circular economy;
●Electric mobility;
●Data and artificial intelligence;
●Software and cybersecurity;
●Operational excellence. [DP MDR-P_01]
ReKnow University is intended for Renault Group employees,
industrial and technology partners’ employees working in the
automotive sector, young people on initial training programs,
employees on reskilling pathways and jobseekers. The
Learning & Development Director is responsible for the
implementation of this initiative. [DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-
P_03; DP MDR-A_02]
ReKnow University’s training offers are designed from
comprehensive dialogue with concerned Renault Group
functions, such as Manufacturing and Engineering, tier-1
suppliers, professional automotive associations and public
authorities. Promotion of these offers to potentially affected
Renault Group workers is made by their management line; a
specific digital learning platform is under construction to
showcase ReKnow University capabilities and allow suppliers’
workers to follow specific modules. [DP MDR-P_05; MDR-P_06]
C. S2-2 – Processes for engaging with value chain
workers about impacts
Renault Group engages with workers in the value chain
through their representatives, including the IndustriALL
Global Union, within the framework of Renault Group 2013 GFA
monitoring committee, which meets at least once a year.
The Group is engaging through its activities as a member of
the Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI), the Global Battery
Alliance (GBA), Recharge (a European industry association
for advanced rechargeable and lithium batteries), the
french automotive platform (PFA, which brings together the
automotive industry in France), and the Global Platform for
Sustainable Natural Rubber (GPSNR). Renault Group is also a
member of the Initiative for Responsible Mining Assurance
(IRMA), a multi-stakeholder organization dedicated to
promoting responsible mining practices. This organization
provides a standard for responsible mining and conducts
assessments of mines to evaluate compliance with this
standard. As a member of all these initiatives, Renault Group
indirectly receives the views of workers in the value chain.
Renault Group is participating in events like the OECD
International Forum on Responsible Mineral Sourcing, where
actors of the value chain are directly or indirectly
represented.
Renault Group also deploys an annual on-site CSR audit
campaign for suppliers. These audits include interviews with
workers to detect potential risks on human rights and
working conditions, including health and safety. For more
details on the risk mapping, see URD chapter 2.3.3.1. The
effectiveness of the engagements with workers is evaluated
qualitatively through feedback and audit interviews. The
Sustainable Procurement Senior Manager is responsible for
ensuring that audits are carried out, including the interview
phase. The effectiveness of the Group’s engagement with
the workers in the value chain is measured through the
detection of improvement areas during the audits, through
sampling, site observations, interviews and implementation
of corrective action plans. For more information on these
corrective action plans, see chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.E. [DP S2-1_03;
DP S2-2_01 to 04; DP S2-2_06; DP S2-4_06]

Sustainable development
02
D. S2-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate and remediate
negative impacts and channels for value chain
workers to raise concerns
Renault Group uses ratings like EcoVadis for its risk
mapping, including for value chain workers, to detect human
rights violations. In 2023, Renault Group initiated a risk
mapping
study
in
collaboration
with
a
third-party
consultancy. This study aims to update and enhance the
Group’s risk mapping with a focus on country-specific and
raw material-related risks. The methodology developed
includes information on public claims into the risk
assessment. Additionally, the on-site CSR audits and
employee interviews serve as a tool for workers in the value
chain to raise their concerns and for the Group to detect
human rights issues. The outcomes of past audits are
considered when determining the audit plan for the
following year.  In case of non-conformities, corrective
action plans are implemented. For more information on
these corrective action plans, see chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.E. [DP
S2-1_04; DP S2-2_07; DP S2-3_01; DP S2-3_02; DP S2-4_05]
WhistleB Renault Group’s whistleblowing system is available
for all value chain workers, in complete confidentiality.
Renault Group annually reminds its suppliers of the
availability of this tool and provides them with a user guide
(for more details on WhistleB and the roles of the different
Renault Group Departments, see chapter 2.2.4.1.2.2). In the
event of an alert, a handling procedure is formalized, and
action plans are put in place. [DP S2-3_02 to 06; DP S2-4_05]
The reporting platform complements other whistleblowing
channels such as employee representatives and the Ethics
& Compliance Department. [DP S2-3_ 02]
E. S2-4 – Taking action on material impacts on value
chain workers and approaches to managing material
risks and pursuing material opportunities related to
value chain workers, and effectiveness of those
actions
Actions which apply to all Renault Group’s suppliers, unless
otherwise mentioned, include:
●Requiring all suppliers to sign the two codes of conduct:
Renault Group’s GFA (in which suppliers are asked to
commit to chapter 1, covering fundamental rights) and
Renault Group’s CSR Guidelines for Suppliers, which cover
the various CSR pillars. By signing them, they commit to
respect and implement the CSR principles in their own
operations and value chain;
●Annually assessing and publishing the overall risk
presented by the main suppliers through an EcoVadis
sustainability evaluation. The EcoVadis rating covers
labor and human rights, environment, ethics and
sustainable procurement. Renault Group expects its
suppliers to achieve a minimum rating of 45/100 annually,
which is regarded by EcoVadis as good sustainability
performance, corresponding to a low CSR risk. It applies
to every supplier. The top 500 suppliers are tracked
through indicators;
●Updating the risk mapping by country to categorize
supplier sites according to levels of criticality, allowing for
the definition of future audits and action plans;
●Conducting annual CSR assessment campaigns on
supplier
sites,
including
a
supplier
assessment
questionnaires or CSR on-site audits, to ensure adherence
to environmental, social, and governance standards. In
the event of a detected major non-compliance, Renault
Group requests the supplier to implement a corrective
action plan, leading to a follow-up on-site audit. No cases
of “Zero Tolerance“ non-compliance were noted in the
2024
audit
campaign.
A
“Zero
Tolerance“
non-
compliance would correspond to a proven case of child
labour, forced labour, harassment or violence against
workers on the site. Problems and incidents detected
during the audits are used for a qualitative mapping of
non-conformities, presented in the Vigilance Plan, which
is updated annually. Non-conformities are detected not
only in human rights but also in health and safety,
environment, and governance, showing that through this
holistic approach, the Group addresses the different
aspects of decent work conditions in the value chain;
●Preparing a due diligence policy for batteries, applicable
to all its battery suppliers;
●Developing strategic partnerships for sustainable raw
materials sourcing;
●Revising the Code of Conduct to encompass suppliers
beyond tier 1.
[DP S2-1_04; DP S2-4_01 to 03; DP S2-4_05; DP S2-4_07; DP S2-
4_08; DP S2-4_10; DP S2-4_11; S2.MDR-A_01; S2.MDR-A_02]
The Chief of Procurement, Partnerships and Public Affairs
Officer is responsible for the above actions. The Supply Chain
Department is also responsible for coordinating a network of
officers within local Supply Chain Departments. The Duty of
Vigilance Steering Committee monitors and reviews the
effectiveness of these measures. A summary is presented to
the Ethics and Compliance Committee once a year. [DP S2-
4_04; DP S2-4_05; DP S2-4_07; DP S2-4_08; DP S2-4_12]
These are ongoing key actions with annual cycles, ensuring that
the strategy’s objectives are met each year. [DP MDR-A_03]
Renault Group’s information systems do not currently allow
the costs associated with implementing action plans to be
evaluated for the scope of workers in the upstream value
chain. Work is in progress to provide the information
required by the standard.
Finally, in relation to fair transition, Renault Group created
ReKnow University in 2021, which represents the Group in
educational partnerships with academic institutions and
industrial companies. [DP S2-4_03; DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
In 2024, Renault Group was not subject to condemnation
regarding the non-respect of human rights to the best of its
knowledge. No complaints or serious incidents related to
human rights were recorded. [S2-4_11]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
207

## Page 210

Renault Group
Sustainable development
CapEx are mainly required to renovate and/or build
dedicated training facilities (“campuses”) and to acquire
educational industrial equipment. OpEx are mostly made of
staff costs (training courses, designers and trainers) and of
various training materials. At the Cléon plant, which houses
one of the three campuses dedicated to electric mobility,
Renault Group has invested in educational resources and
technology to train former internal combustion engine
assembly workers on the production of electric motors. [DP
MDR-A_06]
F. S2-5 – Targets related to managing material negative
impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing
material risks and opportunities
The Group is expanding its audit strategy from previous years
by conducting a greater number of audits and self-assessment
questionnaires (SAQs), resulting in the identification of more
non-conformities than before. Consequently, the focus is not
only on reducing non-conformities but also on managing their
adverse effects within the value chain through corrective
action plans and subsequent follow-up audits.  Thus, in 2024,
the Group significantly increased the number of audits and
self-assessment questionnaires, with 104 audits and SAQs
carried out in 2024 compared to 45 in 2023. This rise in the
number of evaluated supplier sites (more audits and SAQs in
more countries) aims at mitigating more risks. [DP MDR-T_16-
19; RFP S2-5_03]
The Group plans to define a 2030 target in 2025 with a 2025-
2030 plan relating to its risks and negative impacts for
workers in the upstream value chain. It will include:
●The definition of a new baseline taking into account the
sale of HORSE, which will significantly influence the
supplier base;
●The definition of a new sustainability rating that
integrates new criteria in addition to those of EcoVadis.
[S2. MDR-T_14]
Finally, to monitor the effectiveness of its policies and
actions related to fair transition and in agreement with
stakeholders, Renault Group has set a target of 5,000
workers in the value chain trained by ReKnow University by
2030, both in upskilling and reskilling. Progress towards this
target is measured monthly and corrective actions are
taken if necessary. The Group considers this target from the
date of accessibility of ReKnow University to workers in the
value chain. There are no intermediate targets. [DP MDR-
T_01 to 09; MDR-T_11 to 13; DP S2-5_01; DP S2-5_02]
2.2.3.2.1.2 Workers of the downstream value chain of
Renault Group
Within its downstream value chain, Renault Group conducts
annual
“Dealer
Satisfaction
Surveys”
among
the
dealerships’ managing directors and sales managers to
measure their satisfaction on various subjects (such as the
sales commercial policy, training offers, support, vehicles
and services product range, etc.). The Group has also set up
a feedback channel for data privacy, allowing customers
and network staff to assert their related rights. [DP S2-2_06;
DP S2-3_04]

likelihood, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).

2.2.3.3.1 Affected communities strategy
and management of associated IROs
2.2.3.3.1.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and
Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
Renault Group is attentive to the various affected communities
that interact with the automotive value chain, including
indigenous communities, industrial workers and their families,
and rural communities, especially those near mining and
refining facilities. The Group is aware of the potential health
problems and environmental concerns these communities
may face, particularly due to air and water quality, related to
agricultural exploitation as well as mining and industrial
activities. These activities, particularly the extraction of critical
materials like lithium and cobalt, may impact the rights and
well-being of indigenous populations.
Renault Group has identified communities potentially
affected by its activities linked to supply, products, services,
and business relationships. The communities subject to
material impacts by Renault Group’s operations or through
its value chain are:
●Local communities are defined as communities living or
working around Renault Group sites – such as factories,
warehouses or engineering centers – or people affected
by activities on these sites;
●Indigenous communities could potentially be impacted
by some of Renault Group’s activities, notably in South
America and Maghreb;
●Communities located near facilities upstream of the
value chain, potentially affected by extraction and
refining of raw materials;
●Communities,
along
Renault
Group’s
value
chain,
affected by the operations of suppliers’ facilities or by the
activities of logistics or distribution providers (for more
information regarding the value chain, see chapter 2.2.1.1).
[DP S3.SBM-3_01 to _03; DP S3.SBM-3_08]
Through dialogue with concerned communities, Renault Group
has gained insight into their concerns and their different levels
of exposure to negative impacts. [DP S3.SBM-3_07] 
The Group is involved in bidirectional consultations with
NGOs to understand the global impacts of the supply chain,
such as mining activities necessary for component
production. The exchange of information during these
consultations demonstrates Renault Group’s involvement
and transparency regarding environmental concerns and
its responsibilities. For more information, see chapter
2.2.3.3.1.3). [DP S3.SBM-3_04]
In the context of mitigating climate change through the
development of electric vehicles, Renault Group is aware of
the long-term repercussions on communities, particularly
those whose livelihoods are intertwined with the industry’s
supply chains (for more information, see chapter 2.2.2.1.1).
The Group is proactive in its efforts to reduce emissions and
facilitate community resilience, helping communities to
adapt to climate change and its effects on the environment
and local economies. [DP S3.SBM-3_04] 
Also, local communities affected by the impacts on air and
water quality are recognized by Renault Group as key
stakeholders. The Group understands the importance of
addressing these concerns through responsible waste
management and the reduction of hazardous materials
used in production processes (for more information, see
chapters 2.2.2.2.2 and 2.2.2.5.2). [DP S3.SBM-3_04]
Additionally, the Group is attentive to impacts on local
communities linked to water usage in its value chain, especially
from suppliers located in water-stressed regions, and to
potential water pollution from mining and refining activities. This
consideration is part of the Group’s commitment to responsible
environmental management (for more information, see
chapters 2.2.2.2.2 and 2.2.2.3.2). [DP S3.SBM-3_04] 
Moreover, the Group acknowledges that some communities
depend on food resources that may be widely impacted by
the value chain’s water usage and material extraction
processes (for more information, see chapter 2.2.2.4.3).
[DP S3.SBM-3_04]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
209

## Page 212

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Renault Group also recognizes the development of local jobs
and educational opportunities as a material positive impact
for residents, industrial workers and their families, and other
local communities that are situated near industrial facilities.
The Group also considers the opportunity of development of
competitive local sourcing linked to this positive impact.
In addition, Renault Group faces risks such as remediation
costs, penalties, reputational damage or operational
stoppages due to non-compliance with local legislations.
The Group also recognizes the negative impact of
deteriorated life conditions of local and indigenous
communities, due to its or its suppliers’ violation of their
rights. [DP S3.SBM-3_05; DP S3.SBM-3_06]
Finally, the non-respect of indigenous rights by suppliers can
lead to significant risks for Renault Group, such as
deteriorated reputation. Additionally, non-compliance with
local rights requirements can result in remediation costs,
penalties, or further reputational damage for the Group.
[S3.SBM-3_08] 
2.2.3.3.1.2 S3-1 – Policies related
to affected communities
Renault Group’s Vigilance Plan, which covers all geographical
areas where it operates and extends to suppliers, outlines the
commitment to minimize potential negative impacts and
encourage positive impacts on communities along its value
chain. The Group complies with the 2007 United Nations
Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) and
local regulations in terms of property rights. [DP S3-1_01 to 03;
DP S3-1_06; DP S3-1_07; DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_04] 
The Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee is responsible for
implementing and monitoring the Vigilance Plan (for more
information see chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.B) For Renault Group’s own
operations, site managers oversee the identification of local
risks
and
opportunities
through
exchanges
with
communities and authorities, and make sure these
opportunities are deployed by the Group’s teams. The Public
Affairs Department ensures dialogue with public authorities
when relevant. [DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_05; DP S3-1_04] 
To prevent, mitigate and remediate negative impacts, Renault
Group has developed measures engaging all stakeholders in
the territories where it has a significant presence:
●At site level, these measures are identified and prioritized
by the local management team, based on the dialogue
they have with either public authorities or local
representatives of the civil society;
●At Group level, ongoing dialogue between the Sustainability
Department and NGOs helps to improve understanding of
the issues and expectations of stakeholders. In 2024,
dialogue focused on human rights and the battery minerals
supply chain. Some functions, such as the Public Affairs
Department,
have
set
specific
guidelines
regarding
affected communities to ensure a consistent approach
within their teams around the globe. [DP S3-1_05; DP MDR-
P_01; DP MDR-P_05; DP MDR-P_06] 
2.2.3.3.1.3 S3-2 – Processes for engaging with
affected communities about impacts
Renault Group’s engagement with local communities is
multifaceted through regional development agreements,
discussions with public authorities, direct conversations, plant
tours, complaint procedures, environmental information
leaflets, local media relations and adherence to associations.
Renault Group engages with affected communities during
project planning, development, and operations phases.
Engagement with indigenous representatives occurs as
needed, respecting free, prior, and informed consent. Renault
Group adopts a tailored approach in each country, reflecting
the unique legal and cultural contexts. [DP S3-2_01; DP S3-
2_03; DP S3-2_06; DP S3-2_07]
Local Public Affairs representatives ensure that local
elected
representatives
and
local
community
representatives
are
properly
informed
about
site
development plans and the creation of new activities. They
are also committed to consider their feedback in the
development process of new solutions and in the conduct of
the Group’s activities. [DP S3-2_04] 
The Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee monitors impact
measurements on a monthly basis, and a summary is
presented to the Ethics and Compliance Committee once a
year. [DP S3-2_01; DP S3-2_05] 
Renault Group is also committed to carrying out effective
engagement with indigenous peoples’ representatives if
actual or potential negative impacts are arising from its
activities or site development projects and to respect the
principle of the free, prior, and informed consent of these
individuals in the case of potential relocation processes, in
line with the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of
Indigenous Peoples. [DP S3-2_02]

2.2.3.3.1.4 S3-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts
and channels for affected communities
to raise concerns 
Based on media monitoring by the Prevention & Protection
Department and through stakeholder engagement with
NGOs, the Sustainability Department raises concerns to the
Sustainable Procurement Department whenever a potential
community issue is detected at suppliers level (for more
information regarding suppliers, see chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.D).
Depending on the case, the Sustainable Procurement
Department can take different types of action regarding the
concerned supplier, including on-site audits. [DP S3-3_10]
Additionally, any individual, including a representative of
affected communities, can confidentially report an issue to
Renault Group via the WhistleB professional alert system.
The issue will be forwarded to the relevant Department for
resolution (See the detailed description in chapter 2.2.4.1.2.2).
[DP S3-3_10 to 13; DP S3-2_14; DP S3-2_15]
2.2.3.3.1.5 S3-4 – Taking action on material impacts on
affected communities, and approaches to
managing material risks and pursuing
material opportunities related to affected
communities, and effectiveness
of those actions
The Group remains committed to ongoing improvement in
its risk management, stakeholder engagement, monitoring,
and compliance mechanisms, such as risk mapping, and on-
site audits, through the implementation and the monitoring
of the Vigilance Plan while positively supporting local
communities. For example, the Group is committed to
develop a specific vigilance plan for batteries and to
contribute to an initiative related to cobalt sourcing as
outlined in the chapter 2.2.3.2.1.1.E.
The Group addresses its material risks and impacts by
requiring all its tier 1 suppliers to endorse the Renault Group
CSR Guidelines for Suppliers, which are currently being
updated to strengthen suppliers’ commitment to caring for
affected communities and to encompass suppliers beyond
tier 1. Time horizons broadly depend on the complexity of the
action plan to be carried out and on the location of the
impact in the value chain. [DP S3-4_08; DP S3-4_12; MDR-
A_03; MDR-A_05]
Additionally, Renault Group identifies the actions needed
and appropriate in response to actual or potential negative
impacts on affected communities through a risk mapping
process, as presented in the Vigilance Plan. [DP S3-4_05]
Whenever a material risk is identified, Renault Group
manages it according to the process outlined in the
Vigilance Plan. Thus, the effectiveness of the initiatives
implemented by Renault Group is measured, in part, by the
number of incidents reported by signatory parties to the
2013 and 2019 GFAs. [DP S3-4_04; DP S3-4_07]
Renault Group is not aware of any serious human rights
incident/violation related to local communities in 2024. [DP
S3-4_11]
The Group mitigates negative impacts on local communities
near its facilities and advances opportunities, contributes to
positive impacts, and provides remedies to material negative
impacts by working closely with public authorities to enhance
job opportunities and foster employment growth, contributing
to the communities’ well-being, and promoting social and local
dialogue. For example, the implementation of training and
skills development programs for local employees aims not only
to improve individuals’ employability but also to enhance the
Group’s competitiveness and innovation by ensuring a pool of
qualified talent to support its future activities. These initiatives
include offering free professional training in Brazil and
providing scholarships in Colombia to enhance local skill sets,
as well as localizing direct and indirect purchasing spend.
Through the implementation of the 2019 GFA, employees are
encouraged
to
collaborate
with
solidarity-based
associations and companies, with the Group ensuring that
such participation complies with local legislations and
recognizing the time spent on these activities as official
working hours. [DP S3-4_01 to 03; DP S3-4_05; DP S3-4_06;
DP S3-4_09; DP S3-4_10; DP S3.MDR-A_13-14; DP MDR-
A_01 to 03]
Such local ongoing actions allow the Group to mitigate
material risks arising from impacts and their effectiveness is
monitored locally. [DP S3.MDR-A_03; DP S3.MDR-A_05]
2.2.3.3.1.6 S3-5 – Targets related to managing
material negative impacts, advancing
positive impacts, and managing material
risks and opportunities
Renault Group tracks the effectiveness of its policies and
actions
in
relation
to
material
sustainability-related
impacts,
risks,
and
opportunities
through
processes
outlined in its Vigilance Plan. Effectiveness of sustainability
policies
and
actions,
including
those
related
to
communities, is monitored through both the Group’s ESG
dashboard and ratings from various external extra-
financial agencies, summarized on Renault Group’s website.
Dashboard and rating changes are reviewed monthly by
the Group’s Sustainability Committee, with specific calls for
internal actions if progress is not considered under control.
[DP S3.MDR-T_14-19]
