Sustainable development
02
2.2.3.4.1 General introduction 
2.2.3.4.1.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
The primary consumers and end-users of Renault Group
consist of individuals who purchase and/or use any vehicle,
whether for personal or commercial purposes, produced or
financed by Renault Group, regardless of whether they drive
it or not. [DP S4.SBM-3_01; DP S4.SBM-3_02; DP S4.SBM-
3_07]
2.2.3.4.1.2 S4-1 – Policies related to consumers
and end-users
Renault Group’s policies are shaped by internal societal
responsibility principles and comply with international
standards, including the Universal Declaration of Human
Rights
and
the
OECD
Guidelines
for
Multinational
Enterprises, the Ten principles of the Global Compact, the
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and
the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights,
and the ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and
Rights at Work. Thus, Renault Group demonstrates its
commitment to human rights through its policy alignment
with these recognized frameworks and its active support of
sustainable development initiatives. To its knowledge,
Renault Group has not received alerts of non-compliance
with international human rights principles or severe human
rights issues and incidents related to its consumers and/or
end-users in 2024 [DP S4-1_02; DP S4-1_03; DP S4-1_06; DP
S4-1_07; DP S4-4_11; DP MDR-P_04]
2.2.3.4.1.3 S4-2 – Processes for engaging with
consumers and end-users about impacts
and S4-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts and
channels for consumers
and end-users to raise concerns
Renault
Group
maintains
ongoing
interactions
with
consumers and end-users at all stages of the product
lifecycle, including pre-purchase, purchase, and post-
purchase. The Group uses a range of communication
channels such as the Renault Group sales network, the
Customer Relations Service, awareness initiatives, product
certifications, its website, commercial events, personal
evaluations, and questionnaires. The frequency of the
dialogue varies and aims to obtain the consumer and end-
users perspectives before and during vehicle use, with
effectiveness measured through customer and press trials,
and market share indicators.  Renault Group’s direct
engagement through sales representatives, dealerships,
and focus groups allows for the customization of products
and services based on consumers’ and end-users’ feedback,
influencing the Group’s decision-making. [DP S4-1_04; DP
S4-2_02; DP S4-2_03; DP S4-2_05]
Consumers and end-users can voice their concerns through
channels such as Renault Group’s website and regular
surveys, which allow the Group to track issues. Promotion of
these channels for raising concerns occurs through social
media, advertising, and partners’ websites. They are also
communicated on the Group’s website and at the time of
vehicle purchase. The complaints are handled confidentially
and anonymously to respect privacy and data protection. In
all interactions with consumers and end-users, Renault
Group
prohibits
any
retaliation.
Confidentiality
of
consumers and end-users is preserved and Renault Group
employees who interact with consumers or end-users are
required to adhere to the Group’s human rights policy. [DP
S4-3_02 to 06]
Consumers’ and end-users’ satisfaction is directly linked to
the Group’s performance. Therefore, Renault Group’s
commitment to provide high-quality customer satisfaction
is essential, which is reflected in the CEO’s compensation
being tied to the level of customer satisfaction achieved.
2.2.3.4.1.4 S4-5 – Targets related to managing
material negative impacts, advancing
positive impacts, and managing
material risks and opportunities
The Group has set a general target to ensure compliance,
aiming to ensure that its operations and products adhere to
all applicable laws and regulations (See chapter 2.2.4.1.8).
Globally, the views of consumers and end-users are
considered in the setting of targets through the results of
stakeholder dialogue. However, they are not involved in
tracking
performance,
or
identifying
potential
improvements, as these activities are strictly internal to
Renault Group. [DP S4-5_01 to 03]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
213

## Page 216

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.4.2 Social inclusion of consumers
and/or end-users
2.2.3.4.2.1 Financial inclusion
A. SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities
and their interaction with strategy and business model
This section focuses on consumers and end-users with
limited means and therefore rely on products affordability.
The Group acknowledges that the cost of individual mobility
can particularly threaten the inclusion of disadvantaged
individuals in society. As a result, Renault Group strives to
provide enhanced attention to these consumers and end-
users. [DP S4.SBM-3_01; DP S4.SBM-3_03]
Renault Group has identified the negative impact of
unaffordable mobility or loss of resources for low-income
customers. This is mainly due to unsuitable offers of
products or services, or to unsuitable financing offers, or to
the refusal of traditional financial institutions to fund or
insure certain activities.
The Group also acknowledges the risk of revenue loss
associated with a specific segment of insolvent customers
or those engaging in fraudulent activities. [DP S4.SBM-3_04;
DP S4.SBM-3_06; S4.SBM-3_08]
Renault Group has taken the following actions to prevent,
mitigate, and remediate material negative impacts on
consumers and end-users related to social exclusion:
●Design and deployment of the CareMakers programs,
developed by Renault Group, which give access to a
permanent
social
leasing
offer
in
France,
and
progressively in Europe, independently from the social
leasing programs below;
●Application of public social leasing programs, when
available in the countries where the Group operates (like
Italy
and
France)
demonstrates
Renault
Group’s
commitment to making electric mobility accessible to as
many people as possible. Renault Group has expanded its
involvement in the government’s social leasing program
to include more models. However, these measures are
occasional due to their dependency on public funding;
●Conception and sale of services by Mobilize Financial
Services (M.F.S.);
●Development of vehicle ranges that are accessible to
people with financial difficulties.
B. S4-1 – Policies related to consumers and end-users
Renault Group is committed to making transportation more
inclusive for people from all social backgrounds, as part of its
sustainable development strategy, which supports the
Renaulution
plan
with
the
“Caring
Company”
pillar,
encompassing
the
inclusive
mobility
topic
linked
to
CareMakers (for more details on CareMakers, see chapter
2.2.3.4.2.1.D). The Group commits to increasing the beneficiaries
of its inclusive mobility solutions (for more details on this
commitment, see chapter 2.2.3.4.2.1.E). Renault Group’s strategy
on social inclusion is a wide-ranging collaborative process
involving the Leadership Team, the Board of Directors,
employees and external stakeholders such as consumers and
end-users. Renault Group’s VP Sustainability is responsible for
the implementation of the inclusive mobility offer initiatives as
part of the “Caring Company” pillar. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-
P_01 to 03]
C. S4-2 – Processes for engaging with consumers and
end-users about impacts and S4-3 – Processes to
prevent, mitigate and remediate negative impacts
and channels for consumers and end-users to raise
concerns
Mobilize F.S. engages with consumers and end-users through
annual studies and surveys to inform its decisions and manage
material impacts, including concerns about information and
data privacy. These studies help define the Net Promoter Score
(NPS) across all countries. The Director of Marketing and
Strategy, and the Director of Sales and Operations of Mobilize
F.S. ensure that consumer engagement informs the Group’s
approach. Ipsos, a French survey company, confirms the
effectiveness
of
this
engagement
by
assessing
the
representativeness of the studies, specifying the scope,
samples, and number of respondents. Although there are no
dedicated studies focusing on vulnerable individuals, the
verbatim collected during studies could help identify specific
cases needing attention. However, no such verbatim has been
collected so far. [DP S4-2_01 to 06]
Mobilize F.S. provides consumers with multiple contact
channels (letters, emails, internet, or client spaces) to raise
concerns,
communicated
at
the
beginning
of
the
relationship.
Complaints
are
handled
confidentially,
respecting privacy and data protection rights, with the
option for consumers to remain anonymous. [DP S4-3_02;
S4-3_03; S4-3_06]
CareMakers clients are consulted for their feedback, which
helps the Group adapt and refine its offerings. The program
collaborates with each brand’s operational Departments in
France, Spain and Italy to adjust the pricing policy and check
the vehicles availability. Engagement occurs both directly
through the CareMakers website and indirectly through
prescribers such as France Travail or Adie. Customer
feedback is monitored throughout the process and
reviewed monthly. The VP Sustainability has the operational
responsibility to ensure this engagement occurs. The types
of engagements include consultations and surveys. The
effectiveness of engagement is assessed by the Mobility
Club, which conducts reporting and weekly monitoring to
implement corrective actions, with monthly discussions to
adjust the offer and yearly reporting to the Annual General
Meeting of the Mobility Club. [DP S4-2_01 to 06; DP S4-3_01;
DP S4-3_02; DP S4-3_04; DP S4-3_05]

Sustainable development
02
D. S4-4 – Taking action on material impacts on
consumers and end-users, and approaches to
managing material risks and pursuing material
opportunities related to consumers and end-users,
and effectiveness of those actions
Renault Group takes actions to prevent, mitigate, and
remediate material negative impacts on consumers and
end-users
related
to
social
inclusion
through
the
CareMakers programs, its Mobilize F.S. branch and the
development of vehicle ranges that are accessible to
people with financial difficulties.
The CareMakers MDP Mobility program is dedicated to
fostering inclusive mobility solutions by offering micro-
loans. This initiative offers affordable vehicle leasing to help
economically
vulnerable
individuals
in
France
gain
employment mobility and thus provides them with the
financial means to secure or retain employment. The
program is set to expand to include deferred payment
options for people starting new jobs across Europe. The
Group tracks the number of beneficiaries and assesses the
impact through surveys. This program is promoted through
social media and advertising campaigns and is integrated
into the websites of partnering associations. This program is
an ongoing process and applies to all prospect customers.
These credit solutions may be facilitated through MFS. [DP
S4-3_04; DP S4-4_01; DP S4-4_02; DP S4-4_06; DP S4-4_07;
DP S4-4_10; DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
The
CareMakers
Invest
program
supports
social
entrepreneurs who are pioneering mobility solutions. The
program is strengthened by the financial support of Renault
Group, demonstrating a commitment to social innovation
and sustainable mobility. Nearly 83,000 people, including
approximately 24,000 vulnerable people, have gained better
access to mobility as a result of the products and services
provided by companies supported by CareMakers Invest.
These actions are continuous and apply to all downstream
prospect customers in Renault Group’s value chain. [DP
MDR-A_01 to 03; DP MDR-A_05]
In order to identify appropriate actions in response to
negative impacts on consumers and end-users, a study was
carried out prior to the start of the project in partnership
with the Karidea think tank. Renault Group continues to
collaborate with this professional association, and regular
follow-up discussions are organized to keep the study up to
date. [DP S4-4_05]
In addition, through Mobilize Financial Services, Renault Group
actively supports low-income households by offering social
leasing. This includes affordability for families, young drivers,
unemployed individuals, and tailored financing options for
customized vehicles for individuals with disabilities. Mobilize
Financial Services is actively involved in initiatives to prevent
over-indebtedness and to identify clients who are financially
vulnerable, across all its activities and geographies. This
involves putting in place robust measures and strategies to
monitor and assess the financial health of its customers. In
doing so, Mobilize Financial Services can proactively detect
early signs of financial difficulty and offer timely support and
advice to those in need. This commitment to financial
wellbeing means that customers are better equipped to
manage their finances responsibly and avoid the pitfalls of
excessive debt. Mobilize Financial Services has also developed
a comprehensive training program designed to provide its
staff with the skills and knowledge needed to identify
customers in financial difficulty. In France, Mobilize Financial
Services collaborates with Cresus (fondationcresus.org), a
renowned organization that works to prevent and manage
financial
difficulties.
This
partnership
strengthens
the
company’s support for its financially vulnerable customers.
Finally, Mobilize Financial Services offers struggling customers
the possibility of extending their financing contracts, providing
them with more time and flexibility to meet their financial
commitments. [DP MDR-A_01 to 03; DP S4-4_04; DP S4-4_08]
Finally, Renault Group has always been committed to
offering the best value for money in vehicle ranges, while
ensuring that its range includes models that are financially
accessible. In the electric vehicle market, Renault Group
offers vehicles at affordable and competitive prices.
These actions aim to serve all the Group’s consumers and
end-users worldwide. Renault Group approaches each
occurrence on a case-by-case basis due to their great
diversity. [DP S4-4_05]
E. S4-5 – Targets related to managing material negative
impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing
material risks and opportunities
To reduce its negative impacts on consumers and end-users
related to financial inclusion, Renault Group aims for a
cumulated 4,000 lease-to-own arrangements by 2025.
Moreover, CareMakers aims to reach 10,000 beneficiaries of
its inclusive mobility solutions by 2030. The targets are set
based on the ability to complete cases and are shared with
referrers and partners for consultation. Performance
against these targets is discussed at the Annual General
Meeting of the Mobility Club and integrated into Renault
Group’s
sustainability
dashboard.
These
targets
are
calculated in absolute terms and applicable to the global
own operations of CareMakers. The targets were set taking
into account the resources allocated to these programs by
the company. The performance against these targets is
measured on a yearly basis, through CareMakers Invest
impact analysis. [DP S4.MDR-T_01; DP MDR-T_01 to 05; DP
MDR-T_07 to 09; DP MDR-T_13]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
215

## Page 218

Renault Group
Sustainable development
The resources to support CareMakers Mobility’s actions to
reach 10,000 beneficiaries by 2030 are estimated at an
average annual OpEx of more than €2 M over the next six
years. As CareMakers Invest’s purpose is to invest in innovative
companies in the field of inclusive mobility, its actions are
neither OpEx nor CapEx. [DP S4-4_12; DP MDR-A_06]
2.2.3.4.2.2 Product accessibility
A. SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities
and their interaction with strategy and business model
Renault Group acknowledges the importance of social
inclusion as part of its sustainability strategy. The Group’s
approach
on
product
accessibility
encompasses
all
consumers and end-users of its vehicles, with special
emphasis on individuals for whom standard models may not
be suitable, such as individuals with disabilities. Renault
Group is committed to meeting the needs of consumers with
disabilities through its products and services, recognizing
that these consumers and end-users rely on accessible
solutions. Here, consumers and end-users are dependent on
the products available. [DP S4.SBM-3_01 to 03]
Mobility is crucial for enabling consumers and end-users to
engage in their daily activities, providing them with the
means to fulfil their needs. Renault Group has the duty to
provide inclusive solutions that cater to all consumers and
end-users, ensuring that everyone has access to tailored
mobility options. Qstomize is responsible for the design,
manufacturing, and distribution of conversion kits for
vehicles from Renault Group and those of its partners,
catering to both professional and private clients while
adhering to the Group’s quality standards. Renault Group
has noted that its activities related to Qstomize can improve
the usability of products and services for consumers and
end-users with disabilities, wherever Qstomize is active. [DP
S4.SBM-3_05; DP S4.SBM-3_08]
B. S4-1 – Policies related to consumers and end-users
Renault Group is committed to making transport more
inclusive for all, including for people with disabilities, as part
of its sustainable development strategy, which supports the
Renaulution plan, through its “Caring Company” pillar. This
approach focuses on combating discrimination and
upholding
Renault
Group’s
tradition
of
societal
responsibility, and to facilitate inclusion through mobility via
its Qstomize program. This strategy pillar covers the own
operations and downstream value chain of the Group. It is a
wide-ranging
collaborative
process
involving
the
Leadership Team, the Board of Directors, the employees and
external stakeholders such as consumers and end-users.
Renault Group’s VP Sustainability is responsible for the
implementation of the “Caring Company” pillar. [DP S4-
1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 03]
Qstomize recently unveiled its “Qstomize 2025: New
Ambitions, Together” strategy, emphasizing expanding its
product range and extending its geographical reach,
including the development of its inclusive mobility solutions
portfolio. The scope of this strategy extends to all the
brands of Renault Group, and of its partners. The Qstomize
VP is responsible for its application. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-
P_01 to 03]
C. S4-2 – Processes for engaging with consumers and
end-users about impacts
Customers requiring inclusive adjustments can visit their
Renault garage to report needs, which are escalated to the
Qstomize customer service if necessary. Qstomize regularly
attends
major
inclusive
mobility
motor
shows,
like
“Autonomic Paris”, to present its wheelchair-accessible
vehicles to customers and associations. This offers
customers and associations a channel of expression that is
useful to Renault Group. [DP S4-1_04]
Qstomize has also been a partner of the Fédération
Française Handisport (French parasport federation) for
nearly 20 years, enabling it to maintain a constant and
direct contact with key stakeholders in the inclusion of
disabled individuals. [DP S4-1_04; DP S4-4_03]
During new vehicle launches or commercial events,
Qstomize organizes meetings with customers. Discussion
groups are formed to gather customer opinions, and
testimonials are collected to better understand their
experiences and needs. [DP S4-1_04; DP S4-2_01]
Dialogue
with
customers
is
continuous,
given
the
operational nature of Qstomize, encompassing various
occasions such as stakeholder meetings, new vehicle
launches, commercial events, consultations and customer
feedback. Qstomize ensures to maintain close proximity to
consumers and end-users due to the nature of its products
that require constant enhancement. Because of its nature,
Qstomize deals with potentially vulnerable or marginalized
consumers and end-users, making customer engagement
inherently focused on understanding the perspectives of
these groups of individuals. [DP S4-2_02; DP S4-2_03; DP
S4-2_06]
To assess the effectiveness of its engagement with
consumers and end-users, a customer satisfaction survey is
conducted annually for each client typology, including
internal clients, commercial networks, and end-customers.
[DP S4-2_05]
The Qstomize VP holds the operational responsibility for
ensuring that this client engagement occurs and that the
results are taken into account within the Group’s approach.
[DP S4-2_04]

D. S4-4 – Taking action on material impacts on
consumers and end-users, and approaches to
managing material risks and pursuing material
opportunities related to consumers and end-users,
and effectiveness of those actions
Qstomize manufactures and distributes conversion kits for
vehicles from Renault Group and its partners. [DP S4-4_02;
DP MDR-A_01]
In addition to adaptations for people with reduced mobility,
Qstomize also offers conversions for passenger cars, such
as special series and driving school vehicles, as well as
interior fittings, protections, and equipment kits for light
commercial vehicles. Customized layouts are available for
business fleets, meeting the specific needs of customers.
[DP MDR-A_01]
All conversions are carried out at 13 production sites located
near Renault Group factories in France, Spain, Slovenia,
Türkiye,
Romania,
and
Morocco.
These
actions
are
continuous. [DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03]
The number of customized vehicles (300,000 per year) allows
for
the
measurement
of
impact
of
these
actions,
demonstrating the availability and effectiveness of Qstomize’s
procedures across Renault Group’s factories in the EMEIA
(Europe, Middle East, India, Africa) region. [DP S4-4_04]
E. S4-5 – Targets related to managing material negative
impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing
material risks and opportunities
The effectiveness of these actions is evaluated from a
commercial perspective by annual sales of customized
vehicles and the Group’s market position as the sole provider
in Europe offering vehicles for individuals with limited
mobility. Qstomize responds continuously to the needs
expressed by consumers and end-users. Through Qstomize,
Renault Group intends to maintain its leading position in this
market by committing to inclusivity. [DP MDR-T]
2.2.3.4.3 Personal safety of consumers
and end-users
2.2.3.4.3.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
Renault Group acknowledges the importance of consumers’
and end-users’ safety as a cornerstone of its sustainability
strategy. The Group understands that product safety is
instrumental for not only individuals and businesses but for
the general public as road users. [DP S4.SBM-3_02]
For 50 years, Renault Group has been collaborating with the
LAB (laboratory of accidentology, biomechanics and driver
behavior) to enhance road safety. The work on accident
analysis primarily involves documenting accident statistics to
identify benchmark scenarios for Advanced Driver Assistance
Systems (ADAS) that are active prior to an accident, as well as
actual accident configurations for passive safety measures.
Biomechanics studies focus on human tolerance limits,
dummy behavior, and global human body simulation. The
driver behaviours studies include driving styles and types, to
better understand Renault Group’s consumers and end-users
and their use of active safety systems like ADAS. The driver
behaviours studies conducted by the LAB have revealed that
certain consumer and end-user groups are more vulnerable,
including younger, less experienced drivers, older drivers, and
parents with young children. This has led to considerations for
additional safety measures, such as efforts to improve
restraint systems, preventative strategies targeting younger
individuals, and modifications to airbag designs, among
others. The Group is aware of the possible adverse effects on
consumer health, including potential systemic health impact
on consumers and end-users due to non-compliant products
(stemming from hazardous driving environments). Renault
Group is also aware of the risk of remediation costs, penalties
and reputational damage due to non-compliant products. [DP
S4.SBM-3_04; DP S4.SBM-3_06 to 08]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
217

## Page 220

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.4.3.2 S4-1 – Policies related to consumers
and end-users
Road safety is one of the three pillars of the sustainable
development strategy and feeds into Renault Group’s long-
term commitment to more responsible mobility. Renault
Group’s road safety policy is designed to manage its
impacts and risks related to consumers and end-users and
feeds into Renault Group’s long-term commitment to more
responsible mobility. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01]
Renault Group has made a twofold commitment:
●Through its products, by incorporating solutions and
innovations into all its design, manufacturing and marketing
processes in order to protect vehicle occupants and others
exposed to road risks (pedestrians, cyclists, etc.);
●By working with governments and other stakeholders in
helping improve road users’ safety and raise awareness
of this issue.
The Group’s safety approach encompasses four key areas
(prevent, correct, protect, and rescue) to address safety before,
during and after an accident:
●Prevent: prevention involves helping drivers to anticipate
risks by providing them with driving aids. Renault Group
also encourages more responsible driving. Drivers need to
understand the limits beyond which they will be
incapable of controlling their vehicles, and the situations
in which they may put themselves at risk;
●Correct: the quality of road handling and braking
constitutes the fundamental dynamics of the vehicle,
which is instrumental in avoiding accidents. Nevertheless,
there are situations where technology could intervene to
compensate for driver error. This is the purpose of the
active safety system. It intervenes in difficult or
emergency conditions; however, it does not replace the
driver, who remains responsible for their driving;
●Protect: a top priority of road-user safety for Renault
Group is to protect the vehicle’s occupants according to
the nature and severity of the impact, regardless of their
age, size, location in the vehicle, or size of the latter.
Renault Group thus equips both the front and rear seats of
its vehicles with systems to provide optimal passenger
protection.
The
protection
of
other
road
users
(pedestrians, cyclists, etc.) is also considered;
●Rescue: the ambition of this pillar is to optimize the after-
accident response. Renault Group collaborates with
emergency services to optimize help for accident
casualties. These emergency services receive guides and
attend information sessions explaining how to perform
rescue operations on each new vehicle of the brand. They
are also provided with late-model vehicles on which they
can practice victim extraction methods. Innovative
modifications are applied to the brand’s vehicles to
ensure that emergency services interventions are safer
and more effective. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01]
The policy is tailored to the various brands within the Group
across Europe and to the different geographical areas
depending on constraints and local legislations. The policy is
informed by accident data from the LAB, which provides
general statistics and case studies. [DP MDR-P_02]
Communication on the Group’s safety approach primarily
involves
the
equipment
list,
such
as
Airbags,
Belt
pretensioning systems/load limiters, Isofix, Seat Belt
Reminders (alerts triggered when seatbelts are not
fastened), Electronic Stability Control, Driver Monitoring,
Speed Limiter, AEB (Advanced Emergency Braking) Car to
Car (to reduce the risks of collision with other vehicles,
whether stationary or moving), AEB Vulnerable Users, etc.
Moreover,
the
“Human
First“
program
highlights
differentiators such as Safety Score and Safety Coach for
safe driving, QRescue (QR code providing emergency
services with instant access to a vehicle’s technical details,
even without network coverage), and Fireman Access for
post-crash sequences. The equipment list is known to
private customers and supports fleet tender responses in
addition to the New Car Assessment Program (NCAP) rating.
[DP MDR-P_06]
This policy concerns all Renault Group’s activities, including
own operations (vehicle manufacturing, safety system
integration, etc.) as well as upstream and downstream
(working with stakeholders to target areas for improvement,
training in accident response for the Group brands’ vehicles,
but also continuous improvement thanks to the data received).
The VP Cross Carline Software, EE & Innovation oversees the
implementation of the policy. [DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03]
The consideration given to key stakeholders in setting
policies
involves
work
conducted
by
the
Customer
Intelligence Department and the Group‘s relationships with
driving schools (ECF) and working groups at the United
Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) on road
safety. [DP MDR-P_05]
The implementation of the policy is aligned with several
initiatives and commitments from Renault Group. In 2022,
the LAB’s experts began a three-year participation to a
European collaborative project, Horizon 2021 (V4SAFETY),
steered
a
European
industrial
project
(ENOP)
and
participated in two projects of the French interministerial
delegation for road-user safety (USCOMA and THORAX).
Renault Group made a commitment to the United Nations
Special Envoy for Road Safety to promote safer mobility in
2024. [DP MDR-P_04]

02
2.2.3.4.3.3 S4-2 – Processes for engaging
with consumers and end-users
about impacts
Renault Group’s engagement with consumers and end-
users is informed by their perspectives and feedback, which
are considered in the deployment of the Group’s safety
policy. Interactions regarding safety are both direct and
indirect through the LAB studies, which evaluate the
performance of safety systems based on accident analysis.
[DP S4-2_01]
The following Departments are involved in the collection of
consumers’ and end-users’ perspectives and the engagement
and monitoring of issues raised:
●The Industry Quality and Accidentology Department
directly and permanently collects end-users’ feedback
through various channels, such as website (feedback or
complaints),
e-mails
(surveys),
and
the
On-Board
Diagnostics reporting system (vehicle data usage);
●The Engineering Division directly collects feedback from
rescuers through information sessions several times a year;
●The Customer Intelligence Department directly collects
information on car safety as it is perceived by consumers
and end-users. [DP S4-1_04; DP S4-2_01 to 03; DP S4-
3_03; DP S4-3_04]
It is the responsibility of their VPs to ensure that the above
information has been brought to the attention of Renault
Group’s Car Safety Expert Leaders, who frame and refine
policies. The Expert Leaders and the Chief Technical Officer
are
responsible
for
the
engagement
to
happen.
[DP S4-2_04]
Additionally, indirect interactions occur with various
stakeholders concerned with car safety: public authorities,
civil society, and driving schools. [DP S4-1_04; S4-2_01; DP
S4-2_02]
To gain insight into the perspectives of consumers and end-
users that may be particularly vulnerable to impacts, studies
are conducted on road accidents involving children, and on
consumer perception and understanding of safety. [DP S4-
2_06]
2.2.3.4.3.4 S4-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts
and channels for consumers
and end-users to raise concerns
To remediate negative impacts, Renault Group has
developed actions in the “Rescue” pillar of its road-user
safety policy. To improve the rescue of road victims, guides
and training sessions are provided for rescue operations on
new vehicles, and late-model vehicles are supplied for
practice. Innovations such as the QRescue (QR code
allowing rescuers to promptly access vehicles’ technical
information) and Fireman Access technology (allowing a
prompt access to batteries in case of fire) enhance and
expedite rescue operations. [DP S4-3_01]
Concerns
collected
by
the
Industry
Quality
and
Accidentology, Engineering and Customer Intelligence
Departments are also factored to refine these policies (See
chapter 2.2.3.4.3.3). [DP S4-1_04; DP S4-2_01; DP S4-2_02;
DP S4-3_02]
The Group maintains regular contact with consumers and
end-users, thereby ensuring they are aware of the
engagement channels. The Group can therefore easily
obtain feedback and track the effectiveness of these
channels. [DP S4-3_03; DP S4-3_05]
2.2.3.4.3.5 S4-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on consumers and end-users,
and approaches to managing material
risks and pursuing material opportunities
related to consumers and end-users,
and effectiveness of those actions
Regarding its road safety policy, Renault Group has also
implemented actions for the “prevent”, “correct” and
“protect” pillars:
●Safe driving: preventive safety features are proposed by the
teams in charge of product delivery and customer
satisfaction and designed by the Engineering Division. The
aim is to improve driving behaviours and provide ADAS such
as Safety Score and Safety Coach. The Safety Coach
program deploys car safety features that are innovative,
ahead of regulations and addressing the top three root
causes of accidents (speed, use of substances, distraction);
●Crash avoidance: before a crash, ADAS step in to avoid or
reduce severity of accidents. For example, advanced
emergency braking is a feature that detects a risk of
imminent collision and then alerts the driver through a
Human-Machine
Interface
and,
if
needed,
can
automatically activate the braking system. Similarly, the
Emergency
Lane
Keeping
system
prevents
lane
departure and risk of lateral collision by controlling the
steering wheel. These systems are constantly improved
to extend use cases and tested by accredited external
laboratories. Once a product or service is on the market,
Renault Group monitors customer feedback to analyze
and correct any potential safety issues;
●Crash protection: to protect the users of its vehicles, Renault
Group relies on the LAB’s studies to improve structural
behavior and enrich the equipment of its vehicles.
Vulnerable road users (pedestrians, cyclists, etc.) are also
considered with front design evolutions. [DP MDR-A_01; DP
S4-4_01 to 03; DP S4-4_10]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
219

## Page 222

Renault Group
Sustainable development
In order to mitigate the risk of remediation costs, penalties or
reputation damage due to non-compliant products, Renault
Group’s commitment to product safety includes the following
principles:
●Market only safe products and services: Renault Group is
committed to ensuring that all the products and services
it markets are safe for its consumers and end-users. The
technical content of its vehicles is compliant with
regulations. NCAP tests are performed to better cover the
real causes of accidents;
●Monitor product and services: once on the market,
Renault Group actively monitors products and services in
order to adequately identify any potential safety issues;
●Addressing safety issues: in the event that a product or
service is found to be unsafe, despite Renault Group’s
rigorous preventive measures, the Group promptly
implements decisive actions to rectify the issue and
ensure the highest standards of safety are met;
●Inform customers about potential safety risks: Renault
Group
has
developed
robust
strategies
for
communicating with its customers to ensure that they are
informed quickly and clearly of any safety-related recalls
or actions through various communication channels;
●Recall vehicles: whenever a recurring defect with an impact
on user safety is identified by the Quality & Customer
Satisfaction Department, a recall campaign is organized to
eliminate the root causes. [DP S4-4_08; DP MDR-A_01]
Renault Group evaluates the deployment rate of safety
systems across its vehicle line-up, ensuring they are utilized
properly by consumers and end-users, and analyzing accident
data on an annual basis to gauge the real-world impact of
these
features.
Feedback
from
emergency
services,
particularly regarding the QRescue system, is also a critical
component of this assessment. The Group’s involvement in
firefighter extrication training sessions provides valuable
insights into the practical application of these safety systems.
The Group’s structure also allows the expertise to be adapted
to each topic, with an Expert Leader responsible for each of the
four types of safety: road safety, safety on driver assistance
systems and vehicle automation, secondary and tertiary
safety in the event of an accident or rescue, and operational
safety. [DP S4-4_04 to 07]
These actions are carried out on an ongoing basis, are
applicable to the Group’s own operations on all its sites (in
partnership with emergency services) and benefit to vehicle
users (through the ADAS made available for instance).
[DP MDR-A_02; MDR-R_03]
Renault Group’s actions with respect to vehicle safety are an
integral part to its conception process (Security by Design).
Thus, apart from very specific programs such as E-Call, the
European Commission initiative to assist road-accident
victims, accrued for €34 M in the books, the Group does not
segregate these projects within its investments, whether in
R&D or in manufacturing. [DP S4-4_12; DP MDR-A_06]
In other respects, whenever, despite its best efforts, Renault
Group identifies a security defect requiring a vehicle recall
campaign to remedy such defect, the corresponding costs
are monitored by the Quality Department. Such operations
amount to €26 M in 2024. [DP S4-4_12; DP MDR-A_06]
2.2.3.4.3.6 S4-5 – Targets related to managing
material negative impacts, advancing
positive impacts, and managing material
risks and opportunities
The Safety Coach program’s target is to achieve 70%
coverage of accident root causes in Europe by 2030. The
roadmap to reach this target has been discussed with
representatives from driving schools and insurers. This
process will be deployed across all the Group’s markets. [DP
S4.MDR-T_01; DP S4-5_01; DP MDR-T_02; DP MDR-T_03;
DP MDR-T_07]
This target is tracked by indicators at the level of the
Sustainability Department and fed by the information
reported from the field. It is established with the support of
the Group’s expertise and business lines. The target level is
subject to strict evaluations, through tests performed either
internally or by accredited laboratories. For example, for
regulatory purposes, the tests are carried out in accredited
safety test labs and in Renault Group technical centers. On
the consumer side, the tests are carried out in the laboratories
designated by the consumer body (for instance, Euro NCAP
for Europe). This process is deployed in all the Group’s
markets. [DP S4-5_01; S4-5_02; DP MDR-T_04]
Renault Group leverages insights from the LAB and
feedback from the rescuers to learn possible lessons and
identify potential improvements related to this target. [DP
S4-5_03]

2.2.3.4.4 Data privacy
2.2.3.4.4.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
Renault Group is committed to preventing potential
negative impacts that its operations, products, and services
could have on the privacy of its consumers and end-users,
including
its
individual
and
professional
customers,
prospects, employees and candidates. While no specific
category of individuals was identified as vulnerable in
relation to Renault Group’s products and services from a
data privacy perspective, certain connected services may
process sensitive personal information, such as geolocation
data, necessitating privacy impact assessments to ensure
the protection of affected individuals. Consumer personal
information requests (access, delete and correct) related to
connected services require Renault Group to access a
substantial amount of data, depending on the connected
service
used,
and
to
provide
them
with
detailed
explanations. Consequently, responding to these requests
may take longer than for non-connected services personal
data. [DP S4.SBM-3_02; DP S4.SBM-3_03; DP S4.SBM-3_07;
DP S4.SBM-3_08]
Renault Group is aware that it could have a negative impact
due to the misuse of its consumers’ and end-users’ personal
data. The Group has identified the risk of remediation costs
and/or penalties and/or reputational damage due to non-
compliance
with
data
protection
regulations
or
malfunctions, inadequate organization, or insufficient
security of information systems. [DP S4.SBM-3_04; DP
S4.SBM-3_06]
2.2.3.4.4.2 S4-1 – Policies related to consumers
and end-users
Renault Group has a Group-level data protection policy
which is adapted to each subsidiary and country where it
operates, taking into account the specificities of each. [DP
S4-1_01; DP S4.MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_02]
The data protection policies describe the personal data
processing implemented by Renault Group concerning its
customers, prospects, employees, and candidates. Renault
Group’s data protection policies are based on compliance
with the requirements of the General Data Protection
Regulation (GDPR) or other equivalent regulations and apply
in all countries where Renault Group is present, including
outside the European Union. The Group Data Protection
Officer, the Legal Department, and local Data Protection
Officers oversee the implementation of the personal data
protection compliance systems deployed. The policies are
accessible on the internet, on Renault Group’s applications
and are mentioned in the documentation given to
customers, prospects, employees and candidates.
Mobilize Financial Services is committed to upholding the
privacy and protection of personal data across all its
operations in alignment with Renault Group’s data
protection policy, which is designed to safeguard IT assets
where personal data is stored. In addition, Mobilize Financial
Services
complies
with
others
specific
regulations
applicable to financial companies such as banking secrecy.
Mobilize Financial Services has established a personal data
processing policy, which is closely monitored from the
design stage and supported by regular awareness training
that is provided to staff to reinforce the importance of data
protection. This policy is subject to ongoing regulatory
updates and is aligned with GDPR requirements applicable
to the entire Group. The Group conducts first and second-
level controls to ensure adherence to related GDPR
procedures
and
implements
the
principle
of
data
minimization to restrict the collection of personal data to
what it is strictly necessary. A Data Protection Officer (DPO)
was appointed for ensuring compliance with GDPR and
overseeing the governance for the protection of consumer
and employee data. This policy is adapted to local
regulations in countries outside the GDPR scope, and is
aligned
with
internationally
recognized
instruments,
including the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human
Rights. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 04, DP MDR-P_06]
2.2.3.4.4.3 S4-2 – Processes for engaging with
consumers and end-users about impacts
Renault Group takes the perceptions of consumers and end-
users into account in its activities, decisions and strategy
through compliance with the GDPR (or other equivalent
regulations). [DP S4-2_01]
Interactions with consumers and and-users are direct,
enabling them to exercise their personal information rights
(access, modification, deletion, portability) with Renault
Group at any time, as defined by the GDPR. These rights can
also be exercised through each country’s data privacy
protection authority, such as the Commission Nationale de
l’Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) in France. The Group
Data Protection Officer and/or the entities’ Data Protection
Officers within Renault Group manage the process, produce
metrics and report alerts in case of compliance risks.
However, as it applies the GDPR (or other equivalent
regulations), Renault Group does not assess the efficiency of
its interactions with its consumers and end-users. The
protection of personal data is a right that applies to
everyone in the same way and Renault Group ensures it is
properly applied. [DP S4-1_04; DP S4-2_02 to 06]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
221

## Page 224

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.4.4.4 S4-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate and
remediate negative impacts and channels
for consumers and end-users
to raise concerns
Renault Group recognizes the potential negative impact of a
misuse of personal information, thus impacting the right to
privacy and confidentiality. No negative impact emanating
from the processing of personal data has been identified.
Additionally, some personal data processing implemented
by the Group has a positive impact such as improving road
safety. Renault Group initiates an analysis process with the
Legal, IT Security, Risks Management, and Communication
Departments to determine the appropriate action plan in
case a negative impact would occur, prioritizing the
protection
of
consumers.
Renault
Group
prioritizes
continuously strengthening the compliance of existing
processes, improving employee training, and patching
identified risks. [DP S4-3_01]
Channels for raising concerns are accessible on Renault
Group’s website and in any documentation, contractual or not,
dedicated to consumers and end-users. These channels are
subject to daily monitoring by channel and type of request.
Reporting is carried out every week for complex requests, then
summarized in a report. Consumers and end-users can contact
the data privacy protection authority of their country for an
investigation if they are dissatisfied with the responses
provided by Renault Group. The Group does not assess
whether consumers and/or end-users are aware of these
channels, as these types of channels are already specified in
the GDPR. It does not assess either the level of trust of the
consumers in these structures or processes to raise their
concerns or needs and have them addressed. In EU countries,
consumers can file a complaint and assert their rights without
facing
any
retaliation
or
negative
consequences.
[DP S4-3_02 to 06]
2.2.3.4.4.5 S4-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on consumers and end-users,
and approaches to managing material
risks and pursuing material opportunities
related to consumers and end-users,
and effectiveness of those actions
To prevent, mitigate, or remedy potential negative impacts,
Renault Group prioritizes the continuous advancement of
information systems’ security and organization. The Group has
developed a data breach analysis methodology allowing to
assess the data breach risk and/or to limit its impact. People
whose personal data has been breached are informed in
compliance with GDPR rules. When a breach is identified, the
Legal, IT Security, Risks Management, and Communication
Departments will drive the appropriate remediation plan. This
collaboration ensures that the Group not only complies with
GDPR and other relevant regulations but also prioritizes the
protection of consumers. The Group’s commitment is to
safeguard the interests of those affected by the breach,
ensuring that they receive the necessary information and
support to protect their personal data. [DP MDR-A_01; DP S4-
4_01; DP S4-4_02; DP S4-4_08; DP S4-4_10; DP S4-4_11]
The Group has strengthened its resources allocated to
the management of data privacy in compliance with the
GDPR with the appointment of a Data Protection Officer
at Group level, a Data Protection Officer per subsidiary, a
Privacy Ambassador per corporate Department, and
business relays in each function, supported by legal
advisers. To improve the handling of customer requests,
an
update
of
privacy
forms
is
underway,
with
implementation planned for 2025. User satisfaction
measurements will be carried out starting in 2026. [DP S4-
4_01; DP S4-4_12; DP MDR-A_01]
The Group ensures that its own operations don’t contribute
to negative impacts through internal processes which only
collect data which is strictly necessary (Privacy by Design). It
ensures that all employees, including project managers, are
aware of this process. Each project collecting personal data
must be validated by IT security (Security by Design).
Renault Group is deploying a new charter for the use of IT
resources and digital tools, updating the security master
plan, training employees in information protection and
cyber risks, and has implemented an e-learning training on
the protection of personal data for all new hires since 2020.
[DP S4-4_01; DP S4-4_05; DP S4-4_07; DP S4-4_10; DP
MDR-A_01]
To generate positive impacts, Renault Group focuses on
strengthening
compliance
with
existing
processes,
improving employee training and updating the forms for
exercising rights in order to improve the handling of
customer requests. While results are not communicated to
consumers and end-users, the outcomes of action plans are
monitored
within
the
Group
by
various
oversight
committees. Renault Group is continuously developing
activities linked to connectivity and data (mobility services,
connected and autonomous vehicles) that create additional
impacts
on
consumers
and
end-users.
The
Group
participates in working groups organized by the CNIL and
the
European
Automobile
Manufacturers
Association
(ACEA) and takes all necessary precautions to ensure the
secure processing of personal data. [DP S4-4_03; DP S4-
4_06; DP MDR-A_01]
To track the effectiveness of its data privacy actions, the
Group has a project analysis methodology and relies on
statistics. The compliance and effectiveness of the personal
data protection system are controlled by internal control
campaigns, audits carried out by internal audit and project
steering committees. [DP S4-4_04; DP S4-4_07]
Current and future actions address all operations of the
Group wherever it operates. The GDPR was introduced in
2016, and since then the Group has been taking continuous
action to ensure it remains compliant. [DP MDR-A_02; DP
MDR-A_03]

2.2.3.4.5 Information-related impacts
for consumers and/or end-users
2.2.3.4.5.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
Renault Group is dedicated to transparency regarding the
potential effects of its operations on all consumers and end-
users who may be affected. Consumers and end-users
require precise and accurate information on products and
services to prevent the possibility of harmful usage.
[DP S4.SMB-3_02; DP S4.SBM-3_03]
Renault Group has identified a material risk linked to the
improper management of customer complaints and other
requests, or to the non-adequacy of products and services offer,
advertising, and selling practices with customers’ interests, in
particular for Mobilize Financial Services’ (Mobilize F.S.) activities.
The Group has also identified a potential widespread negative
impact related to a lack of accuracy in documentation
regarding products and services (including marketing and
communication). [DP S4.SBM-3_04; S4.SBM-3_06]
If these issues occur for a large volume of vehicles and persist
over an extended period, they could hinder the execution of a
carmaker’s strategy. Such issues would damage the brand
image, subsequently reducing pricing power and/or market
share, even if the root cause lies with a defective supplier. All
consumers and end-users may be concerned by these risks
and negative impacts. [S4.SBM-3_08]
2.2.3.4.5.2 S4-1 – Policies related to consumers
and end-users
A. Policy related to the risk of inappropriate management
of customer complaints and/or other requests
To mitigate the risk of improper management of customers’
complaints and other requests, the Group’s policy is to
respond to all consumer and end-user requests during the
vehicles’ warranty period in all the markets where the Group
is present. The Chief Industry Quality and Accidentology
Officer is responsible for the implementation of the policy.
Through this policy, the Group particularly considers the
interests of its consumers and end-users, who are entitled to
accurate and reactive responses to their requests. [DP S4-
1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 03; DP MDR-P_05]
B. Policy related to the negative impact of a lack of accuracy
of
products-
and
services-related
documentation
(including marketing and communication)
To
avoid
misleading
product
information
in
user
documentation, production of these documents is made by
the Group’s corporate functions, which can only use product
data provided by the Product Approval Department, and
narratives are checked by the Legal Department before
disclosure. The Product Approval Department ensures that
all product data (including for instance dimensions or
weight) and performance values (such as fuel consumption
or CO2 emissions) have been verified by an authorized third-
party service provider. 
The Marketing, Sales & Operations Officer is accountable for
the implementation of the policy, which is applicable to all
communication of the Group. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 03]
C. Policies related to the risk of non-adequacy of
products and services offer, advertising and selling
practices to customer’s interests
To manage the Mobilize F.S. specific risk of non-adequacy of
products and services offer, advertising and selling
practices to customers’ interests and to meet the growing
expectations of consumers and end-users, Renault Group
has adopted a customer-centric approach, grounded in
numerous Mobilize F.S. studies focused on the voice of the
customer. These studies enable a deeper understanding of
MObilize F.S. customers’ needs and expectations, allowing
services to be adjusted accordingly. In parallel, Mobilize F.S.
has implemented an omnichannel strategy to ensure
seamless client relationship management across various
touchpoints (online, through the dealerships, etc.) in all its
locations and activities. A center of excellence supports this
effort by overseeing the implementation and maintenance
of innovative solutions, ensuring a smooth customer
experience. The center of excellence identifies pain points
within the customer journey and sets up action plans.
Additionally, customers can access their information and
track their requests autonomously at any time through their
customer space. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 03]
Moreover, Mobilize F.S. follows the guiding principle of
informing its customers about the Group’s products and
services. All Mobilize F.S.’s insurance contracts comply with
the Insurance Distribution Directive, an EU regulation aimed
at ensuring fair competition and consumer protection in
insurance distribution. In accordance with this regulation,
Mobilize F.S. includes an information document on insurance
products, the Insurance Product Information Document
(IPID), with all its contracts. The IPID provides clear and
concise information about insurance products, making it
easier for customers to understand the services offered.
These actions are under the responsibility of local Mobilize
F.S. branches. [DP S4-1_01; DP MDR-P_01 to 03]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
223

## Page 226

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.4.5.3 S4-2 – Processes for engaging with
consumers and end-users about impacts
To engage with consumers and end-users regarding the risk
of improper management of customer complaints and/or
other requests, the Customer Intelligence Department
handles satisfaction surveys and requests for information
on products and services and customer support for product
usage. The data collected is analysed to drive continuous
improvement and to adapt the strategy of the Group. [DP
S4-1_04; DP S4-2_01]
In addition, Renault Group ensures continuous interactions
with consumers and end-users throughout the entire
product lifecycle: before, during and after the purchase,
through various engagement channels, such as user
surveys, websites, and commercial events. [DP S4-2_03]
Interactions can be direct between consumers and sales
staff via the customer service: any prospect or consumer
can contact Renault Group using different channels (chat,
calls, social networks). Interactions can also be direct in
repair shops and with after-sales services in the event of
dissatisfaction or a technical problem. [DP S4-2_02]
Renault Group Services’ Sales Director has the responsibility
for ensuring this engagement happens. [DP S4-2_04]
2.2.3.4.5.4 S4-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts
and channels for consumers
and end-users to raise concerns
Renault Group tackles every negative impact on consumers
and end-users linked to the lack of accuracy of products-
and services-related documentation on a case-by-case
basis, given their wide diversity. [DP S4-3_01]
Regarding the management of complaints and/or other
customer requests, each time a customer contacts a
dealership for a technical problem with their vehicle, the
factory is immediately alerted by the information systems.
Following this alert, customer protections are put in place,
followed by analysis and treatment of the causes. This factory-
managed process is applied for recently produced vehicles. All
technical customer problems throughout the warranty period
are handled by the Group’s Industry Quality and Accidentology
Department. When a defect potentially impacting a batch of
vehicles produced is identified, the blockage and recall
management process is applied in order to protect customers.
[DP S4-3_02]
The Group tracks the issues raised and addressed and
assesses the effectiveness of this engagement through the
customer relations indicators: volume of cases handled,
processing
times
and
overall
customer
satisfaction
indicators.  When buying a product or a service, customers
are made aware of this channel. [DP S4-3_03; DP S4-3_04;
DP MDR-T_15; DP MDR-T_16]
2.2.3.4.5.5 S4-4 – Taking action on material impacts
on consumers and end-users,
and approaches to managing material
risks and pursuing material opportunities
related to consumers and end-users,
and effectiveness of those actions
A. Actions to prevent, mitigate or remediate the impact
linked to the lack of accuracy of products- and
services-related documentation
In order to avoid the negative impact linked to the lack of
accuracy of products- and services-related documentation,
and to guarantee consumers and end-users accurate, clear
and relevant information about Renault Group’s products,
Renault Academy deploys courses for sales staff. The aim is
to make sure that the latter will provide appropriate
guidance and information to customers on the specific
features of the Group’s products. Renault Group also
provides customers and end-users with tutorial videos
publicly available online, categorized by make and model of
vehicle,
to
assist
them
in
understanding
their
functionalities.  [DP S4-4_01; DP S4-4_02; DP S4-4_06; DP
S4-4_07; DP S4-4_10; DP MDR-A_01]
The general approach to identifying the necessary and
appropriate actions in response to a specific actual or
potential material adverse impact is to collect all available
information on the case, then remedy it and, if possible, put
in place appropriate measures to ensure that it will not
occur again. Renault Group tackles every occurrence on
a case-by-case basis due to their great diversity.
[DP S4-4_05]
The Renault brand joined the UdM’s (Union des Marques)
FAIRe program in France, which aims to provide concrete
responses to the environmental and societal issues facing
brands, in the form of fifteen commitments for more
responsible communication. Renault Group participated in
the World Federation of Advertisers’ (WFA) Planet Pledge
Program in 2024, which encourages the promotion and
reinforcement of attitudes and behaviours that will help the
world meet the challenges set out in the United Nations
Sustainable Development Goals. Finally, Renault Group
innovates with user information to improve safety, such as
the introduction of a Safety Score to analyze driving habits
and provide personalized advice, the Safety Coach that
offers real-time alerts and advice for safer driving or energy-
efficient driving aids. The information provided enables the
vehicle user to make informed decisions and drive
responsibly. [DP S4-4_03; DP MDR-A_01]
These actions aim at serving all consumers and end-users of
the Group globally, on a day-to-day basis. [MDR-A_02; MDR-
A_03]

B. Actions
to
manage
the
risk
related
to
the
inappropriate management of customer complaints
and/or other requests
The Group answers daily to consumers’ and end-users’
concerns and makes sure to process their demands. The
Group monitors the press and social networks for information
provided by consumers and end-users about its products: all
customer opinions published in the press and on social
networks are scrutinized, enabling potential areas of
dissatisfaction to be detected. [DP S4-4_08; DP MDR-A_01]
Depending on root causes, various action plans are defined
and implemented mainly by the Engineering Department
(product redesign), the plant (eradication of the defect) and/
or the Procurement Department (eradication of the defect
at the supplier’s). [DP MDR-A_01]
The Group tracks the efficiency of its actions through the
number of customers who have contacted the customer
service or returned to the repair shop and asked for a
correction. [DP S4-4_04]
With respect to warranty, Renault Group accrued for €862 M
in its books in 2024 (See URD chapter 5, Note 20).[DP S4-
4_12; DP MDR-A_06]
Those actions concern all the consumers and end-users of
the Group globally, and are continuous, with no specific time
horizon. [DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03]
C. Actions to manage the risk related to the non-
adequacy of products and services offer, advertising
and selling practices to customer’s interests
To avoid or remedy the risk of non-adequacy of products and
services
offering,
advertising,
and
selling
practices
to
customers’ interests, and to further enhance consumers’ and
end-users’ access to essential information, Mobilize F.S. initiated
a digital roadmap in 2024 to facilitate the decision-making
process and contract management. The implementation of the
following online tools has started and is to be pursued until 2026:
●Website: these pages offer comprehensive information
about Mobilize F.S.’s products and services, ensuring that
consumers and end-users have an easy access to all
necessary details;
●Interactive tools: features such as the “Help Me Choose“
quiz and the borrowing capacity module assist customers in
identifying which products best suit their needs, making the
selection process more straightforward and informed;
●Personalized solutions: digital solutions, including finance
simulators and pre-approval forms, enable customers to
define personalized offers and receive pre-approval answers,
streamlining the financial decision-making process;
●Customer spaces: these private spaces provide access to
personal data and contract management tools, allowing
customers to manage their contracts efficiently and
securely; 
By integrating these digital resources, Mobilize F.S. ensures
that consumers are well informed and supported throughout
their
entire
journey,
from
initial
inquiry
to
contract
management. [DP S4-4_08; DP MDR-A_01]
Additionally, recognizing the pivotal role of dealership sales
personnel in product sales, Mobilize F.S. ensures that all sales
representatives undergo regular and mandatory e-learning
sessions focused on insurance, totaling twenty hours
annually. This training program equips sales personnel with
in-depth knowledge and up-to-date information, enabling
them to provide consumers and end-users with accurate and
detailed insights about the products. The sales function in
each country is tasked with the responsibility of training new
sales personnel on both financial products and services. This
ensures that all consumers and end-users receive well-
informed guidance and support, empowering them to make
confident and informed decisions about their purchases.
[DP S4-4_08; DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
2.2.3.4.5.6 S4-5 – Targets related to managing material
negative impacts, advancing positive
impacts, and managing material risks
and opportunities
Renault Group is committed to answer all the customer
requests received during the warranty period.

2.2.4.1.1 G1-1 – Business conduct policies
and corporate culture
2.2.4.1.1.1 Group’s management of ethics
and compliance matters
The Ethics & Compliance Department is responsible for
defining
the
Group’s
global
Ethics
&
Compliance
policy,  advising the Group’s top management,  overseeing
the
collection
and
processing
of
professional
whistleblowing,  promoting and monitoring the application
of ethics and compliance rules in the Group’s various
functions
(purchasing,
production,
sales,
engineering,
finance, HR, etc.), managing and updating the Group’s Ethics
& Compliance  and influence peddling management
system, particularly in the event of new requirements or
regulations, and ensuring its implementation worldwide.
[DP MDR-P_01-06; DP G1-1_01]
In addition to its own employee, the Ethics & Compliance
Department relies on a network of more than 100 referents
who deploy and coordinate the Ethics & Compliance policy
locally in Renault Group countries and entities. They rely on
the following bodies: 
●The Group Ethics & Compliance Committee (CECG),
chaired by the Ethics & Compliance SVP, and made up of
representatives of Group functions, is responsible for
validating, evaluating and improving the effectiveness of
the Group’s Ethics & Compliance policies, procedures and
methodologies. It reinforces the protection of the
company and its employees in all countries where
Renault Group operates;
●The Alert Handling Committee (CTA), comprising seven
members and three experts, deals with reports received
by Renault s.a.s., as well as with certain reports sent
directly by whistleblowers from other entities;
●Each Country’s Ethics & Compliance Committee (CECP) is
chaired by the Country Head and led by its Ethics &
Compliance representative. It handles reports and
ensures the local deployment off the Group’s ethics and
compliance policy and procedures. [DP-G1-3_05]

The Ethics & Compliance Department can rely on internal
promotion and dissemination tools such as a newsletter, an
intranet site, internal kits, videos, or posters. These tools are
regularly updated and provide clear and accessible
information to all Renault Group employees worldwide. [DP-
G1-3_05]
The processes digitization is underway, in particular for the
mapping of corruption risks (an annual consolidation of
which is sent to the Renault Group Leadership Team) and
the management of conflicts of interest. [DP MDR-P_01-06;
DP G1-1_01; DP G1-3_05]
2.2.4.1.1.2 Business conduct policies
Renault Group’s Ethics & Compliance policy integrates
ethical values and behaviours into daily operations,
contributing to sustainable performance and protecting its
reputation and legacy. To prevent breaches of ethics and
integrity, the Group implements compliance rules and
processes, supplemented where necessary by action plans.
These policies are available on Renault Group’s Ethics &
Compliance intranet. They include the Code of Ethics (2024),
the Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct (2021), the Anti-
Corruption and Influence Peddling Management System
(2023), business-specific Codes of Conduct (for activities
requiring stricter ethical standards, most of which published
in 2024), and all the procedures for implementing Ethics and
Compliance.  The Code of Ethics and the Anti-Corruption
Code of Conduct, signed by the Chairman of the Board of
Directors and CEO of Renault Group, emphasize their direct
involvement to comply with all applicable laws, including the
French law “Sapin II“, the United Nations Global Compact,
as well as other applicable anti-corruption laws and
regulations, such as the UK Bribery Act and the US Foreign
Corrupt Practices Act. [DP MDR-P_01-06; DP MDR-P_03; DP
MDR-P_04; DP MDR-P_06]
Renault Group’s Code of Ethics is a guide for employees,
emphasizing legal, social, societal, and environmental
commitments. It covers compliance with rules and
processes,
employee
guidance,
business
ethics,
fair
relations
with
suppliers
and
shareholders,
resource
safeguarding, corporate citizenship, customer safety, social
and environmental commitments, asset protection and
data protection laws. The Code outlines the monitoring
process, including the right to report violations discreetly
and seek advice from the Ethics & Compliance Department.
It applies to all individuals contractually bound to Renault
Group worldwide. It is overseen by the Ethics & Compliance
Department. The Code of Ethics considers the interests of
key
stakeholders,
including
customers,
suppliers,
shareholders and employees. As an employer, Renault
Group emphasizes non-discrimination, fairness, diversity,
and assistance to people with disabilities. [DP MDR-P_01 to
03; DP MDR-P_05]
The Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct underscores the Group’s
commitment to behave with integrity. It specifies the Group’s
goals and commitments, describes the risks, and outlines how
to manage them, including proper handling of gifts and
invitations, conflicts of interests, sponsorship activities,
representation
of
interests,
business
interactions,
and
accounting transactions. It also describes the monitoring
process, which includes the Third-Party Integrity Management
System (TIM), controls (including accounting controls) and a
whistleblowing system overseen by the Ethics & Compliance
Department. The Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct applies to
all
individuals
contractually
bound
to
Renault
Group,
regardless of hierarchical level, geographic location, or entity.
Stakeholders
include
suppliers,
service
providers,
subcontractors, dealers and customers. It is overseen by the
Ethics & Compliance Department. [DP MDR-P_01 to 03; DP
MDR-P_05]
The Code of Ethics and the Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct
are complemented by dedicated Codes of Conduct for
business lines or activities requiring specific guidelines. They
are dedicated to the activities related to stock market,
global security, audit, customs, representation of interests
and procurement. These policies are all available on Renault
Group’s global website, and therefore available to all
stakeholders
concerned.
These
dedicated
Codes
of
Conduct have different scopes of application and different
seniority levels accountable for their implementation,
described in the table below. [DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_06]
Dedicated code of conduct
Scope of application
Most senior level accountable
for the implementation of the policy
Code of conduct for Global security
All Renault Group legal entities in all countries
where the Group operates
Head of Prevention and Protection Department

All Renault Group legal entities in all countries
where the Group operates
 Chief Legal Officer

All Renault Group legal entities in all countries
where the Group operates

where the Group operates

where the Group operates

[DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03]

227

## Page 230

Renault Group
Sustainable development
The Code of Conduct for Interest Representation Activities
is described in detail below in chapter 2.2.4.1.4.
The Anti-Corruption and Influence-Peddling Management
System is a continuous improvement process. It is built around
the involvement of senior management, the mapping of
corruption risks, and their management, which is broken down
as follows:
●Prevention: Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct and Codes
of Conduct dedicated to the most exposed business lines,
training, and analysis of third parties;
●Detection: whistleblowing system and control systems
(including accounting controls);
●Remediation: corrective action plans and disciplinary
regime. [DP MDR-P_01]
All companies controlled (according to the French Commercial
Code) by Renault Group, in France and abroad, must comply
with the French law on transparency, the fight against
corruption and the modernization of economic life (known as
“Sapin II“), as well as other anti-corruption laws and regulations
applicable to them. The Leadership Team is accountable for
the application of the Anti-Corruption and Influence Peddling
Management System. [DP MDR-P_02 to 04]
For more information on the administrative, management
and supervisory bodies related to business conduct, ethics
and  compliance, see chapter 2.2.1.1.2.1.
2.2.4.1.1.3 Promotion of corporate culture
In 2024, Renault Group deployed a new platform of values
and associated behaviours for the Group. The definition of
this new platform involved contributions from the Group’s
top management, feedback from the 2023 Global Employee
Survey, multi-country and multi-function focus groups and
interviews.
The
co-constructed
values
platform
was
evaluated by all employees before final release. For the
launch, a workshop was assigned for all Renault Group
managers to provide support and a presentation guide to
introduce the project to their team and initiate collective
reflection on actions to embody the new behaviours. The
new values and behaviours platform is integrated into all
Human Resources processes. [DP G1-1_01]
For example, the performance management process has
been reviewed and integrated into the new professional
behavior grid; the soft skills training offer has been revised
to match the new expectations for professional behaviours,
with two offers available: one for managers and one for all
employees. [DP G1-1_01]
2.2.4.1.1.4 Training on business conduct
and Ethics & Compliance 
The training program dedicated to business ethics has been
renewed since 2023. Renault Group addresses corruption risks
through customized training programs, categorized according
to the level of risk. “Generic Risk” training, mandatory for all
white-collar employees, includes four e-learning modules
available in fourteen languages for easy access and
understanding by Renault Group’s employees worldwide:
“Ethics within Renault Group”, “Managing Conflicts of
Interests”, “Anti-Corruption”, and “Whistleblowing”. Each of
these
modules
has
been
completed
by
more
than
50,000 employees. [DP G1-1_10]
The module “Ethics within Renault Group” describes the Ethics
& Compliance of the Group, as an employer and as a company
and how to act in different situations. The goal of this e-
learning is to allow the employees to discover the Ethics &
Compliance within Renault Group and to recognize the non-
ethical situations and act consequently. [DP G1-1_10]
The module “Managing Conflicts of Interests” has the
objective of enabling Group employees to understand,
identify and prevent the risks associated with conflicts of
interest.  [DP G1-1_10; DP G1-3_06]
The
“Anti-Corruption”
and
“Whistleblowing”
modules
describe what constitutes a report and potential corruption
risk situations. They allow the employees to learn the
definitions of corruption and influence peddling, to
understand the “Sapin II“ law and its application scope, to
identify the at-risk situations in a professional environment,
and to apply the best practices to prevent and detect
corruption. [DP G1-1_10; DP G1-3_06]
Some training courses, rolled out in 2024, are dedicated to
specific business lines (finance, real estate, procurement...).
For blue-collar workers, a video was broadcasted in
factories in 2022, available for use whenever factories wish
to run a campaign.  The “Specific Risks” training targets
business lines with higher exposure, identified via corruption
risk mapping, such as procurement, sales or finance,
including customs. [DP G1-1_11; DP G1-3_05; DP G1-3_06]
To ensure the effectiveness of these trainings, Renault
Group closely monitors the completion rates of both the
“Generic Risk“ training and “Specific Risks“ training.
Training is considered complete once the learner achieves
80% of correct answers on the final quiz. Renault Group aims
for a 90% completion rate among white-collar employees,
with the training’s validity extending for three years. These
mandatory trainings are required for all new recruits at
Renault Group and must be renewed every three years to
maintain compliance and ensure ongoing awareness of the
Group’s ethical standards and practices.  [DP G1-1_10;
DP G1-3_06]
Renault Group ensures that its administrative, supervisory,
and management bodies receive tailored anti-corruption
and anti-bribery training. In 2024, 100% of top management
brands’ committees, including the SVP Ethics & Compliance
and
members
of
the
Leadership
Team,
completed
mandatory e-learning modules on Ethics, Anti-Corruption,
and Conflict of Interests. These trainings are specifically
adapted to their governance roles, covering ethical
decision-making,
oversight
responsibilities,
and
due
diligence. The training is renewed every three years to
maintain compliance and awareness. In 2024, the Board of
Directors has also received training on anti-corruption.
[DP G1-3_08]
