2.2.4.1.2 G1-3 – Prevention and detection
of corruption and bribery
2.2.4.1.2.1 Controls and management processes
in place to prevent corruption and bribery
In addition to the preventive measures already mentioned, a
Third-Party Integrity Management Process has been
implemented across the entirety of Renault Group’s
operations. In an international context of sometimes
uncertain and risky business relations, Renault Group
complies with and promotes ethical practices that are in line
with applicable laws and regulations, in the fields of
prevention and detection of corruption and influence
peddling, the fight against money laundering and the
financing of terrorism, export controls and international
sanctions regimes. Renault Group is subject to French law
“Sapin II”, which requires the implementation of measures to
prevent and detect corruption and influence peddling,
including “procedures for assessing the situation of
customers, tier-one suppliers and intermediaries” with
regard to corruption risk mappings. Within this context,
Renault Group closely examines its suppliers, customers and
other third parties with whom it has a commercial or non-
commercial relationship throughout the world, taking into
account corruption risk maps, i.e. the various categories of
third parties identified as presenting a risk of corruption. The
Ethics & Compliance Department is responsible for the
implementation of these measures to prevent and detect
corruption. [DP MDR-P_01 to 05]
The
Ethics
&
Compliance
Department
conducted
compliance tests based on the previous self-assessment of
the “Sapin II“ anti-corruption system. In 2024, these
compliance tests were conducted in fourteen entities and
corporate functions to verify the effectiveness of the
implementation of the corruption prevention system.  The
Ethics & Compliance Department revamped the compliance
control approach of the “Sapin II“ corruption prevention
system. This new set of controls was defined in coordination
with Finance Integrity and Internal Control Departments.
Implementation of this new compliance control approach
will start in 2025.
2.2.4.1.2.2 Whistleblowing system for investigating
and dealing with allegations and incidents
of corruption and bribery
Renault Group has deployed a whistleblowing system
(WhistleB), using a confidential, secure platform for
collecting reports. It is open to all employees (including
individuals seeking employment and former employees),
external and occasional staff, shareholders, members of the
administrative, management or supervisory bodies of
Renault Group’s entities, as well as employees of Renault
Group entities’ service providers (e.g. dealers, suppliers,
partners) and their subcontractors or, when it comes to legal
entities, to the members of the administrative management
or
supervisory
bodies
of
these
contractors
and
subcontractors, as well as to their staff members. WhistleB
allows for these individuals to report facts contrary to the
law, the Code of Ethics or the Anti-corruption Code of
Conduct. [DP G1-1_02; DP G1-3_01] 
The whistleblowing system also covers the 2017 French law
related to the Duty of Vigilance of parent companies and
ordering companies (known as the “Duty of Vigilance Act”),
which provides for the establishment of a mechanism for
reporting relating to the existence or occurrence of serious
violations of human rights or fundamental freedoms, health
and safety of people as well as environment. The
whistleblowing system is complemented by the regular
internal channels for reporting whistleblowing, which are  the
management line, the Ethics & Compliance Department or
Department of Professional Alerts, the Human Resources
Department, employee representatives and the network of
Ethics & Compliance officers. [DP G1-1_02; DP G1-3_01; DP
MDR-P_04]
This system is governed by a publicly available alert
management procedure, to facilitate the reporting of any
concerns related to ethics and compliance. It applies to all
actors concerned by the whistleblowing system and the Ethics
&
Compliance
Department
is
accountable
for
the
implementation of this procedure. [DP G1-1_05; DP G1-3_01;
DP MDR-P_01 to 03; DP MDR-P_06]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
229

## Page 232

Renault Group
Sustainable development
The platform is managed by an external service provider
and can be accessed at any time, any day of the week, on a
professional or personal computer, tablet or smartphone
online
at
https://renault.whistleb.com.
The
global
whistleblowing system is available in fifteen languages.
[DP G1-1_02; DP G1-3_01]
It ensures the confidentiality of exchanges and therefore
enables the whistleblower’s identity to be protected under
the conditions provided for by the applicable national laws.
[DP G1-1_05]
Renault
Group
annually
reminds
its
suppliers
and
subcontractors of the possibilities offered by the WhistleB
whistleblowing system, accompanying the communication
with a detailed user guide. [DP G1-1_05]
Reports relating to Renault s.a.s. are handled by the
Department of Professional Alerts (within the Ethics &
Compliance
Department)
and
the
Alert
Handling
Committee, which is chaired by Renault Group’s SVP Ethics
& Compliance. The Alert Handling Committee is responsible
for managing investigations into reports to confirm their
validity, collect evidence, and, if allegations are confirmed,
inform the appropriate managers to take follow-up actions,
including corrective measures. In other entities, alerts are
handled by the Ethics & Compliance referent and/or the
relevant Country Ethics and Compliance Committee,
chaired by the Country Head. A statistical reporting on
whistleblowing worldwide is presented annually to the
Group Ethics and Compliance Committee (CECG) and to the
Audit and Risks Committee. [DP G1-1_08; DP G1-3_03]
All individuals involved in the whistleblowing system shall
perform their tasks with independence and impartiality. The
Alert Handling Committee ensures that reports involving
members of the network or managers are addressed with the
highest level of scrutiny and care. [DP G1-1_08; DP G1-3_02]
Disciplinary measures are in place across all Group entities
to address breaches, with sanctions for non-compliance as
specified in the Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct. These
disciplinary measures are consistent with local laws and
may be up to dismissal for non-compliance with applicable
laws, the Code of Ethics, the Anti-Corruption Code of
Conduct and internal procedures. Each year, to avert future
non-compliance, Renault Group methodically records and
analyzes the causes of these disciplinary sanctions imposed
on employees, while respecting the rules of data protection
and confidentiality. [DP G1-3_01]
Within Renault Group, 765 new reports were registered
worldwide in 2024. Following investigations, it was determined
that 29% were substantiated. The main non-compliances
identified included cases of unethical behavior, moral
harassment and conflicts of interest. [DP G1-3_03] 
In compliance with the “Sapin II“ law, internal procedures
provide for the strict confidentiality of the identity of the
author of a report, of the person(s) targeted or implicated by
such a report and of any third party mentioned in the report.
Enhanced confidentiality commitments are signed by
individuals
in
charge
(including
amendments
to
employment contracts where appropriate) and confidential
information is managed through secure emails and
encrypted containers. The WhistleB platform is entirely
confidential, operated by an external operator without
access to the data. Only the people in charge of processing
reports have access to the platform.   Retaliation is strictly
prohibited. This is applied to Renault s.a.s. and its
subsidiaries,
in
France
and
abroad.
This
action
is
continuously held to ensure continuity of the protection and
confidentiality of whistleblowers. [DP G1-1_05; DP G1-3_02;
DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
2.2.4.1.3 G1-4 – Incidents of corruption
or bribery
There were no breaches of anti-corruption and anti-bribery
procedures and standards in 2024; therefore, no action was
necessary. [DP G1-4_03]
Corruption and bribery
2024
Convictions for violation of anti-corruption
and anti-bribery laws
 -
Fines for violation of anti-corruption
and anti-bribery laws

To strengthen its anti-corruption management system,
Renault Group achieved ISO 37001 certification in 2024,
applicable to all the Group’s entities. The ISO 37001 standard
sets out the criteria that a company shall meet to establish,
maintain and improve an effective anti-corruption system.
In 2024, the Ethics & Compliance Department, 15 global
functions of Renault s.a.s., 2 entities (Mobilize F.S. and RRG),
5  countries and 6 (French) subsidiaries were audited. [DP
MDR-A_01 to 05]
2.2.4.1.4 G1-5 – Political influence and
interests representation activities
Renault Group’s interests representation activities align
with its strategy, protecting and promoting its reputation,
supporting
company
development,
seeking
success
conditions for current and future projects, and supporting its
climate ambition to contribute to carbon neutrality.  The
ambitions of this strategy consider the requirements set out
by the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the
Paris Climate Agreements. The Group reaffirms its strong
commitment to a sustainable mobility respectful of its
ecosystems. [DP G1-5_09]
The
Group
maintains
a
continuous
dialogue
with
stakeholders, including civil society, NGOs, and public
authorities, to ensure that interests representation activities
are conducted in compliance with legal international,
European and national transparency requirements and
existing internal codes. [DP G1-5_09]
The Group’s positions are communicated to public decision-
makers,
directly
or
through
associations
or
unions.
All representation actions are carried out in close
coordination with the Group’s Public Affairs Department, to
ensure consistency of approach, and compliance with legal
procedures. [DP G1-5_09]

In that regard, the Group complies with transparency
requirements in accordance with the applicable local
regulations. Renault Group is registered in France in the High
Authority for Transparency in Public Life register, as well as
in the EU transparency register (n° 946343776-69) and
annually submits a summary of its interests representation
activities to these respective authorities, as well as the
resources devoted to these activities. [DP G1-5_10]
The main topics addressed through Renault Group interests
representation activities in 2024  include:  
●The deployment of electric and low-carbon mobility
solutions,
with
strong
cooperation
between
all
stakeholders to define a transition path, shape the
electric mobility ecosystem and develop and improve the
main tools for decarbonizing road transport;
●The creation of a regulatory framework that achieves an
optimal balance between industry and environmental
realities and thus achieve net zero carbon by 2050;
●The
full
integration
of
environmental,
social
and
governance criteria into legislation and Renault Group
activities. [DP G1-5_09]
Renault Group is not politically active and does not fund
political parties as part of its public representation. The Group
is not involved in sponsoring local, regional or national political
campaigns.
Renault Group’s interests representation activities are
governed by a public framework of rules consisting of Renault
Groups’ Code of Ethics, the Anti-Corruption Code of Conduct,
and the Code of Conduct for Interest Representation
Activities, published in April 2024, which states that “Renault
Group interests representation activities support Renault
Group’s climate ambition“. In this respect, Renault Group is a
member of the SBTi’s 1,5°C Business Ambition campaign.
[DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_06]
This framework ensures that the Group’s activities in
representing its interests are in line with its commitment to
contribute to carbon neutrality and the improvement of its
industrial and technological activities. It is applicable to all
employees and those working directly or indirectly with
Renault Group, providing guidance on the principles and
commitments that should shape professional relations both
internally and externally. The framework outlines the
expected behavior in case of ethical breaches and serves as
a decision-making tool to prevent and address corruption.
[DP MDR-P_01; MDR-P_02]
Specifically, the Code of Conduct for Interest Representation
Activities sets the guiding principles for Renault Group’s
representatives who interact with public decision-makers,
ensuring that the efforts in representing interests are
conducted in a transparent and ethical manner. In that
respect, all interests representation activities carried out by
Renault Group’s representatives, in all countries, are subject to
the rules set out in this Code of Conduct for Interest
Representation Activities and must be carried out in
coordination with the Group’s Public Affairs Department. The
local representatives of Renault Group’s Public Affairs function
must ensure that the provisions of this Code are known to all
individuals required to carry out interests representation
activities in the concerned country.  In the event that Renault
Group engages external counsel to assist it in its interests
representation activities, the principles of the Code of Conduct
for Interest Representation Activities would also apply and the
said counsel would be asked to commit to apply these
principles as far as they are concerned.  [DP MDR-P_01;
DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_06]
Renault Group’s Code of Conduct for Interest Representation
Activities draws inspiration from international, European, and
national
regulatory
frameworks
governing
interests
representation
activities.
These
include
the
OECD
Recommendation on Public Integrity (OECD/LEGAL/0435), the
OECD Recommendation on Transparency and Integrity in
Lobbying and Influence (OECD/LEGAL/0379), the Agreement
between the European Parliament and the European
Commission on the transparency register for organizations
and self-employed individuals involved in EU policy-making
and implementation (OJ L 277, 19/09/2014) as well as the French
law No. 2016-1691 on transparency, the fight against
corruption and the modernization of economic life (“ Sapin II“
law). [DP MDR-P_04]
The Public Affairs Department of Renault Group is
accountable for the implementation of the Code of Conduct
for Interest Representation Activities. The Audit and Risks
Committee of the Board of Directors monitors the
implementation of this Code as part of its overall oversight
of the ethical framework deployed within the Group by the
Ethics & Compliance Department. In addition, the Strategy
and Sustainability Committee regularly reviews an ESG
dashboard containing numerous performance indicators,
including those about transparency. The same Committee
also monitors certain interests representation activities as
part of its review of the strategy and actions to be
implemented in terms of environmental, social and societal
responsibility of the company, as well as sustainable
development. [DP G1-5_01]
For more information on the appointment of the members of
the Board of Directors, see chapter 2.2.1.1.2.1. [DP G1-5_11]
Renault Group is a member of various think tanks,
associations, chambers of commerce, and automotive
industry associations, reflecting its industrial activities in
different countries. In that regard, Renault Group is
committed to publishing a report by the 2025 Annual
General
Meeting
on
its
membership
in
industrial
associations which undertake climate advocacy actions.
This report will be based on objective criteria such
as Renault Group’s membership fees, Renault Group’s role in
governance, its participation in the associations’ working
groups, or the commitment of these associations regarding
the Paris Agreements’ objectives. The Group will regularly
update this report thereafter. This action demonstrates
Renault Group’s dedication to transparent and responsible
interests representation practices that support its strategic
objectives and contribute to sustainable development.
[DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
During the review of its business association memberships in
2024, the Group identified an instance of indirect political
financing in Switzerland. Renault Suisse SA, similarly with
other automotive manufacturers, is a member of an industry
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
231

## Page 234

Renault Group
Sustainable development
association that operates in the Swiss automotive sector.
This association makes annual contributions to Swiss
political parties, which are transparently listed on the Swiss
Federal Audit Office’s website, in accordance with Swiss
Federal law. Switzerland is among the few OECD nations
without state funding for political parties, which means that
political parties are largely funded through private donations.
Therefore, these contributions are made independently of
Renault Suisse SA or any specific counterparty.
Renault Suisse SA operates in compliance with Swiss
regulations and in alignment with local business norms.
Renault Suisse SA’s membership in these associations is
conducted responsibly, without direct affiliation or influence
over the political landscape. The case described above
reflects a cultural specificity of the Swiss industry
representative
associations.
Apart
from
this
Swiss
specificity, Renault Group is not politically active and does
not finance political parties as part of its interests
representation
activities.
In
line
with
the
Group’s
commitment to neutrality as outlined in its Code of Ethics,
Renault Group has engaged in a constructive dialogue with
its subsidiary, to raise its concerns on the situation while
safeguarding the interests of both the Group and the
relationships with the associations. [DP G1-5_02]

2.2.4.1.5 G1-2 – Management of relationships
with suppliers
Renault Group’s approach to its relationships with suppliers
includes a risk mapping of the value chain. The relationships
with suppliers are assessed using external databases,
regular audits, EcoVadis questionnaires, and internal
specialists. An audit plan for 2024 is in place for China,
Morocco, India, Türkiye, and Brazil.  [DP G1-2_02; DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
The suppliers’ selection criteria include compliance with
fundamental rights, the rating on EcoVadis questionnaires,
and adherence to the Code of Conduct. Renault Group
calculates a sustainability rating for suppliers, used with
other
criteria
(quality,
cost,
delivery,
development,
management,
sustainability
and
risk). 
[DP
G1-2_03;
DP MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
These actions cover the entire Renault Group and its
subsidiaries, aiming to standardize practices and ensure
compliance. They are ongoing initiatives which are part of a
long-term strategy to ensure compliance and improve
supplier relationships across the entire Renault Group and
its subsidiaries. [DP MDR-A_02; DP MDR-A_03]
For more information on the administrative, management
and supervisory bodies related to suppliers’ management,
see chapter 2.2.1.1.2.1.
2.2.4.1.6 G1-6 – Payment practices
Renault Group is permanently committed to avoid any delays
in payment to its suppliers. Payment schedules are adapted to
the specific legal and regulatory conditions of each country
and guidelines are issued on payment terms by entity and
supplier.
An
information
system
processes
invoices,
calculating payment dates based on payment conditions and
prioritizing payments accordingly. [DP G1-2_01]
The vast majority of payments are made by bank transfers,
and efforts are made to streamline payment deadlines
internationally for better monitoring and adherence to
deadlines. An annual internal control questionnaire was
launched in 2023 to monitor payment practices and
deadlines in each country. Renault Group aims to optimize
information sharing with suppliers through “Renault Suppliers
Portal”, offering an information platform to improve exchange
quality. The “Supplier Payment Details“ service for the
exclusive use of Renault Group suppliers allows registered
suppliers to quickly access secure payment information
continuously, including detailed information about their latest
payment deadlines. [DP G1-2_01]
The main measures taken to continue improving payment
practices
include
enhancing
the
internal
control
questionnaire, providing more precise instructions to
buyers, and improving the management of the supplier
database,
particularly
regarding
default
payment
deadlines. Additionally, a process mining analysis and
improvement tool has been deployed, and a project to
standardize accounting systems has been initiated. The
expected outcomes of these actions are, in the long term, to
harmonize payment practices across countries, to continue
improving them, and to ensure monitoring and controls to
prevent payment delays. [DP MDR-A_01]
Renault Group addresses potential remediations on a case-
by-case basis in disputes regarding payment delays. [DP
MDR-A_01; DP MDR-A_04]
These actions will gradually cover the entire scope of
Renault Group, encompassing all activities. Renault Group’s
payment practices primarily concern its suppliers, including
local and international vendors, subcontractors, and service
providers. All of Renault Group’s progress actions regarding
payment practices are part of a multi-year plan. Whether it
is the improvement and deployment of the internal control
questionnaire launched in 2023 and conducted annually, or
the implementation and deployment of new digital tools,
this is a continuous effort aimed at enhancing the Group’s
practices, sharing information, and ensuring transparency.
[DP MDR-A_02; MDR-A_03; MDR-A_05]

are based on N-1 figures, as the 2024 data were not available at the time of collecting information and drafting the report.

2.2.4.1.7 G1-7 – Fair competition
As required by ESRS 1, the materiality of the impacts and risks below is based on an assessment of their probability of
occurence, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).
Topic
Impact, risk
or opportunity 
Description

and/or business model
Fair competition
(antitrust)

Fines, unenforceability of contracts, orders

for compensation and reputational damage, and

All Group activities
2.2.4.1.7.1 Policies adopted to ensure Renault Group’s
conformity to the rules guaranteeing
fair competition
Renault Group’s Global Antitrust Policy outlines prohibited or
risky practices, as well as the rules and best practices that
employees must adopt to prevent the occurrence of anti-
competitive practices. It also specifies the circumstances in
which employees must seek advice from the Legal Department.
Through its Global Antitrust Policy, the Group is committed to
complying with the European Union competition law and the
national competition regulations applicable to its activities in
each country where it operates. [DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-P_04]
This policy is referred to in Renault Group’s Code of Ethics, which
ensures its external communication. This policy is accessible to
all employees via the Compliance Antitrust SharePoint and
Renault’s Electronic Cabinet, which contains all the Group’s
procedures. The Global Antitrust Policy has been circulated in
the organization through its compliance program, which
includes the requirement for employees to complete an online
training course designed to raise their awareness of
competition rules. This policy applies to every manager,
employee, apprentice and temporary worker of Renault Group
and its subsidiaries, around the world. With the support of the
Chief Legal Officer, the Global Antitrust Policy is steered by the
Group’s Antitrust Team of the Legal Department. The rules it
contains consider constraints and operational requirements.
[DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_03; DP MDR-P_06]
The Global Antitrust Policy is regularly updated (latest
updates made in April 2024) to reflect the evolution of the
Group’s activities, the rules of the competition law, and, if
need be, to adapt its content in case any difficulties
encountered in its operational implementation. It is
supplemented by a handbook which describes concrete,
operational, and business-oriented rules. [DP MDR-P_05]
To ensure the proper implementation of fair competition rules
on markets where Renault Group is active, controls are carried
out on three levels: 
●First-level
controls
are
performed
by
antitrust
representatives, who are specially trained in antitrust
regulations applicable to their activities and the content
of this policy, within their respective areas. Antitrust
representatives act as intermediaries within the Group for
the Legal Department. Their purpose is to monitor the
correct application of the policy by operational teams by
conducting
annual
interviews
using
a
control
questionnaire prepared by the Legal Department and
deployed with each central function and the subsidiaries
of Renault Group. The goal of these controls is to assess
the risk levels and the effectiveness of preventive
measures applicable to each corporate function and
entity. Antitrust representatives must report any antitrust
issue ’s alert to the Legal Department’s Antitrust Team;
●Second-level controls are carried out by the Internal
Control
Department,
using
the
French
Securities
Commission “Autorité des Marchés Financiers” internal
control questionnaire. These are annual substantive and
formal checks that validate the correct execution of the
first-level controls. The controls showed a global result of
91% of application (AMF QCI campaign 2024);
●Third-level controls are designed to assess the relevance
and effectiveness of this policy within the Group. They are
organized and conducted by Renault Group Internal Audit
Department. 
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
233

## Page 236

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Key controls are updated annually to ensure their continued
relevance, to verify and enhance the objectives set, and to
assess the commitment of managers. [DP MDR-A_01 to 03;
DP MDR-A_05]
Contingent liabilities relating to competition law are
disclosed in URD chapter 5, Note 28 to the Consolidated
financial statements.
2.2.4.1.7.2 Actions and resources implemented
to ensure Renault Group’s conformity
to the respect of the rules guaranteeing
fair competition 
Actions taken by Renault Group to ensure its compliance
with the rules guaranteeing fair competition include the
development of a risk mapping, an e-learning program,
regular controls (described above) and the creation of a
control questionnaire to verify the good understanding of
the of antitrust rules by its workforce. [DP MDR-A_01]
To identify the risks related to the non-respect of fair
competition rules, Renault Group conducts a mapping of the
antitrust risks of corporate functions and subsidiaries most at-
risk. The Group’s top management, as well as all Renault
Group’s white-collar employees (about 60  000 people),
regardless of their country and Department or subsidiary
affiliation, have been particularly targeted by these programs,
to raise awareness to the respect of these rules. [DP MDR-
A_01; DP MDR-A_02]
To ensure these most at-risk employees are aware of risks
identified in terms of fair competition, Renault Group has
developed an antitrust e-learning program. It aims to raise
awareness on antitrust issues and to disseminate a culture of
compliance in the processes and day-to-day activities, which is
a key element in preventing anti-competitive behavior. It
incorporates the policy’s principles and includes simulations,
making employees aware of the rules and expectations of the
Group regarding antitrust. The completion of this training is
mandatory and must be completed every three years. [DP
MDR-A_01 to 03]
To ensure that managers and employees of corporate
Department and subsidiaries remain aware of the rules
outlined in the Global Antitrust Policy and to identify
potential violations, a control questionnaire has been
established. It supports the identification of each function’s
and entity’s risk exposure while ensuring that managers at
different levels of the organization are involved in the Global
Antitrust Policy and responsible for its application. This
questionnaire must be completed annually as part of the
Internal Control questionnaire of the Autorité des Marchés
Financiers (AMF)’s campaign by corporate Departments
and subsidiaries subject to antitrust Internal Control
questionnaires. [DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
The development and animation of the preventive actions
are supported by the Group’s Antitrust Team which is
composed of five specialized jurists. It conducts regulatory
watch activities to ensure continuous compliance with
changing developments of antitrust regulations. The
involvement of the Corporate Antitrust Team, Antitrust
representatives and various back-ups in each Department
ensures a comprehensive global management of the
antitrust compliance program in order to maintain fair
competition in the markets where Renault Group operates.
[DP MDR-A_06] 
The assessment of the effectiveness of the preventative
actions is a twofold process, updated annually by the Risks
Management Department, which includes:
●Monitoring of key risk indicators at Group level;
●Reviewing the risk mapping with a detailed view by type
and by entity.

2.2.4.1.7.3 Tracking effectiveness of policies
and actions implemented to ensure
Renault Group’s compliance with the rules
guaranteeing fair competition
Currently, Renault Group measures the efficiency of its
antitrust e-learning by tracking the completion rate by its
employees. Renault Group‘s objective in 2024 was to
achieve an antitrust e-learning completion rate of 80%
among Renault Group’s corporate functions and main
subsidiaries, with an ultimate goal of 90% completion rate.
The completion rate is monitored through the annual
Internal Control questionnaire of the AMF, the Antitrust
representatives being responsible for implementing this e-
learning training program on their respective scopes. As of
December 2024, the completion rate within the Group was
84%. Renault Group commits to comply with antitrust
regulations and therefore does not plan to formalize any
new target, considering quantitative targets are not
relevant for this topic. [DP MDR-T_16 à 19]
Renault Group defined this target using the results of its risk
mapping used to identify high-risk areas in terms of
antitrust, leading to several targets to be reached to reduce
the risks. The target is based on the assumption that high
participation will enhance compliance. It has an exclusively
internal
impact,
on
central
functions,
subsidiaries,
employees and operational processes, as such. Internal
stakeholders have been involved in setting the antitrust
target. Data is collected through the Antitrust SharePoint
and internal reporting systems, ensuring accurate tracking
and assessment of progress. [DP MDR-T_04; DP MDR-T_09]
2.2.4.1.8 G1-8 – Compliance to laws and regulations
As required by ESRS 1, the materiality of the impacts and risks below is based on an assessment of their potential severity and
likelihood, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).
Topic
Impact, risk
or opportunity 
Description 
Time horizon 
Location in value chain
and/or business model
Law and regulation
compliance
Risk
Remediation costs and/or penalties and/or
reputational damage due to non-compliance
with internal and external regulations
Short term
All Group activities
2.2.4.1.8.1 Policies adopted to manage material
sustainability matters related to law
and regulation compliance 
Key points, scope and governance
Renault Group’s compliance policies aim to mitigate the
exposure of the Group and its management to the
consequences of risks arising from non-compliance with
applicable laws and regulations.
For legal functions and entities excluding Mobilize Financial
Services (Mobilize F.S.), the scope of the policy is defined by a
list of 20 regulated domains, such as export control or
intellectual property. This list is drawn up jointly by the Ethics
&
Compliance
Department,
the
Risk
Management
Department and the Legal Department.
On the proposal of the Group’s Ethics & Compliance
Department, each of these domains is assigned to a
Department, which appoints a Prescriber from among its
members, whose role is to:
●Coordinate
the
regulatory
compliance
program
(“Compliance Wheel“) in the domain;
●Define the appropriate procedures and controls with the
Internal Control Department;
An annual review is made by the Audit and Risks Committee,
highlighting the main regulatory risks identified and the
progress made. The Internal Audit Department regularly
assesses the robustness of these programmes through
audits.
Mobilize F.S. has its own policy and is committed to fostering
a culture of compliance through extensive training and
communication
efforts,
including
the
Mobilize
F.S.
Compliance Charter, which requires all employees to
comply with all applicable laws and regulations. All Mobilize
F.S. risks, including compliance matters, are monitored by its
Board of Directors’ Risk Committee. [DP MDR-P_01 to 03]
Key stakeholders involved
Customers,
employees,
auditors,
financial
authorities,
investors and shareholders want to avoid the consequences
of a proven risk, namely administrative, financial and
criminal sanctions against the Group or its managers, as
well as damage to its reputation. [DP MDR-P_05]
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
235

## Page 238

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.4.1.8.2 Actions and resources in relation
to material sustainability matters related
to law and regulation compliance
Key actions, scope, time horizons
The following actions are carried out on an ongoing basis:
●Update of the regulatory risk mapping, which includes the
definition and implementation of control measures for
these risks, leading to the possible update of the list of key
compliance domains;
●Definition and deployment of compliance programs by
Prescribers, according to the “Compliance Wheel“, and
the necessary resources;
●Regulatory watch identifying regulations at the global level
with a long-term vision to 2035, to ensure proactive
compliance. A roadmap is established to comply with future
regulations and laws;
●Compliance checks, structured on three levels:
– Level 1 controls focus on the existence, formalisation
and traceability of compliance measures, including
self-assessments to ensure that compliance measures
are in place and documented;
– Level 2 controls examine the existence, frequency and
compliance with action plans, and ensure that
compliance activities are carried out regularly and
followed as intended;
– Level 3 controls involve an internal audit or an external
body to assess the robustness and effectiveness of the
compliance mechanisms in place, as well as the
resilience of the system;
The scope of controls depends on the domains and the
associated risk levels, covering all the Group’s activities and
operations with varying frequency depending on the priority
level of the area. [DP MDR-A_01 to 03]
2.2.4.1.8.3 Tracking effectiveness of policies
and actions through targets related
to law and regulation compliance 
For functions and legal entities excluding Mobilize F.S., the
effectiveness of the actions taken is measured by an
assessment of the maturity of each domain:
●For existing major domains, maturity is assessed using the
RICS (Referential Internal Control Standards) questionnaire
deployed by the Internal Control Department;
●For new major domains, a maturity measurement tool, based
on a standardised assessment grid of 20 criteria, is used.
In both cases, an annual update on the maturity of the domain
is presented to the Ethics and Compliance Committee or the
Risk and Internal Control Committee.
For Mobilize F.S., all risk management rules are subject to
controls, the results of which are presented every quarter to
the Internal Control and Compliance Committee. [DP MDR-
T_16 t 19]
2.2.4.1.9 Methodological notes
Renault Group sets its governance metrics based on robust methods. They are subject to internal controls and verifications,
without being systematically validated by an external body.
G1-3 – Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery
Two methods are used to collect data related to employee training:
●The learning management system, Learning@RenaultGroup, covering almost 93% of the reporting perimeter;
●A questionnaire is sent to the countries to collect data for entities not included in the learning management system,
Learning@RenaultGroup.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Percentage of functions-at-risk
covered by training programmes
All “white-collar” employees are considered as at-risk and are required to complete the mandatory
“Anti-Corruption“ e-learning course. In this regard, 100% of functions-at-risk are covered by this
training program.

02
G1-4 – Confirmed incidents of corruption or bribery
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Number of convictions for violation of
anti-corruption and anti- bribery laws
Corruption is defined in the Group’s Code of Ethics – Part 2, in accordance with its delineation and
application under the French Sapin II legislation.
Amount of fines for violation of
anti-corruption and anti- bribery laws
The Legal Department is responsible for transmitting known information about fines and confirmed
convictions of Renault Group to the Ethics & Compliance Department. These information are based
on confirmations requested twice a year by the Ethics & Compliance Department from the
Legal Department.
G1-5 – Political influence and lobbying activities
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Financial political contributions made
To determine the amount of financial political contributions made, a questionnaire is sent to all public
affairs departments in the countries where Renault Group operates. These countries must report the
total amount of contributions made to each association, taking into account all the Group’s legal
entities in the country, and identify if they fund political parties. When cases of political contributions
are noticed, further request for information are sent to the country and the relevant associations to
determine the nature, amount, purpose and recipient of the participation.
In-kind political contributions made
The amount of indirect financial political participation is estimated using a calculation formula based
on Renault Group’s participation in the association’s annual turnover, the proportion of the political
donation within that turnover, and subsequently Renault Group’s corresponding contribution to that
donation. When the current year’s association figures are not available, the calculation is based on the
previous year’s ratio.
G1-6 – Payment practices
Renault Group does not have policies that discriminate
SMEs in terms of payment practices. Renault Group’s
existing systems do not provide information on some
supplier attributes such as their categorization as SMEs, but
the Group is working to address this issue.
Renault
Group,
as
part
of
its
sustainable
supplier
management policy, examines on a case-by-case basis the
ways to support suppliers, including, where appropriate,
exceptional financial support.
Metric label
Description and methodology [DP MDR-M_02]
Average number of days to pay
invoice from date when contractual
or legal term of payment starts to be
calculated
To determine the average number of days to pay an invoice from the date on which the contractual or
legal payment period starts, the calculations are based on the payment terms negotiated with
suppliers. In the absence of negotiated terms, the legal deadlines specific to each country apply.
Payment terms are counted from the invoice issuance date to the payment date.
Intragroup transactions are excluded from the reporting scope, and the calculations are based on the
value of the invoices and not only the number of invoices.
95% of the scope is reported based on actual data as of the end of December 2024. The remaining
scope is constructed by extrapolating the actual rates
Number of outstanding legal
proceedings for late payments
To determine the number of ongoing legal proceedings related to payment delays, a collaboration is
carried out between the Legal Department and the local Accounting Manager.

APPENDIX
IRO-2 – Disclosure Requirements in ESRS covered
by Renault Group sustainability report
The process to determine material information to disclose is
based on the principles outlined in ESRS 1 section 3.2. This
process involves a detailed analysis at the datapoint level to
ascertain whether the prescribed information relates to one
or more material CSRD sub-topics. If a particular datapoint
is deemed material, it is included in the sustainability
statement. Renault Group has not relied on thresholds;
instead, the process is based on the results of the double-
materiality analysis. [DP IRO-2_13]

2.3 Vigilance plan
2.3.1 Vigilance plan governance
Since 2021, Renault Group has set up a governance structure
dedicated to the Duty of Vigilance. This governance better
defines the various levels in the company that are involved
in preparing, approving and effectively implementing the
Vigilance Plan:
●Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee: the action plan is
coordinated
and
monitored
by
the
Sustainable
Development Division through a Steering Committee. The
committee validates the content of the vigilance plan, in
particular the annually updated risk mapping.
●The Strategy Director, who reports directly to the CEO, is
the sponsor of the Vigilance Plan.
●The Group Ethics and Compliance Committee (CECG): the
results of the Vigilance Plan are presented once a year to
the CECG. The CECG is made up of members representing
all functions of the Group. This committee is responsible
for
validating,
evaluating
and
improving
the
effectiveness of the ethics and regulatory compliance
mechanisms in place.
●The members of the Select Group Works Council who are
signatories to the 2013 Global Framework Agreement:
they were informed before the publication of the
Vigilance Plan. The Select Group Works Council, which
represents employees, is made up of a secretary and 10
deputy secretaries. It plays a decisive role in the respect
and quality of social dialogue.
●The Leadership Team: key issues relating to the duty of
vigilance may be referred to the Leadership Team for
information or decisions.
●The Board of directors: The Strategy and Sustainable
Development Committee approves the key points of the
Vigilance Plan, including the mapping of Human Rights
risks (details in section 3.1.6.3).
Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee
Composition
Chairperson
Director of Sustainable Development
Permanent Members: 1 representative
from each of the following divisions
Sustainable Development Division;
Human Resources Division;
HSEE Division (Health, Safety, Environment, Ergonomics);
Prevention and Protection Division;
Ethics Division;
Risk Management Division;
Purchasing Department;
Public Affairs Division;
Legal Division
Specialists as required
Planning and coordination
The Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee meets once a month under normal circumstances.
In 2024, the Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee held 11 plenary meetings.
Note: Renaut Group’s commitments as well as its actions to
fight climate change and monitoring indicators are
described in detail in our 2024 Universal Registration
Document (CSRD – ESRS E1) and in the Climate Report.
Details regarding governance, risk management policy and
financial resources mobilized as well as operational levers
and planned innovations to reduce emissions (all Scopes of
the GHG Protocol) are described in the CSRD (ESRS E1).
Responsibilities
Tasks
Details of duties
Strategy
Definition of the strategic priorities for the Vigilance Plan.
Priorities
Roadmap decision, direction and progress monitoring.
Preparing presentations for the Risk, Ethics and Compliance Committees.
Supervision
Monitoring of indicators, including the IMCR (Regulatory Compliance Maturity Index) and the CHRB
(Corporate Human Rights Benchmark).
Review of current actions and results.
Definition of the next steps.

2.3.2 Human rights and fundamental freedoms
2.3.2.1 Risk mapping: identification, analysis and prioritisation
Methodology
As part of its duty of vigilance, Renault Group has drawn up a
mapping of the risks that its own activity poses to women and
men in terms of Human Rights.
The methodology adopted is part of the same system as the
Group’s major risk management system. The last update
was at end 2024.
Nine risks have been identified, analysed and prioritised by
specialists in the following divisions:
●Human Resources (Diversity and Inclusion, International
Labour Relations, Compensation and Benefits), Ethics &
Compliance, Prevention & Protection,
●Sustainable Development and
●Risk Management.
The Group HRD validates annually the mapping and the
associated action plan.
This mapping is based on:
●a list of risks resulting from a benchmark carried out with
industrial companies in the automotive, food processing,
luxury goods, energy and transport sectors;
●the expertise of contributing functions;
●a process for analyzing different types of alerts, including
WhistleB alerts (See 2.3.6), but also alerts from other
stakeholders such as worker representatives, NGOs,
investors or even extra-financial rating agencies;
●monitoring of the social, economic and regulatory
context of the regions in which the sites are located. A
watch has been set up on human rights and fundamental
freedoms. Any new event (new country of operation, new
geopolitical situation) will be analysed and may impact
the risk mapping.
Results
Identification, analysis and prioritisation
Risks
Net criticality* -
All types
of sites
HR01. Enslavement, forced labour and human
trafficking
HR02. Child labour
HR03. Indecent working conditions
HR04. Violation to freedom of unions,
association & collective bargaining
HR05. Harm to individuals, including invasion
of privacy and harassment
HR06. Discrimination in hiring
HR07. Discrimination in employment
HR08. Compensation – living wage
HR09. Risk of negative impact on local
communities

* This criticality is net of the control mechanisms already in place. It is the result of combining the impact and probability of the various risks. It does not take into
account the impact of progress plans that are underway, have not yet produced their effects, or are planned.
** To ensure consistency within the Group, the impacts are assessed using the same impact grid as the one used by the Risk Management Division for Group Risk Factors.

2.3.2.2 Procedures for regular assessment of the situation of the Group
and its subsidiaries based on the risk mapping
The assessment of the situation of the Group and its
subsidiaries in view of the risk mapping is based on the
following pillars:
●monitoring the implementation of human rights and
fundamental
freedoms
policies
defined
in
global
framework agreements.
●in the event of whistleblowing, a handling process is
formalised (See section 2.3.6).
With regard to the risks of malicious acts that could affect
individuals:
●the Group Prevention and Protection Division regularly
conducts self-assessments of the risk level of each of our
sites.
●a country risk map is drawn up and regularly updated to
adapt measures to prevent malicious acts.
2.3.2.3 Actions to mitigate risks or prevent serious harm
Risks
Mitigation and prevention measures
Enslavement, forced labour and human trafficking
●Implementation of the 2013 Global Framework Agreement (GFA) and ILO Conventions 29 and
105: elimination of all forms of forced and compulsory labour.
Child labour
●Implementation of the 2013 GFA and ILO Conventions 138 and 182: the minimum age defined
by Renault Group is 15 years. Beyond 15 years, the regulations of each country apply.
Indecent working conditions
●The action plans related to this risk are managed by the HSEE Division and are described in
section 2.3.3.
Violation of the freedom of unions, association
& collective bargaining
●A Global Group Works Council, bringing together staff representatives from several countries,
is the key forum for an open, responsible international social dialogue.
●For career management, implementation of a “People Review” process with the involvement
of the HR Division. One of the aims is to prevent discrimination against unionised employees.
●88.3% of the Group’s employees are covered by a collective bargaining agreement at branch
and/or company level.
Harm to individuals, including invasion of privacy and
harassment*
 
 
 
*This risk includes potential malicious acts
on site or while travelling
●Implementation of the Telework Addendum 2021 to the 2019 GFA.
●Implementation of mechanisms for the protection of personal data: see section 2.2.3.1.6.1  of
this Document.
●Apply the rules and processes that make up the security management system.
●Provide security rules for travellers and expatriates to follow according to the level of risk
based on the country risks.
●Use a digital tool enabling travellers to report their position and receive alerts in the event of
an incident.
●Training courses in information protection and cyber risks are provided on several platforms,
including e-learning (since 2021), French Home Office information sessions, and targeted
training courses.
●Zero Discrimination Policy for all types of discrimination (See Diversity & Inclusion Charter).
●Guide to handling discrimination alerts for the HR function.
Discrimination in hiring
●Implementation of the 2013 GFA and ILO Conventions 100 and 111.
●Zero Discrimination Policy for all types of discrimination, at all stages of working life in
accordance with the Diversity & Inclusion Charter (2022).
●Inclusive recruitment guide for all recruiters, including managers.
Discrimination in employment
●For career management, implementation of a “People Review” process.
●Zero Discrimination Policy for all types of discrimination, at all stages of working life in
accordance with the Diversity & Inclusion Charter (2022).
●“Diversity & Inclusion” plan with initiatives and training programs focused on workplace
discrimination awareness.
●Pay policy with rules that allow for the analysis and correction of pay differentials between
positions with similar levels of responsibility and performance, including between women
and men.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

Renault Group
Sustainable development
Risks
Mitigation and prevention measures
Compensation: living wage
●Implementation of ILO Convention 100 in the 2013 GFA: equal pay for work of equal value.
●Application of legal and conventional provisions applicable in the countries on the
remuneration structures.
Risk of negative impact on affected
communities (including local communities
and indigenous peoples)
●Prevention: An ongoing dialogue is established between NGOs and the Sustainable
Development Department to ensure that local issues are understood. In a spirit of prevention,
since 2024 the Group’s Public Affairs Department has implemented a guideline to ensure the
consistent dialogue with the affected communities and the local public authorities
concerned. Against this backdrop, the Group updated its mapping of affected communities
and identified potential impacts on indigenous peoples in South America and the Maghreb,
as well as on the ends of its value chain (see Ch. 2.2.3.3).
●Revitalisation: Wherever the Group is established, it contributes to the revitalisation of the
employment pool in collaboration with the local authorities.
●Revitalisation: where there is a significant impact on employment, revitalisation plans are
implemented to develop employment.
●Implementation of the 2019 GFA: employees who wish to commit themselves to solidarity-
based associations and companies have specific arrangements, in compliance with local
legislation, as the time spent on these activities can be considered as working time.
In the event of difficulties in implementing global framework
agreements, a memorandum signed in January 2018 with
the signatories is an action guide for dealing with them
jointly.
Training
Since 2019, the “Together in Diversity” e-learning course,
available in seven languages and covering several types of
diversity (disability, gender, intercultural, intergenerational,
religion, sexual orientation and identity and trade unionism)
and inspired by real cases of workplace discrimination, has
enabled employees to better identify cognitive biases, combat
them and thus improve inclusion.
Since early 2023, e-learning has been available in French and
English to raise employee awareness of human rights issues in
companies and the measures implemented by the Group.
Since 2024, a series of several e-learning modules has been
developed:
●Available in 12 languages, an e-learning module supports
managers and teams in welcoming and integrating
employees with disabilities, in particular autism, DYS, ADHD,
hearing impairment, visual impairment, reduced mobility
and intellectual disability.
●An “Inclusive Management” e-learning module, available in
10 languages, supports managers with good managerial
practices to be implemented to get the best out of all
employees in a discrimination-free work environment based
on respect and consideration for everyone.
●A
“Hiring
permit”
e-learning
module,
available
in
13  languages and dedicated to recruiters, including
managers, aims to promote a fair and inclusive recruitment
environment within Renault Group.

Focus: Diversity and Inclusion
In addition to the risk mitigation and prevention measures listed in this table, Renault Group has set itself ambitious targets for diversity and
inclusion.
The Renault Group Diversity and Inclusion Plan is based on four pillars:
●
Ensure fair and respectful treatment of all;
●
Promote an inclusive work environment;
●
Facilitate the integration and development of all in the company;
●
Increase the representation of various under-represented forms of diversity, particularly women, at all levels, in all professions and in all
countries where the company operates. By 2030, Renault Group aims to have 30% women in management positions, 40% in 2040 and
50% in 2050.
In this context, Renault Group is implementing actions in several areas, starting with the Zero Discrimination Policy to guarantee a respectful
and inclusive environment for all forms of diversity and eliminate all types of discrimination in day-to-day activities and at all stages of
working life – including recruitment, training, pay and career development. The action plan, which is reviewed each year, has several
priorities, starting with gender diversity and disability, with the implementation of programmes, training and other initiatives focused on
raising awareness of discrimination among all employees and on the development and inclusion of diversity.
To strengthen its commitment to promoting diversity and combating discrimination, Renault Group has signed several charters with
international organisations and is involved in inter-company networks that exchange best practices in Diversity & Inclusion:
●
the UN Women’s Empowerment Principles and, in France, the #StOpE Charter (Stop Everyday Sexism in the Workplace),
●
UN Free & Equal standards against discrimination of LGBT+ people  and in France, the Autre Cercle Charter for the inclusion of LGBT+
people in the workplace,
●
The International Labour Organisation’s Global Business and Disability Charter and, in France, the #ManifestInclusion Charter to promote
the inclusion of individuals with disabilities in economic life,
●
in France, the Charte des 50+ for the promotion of employees over 50.
2.3.2.4 System for monitoring the measures implemented
and evaluating their effectiveness
The Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee monitors
measures on a monthly basis. A summary is presented to the
Ethics and Compliance Committee once a year (for details,
see section 2.3.1 Governance of the Vigilance Plan).
The effectiveness of the initiatives put in place is measured
notably:
●in light of the number of incidents reported by signatory
parties to the 2013 global framework agreement, resulting in
the implementation of the measures provided for in the
201 memorandum mentionned in section  2.3.2.3), we can
state that in 2024, as in previous years, no incident was
subject to a measure in the framework of the memorandum.
●through reports in the whistleblowing tool.
●through monitoring the number of malicious acts.
The figures below also reflect the effectiveness of measures
taken by the Group:

2.3.3 Health, safety, ergonomics
2.3.3.1 Risk mapping: identification, analysis and prioritisation
Methodology
As part of its duty of vigilance, Renault Group has drawn up a
mapping of the risks that its activities pose to persons
present on the sites. The last update was in 2024.
This mapping is based on:
●risk assessment performed by specialists for every work-
related activity;
●statistical analysis of accidents and occupational
illnesses that occurred over the past five years at Group
sites (See 2.3.3.3);
●knowledge of the variety of activities at industrial, tertiary
and testing sites.

This mapping is updated on the basis of the PDCA (Plan, Deploy, Control, Act) dynamic at least once a year or according to

2.3.3.2 Procedures for regular assessment of the situation of the Group
and its subsidiaries based on the risk mapping
The risk situation assessment is based on four pillars:
A. Site self-assessments;
B. Field audits by corporate and or by teams composed of members of the HSEE and other networks and members of
corporate to align the levels of requirements;
C. Coaching, along with assessments, based on the HSEE Mandatory Rules and associated key requirements of the various
HSEE areas (HSEE in projects, Safety, Burns and asphyxiation prevention, Ergonomics, Industrial hygiene, and Environment);
D. Statistical analysis of frequencies of occupational accidents and illnesses.
The results of the assessments are carefully reviewed to cross check the Group’s HSEE strategy and strategic road map, and allow
fine tuning when necessary.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

Renault Group

2.3.3.3 Actions to mitigate risks and prevent serious harm
Risks
Mitigation and prevention measures
Illnesses of physical origin
(vibrations, noise, etc.)
●Apply Industrial Hygiene and Health (HI&S) Mandatory Rule #8, encompassing 12 key requirements.
Chemical-induced illnesses
●Apply Health Mandatory Rules #1, 2, 4, 5 and 6, each encompassing 6 to 9 key requirements, and implement a
substitution policy for the most harmful substances.
●To limit chemical risks, any plan to use a new chemical product in the Group involves assessing the related
health and environmental risks and ascertaining compliance with regulation and internal standards prior to
approval and use. Whenever these chemicals are used,
a notice is provided stating the risks and instructions to use them safely.
Psychosocial disorders
●Apply Health Mandatory Rule #7, encompassing 6 key requirements.
Accidents with machinery
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rules #2 and 3, encompassing 11 key requirements regarding risk prevention related to
machinery intervention and 12 key requirements for the safety of machinery installations.
●Implementation at all sites of the checklist approach to machine safety inspections, which applies to new and
serial life machinery.
●Provide safety design standards and guidance to drive compliance with the machinery directive (ISO, CCS, etc.).
Slips, falls and trips
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rule #1, encompassing 4 key requirements.
●Slips, trips and falls prevention guidance.
Pedestrian and vehicle traffic
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rule #4, encompassing 14 key requirements.
●Traffic memorandum: Safe Site, Safe Vehicle, Safe Driver.
●Storage rack memorandum.
Falls from height
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rule #7, encompassing 4 key requirements.
●Implement appropriate safety EN, ISO standards (for example, ISO 14122 parts 1-4).
Burns and asphyxiation
●Apply from 2022 the 6 “Burns and asphyxiation” Mandatory Rules.
Lifting
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rule #5, encompassing 6 key requirements and deployment of the standard on the
dies storage in stamping.
●Pre-use check by operator and periodical check by competent person.
Electrical
●Apply Safety Mandatory Rules #2 (LOTO / Consignation on EV and electrical installation before intervention)
and #3 (isolation adequately designed).
●Implement appropriate safety EN, ISO standards (e.g., EN 50110, NFC 18510, NFC 18550).
Manual manipulations
(Except MSD)
●Integrate manual handling and manipulation in Specific Workstation Induction.
●Supervision during workstation observation.
Musculoskeletal disorders (MSD)
and ergonomics-related
accidents
●Apply the Ergonomics Mandatory Rules, encompassing 16 associated key requirements.
HEALTH
SAFETY
ERGONOMICS

Group Mandatory Rules visuals
Management Policy and System
As part of Renault Group’s commitment to health and
safety, a dedicated department has been in place since
2016, reporting directly to the Leadership Team.
Renault Group’s commitment is also reflected in the CSR
announcement (April 2021) to achieve zero accidents and
occupational illnesses and in the signing of a Health and Safety
policy by the Group CEO and HR Director (October 2021).
Our ambition is therefore “zero accidents and occupational
illnesses” by 2030. We have drawn up a strategic roadmap
that shows how we will progress towards this ambitious
objective.
To deal with risks, the Group has established qualitative
standards called Mandatory Rules and associated key
requirements that cover industrial hygiene, health, safety,
ergonomics, risks of burns and asphyxia, and environment in
projects and in mass production.
We are in the preparatory phase for ISO45001 certification with
25 health and safety managers already trained by AFNOR and
1  site (Bursa in Türkiye) already certified, which will serve as a
benchmark.
The Internal Control Division has set up a dedicated procedure
for checking that sites are aware of and take into account the
health, safety and ergonomics management system.
We are drawing up HSEE guidelines presenting the
organization, roles and responsibilities of Renault Group,
describes the HSEE management system and processes of
Renault Group, details the commitments of the Group
(policy, challenges and ambitions), and summarizes all the
elements aimed at meeting them (procedures, tools,
training, etc.).
The HSEE vade-mecum was published at the end of 2024
and is a critical tool to foster a safety culture and promote
sustainable performance within the Group to reduce
accidents, occupational illnesses, deliver carbon-neutral
sites and reduce the environmental footprint of Renault
Group (water consumption, air emissions, discharges into
water, waste and biodiversity). 
Communication
Each month, the HSEE Division communicates widely (to more
than 1,000 people of various functions and hierarchical levels)
the statistical results and progress of all the initiatives within its
scope. This communication mainly includes results and
thematic summaries, including information from regional, site
or country HSEE coordinators, corresponding to the sharing of
best practices.
A workshop managers’ meeting dedicated to each of the
areas of health, safety and ergonomics brings together all
members of the network each month to discuss new initiatives
and their progress and share best practices.
Project Management
The HSEE project team has developed 5 Mandatory Rules
and 22 key requirements (5MRs/22KRs). These rules have
been integrated into the company’s quality management
system.
The
contributing
business
lines
have
been
identified, and training has been carried out to ensure that
the mandatory rules are applied in projects.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
253

## Page 256

Renault Group
Sustainable development
In 2024, 25 projects applied these Mandatory Rules. In
addition, the Process Engineering Department has included
a chapter on HSEE requirements in the specifications in
order to ensure that the suppliers’ technical solutions are
compliant from day one, for vehicle projects.
Training
Employee awareness and skills are among the main drivers
of the overall HSEE strategy.
To raise awareness of health and safety among employees
and to ensure the necessary skills, a three-tiered training
program has been defined:
Tier 1 – HSE basics with training such as: Safety Workstation
Induction (SWI) training, virtual reality hazard hunt, new
employee
induction,
general
introduction
to
HSEE
Mandatory Rules, safety dojo, and accident management.
Tier 2 – Recognised qualifications with external training
courses conducted by reputable training organisations such
as: NEBOSH and ISO45001 AFNOR on HSE management
systems, machine-related safety (e.g. in-house or CMSE®
training).
Tier 3 – Specific theme-based or technical training covering
HSEE-specific subjects targeting employees that may be
exposed to risks associated with their activity, such as LOTO
(Lock-Out Tag-Out – identification and isolation of supply
energies), electric vehicles, machinery safety, chemical safety,
use of lifting equipment, operating handling equipment, and
ergonomics.
In addition to training provided at corporate level, sites are
also very involved in training processes addressing their
specific needs (local authorizations: electricians, crane
operators, forklift drivers, etc.).
Video simulations of specific risks identified during accident
investigations (some of the most serious) are produced and
shared widely throughout the Group to raise awareness.
Since 2019, 29 simulations have been created and shared at
various levels of the company.
Once the appropriate risk control measures have been
identified, they are summarized and shared in a cross-
functional memo, and the deployment of the associated
action plans is monitored monthly. To date, 36 cross-
functional memos have been or are being deployed.
Other risk reduction actions
Psychosocial disorders
Renault Group clearly affirms its commitment to well-being
and mental health in the Health and Safety at Work Policy
signed in 2021.
HI&S Mandatory Rule #7, with 6 key requirements, is
dedicated to psychological health. It was defined in
partnership between the HSEE Division, the medical
coordinators and the HR function. Its deployment was
initiated in 2022 at industrial and engineering sites. To date,
nearly 30 committees have been appointed and have begun
to implement the requirements for the promotion of
psychological health and well-being at work.
Chemical-induced illnesses
The Industrial Hygiene team works daily to control worker
exposure to hazardous substances and chemical compliance
covering CLP/GHS/REACH regulations and to reduce the
number of the most hazardous chemical substances used at
Renault
Group
sites,
including
products
containing
carcinogenic, mutagenic, and reprotoxic substances and
endocrine disruptors. Since the commitment made in 2021 to
replace 50% of the most dangerous products by 2030
compared with the number recorded in February 2021, 54.1%
have been eliminated, so the target has already been exceeded.
Based on the results of the self-assessments of the
8  Industrial Hygiene Mandatory Rules/60 associated key
requirements, the audits carried out in 2023 and the
seriousness of the potential consequences for health, a
priority action programme was established and rolled out
incrementally throughout 2024 with a monthly follow-up on
their progress and verification of their implementation by
the HSEE Division. Particular emphasis has been placed on
training operators working in positions where the most toxic
substances are handled or emitted and on preventing
psychosocial risks and vibration risks.
In 2024, 8  business line audits on these 8  Mandatory Rules
were conducted.
Training in the regulations on the transport of dangerous
goods was also provided, with almost 149 people trained,
more than half concerning Li–ion batteries.
Ergonomics: Musculoskeletal disorders (MSD)
In 2024, the Ergonomics Department supported 22 new
vehicle projects, developed in all Renault Technical Centers
(France, Romania, Brazil, Türkiye), with an emphasis on the
inherent ergonomic design. In parallel, the new ergonomic
management system, which consists of three pillars
(Ergonomics memorandum, Ergonomics evaluation method
and Ergonomics Mandatory Rules), is now deployed in all
industrial and engineering sites.
1. Ergonomics memorandum
The ergonomics memorandum is a reference guide
covering standard ergonomic principles adapted to all
areas of activity. Six specific ergonomics memoranda have
been finalized and deployed: Bodyshop, assembly shop,
paint shop, stamping, logistics, metal bodywork, and sales &
aftersales.
2. Ergonomics evaluation method (M2E)
The new workstation ergonomics assessment is now
deployed in all industrial sites. The aim is to perform in-depth
risk assessments, in order to define action plans to reduce
musculoskeletal disorders and thus the associated number
of days off work. All workstations at industrial sites are
managed with this tool. All managers are trained by the
human factors experts of the plants, and 12,755 workstations
have been assessed.

3. Ergonomics Mandatory Rules
The key criteria and experience gathered from all previous
works have been consolidated into six Mandatory Rules. Each
industrial site carries out its self-assessment annually. The
Ergonomics Department carries out several surveys each year:
in 2024, 4 audits were carried out within the Group.
All these specifications are used upstream in projects to
avoid the introduction of new ergonomic strains whilst
simultaneously applied in serial life to enhance working
conditions.
Traffic (risks associated with logistics and internal traffic
activities)
In 2024, the HSEE Division, in collaboration with the site and
corporate logistics departments, is continuing to improve
the tools and standards developed previously, including a
standard on expectations for handling equipment, a
standard on rolling stock, an update of the Traffic
Memorandum and a “Safe Site, Safe Vehicle, Safe Driver”
(SSSVSD) risk assessment method based on the Traffic
Memorandum.
The sites conducted a “Safe Site, Safe Vehicle, and Safe
Driver” (SSSVSD) self-assessment allowing them to evaluate
their level of compliance with the rules and prioritize their
prevention actions. Renault is continuing its collaboration
with the Association of European Vehicle Logistics (ECG) to
reduce the number of accidents when delivering cars.
Renault plays an active role in working groups, specifically
on accident analysis, safe loading/unloading processes and
delivery site safety.
Machinery (risks associated with machinery use)
Tools and procedures have been developed to identify and
mitigate machine-related risks. These include, to name only
a few, the safety report for the approval of new installations,
inspection control sheets, and procedures for working under
power. Tools such as the “machine interaction” application
contribute to safe conditions for interaction with machines.
The machinery safety inspection check-list tool, launched in
2021, has resulted in 1,078 addition inspections carried out
in 2024.
The monthly workshop managers’ meeting initiated in 2020
continues and brings together site health and safety
managers and machine maintenance experts. Various more
or less technical machine safety topics are covered.
More than ten sites received in 2024 coaching for
inspections and machinery safety by corporate machine
safety specialists.
Thermal (risks of burns and asphyxiation)
The HSEE Division and the Prevention and Protection
Division have introduced a new procedure to ensure the
safety of work involving hot spots (fire permits), such as
welding or cutting with a blowtorch. This “fire permit” is
established in order to prevent the risk of fire and explosion
caused by hot spot work. Feedback on this new procedure
has been positive.
In 2024, the HSEE Division carried out coaching sessions on
the 6 Mandatory Rules for preventing burns and/or
asphyxiation and the 57 associated key requirements at a
number of industrial sites. The sites conducted their own
self-assessments. These enable them to assess their level of
compliance with the rules and to prioritize the preventive
actions to be implemented.
Electrical risks (related to the electric vehicle)
The HSEE dimension has been strengthened in new electric
and hybrid vehicle projects from the product and process
design stage.
Safety is integrated upstream in the design of the product by
standard solutions like safe design of high-voltage batteries,
by electrical lockout/tagout procedure for safe assembly and
maintenance on each vehicle. All the players involved in these
projects have been trained. A specific training programme is
periodically
updated
with
the
support
of
certified
organisations (e.g. APAVE or Bureau Veritas). The theoretical
part of the training program was supplemented by several
practical exercises. It incorporates more practice with a
specific
Dojo
(safety
school)
focused
on
hazard
characterization and the implementation of safety rules.
The new training program has already been implemented on
several major sites. Deployment continued through our
Academies. More than 5,000 people have been trained since
2020, including 600 in 2024 at our production sites.
Renault Group HSEE Division is represented in the AFNOR
working group dedicated to the NFC18550 standard review
(NFC 18550 standard: Electrical risk prevention – Work
activities on electric and hybrid vehicles).
With regard to transport and the VE battery, guides and
training sessions on the regulatory requirements for suitable
packaging and testing the battery before transport have
been implemented by the in-house safety advisor for the
transport of dangerous goods.
Management
of
safety
of
outside
companies
and
concurrent activities
To support its subcontractor management procedure and
ensure the safety of subcontractor interventions on its
premises, Renault Group has chosen to digitize the
prevention
plan
through
a
platform
dedicated
to
subcontractor
management,
called
CMT
(Contractor
Management Tool).
In addition to the CMT tool and to improve the risk
assessment and increase the skills of the managers of
Renault subcontractors, the HSEE Division launched a two-
day training course on the legal requirements related to
subcontractor management and the assessment of
interference risks. This training is based on the CARSAT
training module. By the end of 2024, around 1,200 people
were trained, representing around 2/3 of the population
concerned in France. All targeted employees are expected
to be trained by mid-2025, and appropriate training and the
platform will then be deployed in all other countries where
Renault operates.

2.3.3.4 System for monitoring the measures implemented
and evaluating their mitigation effectiveness
The Duty of Vigilance Steering Committee monitors
measures on a monthly basis. A summary is presented to the
Ethics and Compliance Committee once a year (for details,
see section 2.3.1 Governance of the Vigilance Plan).
HSEE is building its digital expertise
The DHSEE is going digital with a variety of applications
managing, for example, the reporting of occupational
accidents and illnesses, alerts and analysis of root causes,
the management of chemical products, the evaluation of
movements and postures, the management of outside
companies,
as
well
as
regulatory
monitoring
and
compliance. Eventually, these tools will cover all aspects of
HSEE. Digitalization helps to measure the effectiveness of
the measures implemented.
Statistical evaluation to complement field evaluation
Health and safety figures are audited by the independent
third party to a reasonable level of assurance since the 2022
financial year.
A total of 15 10MR/74kKR audits (including 5 factory audits)
were conducted in 2024, bringing the total number of audits
conducted since 2017 to 394. In addition, Health department
and Ergonomics department have respectively performed
8 audits and 4 audits according to their respective
Mandatory Rules.
After a significant reduction of the FR113, the Group decided
to change the main indicator of occupational accidents to
FR214 (lost-time accidents) in 2021. The objective is to rapidly
reduce the FR2, while maintaining a stable FR1, thanks to all
the HSE measures initiated.
The Group also promotes the reporting of low-severity (first
aid) accidents and continues to improve the reporting
process, even in countries where reporting is not mandatory.
No targets are set to encourage their reporting. They do not
appear in the graph below.
The combination of preventive and corrective actions
described above has resulted in a reduction of the FR2 from
a high of 3.7 in 2018 to 1.35 in 2024.
At the same time, the percentage of compliance with the
Safety 10 Mandatory Rules / 74 Key Requirements of
industrial plants (biggest contributors to accidents and
occupational illnesses) increased from 57.4% in 2018 to
66.9% at end-2023.
These developments demonstrate the effectiveness of the
implemented strategy.
Health and Safety results (such as FR2) may constitute a
quantifiable variable remuneration criterion, both for
employees and for senior executives, depending on local or
national
agreements
(for
employees)
and
the
recommendations of the Governance and Remuneration
Committee (for senior executives).
Occupational accidents: Trends15
Change in occupational accident frequency
and severity rates
Change in the number of occupational
accidents per year
The trend is therefore encouraging and is moving towards
the Group’s objective of zero accidents by 2030, with an
intermediate step of 1.0 expected in 2025.
