Risk and control
04
The corporate functions that play a major role in the risk control and management system include:
Corporate
functions
Main tasks
Risk Management
Department (DMR)
●Updates the mapping of the Group’s major risks.
●Monitors the associated action plans intended to reduce the impact or probability of the main risks identified.
●Provides support to the operational entities, corporate functions and projects to map their risks and reduce these risks.
See paragraph 4.1.3 for more details on the risk management system.
Internal Control
Department (DCI)
●Provides reasonable assurance on the level of internal control by assessing its maturity and effectiveness: deployment of
self-assessment questionnaires and performance of compliance tests.
●Ensures that action plans are identified and implemented to correct the shortcomings identified.
See paragraph 4.1.4 for more details on the internal control system.
Performance and
Control Department
●Ensures that all personnel comply with management rules.
●Assists operational staff in the coordination and monitoring of their action plans.
Quality Division
●Defines the Group’s Quality policy and sets Quality objectives.
●Manages the reference framework for project development milestone expectations and the Group’s Quality Management
System.
●Conducts compliance assessments at milestone stages and compliance assessments of the Quality Management System.
Ethics & Compliance
Department (DEC)
●Manages the anticorruption compliance system.
●Ensures that the regulatory compliance systems are robust.
Health, Safety,
Environment and
Ergonomics Division
(HSEE)
●Develops and deploys the HSEE policy within the Group’s entities and projects to prevent harm to people (occupational
accidents and occupational illnesses) and the environment:
– Designs and deploys standards and training.
– Coordinates a control system to verify compliance with standards.
Prevention and
Protection
Department
●Develops and deploys the Group’s overall security policy to protect the company’s people and its tangible and intangible
assets.
●Prevents and anticipates risks: crisis management and monitoring system, awareness-raising and training.
●Interfaces with the relevant institutional services.
IS-IT Division
●Is responsible for the development, maintenance and quality of all the Group’s information systems
●Leads the Group’s digital transformation.
●Ensures the cybersecurity of information systems and vehicles and oversees compliance with data protection regulations.
Legal Department
●Helps to ensure that the company’s activities comply with the applicable laws and regulations in force;
●Manages legal risks associated with agreements or contractual clauses;
●Protects the Group’s interests in managing litigation risks, where necessary with specialised external lawyers;
●Advises senior management on crisis management and, more generally, legal issues affecting the company’s overall strategy.
Sustainable
Development
Department
●Defines Renault Group’s sustainable development strategy and steers performance across all functions.
●Defines the objectives and deploys the company’s environmental (decarbonisation, circularity and biodiversity) and social
business (solidarity mobility) roadmaps.
●Manages the Group’s reputation and ESG reporting to investors, NGOs, employees and communities, as well as its
publications (CSRD, taxonomy, integrated report, climate report and vigilance plan).
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
363

## Page 366

Renault Group
Risk and control
Third line of control: Internal Audit Department
The
Internal
Audit
Department
(DAI)
conducts
an
independent and objective assessment of the corporate
governance, risk management and control processes as
defined within the Group. The mission, role, responsibilities
and scope of the Internal Audit Department are defined in an
audit charter, the updated version of which was approved by
the Audit and Risk Committee (CAR) in February 2023.
Through its recommendations, the DAI helps to improve the
safety of operations and optimise the company’s overall
performance. At the end of each assignment, the DAI
distributes a report and a summary note, which are
systematically distributed to the areas audited, the functions,
entities and/or projects in question, the Chief Executive
Officer and the Chairman of the Group. The summary note
includes an opinion issued by Internal Audit that aims to give
an overall assessment of the level of control of the audited
activities: controlled, moderate, significant or major risk.
The DAI covers all entities and activities of Renault Group’s
automotive branch and mobility services.
The financial branch (Mobilize Financial Services) has its own
internal audit structure (See chapter 4.1.6.).  For entities in
partnership with Renault Group, Internal Audit may intervene if
the partner agrees and according to the partnership contract.
For activities entrusted to third parties, intervention by Internal
Audit is possible if the contract’s audit clause so provides.
The audit plan is made on an annual basis and covers a
rolling three-year period. It is verified by Senior Management
and approved by the Audit and Risk Committee (CAR). This
audit plan is revised as often as necessary to take into
account additional requests or necessary adjustments.
Internal Audit assignments make it possible to:
●Assess the compliance of processes and their application
with the rules, standards, laws and regulations in force;
●Assess the effectiveness of processes and the execution
of operations;
●Verify the quality of the controls performed by the
operational departments, the support and control functions;
●Suggest areas for improvement or progress in the form of
recommendations;
●Fight against fraud and corruption;
●Verify the effective implementation of the recommendations.
Following the recommendations made in each audit report, an
action plan defined by the audited entities is approved by the
internal audit function. Recommendations have three levels of
criticality (high, medium and low). The DAI ensures that the
recommendations are implemented. At least every six months,
it prepares a progress report on recommendations of high and
medium criticality for the Leadership Team and the CAR.
The DAI is certified by the IFACI1. This certification, in
accordance with the standards for the professional practice of
internal auditing (référentiel professionnel de l’audit interne –
RPAI), comprises 25 general requirements divided into 100
detailed requirements across five categories: positioning,
steering, GRC (governance, risks and compliance) assessment
programme, professionalism and audit processes.
Synchronisation of activities
The Risk Management, Internal Control, Ethics, and Internal
Audit Departments work closely together on a regular basis
to ensure a shared vision of the Group’s priority risks and
issues, to define a consistent approach and to ensure
effective monitoring between the second and third lines of
control. They synchronise their activities and ensure that
their combined actions provide reasonable control of risks.
This cooperation is facilitated and strengthened by the use
of a Governance, Risk and Compliance digital tool shared by
several of the key players in the second and third lines of
control: Risk management, Internal Control, Quality Audit
and Internal Audit. This tool has been in place in the
company since 2024.
4.1.2 Governance of risk management
The first two lines of control report on risk management and
internal control to the Risk and Internal Control Committee
(CRCI), which is also responsible for validating and regularly
assessing the effectiveness of the internal control and risk
management systems. This committee is chaired by the
Group’s Chief Financial Officer and co-chaired by the Director
of Audit and Risk. Its permanent members are the
representatives of the following functions: Risk Management,
Internal Control, Internal Audit, Performance and Control,
Ethics and Compliance, Legal, Human Resources and Quality.
Representatives from other corporate functions are invited
based on the agenda. It meets six times a year and examines,
in particular, the mapping of the Group’s major risks and the
plan for addressing them, the results of the annual internal
control self-assessments and the monitoring of associated
action plans, as well as proposals to improve the systems. The
CRCI may decide on corrective actions or request additional
information.
The first two lines of control also report to the Leadership
Team on an ad hoc basis through thematic presentations.
The second and third lines of control present the results of
their work to the Audit and Risk Committee (CAR), whose
duties are defined in chapter 3.1.6.1.

In the course of their duties, the Statutory Auditors assess
the level of risk management and internal control applicable
to the preparation and processing of accounting and
financial data as part of their work and, when necessary,
issue recommendations.
In the operating entities, functions and projects, the
Management Committee ensures the effectiveness of the
risk control and management systems and their progress.
4.1.3 Risk management
The Group applies a risk management approach at the
Group level, also applicable to the entities, Corporate
Functions and vehicle projects:
●Identifying risks with management and stakeholders;
●Prioritising risks according to their criticality, determined
on the basis of their residual impact and probability of
occurrence (after taking into account the control and
treatment plans in place);
●Defining ways to handle risks: elimination, prevention,
protection or transfer;
●Identifying and monitoring additional actions to be
applied to reduce residual risks;
Each risk is formalised through a standardised risk sheet and
risks are consolidated in a risk mapping on the relevant scope.
The mapping of the Group’s major risks is presented to the
CRCI, the Leadership Team and the CAR, who validate it.
The major risk factors to which the Group is exposed are
described in chapter 4.2.
To carry out its duties, the Risk Management Department
(DMR) relies on two networks in particular:
●The network of operational risk managers (RMO) within the
operating entities (countries, commercial and/or industrial
subsidiaries), corporate functions and the quality function
for vehicle projects. These RMO are the DMR’s relays for the
operational
implementation
of
risk
management
procedures within these scopes;
●The network of expert risk managers (RME) covering
specific risk areas. They consult on the standardised risk
management plans in their area of expertise.
To draw up the audit plan, the Internal Audit Division (DAI)
relies in particular on risk mapping to identify the most
relevant audit topics and assess risk coverage. Following
the auditing assignments, the DAI provides the DMR with
insight on the effective level of control of risks.
In 2024, the DMR focused its activities on:
●consolidating treatment plans and processes to improve
the control of the major risks;
●regular reporting to the governance bodies on major risks:
CRCI, Leadership Team and CAR;
●updating the mapping of the Group’s major risks using a
top-down process. This exercise involved interviewing
around 60 key managers of the Group and analysing the
mappings of the entities, corporate functions and vehicle
projects carried out at the end of 2023 and in 2024;
●updating
the
procedural
framework
for
risk
management;
●assisting the operating entities, corporate functions and
new vehicle projects for the implementation of risk maps
produced with the operational risk managers concerned
and for the monitoring of treatment plans;
●Rolling out the new Governance, Risk and Compliance
(GRC) tool to the network of operational risk managers as
well as the associated training.
In addition, awareness-raising actions for Group employees
about risk culture and the fundamentals of risk management
were continued by the DMR, particularly through e-learning
modules and in-person or distance learning courses.
4.1.4 Internal control
The internal control system is based on the various
guidelines and tools detailed below.
Ethical guidelines and corporate functions
of the Group
The company’s corporate functions define the policies and
standards to be deployed, which are then rolled out as
procedures and operating methods to frame operational
processes, in accordance with the principles outlined in the
Ethics Charter, the anti-corruption code of conduct and the
dedicated Codes of Ethics.
Based on the main risks identified with functions, relating to
operational processes, and likely to have a significant
impact on the achievement of objectives, the Internal
Control Department (DCI) manages internal control topics
within the functions:
●Sharing the “metarules“ defined by the functions, to be
applied within the activities;
●Identification and formalisation of the key controls aimed
at controlling the identified risks.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
365

## Page 368

Renault Group
Risk and control
The metarules and controls managed by the functions are
then included in the internal control self-assessment
questionnaire.
The DCI has thus defined an internal control standard,
structured around approximately 80 controls, grouped into
10 areas.
These elements are made available to all the Group’s
employees in the Referential Internal Control Standard
(RICS) on the internal control website.
These internal control guidelines are updated every year to
consider the evolution of identified risks and organisational
changes. Recommendations made following internal audits
or by the statutory auditors are taken into account during
this update.
The
DCI
relies
on
a
network
of
internal
control
correspondents who are affiliated with the Performance
and Control function of the subsidiaries and Corporate
Functions and are the key contacts for internal control
issues. They lead and ensure the implementation of internal
control objectives within their scope.
Control activity
Once a year, the DCI leads an internal control self-
assessment campaign covering the Group’s consolidated
entities. The CEOs of the entities validate these self-
assessments and commit to defining and implementing
action plans to remedy any internal control shortcomings
identified. These action plans are regularly monitored by
the DCI.
Since 2024, the self-assessment campaign has been fully
digitalised and monitored in a Governance, Risk and
Compliance (GRC) tool shared with the Risk Management,
the Internal Audit and the Quality Auditing Departments.
The results of these self-assessments are presented to the
Risk and Internal Control Committee (CRCI) and the Audit
and Risk Committee (CAR) once a year.
Compliance tests are conducted based on a sample of
entities by the internal controllers of the DCI to verify the
quality
of
the
self-assessments. 
These
tests
cover
approximately 10% of the check points assessed as
“effective” by the entities. Any significant deviation from the
expectations of the guidelines gives rise to an action plan.
The recently acquired companies are integrated into this
process
during
the
first
campaign
following
their
integration.
The DCI’s work in 2024 focused on:
●Continued action to improve the corruption prevention
system and support for the operational staff concerned; 
●Overhauling the internal control guidelines (RICS) taken
into account for the 2024 self-assessment campaign;
●Running training and webinars dedicated to supporting the
network of internal control correspondents and advisors on
their general knowledge of the control environment, the
specific points in the referential internal control standard
and modules targeted at getting started with the GRC tool;
●Organisation of a two-day on-site seminar bringing
together all internal control correspondents, with the
participation of internal audit and risk management;
●Continuation of preventive and detection controls by
strengthening the methodology applied to testing. 
The tone at the top in terms of internal control was reaffirmed
by the circulation of a message from Renault Group’s Chief
Financial Officer as an introduction to the annual self-
assessment questionnaire as well as his participation in the
internal control seminar held in April 2024.
Internal delegations and separation of offices
In addition to the hierarchical links, the Group has put in
place functional links so that corporate functions managers
can animate transversely their network of correspondents.
The decision-making process is based on a system of
internal delegation that determines in which areas and at
which levels operational managers may make decisions. All
the rules for delegating decision-making authority are
communicated to personnel via the intranet. Decision
requests are tracked in a workflow that applies the rules
specifying the persons to be involved, in accordance with
internal control procedures or documented in the minutes of
the committees responsible for making the decision.
The principle of segregation of duties and tasks is required
at all hierarchical and functional levels within the Group and
within the computer systems to facilitate independent
control and separate tasks and functions corresponding to
operations, the protection of property and their booking for
accounting purposes.

4.1.5 Quality and reliability of financial,
accounting and management disclosures
Senior
Management
communicates
Renault
Group’s
general objectives as part of the multiyear plan and annual
budgets, as well as the allocation of resources to the
operating
segments,
brands
and
functions.
The
Performance and Control function is responsible for
managing and measuring economic performance at the
various levels of the organisation (Group, operating
segments, brands and functions). Under the Group’s
management structure, its role is to:
●Set out the Group’s economic objectives and budget;
●Participate in the implementation of the internal control
system and Renault Group’s risk management approach;
●Coordinate the Group by measuring the performance of
entities, operating segments, brands and functions and,
in particular, by monitoring metrics relating to operating
margin for all operating segments and free cash flow for
the Automotive operating segments;
●Analyse proposed management decisions at any level from
an economic perspective, check their consistency with
standards, plans and budgets, judge their economic
relevance and give an opinion and recommendation on them.
Within the Global Financial Reporting and Controls
Department (GFRC), the Finance Delivery function is
responsible
for
producing
the
Group’s
consolidated
financial statements, and the Finance Integrity function is
responsible
for
structuring
the
controls
to
ensure
compliance with laws and regulations. Responsibility for
preparing the financial statements of the subsidiaries lies
with their Chief Financial Officers and Accounting Directors,
who report hierarchically to the Chairmen and Chief
Executive Officers of these subsidiaries and functionally to
the Head of the Finance Integrity function.
The Group has integrated information systems that enable
the simultaneous production of financial statements,
according to local guidelines and IFRS. This mechanism thus
guarantees the consistency of data in a context of
centralisation and consolidation of information within a
short time frame.
For the preparation of accounting and financial information,
the Group relies on FIRST (Financial Internal Reporting
STandards), an internal accounting manual drawn up in
accordance with IFRS indicating the Group’s fundamental
principles. These standards are continuously updated by the
Accounting Standards Department.
The consolidated financial statements are produced monthly
and published on a semi-annual and annual basis. Pre-close
statements are produced twice a year: May  31 for the June
close and October  31 for the December close. Summary
meetings are organised with the statutory auditors and
attended by senior members of the Finance Division and the
Chief Financial Officer as part of ongoing discussions. The
CAR (Audit and Risk Committee) is involved at every key stage
of the approval process for financial and accounting
disclosures. Consolidated turnover is published quarterly.
Within
the
Global
Financial
Reporting
and
Controls
Department (GFRC), the Internal Control function contributes
to the quality of financial information and performs its duties
based on the AMF reference framework. It covers not only the
processes involved in preparing financial information for
closing the accounts, forecasting phases or financial
communication but also the upstream operational processes
involved in producing this information.
Key components of the process for controlling
financial and accounting disclosures
Renault Group manages the decentralised operations of the
subsidiaries in its three operating segments in France or
abroad by relying on the following key strategies to deliver
high-quality accounting and financial information while
reducing the time needed to prepare the financial statements:
●Standardisation of operational systems upstream of
accounting is systematically sought;
●Deployment of the financial and accounting modules of
the ERP2 chosen by the Group in the industrial and/or
commercial, engineering and sales financing entities
throughout the world.
This
software
package
ensures
the
reliability
and
consistency of processed data. In particular, the definition
and monitoring of user profiles helps to ensure that the rules
of task separation are respected.
Control of basic transactions handled by operational systems,
where the initial control takes place, is key to ensuring that
accounting and financial data are reliable. The operational
systems feed data to the auxiliary accounting systems
through interfaces. These interfaces, which are continually
monitored to ensure that they capture all economic events for
each upstream process, then rapidly and regularly send the
data to the centralised accounting system.
The accounting teams have worked with IT staff to develop
a security process to protect the ERP in the event of a major
malfunction. A business continuity plan has been prepared
at corporate level. It is deployed in those subsidiaries that
use the ERP.
2 Enterprise Resource Planning system.

## Page 370

Renault Group
Risk and control
Statutory auditors’ charter
In connection with the statutory audit of its accounts,
Renault Group took the initiative in 2004 to draft a charter
concerning the tasks and independence of the statutory
auditors and sign it with them. This charter governs the
relationship between Renault Group (the parent company
and the French and foreign subsidiaries) and its statutory
auditors. The charter was updated in 2020 as part of the
renewal of the Board of Auditors in 2020 to consider
regulatory changes relating to statutory audits.
Financial communication
Renault Group has chosen to entrust all its financial
communication to the Investor Relations Division within the
Finance Division and to provide it with the functions
necessary to accomplish this task. The Investor Relations
Division is responsible for:
●Communication with the financial markets;
●Relations with investors and individual shareholders;
●Relations with financial rating agencies;
●Relations with analysts and investors specialised in
socially responsible investments;
●Relations with the regulatory authority (AMF);
●Steering the preparation of the annual and half-yearly
earnings reports and quarterly information;
●Filing the Universal Registration Document with the AMF,
produced under the responsibility of the Global Financial
Reporting and Controls Division.
4.1.6 Specificities of Mobilize Financial Services
Mobilize Financial Services (Mobilize F.S.) has a global
internal control system that aims to identify, analyse and
manage the main identifiable risks in relation to the
Company’s objectives. The Mobilize F.S. Group’s Internal
Control Committee has validated the general framework for
this system, which is described in the Internal Control
Charter.
This charter defines the system applicable to the French
and foreign companies over which Mobilize F.S. has
effective control and specifies in particular:
●The general arrangements for managing internal control;
●The local arrangements for subsidiaries, branches and
joint ventures;
●The special arrangements for various functional areas.
Risk control at Mobilize F.S. is overseen on three levels by
separate functions:
The first line of defence is exercised by the operational
functions in charge of day-to-day risk management as part of
the activities in their area of expertise. These functions
decide and are responsible for taking risks in the conducting
of transactions and the objectives assigned to them. They
exercise
this
responsibility
in
accordance
with
the
management rules and the risk limits defined by the
corporate functions.
The corporate functions are responsible for the definition of
rules, the management methods and measuring and
monitoring risks at the company level. Each corporate
function, in its area of expertise, manages and oversees the
risk management system through guidelines and objectives
for each country. Risks are monitored in periodic dedicated
committee meetings both in the subsidiaries and centrally.
These functions rely on local representatives for risk
measurement and exposure monitoring and ensure that
limits are respected at the Group level.
The second line of defence includes:
●The Internal Control Department, in the Risk Management
function, and the internal controllers of the Group entities,
which control the level of compliance of transactions
with the management rules defined in the procedures.
More specifically, they verify the relevance of the first line
of defence;
●The Risk and Banking Regulations Department, in the Risk
Management function, which oversees the deployment of
the Group’s risk governance policy, verifies the efficiency of
risk
management
by
the
corporate
functions
and
compliance with the established limits and alert thresholds,
ensuring that the Risk Committee of the Mobilize F.S. Board
of Directors is notified when those thresholds are exceeded;
●The
Compliance
function,
which
coordinates
the
compliance measures and ensures that they are properly
implemented throughout Mobilize F.S..
The third line of defence is the internal audit function, which
aims to provide assurance to the Mobilize F.S. Board of
Directors and Senior Management about the degree of control
over transactions and the oversight exercised by the first two
lines.
The risk management system covers all the macroprocesses
of Mobilize F.S. and includes the following tools:
●A list of the main critical and significant risks for which a
pilot, appetite level, alert thresholds and limits are defined
(Risk Appetite Framework). For each risk, a detailed
analysis is performed that identifies the components of
the risk and the management and oversight principles
that keep it in line with the risk appetite level. These
elements are reviewed at least once a year in connection
with the Mobilize F.S. Group’s business model and
strategy;

●The
mapping
of
operational
management
rules
contributes to risk management; it is deployed in all of the
Mobilize F.S. Group’s consolidated subsidiaries. This
mapping is updated regularly by the corporate functions.
The level of control of operational management rules is
assessed annually by the designated process owners,
who are responsible for risk management in their
respective areas of competence in line with Group
standards, for defining and updating the corresponding
procedures and first-level controls;
●A regulatory monitoring system run by the Risk and
Banking
Regulations
Department,
in
the
Risk
Management function, in liaison with officers in the
corporate functions, with a view to monitoring, analysing
and informing operational staff of any regulatory
changes affecting Mobilize F.S.;
●The
incident
collection
database
helps
to
identify
malfunctions that correspond to predefined criteria and
enables the corrective and preventive measures required to
control risks to be put in place. This database is used for
internal and regulatory reporting purposes. The system sets
thresholds for immediately communicating incidents to the
Mobilize F.S. Executive Committee, the Mobilize F.S. Board of
Directors, the Renault Group Ethics and Compliance
Committee (CEC), the French Prudential Supervisory
Authority (ACPR) and the European Central Bank.
Bodies and actors involved in internal control
and risk management within Mobilize F.S.
The Board of Directors of Mobilize F.S., as supervisory body,
has the following responsibilities:
●It decides on the bank’s business strategy and monitors
the implementation of supervisory procedures by the
Executive Directors and the Executive Committee to
ensure effective and prudent management;
●It approves and periodically reviews the strategies and
policies for taking on, managing, monitoring and
reducing risk;
●It examines the governance model, periodically assesses
its effectiveness, and ensures that corrective action is
taken to remedy any shortcomings;
●It oversees the publication and communication processes
and checks the quality and reliability of information due to
be published and disclosed by the company.
Within this framework, it devotes at least one annual meeting
to reviewing the internal control system and validates the
annual report on internal control sent to the ACPR.
In carrying out its tasks, the Board of Directors relies, in
particular, on the work of four specialised Committees of
this Board:
●The Audit and Accounts Committee meets four times a
year. It is responsible for preparing, presenting and
monitoring the financial statements, overseeing the
statutory audit of the separate and consolidated financial
statements, monitoring the independence of the statutory
auditors and the definition of their non-auditing services,
recommending the appointment of the statutory auditors
and monitoring their rotation, verifying the effectiveness of
internal control and risk management systems, reviewing
the audit plan, analysing the audits carried out and
reviewing investments in unconsolidated companies;
●The Risk Committee meets eight times a year. Its tasks
include examining the risk maps and validating their
definition, analysing and validating risk limits at the Mobilize
F.S. level in line with the Board’s risk appetite. It assists the
Board in terms of control. It is also responsible for analysing
action plans in the event that limits or alert thresholds are
exceeded and for examining the pricing systems for
products and services. In parallel with the Compensation
Committee, it also has the task of examining whether the
compensation policy is compatible with the company’s risk
exposure. With a view to advising the Board of Directors, this
committee is also responsible for the analysis and approval
of the internal control report, compliance with capital
(ICAAP) and liquidity (ILAAP) regulations. The recovery plan
and significant aspects of the rating and estimating
processes derived from the company’s internal credit risk
models are also concerned;
●The Compensation Committee meets at least twice a
year. It examines the compensation policy for company
officers and the Head of Risk Management annually and
prepares decisions for the Board of Directors regarding
the compensation of individuals who have an impact on
risk and risk management;
●The Appointments Committee meets at least twice a year.
One of its tasks is to recommend directors to the Board of
Directors. It is also in charge of the annual review of the
Board of Directors, including its structure, membership,
gender diversity and breadth of directors’ knowledge, skills
and experience. It submits nominations to the Board for
Executive Directors, the Chief Executive Officer, Deputy
Chief Executive Officers and the holders of key positions.
The Executive Committee, the Group’s Senior Management
body, directs the Mobilize F. S. policy and strategy.
Senior Management relies on the following Committees to
oversee the Group’s risk management:
●The Financial Committee, which reviews the following
topics: economic analysis and forecasts, cost of funds,
liquidity risks, interest rate risk and counterparty risk in
the different areas and subsidiaries of the Group. The
balance sheet and income statement of the Mobilize F.S.
holding company are also analysed to make the
necessary adjustments to intra-Group transfer pricing;
●The Capital and Liquidity Committee steers the evolution
of capital and refinancing needs according to business
activity forecasts;
●The Credit Committee, which approves commitments
exceeding the authorisation limits of subsidiaries and the
Group Head of Commitments;
●The Credit Risk Committee, which validates the action
plans in the event that the cost of risk levels validated for
each country as part of the budget process are exceeded;
●The Performance Committee, which ensures that the risk
and cost of capital are taken into account in the
profitability analysis by subsidiary and by segment;
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
369

## Page 372

Renault Group
Risk and control
●The
Regulatory
Committee,
which
reviews
major
regulatory changes, prudential supervision and action
plans, and validates internal rating models and the
associated management policy;
●The Internal Control, Operational Risk and Compliance
Committee oversees the entire Group internal control
system, monitors the results of first-level, second-level
and third-level controls on processes throughout the
Group, and decides on the necessary adaptations of
resources, systems and procedures. It defines, manages
and monitors the principles of the operational risk
management policy and compliance control system. It
also keeps track of action plans. This body also exists
within the Mobilize F.S. Group subsidiaries;
●The New Product Committee, which approves new
products before they are marketed, by ensuring the
compliance of the new products with the Group’s
commercial policy, the Group’s budget requirements,
locally applicable legislation and Mobilize F.S. Group risk
governance;
●The Sustainability and Climate and Environmental Risk
Committee, which monitors the Group’s exposure to physical
and transition risks, and emission-reduction targets.
The Mobilize F.S. Group Regulatory Compliance Officer
reports to the Chief Executive Officer. This Officer is
responsible for compliance by Mobilize F.S. in areas such as
the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing,
ethics, whistleblowing, prevention of corruption, legal, tax
and regulatory oversight and the related control plan.
The Director of the Internal Control Department (DCI), who
reports to the Head of Risk Management, is responsible for
the permanent control of the organisation and direction of
the general Internal Control system for the entire Group. In
terms of internal control supervision in the Mobilize FS Group
subsidiaries, the DCI is supported by internal controllers,
who report to it functionally. Similarly, the DCI is supported
by employees within the central functions to manage the
internal control supervision system within the Mobilize FS
Group divisions.
The Director of the Risk and Banking Regulation Department
(DRRB), who reports to the Chief Risk Officer, ensures the
deployment of the Risk Governance Policy within the Group
and ensures its consistency with the Risk Appetite
Framework
defined
by
the
Board
of
Directors.
The DRRB ensures the reliability of risk measurement
metrics, the completeness of risk management systems for
each risk and the effective exercise of such management.
More specifically, the DRRB verifies the effectiveness of the
reporting and alert feedback channels from the subsidiaries
to the corporate functions and prepares a summary report
on the risks for the management bodies and the Risk
Committee of the Board of Directors. Where appropriate, the
DRRB verifies the adequacy of the corrective measures
developed in the event of failures and their effective
implementation by the management functions. It plays a
central role in monitoring the Group’s compliance with
applicable prudential regulations.
The Audit and Periodic Control Director for the Mobilize F.S.
Group reports to the Chief Executive Officer. Independent of the
Permanent Control Department, this Director works with the
various subsidiaries based on a multiyear audit plan validated
by the Audit and Accounts Committee. Audits result in written
reports including recommendations that are submitted to the
Internal Control Committee and the Audit and Accounts
Committee. The findings and recommendations are presented
in the annual report on internal control.

4.2 Renault Group risk factors
Renault Group identifies risk factors to which it is exposed
using the formalised risk management approach, which is
outlined in chapter 4.1.
The risk factors presented in this chapter are, as of the date
of this document, those identified by the Group, the
criticality of which is assessed at higher levels of risk and
which may have a significant adverse effect on its image,
assets, the conduct of its activities, its performance or the
achievement of its objectives.
Risks were classified into four distinct categories: strategic,
operational, legal and compliance, and financial risks.
This in-depth work, combined with the evolving economic
and
geopolitical
context
and
the
rapidly
changing
automotive industry, has made it possible to update the risk
mapping compared with previous publications. Some risks
have changed in terms of content or net criticality level,
some have been added, and some have been removed.
In 2024, the mapping of major risks was thoroughly reviewed
using a top-down approach, with more than sixty key
managers of the Group interviewed.
However, it cannot be ruled out that other risk factors currently
considered insignificant or not identified could adversely
affect Renault Group in the future. Also, possible changes in the
Group’s medium-term strategic plan could result in changes in
the nature or relative importance of risk factors. It should be
noted that the major risk mapping is updated every year, in
close
collaboration
with
the
preparatory
work
and
implementation of the strategic plan, so that the plan
integrates the appropriate action plans designed to respond to
the operational or strategic risks identified.
The major risk factors are summarised in the table below by
risk category and decreasing order of criticality.

## Page 374

Renault Group
Risk and control
4.2.1 Strategic risks
Risk of inadequate product, service and innovation strategy
Description
Risk management
In a highly competitive automotive market that is
constantly changing in terms of regulations, customer
expectations, trends and technologies Renault Group is
exposed to the risk of an insufficient match between its
capacity for innovation, its range of products and services
and the expectations of different markets. Such a situation
could have a negative impact on its sales, turnover or
income statement. Specific risks have been identified
relating to:
●The enhancement of the technological content of vehicles
and associated ecosystems, particularly in the areas of
vehicle connectivity and associated services, electronics
and software, and electrified vehicles;
●The continuous increase in vehicle costs driven by stricter
regulations and an inflationary environment, with the
potential difficulty in passing on these costs to sale prices,
thus weakening the economic equations and the future of
certain product;
●The ongoing transition of the engine offering in terms of
technologies (combustion, hybrid and electric engines)
and mix: this range of technologies may not be perfectly
suited to customers’ budgets, their uses, the CO2 emission
thresholds of various countries/markets and the Group’s
CAFE trajectory;
●Preserving ROCE (or return on capital employed), with the
ambition for it to exceed 30% from 2025, while limiting R&D
and Capex spending to a maximum of 8% of the Group’s
revenue, which could increase the risk related to decisions
made regarding innovations and their alignment with
customer expectations.
In addition to certain risks mentioned above, the electric
vehicle activity is particularly affected by:
●Intense competition from both long-standing market
players and newer players, especially Asian companies in
2024, heavily supported in their domestic markets,
specializing in the sale of electric or electrified vehicles
(hybrids, plug-in hybrids, or range-extended vehicles);
●The volatility of consumer demand for electric vehicles,
especially due to price, volatility in oil and electricity costs
depending on the period and country, autonomy, safety
and perceived quality;
●Changes in public policies related to evolution of public
policies related to electric vehicles: incentives for
purchase, rules related to CAFE and development of
charging infrastructure.
Should the reference assumptions used in the Group’s
product development decisions be strongly called into
question, the Group may have to recognise impairment on
fixed assets (investment and capitalised development
expenses, depreciated over the life of the vehicle) or book a
provision to cover the contractual indemnities to be paid, if
any, due to the failure to meet a minimum purchase volume.
For example, in 2024, an impairment loss of €0.3 billion was
recognised
on
specific
vehicle
developments
and
production assets (€0.5 billion in 2023).
The definition of the Group’s future products is based on customer studies and
analyses of automotive competitors, so that market expectations and
developments and industry trends can be identified. It is also increasingly
informed, on a global scale, by anticipatory technology watch by all of the
Group’s development stakeholders, of the automotive industry and beyond.
These teams are based or have direct links with the most innovative
geographical areas, for example in China, Israel and the United States. The
development of new models or components is decided based on this work and
an evaluation of the anticipated profitability, calculated over the projected life
cycle.
The organisation of Renault Group into brands and Business Units, supported
by partnerships, enables it to best meet market expectations. The main
characteristics are:
●Organisation by brand makes it possible to be as close as possible to
consumer expectations. The brands are able to make the most relevant
choices and decisions to strengthen their identity and can rely on the
expertise of the central functions and the bodies and technologies
developed by Renault Group or with its partners;
●The Ampere Business Unit, which develops expertise in electric and software
technologies, also focuses on understanding the market and its evolution: in
2024, Renault Group notably expanded its presence in China with the
establishment of the ACDC (Advanced China Development Center) entity by
Ampere, which enables close monitoring of market trends and automotive
players;
●A governance structure that allows for the development of a specific and
complementary strategy for each of the brands and Business Units, while
also ensuring decision-making at the group level during meetings dedicated
to product plans and strategic developments (particularly the bi-annual
Strategy Days);
●The numerous partnerships established by the group (See section 1.3.5) that
allow the group to leverage the best innovation practices on the market.
Beyond the control provided by this organisation, to ensure the robustness of
the product plan, the Group strives to:
●Offer a diverse and balanced product portfolio that is based on
complementary brands, meets customer expectations across different
segments and markets and reduces the risk of dependence on a single
market, segment or customer type. In the electric vehicle market, in 2024,
Renault Group rounded out the affordable and competitive vehicle range
initiated by the Dacia Spring with the launch of the Renault 5, which will soon
be complemented by the Renault 4 and Twingo;
●Offer a diversified and adaptable engine portfolio (ICE, LPG, hybrid, plug-in
hybrid, electric, hydrogen) to meet customer expectations in different markets
and enable us to respond to any changes in the mix.

04
Risk of ineffectiveness of M&A and partnerships
Description
Risk management
Renault Group’s strategic development relies on partnerships,
whether capital-intensive or not, as well as OEM partnerships.
Since the launch of Renaulution, this lever is used significantly in the
electric vehicle value chain (suppliers of batteries, semiconductors
and electric motors), in the development of the Software Defined
Vehicle, in new mobilities and in the development of commercial
vehicles, allowing them to benefit from the best technologies and
new skills.
A lack of control over the process of selecting and managing
partnerships could restrict Renault Group’s ability to drive its
development in these new value chains with financial and
reputational impacts: the need to internally develop topics currently
outsourced, over a longer time frame, and failure to deliver on the
plan communicated to investors.
Furthermore, if the value of the partnership is reduced or the partner
defaults, the financial impact on the Group’s accounts could be
significant.
Renault Group has put in place a centralised governance system for
partnerships, co-managed by five permanent key functions: the
Finance Division, the Partnership Department, the Legal Department,
the Strategy Division and the Organisation and Transformation
Division.
This governance ensures that partnerships are managed in line with
the Group’s strategic objectives while providing support to the
business lines and Business Units that are the business owners of
them.
In 2024, progress actions focused on improving the decision-making
process, aligning acquisitions with the Group’s strategic directions
and properly integrating acquisitions (Post-Merger Integration).
Each year, all investments are reviewed, and an impairment test is
conducted whenever there is an indication of a loss in value
(impairment rules for non-controlled entities are detailed in note 2–J of
the consolidated financial statements and impairment rules for
associated companies are outlined in note 2–M, section 5.2.2.6.2).
Impairment losses recognised in 2024 on equity interests are reported
in note 13 to the consolidated financial statements (section 5.2.2.6.4).
Risk of insufficient response to disruption of after-sales
Description
Risk management
The after-sales activity is a recurring pillar of Renault Group’s
profitability: it is currently facing major risks of disruption, such as
the development of digital tools that transform the commercial
relationship with the customer and the arrival of new players that
are
changing
the
traditional
after-sales
model.
These
developments
pose
threats
to
the
Group’s
profitability
of the after-sales activity.
The three main risks facing the after-sales activity are:
●the growth in the number of electric vehicles, which causes the
average cost of maintenance and servicing operations to fall due
to the less complex nature of electrical components;
●regulatory changes (GSR2), which increase the number of
advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and reduce
accidents and therefore the number of repair shop visits;
●the independent aftermarket (IAM), which represents a
significant and growing share of the market and is giving rise to
new players with a strong digital component.
The potential impact is primarily financial, with the risk of being
unable to meet the expected profit levels for the after-sales
activity, but also potentially legal and reputational, if replacement
parts cannot be supplied for ten years after a vehicle is
discontinued.
To meet this challenge, the Group has implemented a three-pronged
after-sales strategy:
●provide a customer experience at the best level in line with the
promises of each of the brands by relying on common, modernised
assets (use of connectivity data and remote software updates,
digitisation of the customer experience, logistics tools) to generate
more value;
●deliver offers adapted to the entire life cycle of the vehicle, in terms
of repairs and customisation, to increase customer loyalty,
particularly among those who own an older vehicle;
●develop a relevant and qualitative independent after-sales offer
(IAM) based on the manufacturer’s experience, both physical and
digital, to win over new multi-brand customers and win back
customers who have left our networks. This offer is supported by the
companies Motrio, Kadensis and Fixter.
Progress actions were strengthened in 2024:
●with the Purchasing function, regarding suppliers in difficulty, to
enable continuity of supply of spare parts and find repair solutions
for our customers’ vehicles;
●with the IS-IT Division, concerning the digital transformation of
after-sales: structuring the roadmap and prioritising projects,
particularly those relating to next-generation connected services.
Improvement of indicators relating to backlogged orders (-40%
compared with 2023 in value) and loans for immobilised vehicles
(-55% compared with 2023) illustrates the progress made in delivery
times for spare parts.

Renault Group

4.2.2 Operational risks
Risk of supply chain disruption
Renault Group’s business relies on a complex system of supply and delivery chains, both upstream and downstream of its production
facilities. Various components of these chains may prove to be defective, despite the existing control system. Such defects could
negatively impact sales, turnover, the Group’s profits or customer satisfaction. They are part of a situation of interdependence of the
Group’s industrial network extended to its suppliers, in an unstable economic and geopolitical context, and can be analysed according to
the following typology:
●supplier failure;
●disruption of supply of raw materials;
●disruption of supply or transport systems
This risk, as outlined, is subject to cross-company governance involving a multidisciplinary team (engineering, procurement, and supply
chain functions) to ensure a holistic view and develop shared resilience strategies, for example regarding the robustness of supplier
panels, the anticipation of supplier difficulties, the robustness of business continuity plans, the medium and long-term capability vision
shared with suppliers (materials, EV components, vehicle logistics).
Supplier failure
Description
Risk management
Renault Group relies on a network of suppliers of Bought-Out Parts that
includes more than 500 supplier groups, with around forty groups
representing approximately 50% of purchase demand. Renault Group
also relies on around 200 strategic service provider groups in addition
to several thousand small suppliers working in these areas.
Renault Group suppliers may present risks of failure that may lead
to the undesired interruption of commercial relations and
generate risks in terms of compliance in the design and production
of parts, compliance with delivery deadlines and provision of the
necessary production capacities.
The nature of risks affecting suppliers can be diverse: financial,
strategic, customer or supplier dependency, or industrial, related
to the supply chain.
HORSE has become the leading first-tier supplier for component
parts, gearboxes and engines, and its suppliers are very sensitive
to variations in the economic environment.
Renault Group also pays particular attention to the ESG
(Environment, Social, Governance) risks applied to its suppliers
relating to safety and respect for human rights, respect for the
environment, carbon impact and compliance, in particular with
regard to anticorruption.
In 2024, the effects of the semiconductor crisis and rising energy
prices partially faded. However, the attrition of vehicle markets at
the end of the year, and in particular the electric vehicle market,
weighed heavily on suppliers’ cash flow and their production
scheme. This was evidenced by the fall in the stock market
valuations of all automotive equipment manufacturers, as well as
the announcement of numerous redundancy and restructuring
plans, including by equipment manufacturers that are world
leaders in their field.
These factors contributed to a significant increase in the
proportion of suppliers at risk across the entire value chain. This
proportion, considered acceptable in a range between 5% and 10%
of the purchasing volume, is around 25% to 30% at the end of 2024,
which increases the risk of failure.
The Group applies a comprehensive risk management system:
●a supplier risk prevention policy designed to make suppliers
responsible for their own risks, compliance, the robustness of their
supply chain and their financial and strategic management;
●capacity guidelines aimed at controlling, within a two-year time
frame, supply risks not covered by the existence of available
industrial capacities;
●a process for detecting non-compliance (quality, traceability) of
delivered parts;
●a supplier risk management system incorporating multicriteria ratings
on, in particular, past and forecast financial risks, strategic risks
(dependence,
Renault
exposure),
industrial
and
supply
risks,
technological breakthroughs, climate risks, social and ESG risks.
Since 2024, the suppliers with the greatest business impact (in
particular, those producing parts fitted on more than a million
vehicles per year) have had their business continuity plan analysed
more closely with monitoring of action plans in the commodities
committees.
The entire system enabled Renault Group to protect its procurement
in 2024 without suffering a major loss of production.
The management system relating to ESG risks is included in Renault
Group’s vigilance plan and described in section 2.3.5.

04
Disruption in supply of raw materials
Description
Risk management
The main raw materials (steel, plastic, aluminium, copper, as well as
lithium, nickel and cobalt for electric vehicles) account for about a
quarter of the total value of purchase of parts and materials used in
the manufacture of new vehicles produced by the Group and present
specific risks, in particular:
●potential supply restrictions due to insufficient supply relative to
demand, a production failure by our suppliers (natural disaster,
social conflict, etc.) or disruptions in the supply logistics chain;
●prices, which can fluctuate significantly and suddenly, for example
due to volatility in prices of energy and certain minerals,
particularly for materials used in electric batteries;
●potential tensions over access to the market for industrial metal
waste, particularly steel and copper, which is needed to increase
recycling to support carbon neutrality trajectories for raw
materials;
●non-compliance with ESG (Environment, Social, Governance)
criteria, particularly for mining operations.
If these risks were to materialise, this would result in cost increases,
production stoppages or reputational impacts, ultimately with a
significant impact on Renault Group’s profitability.
The risks associated with materials management are dealt with in a
monthly meeting between the Engineering Division and the Purchasing
Division, which makes it possible to validate the technical strategies
and supplier panels on the various material technologies, for mass
production procurement as well as for future vehicle projects.
In its purchasing, technical, monitoring and financial hedging policies,
Renault Group includes systems to identify and limit supply and over
cost risks:
●reduction in the use or substitution of sensitive materials (absence
of rare earths, for example, in new electric vehicle engines);
●implementation of a multi-sourcing strategy for high-stakes
materials such as steel or cast aluminium to optimise costs while
securing supplies (this enabled Renault Group to be resilient in the
face of the supply crisis in the flat aluminium sector in Europe in 2024
by implementing substitution solutions with no impact on its
industrial production);
●with regard to the materials contained in purchased parts, support
for suppliers to enable them to use substitute materials quickly in
the event of a supply crisis; 
●increasing
use
of
recycled
materials
and
control
of
the
management of its industrial waste (steels, aluminium, plastics) to
meet its growing needs. The Future Is NEUTRAL, Renault Group’s
circular economy entity, is strategic for optimising the collection
and reuse of its waste with material suppliers;
●securing the supply of responsible strategic materials contained in
batteries and association with players in the value chain (battery
producers, cathode active material suppliers, etc.) to share
qualification and use; with this in mind, agreements for the supply of
lithium, low-carbon nickel and cobalt have been signed since 2021;
●continuous monitoring of markets and suppliers for critical
materials and monthly reassessment of price forecasts for key
materials;
●implementation of a systematic financial hedging policy for key
indexed materials to reduce risks and limit exposure to market
fluctuations, and negotiation of fixed annual contracts for non-
indexed materials.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
375

## Page 378

Renault Group
Risk and control
Disruption of supply or transport systems
Description
Risk management
This risk relates to possible failures in the planning, production,
transport or delivery of parts, upstream of production sites, or
vehicles to the sales network, as well as a failure to control supply
chain costs.
These failures may be due to: 
●insufficient transport capacity because of a lack of drivers (impact
of the war in Ukraine) or means of transport (lorries, boats,
containers) or due to port congestion;
●cyberattacks or information system failures at logistics providers
or customs systems in the countries concerned;
●the geopolitical context (e.g., border closures or insecurity in certain
geographical areas);
●natural disasters affecting our logistics flows or storage areas;
●new regulations, including those relating to European road transport
(e.g., Mobility package for drivers).
The occurrence of these risks may delay the supply of parts or
vehicles, cause damage to stored vehicles and generate significant
additional costs. This may impact customer satisfaction, sales,
turnover and profits of the Group.
To deal with this risk, Renault Group has set up a comprehensive
prevention
and
protection
system,
which
is
becoming
increasingly robust:
●integrated Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP) process for plants,
suppliers and logistics, enabling the balancing of commercial
demand with industrial response through the efficiency of new
digital tools;
●enhanced collaboration with our logistics suppliers on medium- and
long-term volume visibility to limit the risk on transport capacity (on
road and at sea);
●gradual implementation since 2023 of a supply chain control tower,
making it possible, through the digitisation of processes and an end-
to-end vision, to anticipate and manage crises in a coherent,
coordinated way within Renault Group. For example, for the
transport of parts, the Control Tower provides real-time information
on the risks of truck delays, measures the impact on stocks in plants
and suggests alternative solutions to avoid a plant stoppage
(emergency transport, change of route or alternative lorry);
●deployment of an action plan to reduce cybersecurity risks in supply
systems and requirement for TISAX certification when joining the
logistics provider panel;
●a central crisis management system for the supply chain, tried and
tested over many years, and the involvement of all the business lines
concerned to ensure the best possible response to one-off crises
and unforeseen events.
The successive crises (COVID-19, climate disasters, the electronic
components crisis, earthquakes in Morocco and Türkiye, the war in
Ukraine, and more recently the Red Sea crisis) have demonstrated the
supply chain’s ability to operate in a closed-loop system with various
sectors to respond swiftly to environmental fluctuations.

04
Risk of inadequate product and service execution
Description
Risk management
In a highly competitive automotive market undergoing significant
transformation in terms of both technologies and players,
development timelines and costs, Renault Group faces the risk of
not developing products, software and services at the expected
level of competitiveness in terms of time frame, cost, quality or
innovation.
This risk could stem from:
●increased complexity of technologies, particularly in software
and connectivity;
●a complex organisation generating numerous interfaces;
●a lack of control over relationships with subcontractors and
partners regarding division of responsibilities, intellectual
property,
cost
control
or
difficulty
integrating
modules
developed by these multiple suppliers;
●A lack of skills to support the transition from combustion engines
to hybrid and electric engines and to software;
●The many new regulations to consider in a short period;
●increased
competition
from
certain
players,
including
automotive manufacturers or battery suppliers, particularly from
Asia, benefiting from significantly lower production costs than
European manufacturers due to economies of scale, direct and
localised access to raw materials, lower energy and labour costs
and potentially subsidies.
These factors could result in entry barriers and costs that are too
high in relation to forecasts or market expectations or delayed
start of sales. A quality or safety defect could lead to additional
costs and customer dissatisfaction, impacting Renault Group’s
image. Overall, the occurrence of this risk would affect Renault
Group’s sales, turnover and profitability.
To manage this risk, the Engineering Division, in continuous
transformation, relies on competency centres, notably with the
creation of Ampere, the specialist in intelligent electric vehicles,
batteries, and software, which has demonstrated its ability to
innovate while respecting the Group’s standards. At the same time,
the new role of Platform Global Leader, across Business Units, aims to
ensure profitability for the various brands. 
Engineering is increasingly relying on the decentralised engineering
centres of Renault Group that are enhancing their skills. Beyond
activities for adapting vehicles to the local context, they perform
cross-functional tasks, taking on vehicle project design increasingly
early in the process. This policy is an important lever for reducing
project entry tickets.
In 2024, Renault Group launched the “Leap100“ plan, which aims to
reduce the development time of future vehicles by up to 100 weeks,
through increased parts sharing, digitalisation, and simplifying the
development process.
Renault
Group
created
the
entity
ACDC
(Advanced
China
Development Center), led by Ampere. This team of engineers and
buyers works closely with Chinese partners. Thanks to it, Renault
Group aims to learn from the Chinese ecosystem to improve and
accelerate its development processes.
The “Renaulution Virtual Twin“ digital project has equipped
engineering with a new data management tool and provides a
collaborative platform from design to after-sales. Advances in digital
simulation
have
improved
design
robustness,
increased
the
efficiency of validation processes, facilitated relationships with
suppliers and partners and reduced development costs. This project
is supported by a massive deployment of artificial intelligence.
The partnership strategy, notably driven by Ampere in the fields of
software, batteries, and power electronics, helps reduce costs,
maintain high technological agility, while retaining intellectual
property of strategic innovations. With this agility, Renault Group
successfully integrated LFP (Lithium, Iron, Phosphate) technology into
its cars in 18 months. In software, Ampere will offer the first European
Software Defined Vehicle with the launch of FlexEVan in 2026.
To support these evolutions, engineering has deployed an ambitious
skill development plan since 2023, particularly focused on electric
technologies. Since 2024, skills have been subject to annual and
targeted evaluations to identify and fill specific training needs.
Renault Group has also implemented a robust quality plan including:
●quality assurance systems at the core of the Group’s operational
activities;
●an organisation and activities related to product safety and general
safety, aiming to secure risks linked to the physical integrity of users
of its products;
●a market surveillance system to quickly identify sources of
customer dissatisfaction and act accordingly: correction of quality
issues, vehicle recall process, especially in the case of regulatory or
safety issues.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024

## Page 380

Renault Group
Risk and control
Risk of cyberattack and failure of information systems
Description
Risk management
The conduct of Renault Group’s activities depends, continuously and
increasingly, on the proper functioning of its information systems.
In this area, the Group faces many challenges that increase its
exposure to threats and make cybersecurity a major issue, particularly:
●cloud strategy;
●digitisation;
●industry 4.0;
●increased connectivity and dependence on vehicle software;
●development of connected services;
●strengthening of the cybersecurity regulatory environment.
The types of risk that could adversely affect the Group’s activities
are as follows:
●global or partial cyberattacks, targeting the Group’s interests or,
as a side effect, national interests (cybercriminality). These attacks
may aim to access sensitive data (strategic, product, service or
personal), steal or alter such data, or block services or even all of the
Group’s information systems;
●incidents that could affect the integrity or continuity of services
hosted in our infrastructures, including those of our partners and
suppliers, and in vehicles could be affected;
●non-compliance with IT standards or practices required by
legislation, external authorities or contracts with suppliers or the
state of the art;
●non-compliance with UN Regulation No. 155, which may impact
the certification of Renault Group’s CSMS (Cyber Security
Management System) as well as the approval of vehicles.
The materialisation of these risks, despite the continuous
strengthening of systems aimed at controlling them, could have
major
financial
impacts
associated
with
the
temporary
suspension of the Group’s activities (turnover, net income),
remediation costs for vehicles and infrastructure, penalties or
increased insurance premiums.
There could also be adverse impacts on the Group’s image and the
confidence of third parties and customers in the Group and its brands.
Insufficiently robust and sustainable control of the risks associated
with connected vehicles and services could lead to adverse impacts
on safety and the reliability of data, services or vehicles.
The general control of these risks is currently provided ensured by:
●a company-wide cybersecurity organisation to protect all Group
assets and products;
●a governance at the highest level of the company, the Leadership
Team, broken down into committees that monitor and assess the
effectiveness of information security processes and measures;
●the deployment and continuous improvement of standards to adapt
them to changes in regulations, the state of the art and the risks
affecting the Group’s activities, services and products (risk mapping
updated each year):
– the common Cybersecurity Policy for the entire Renault Group
and the organisational and technical rules for cybersecurity;
– the security master plan, regularly presented to the Audit and
Risk Committee (CAR);
– the resulting cybersecurity roadmap, validated by the CEO;
– the operational processes.
Since 2021, Renault Group has deployed a program of tests,
strengthened since 2023, on the physical integrity of infrastructures
supporting critical and strategic applications and the integrity of
data in information systems.
In 2024, Renault Group carried out two crisis exercises to verify the
efficiency and resilience of the organisation. The cybersecurity policy
has been updated and deployed across the group, and Renault
continued to improve its risk management system through:
●the regular review of performance indicators;
●the rollout of the safety programme at the Group’s various plants;
●the development of vehicle cybersecurity and associated services
in connection with the need to comply with the new regulations
(UNECE R155 & R156) on vehicle cybersecurity;
●an enhanced supervision (Security Operations Center – SOC) of
systems in all the Group’s domains (in particular IS/IT, vehicles,
connected services, cloud infrastructure and plants);
●some continued efforts to raise awareness, provide training and
develop cybersecurity skills;
●the strengthening of the protection of the Group’s systems/
infrastructures (including those that use cloud hosting);
●the participation in international and national ecosystem interest
groups (e.g. PFA, Campus Cyber, ACEA and OICA).

Risk and control
04
Risk related to geopolitical instability and market volatility
Description
Risk management
Renault Group has industrial and commercial operations in many
countries. It is exposed to various risks stemming from geopolitical
instability (tensions, armed conflicts, social unrest, political
instability), an unfavourable economic context (energy prices,
inflation and declining purchasing power, public deficits), and
regulatory constraints, which vary across regions and could
destabilise markets in a highly competitive environment.
These situations may lead to import/export restrictions, economic
sanctions, disruptions in logistics flows (cf. risk of supply chain
disruption), protectionist measures (increased customs duties),
unstable public policies and sudden market or segment variations.
For Renault Group, this could result in employee insecurity, supply
disruptions, business interruptions or asset losses, additional costs,
declines in sales, restrictions on transfers of monetary flow to the
parent company, or expropriations of Group assets, ultimately
impacting the Group’s income statement or balance sheet.
In 2024, the international context was marked by persistent
instability with the ongoing war in Ukraine and the extension of
conflict to the Middle East (war in Gaza and Lebanon, attacks on
merchant ships in the Red Sea), combined with anticipation of the
potential consequences of the US presidential election results.
Despite disparities between countries, global growth remained stable
in 2024 while inflation continued to fall. Global trade also recovered.
However, protectionist measures and geopolitical tensions continue
to pose risks of trade and supply chain disruptions.
In Europe, several countries (such as Germany and France) have
adjusted their subsidies for electric vehicle sales, which has slowed
the growth of the electric vehicle market segment.
Outside Europe, the countries or areas that expose the Group most
by their weight in sales and/or total production are Morocco,
Türkiye and Latin American countries. The respective weights in
the Group’s vehicle sales and production are as follows for each of
these countries or areas (See chapter  1.3): Morocco (3%, 17%),
Türkiye (7%, 14%) and the “Americas” (11%, 11%). Despite their
limited weight in the Group’s vehicle sales and production, China
and the Asia-Pacific region occupy a crucial place in the battery
and electric-vehicle value chain.
In terms of industrial location, Renault Group’s geographical choices
are made considering the risks of instability. Calculations of expected
returns on investment include country-specific risk premiums. From
an operational standpoint, the Group continuously increases the level
of local integration to reduce the impact of political and foreign
exchange risks and make its products more competitive.
The “star“ invoicing scheme developed by Renault Group (where
manufacturing subsidiaries sell their exported production to Renault
s.a.s., which then resells it to commercial subsidiaries and
independent importers) enables the centralisation of non-payment
risk management and the implementation of coverage under
competitive terms. Ampere s.a.s. applies the same scheme for resale
to sales subsidiaries (and indirectly through an invoice to Renault
s.a.s. for independent importers).
The management system implemented to adapt to variations in
logistics flows caused by geopolitical crises or unexpected market
changes is described in the paragraph on supply chain failure risks.
On the commercial front, Renault Group’s exposure risk is limited by
the diversity of the countries where it sells its products.
Renault Group is organised into Business Units/brands responsible for
responding to unexpected market changes by adjusting their
production and commercial actions accordingly.
In 2024, Renault Group established a multidisciplinary control tower at
the group level to ensure precise management of its response to
changes in the electric vehicle segment in Europe, ensuring
compliance with CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) regulations
and safeguarding commercial and financial performance.
The conflict in Ukraine and its consequences on the Group’s activity
continue to be monitored on an ongoing basis. In 2024, Renault Group
strengthened its organisation for export control by recruiting additional
staff for the dedicated team and implementing multidisciplinary
governance. This team is responsible for monitoring sanctions applicable
to the Group’s activities and ensuring compliance with them. Sanctions
are analysed and translated into action plans mobilising all the divisions
involved to ensure that the Group’s activities always comply with the
applicable regulations.
Overall, Renault Group has continued to control fixed costs and
restructure industrial capacity to manage the company’s break-even
point, enhancing resilience in the event of risks significantly affecting
its operations. The global inflationary context is incorporated into the
Group’s budget assumptions and market forecasts. Sensitivity
analyses are conducted based on scenarios.

## Page 382

Renault Group
Risk and control
Risk of ineffective digital transformation
Description
Risk management
The digital transformation aims to support Renault Group’s strategy
by enabling all functions to improve productivity, reduce costs and
create new sources of value.
This transformation involves both organisational and technical changes.
The growing complexity associated with the numerous interfaces
between the information systems of the various functions, a lack of
internal and external skills and inefficient governance are all risks
that could lead to poor quality of products or platforms, a delay in the
digital transformation, insufficient productivity in the business lines
and ultimately a lower-quality customer experience and a loss of the
Group’s competitiveness.
The digital transformation is led by the IT function. It provides monthly
reports to the Leadership Team, which monitors the progress of the
digital plan’s implementation and ensures that it is consistent with the
Group’s strategic directions.
To break down silos and strengthen the alignment between strategy
and project execution, Renault Group has structured its initiatives
around value chains, covering projects and solutions such as vehicle
development, industrial efficiency, end-to-end supply chain and
connected services.
These value chains have defined and regularly adapt their three-year
digital plans in collaboration with the Group’s management team.
Operational management is conducted monthly.
On the technical front, architects ensure the coherence and
rationalisation of the products used to manage obsolescence and
control operating costs.
The deployment of artificial intelligence (in line with the Group’s
“Responsible AI“ approach) represents a powerful lever, used by the
Group to support and increase productivity in business activities. For
example, AI contributes to the productivity of the supply chain:
optimisation of transport routes and truck filling.
To further optimise and prioritise investments in line with the digital
strategy, the IS-IT Division launched an action plan at the beginning of
2025 to improve the precision of its financial indicators.
Lastly, the Group regularly engages with other key industry players to
share best practices. This active benchmarking enables Renault
Group to stay at the forefront of digital transformation.
